Stimulus / Scenario: Germany economy: trade surplus, ageing population, luxury car exports, labour mobility, declining trade union membership
Question Text: Source material about Germany’s economy
Calculate the number of Germans aged over 65 in 2017.
Explanation: Accept 17.8 m and 18 m.

Question Text: Identify two measures of living standards.
Markscheme: GDP per head / average income; HDI; quality of education / education
Explanation: If more than two are given, consider the first two.

Question Text: Explain one cause of globalisation.
Markscheme: Fall in transport costs reducing costs of exporting and importing / increasing international trade; Improvement in communications due to advances in technology / increasing growth in MNCs / encouraging consumers to buy from other countries / enabling branches in different countries to keep in touch.
Explanation: One mark for the cause identified and one mark for the explanation.

Question Text: Explain two advantages of an increase in the occupational mobility of labour.
Markscheme: Lower unemployment / increase employment workers losing one job will find another job more quickly / find another job more easily / maybe more skilled / reduce frictional unemployment / reduce structural unemployment; Higher output / higher GDP / economic growth firms experiencing higher demand will be able to expand more quickly / more elastic supply / quicker adjustment to changes in market conditions / workers may be able to multitask / change roles / may be more skilled / fewer unfilled vacancies / greater use of resources / fewer idle resources.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two advantages identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. Do not credit 'more skilled' more than once.

Question Text: Analyse why the price of German luxury cars may have increased in 2018.
Markscheme: Increase in demand rise in incomes people buy more luxury cars as they get richer / luxury cars become more affordable; rise in price of US luxury cars these are substitutes to German luxury cars; Fall in the price of petrol / gas petrol / gas is a complement to cars firms can raise revenue / profits by increasing price; Decrease in supply rise in wages increasing costs of production.

Question Text: Analyse whether the strength of German trade unions increased from 2013–2016.
Markscheme: Overview: Trade union membership fell unemployment fell and / or wages rose. Analysis of why trade union strength may have fallen: Trade union members represented a smaller proportion of labour force / proportion fell from 42.7% to 39.3% / number of members fell by approx. 5.6%; Fewer members so funds of / subscriptions to trade unions may have fallen reduces ability to take industrial action / reduces collective bargaining strength; Lower unemployment / higher wages may mean workers feel less need to join a union. Analysis of why trade union strength may have increased: Lower unemployment / higher wages may be the result of successful collective bargaining firms would have found it more difficult to replace members asking for better wages / better working conditions / firms may be reluctant to dismiss staff industrial action would have more effect / collective bargaining power may be stronger; Output / profits / demand for products may have increased. Comments: Other factors may have influenced trade union power e.g. government trade union legislation; Other factors may have influenced wages / unemployment e.g. national minimum wage / higher output / more skilled labour force.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an ageing labour force will reduce productivity.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might reduce productivity: May discourage investment workers will have less capital equipment to work with; Older workers may find it more difficult to adapt to new technology may resist change there may not be the time to train them may be less mobile; Older workers may be less fit less able to do manual work may have time off sick may have less energy / get tired work slower. Reasons why it might not reduce productivity: May encourage investment because of shortage of workers / desire to rely less on labour / help workers; Older workers may have more experience built up more skills / qualifications may need less training / have received more training may train young workers make fewer mistakes / produce better quality output; May be more reliable e.g. better punctuality; May stay with the employer longer / lower rate of labour turnover.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not immigration will increase a country’s budget surplus.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might increase a budget surplus: May increase size of the labour force quality of the labour force may rise employment may rise more people with incomes higher tax revenue more income tax / direct tax revenue more spending / higher total demand more indirect tax revenue higher profits may increase corporate tax revenue. Reasons why it might not increase a budget surplus: Some immigrants may be dependents some may be unemployed increase government spending on state benefits / unemployment benefits pensions; The government may have to spend more on education healthcare housing / infrastructure; Labour force / population may not increase if matched by emigration; Larger population size may result in the government having to spend more on measures to reduce pollution.
Explanation: Increase in the size of the labour force linked to why it might.

Stimulus / Scenario: India economic growth driven by government spending and exports; unemployment rising; risk of inflation
Question Text: Scenario about India’s economy
Define economic growth.
Markscheme: An increase in the country's / economy's output. An increase in real GDP over time. An increase in output. An increase in a country's / economy's productive capacity. An increase in productive potential.
Explanation: Allow one mark for higher income / average income / GDP per head.

Question Text: Explain the possible opportunity cost to India of exporting more textiles.
Markscheme: Opportunity cost is the (next) best alternative forgone. Textiles that have been exported might have been sold on the home market may have increased choice lowered price. Resources may have been moved to producing the textile exports / resources used to produce textiles could have been used to produce other products output / exports of other products may have been reduced these products may provide more revenue / lose revenue from not exporting these products.

Question Text: Analyse how higher government spending could increase economic growth.
Markscheme: Higher government spending can increase total (aggregate) demand / government spending is a component of GDP. Higher government spending on education and training / healthcare / infrastructure can raise productivity / skills / efficiency / quality of labour attract MNCs increase productive potential lower costs of production increase employment / reduce unemployment lower prices increase output increase exports. Government spending on benefits will increase purchasing power of the poor raising consumer expenditure. Increased government subsidies could reduce costs of production increase investment increase profits provide an incentive for firms to produce more.
Explanation: A maximum of 3 marks for a list-like approach.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government can reduce unemployment without increasing inflation.
Markscheme: Why it might: workers who have been unemployed for some time may accept jobs without a rise in wages; government spending on education and training may increase both total (aggregate) demand and total (aggregate) supply / may reduce costs of production; government subsidies may encourage firms to produce more and take on more workers and lower prices; tax revenue may be raised to finance any increase in government spending; government may reduce trade union power which could encourage employers to take on more workers.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Example answers are provided in the mark scheme for each level.

Stimulus / Scenario: Netherlands cycling economy: 1.3 bikes per person, inelastic demand, government promotion
Question Text: Scenario about bicycles in the Netherlands
Identify one difference between land and labour.
Markscheme: Land is a natural resource labour is a human resource / people; Reward for land is rent reward for labour is wages; Land is likely to be more geographically immobile than labour; Land may be more occupationally mobile than labour; Land may be in more fixed supply / scarcer than labour.
Explanation: For human resource / people not accepting workers. Accept raw materials for natural resource.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why demand for a product may be price-inelastic.
Markscheme: Lack of close substitutes not able to switch to other products if the price of the product rises; A necessity difficult to do without; Takes up a small part of income change in price will not be significant; Addictive people find it difficult to stop consuming it; New trend brand loyalty being enforced by advertising; Time / short period of time may take time to find an alternative.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse why households in one country may borrow more than households in another country.
Markscheme: A lower interest rate reduces the cost of borrowing; Financial institutions may have more funds available to lend in one country making it easier to borrow; Households / financial institutions may be more confident that they can repay employment / income may be rising in one country; Borrowing may be more socially acceptable in one country people may not mind getting into debt; Younger households may be less worried about debt; Rise in unemployment / fall in GDP / recession in one country / lower income may result in borrowing to buy basic necessities; Inflation borrowers will gain if the rate is higher than the rate of interest.
Explanation: Accept an approach that analyses why households in the other country borrow less. A maximum of 3 marks for a list-like approach.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should encourage more people to cycle.
Markscheme: Why it should: reduce external costs; improve the environment; improve people's health; reduce traffic congestion; may reduce need to import petrol. Why it should not: may involve costs e.g. building cycle paths; may result in unemployment in car industry; may lead to more imports of bicycles; market forces will result in the most efficient allocation of resources.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Example answers are provided in the mark scheme for each level.

Stimulus / Scenario: Egypt smoking rates high; taxes on cigarettes; policies to reduce inflation and improve trade balance
Question Text: Scenario about Egypt
Identify two qualities of a good tax.
Markscheme: certainty / easy to understand / people know how much to pay; convenience / easy to pay; economy / cheap to collect; efficiency / not harm performance of markets; equity / fair / based on ability to pay; flexibility / easy to change

Question Text: Explain two reasons why governments tax cigarettes.
Markscheme: To raise revenue demand for cigarettes is price-inelastic / smoking is addictive / to spend on e.g. education; To discourage consumption / to discourage production they are a demerit good / cigarettes are more harmful than smokers realise / they cause health problems; To reduce external costs / they create external costs impose a cost on third parties / cause pollution / health problems for non-smokers; To improve the current account position cigarettes may be imported.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how a central bank could reduce inflation.
Markscheme: A central bank could use contractionary monetary policy. A central bank could increase the rate of interest increase saving reduce borrowing reduce consumer expenditure reduce total (aggregate) demand reduce demand-pull inflation. A central bank could reduce the money supply print less money / sell government bonds restrict bank lending fewer loans may reduce investment reduce consumer expenditure. Raising the exchange rate may reduce the price of imports lowering cost-push inflation. One effect of a lower interest rate might be to lower costs of production reduce cost-push inflation.
Explanation: Accept but do not expect reference to increasing reserve / liquidity ratio. Maximum of 3 marks for a list-like approach.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in government subsidies will reduce a deficit on the current account of the balance of payments.
Markscheme: Why it might: reduce costs of production lower prices, making products more internationally competitive; export revenue may rise; import expenditure may fall. Why it might not: firms may not lower prices; firms may be encouraged to be inefficient; demand for exports may not rise if quality is poor; demand for exports and imports may be price-inelastic; other countries may retaliate by imposing trade restrictions / giving subsidies; subsidies to consumers can increase demand for imports and divert exports to the home market; subsidies to producers may increase demand for imports of raw materials and capital goods.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Example answers are provided in the mark scheme for each level.

Stimulus / Scenario: Sweden mixed economy, operating inside PPC, teachers recruited from other professions
Question Text: Scenario about Sweden
Define a mixed economic system.
Markscheme: One with a public / government sector and a private sector / use of market forces; One which makes use of the price mechanism and government directives.

Question Text: Explain the difference between a point inside a PPC and a point outside a PPC.
Markscheme: A point inside indicates unemployed resources inefficient use of resources / wasted resources output is below potential output an attainable point. A point outside is unattainable not enough resources / beyond productive potential / only achievable with more resources / only achievable with better quality resources / not achievable with current resources / will be possible with future economic growth.
Explanation: Maximum of 2 marks for point inside and a maximum of 2 marks for point outside.

Question Text: Analyse why an actor may decide to become a teacher.
Markscheme: Wage rate may be higher enable more goods and services to be purchased higher living standards demand for teachers may increase; Greater job security actors often experience frictional/casual unemployment; More sociable/convenient working hours may fit in more with having children teachers may also enjoy longer holidays; Working conditions may be better may be a safer environment may have better pensions; May find they are better qualified / suited to be a teacher more likely to be successful in the career; May gain more job satisfaction enjoy helping children learn.
Explanation: Maximum of 3 marks for a list-like approach.

Question Text: Discuss whether all monopolies have low costs of production.
Markscheme: Why some have low costs: may earn high profits and so may have incentive to reinvest; may be small e.g. a local monopoly / low total costs; may be large and so may experience economies of scale; state-owned monopolies may be subsidised. Why some do not have low costs: lack of competition may reduce pressure to keep costs low; may be small so do not benefit from economies of scale; may be large so experience diseconomies of scale / high average costs; may be producing in a country with a high inflation rate.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Example answers are provided in the mark scheme for each level.

Stimulus / Scenario: Nigeria economy: dependence on oil exports, government spending policies, investment issues, rapid population growth, rice tariffs
Question Text: Source material about changes in the Nigerian economy
Calculate the value of Nigeria’s exports of goods in 2017.
Explanation: $ is not essential.

Question Text: Identify two examples of capital goods.
Markscheme: [delivery] vehicles; machines; [office] equipment

Question Text: Explain whether Nigeria had a budget deficit or a budget surplus in 2017.
Markscheme: budget deficit; government spending exceeded tax revenue; government spending was $19.5bn and tax revenue was $12.9bn.

Question Text: Explain how government spending on training may increase tax revenue in the long run.
Markscheme: Training may increase skills of workers raise productivity raise wages / incomes increase job opportunities / employment / reduce unemployment increase income tax revenue; Higher wages may increase spending / demand increase indirect tax revenue; A more productive labour force may reduce costs of production raise profits increase corporation tax revenue.

Question Text: Analyse how living standards in Nigeria compare with living standards in Ethiopia in 2017.
Markscheme: Nigeria's higher GDP per head (income) suggests Nigerians may have been able to enjoy more goods and services; Nigeria has a lower life expectancy suggesting poorer healthcare; Nigeria has a higher average number of years spent at school suggesting the labour force may be more educated; Nigeria has a higher percentage of underweight five-year olds suggesting poor nutrition / income inequality; Overall comment: Nigeria's income per head and average years spent at school suggests a higher living standard but life expectancy and percentage of five years underweight suggests a lower living standard.

Question Text: Analyse how a government could encourage firms to increase their investment.
Markscheme: Reduce the rate of interest making it cheaper to borrow encouraging borrowing as consumer spending is likely to rise; Lower corporation tax increasing the funds available to invest increasing the incentive to invest as return from investment would rise; Create greater economic certainty / confidence by e.g. keeping inflation low and stable / not changing government spending and taxation frequently making it easier for firms to plan / it can take time to get a return from investment; Provide subsidies/grants/loans decreasing cost of investments encouraging MNCs; Raise tariffs increasing demand for home produced goods; Less government regulation/policies reduces costs for firms more willing to invest.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in Nigeria’s population will benefit its economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Fall in death rate may indicate improved healthcare; Total (aggregate) demand will increase may enable existing firms to expand / increase output new firms to set up attract MNCs; Labour force may increase causing total (aggregate) supply to increase / raise productive capacity increase output / GDP; May move country towards optimum population if there are unused resources; Net immigration will increase labour force and reduce dependency ratio; More tax revenue can be spent on improving e.g., public services. Reasons why it might not: Birth rate is rising will increase dependency resources which may have increased economic growth may have to be devoted to rearing the children; Labour force may fall in the short run to look after the children; Pressure will be put on the environment there may be more pollution causing overcrowding / poor health; If food output cannot be increased may be more malnourishment/famine / poverty more food is required / food may have to be imported food prices may increase; Population is rising but age structure may be changing as there is net emigration of young people may result in reduction in labour force; [Opportunity] cost for government which has to spend on health/education rather than infrastructure; Increase in output to meet demand results in faster usage of resources in country and shortages.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in Nigeria’s import tariff on rice would increase the output of Nigerian rice.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: A higher tariff may raise the price of imported rice may reduce demand for / consumption of imported rice may cause Nigerians to demand more home-produced rice as it is cheaper effect would be greater, the greater the elasticity of demand for the import of rice may enable Nigerian rice farmers to raise their price / make more profit; A tariff may raise government tax revenue some of this could be used to subsidise Nigerian rice farmers / modernise farming methods and so raise productivity. Reasons why it might not: Nigerian farmers are less productive than Asian competitors have high(er) costs of production Nigerian price may still be higher even with the higher import tariff; The supply of Nigerian rice may be price-inelastic so even if Nigerian farmers could sell their rice for more, they may have difficulties adjusting supply; More Nigerian rice will not be sold if quality is poor consumers my prefer the varieties of rice produced in other countries other countries retaliate by imposing higher tariffs current account surplus may be reduced; Increase in import tariffs may be small making little difference to import prices and consumption; Not enough resources/ factors of production e.g., farmers to produce more rice in Nigeria.

Stimulus / Scenario: Great Pacific Garbage Patch pollution affecting fishing economies and increasing social costs
Question Text: Scenario about ocean plastic pollution
Identify the two types of costs that make up social cost.
Markscheme: Private costs; External costs.

Question Text: Explain two causes of a shift to the right in the demand curve for fish.
Markscheme: Rise in the price of a substitute for fish people will switch away from buying e.g. chicken to buying fish; Fall in the price of a complement for fish people will buy more of the complement; A rise in income people will be able to afford to buy more fish; A report stating eating fish is good for health / changes in tastes people may buy more fish to try to increase their life expectancy; Increase in population size more people to buy fish; A successful advertising campaign may persuade more people to buy fish.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two causes identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how cleaning up pollution could benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Will reduce external costs improve health / less sickness raise quality of life / standard of living; Less (government) spending on healthcare more can be spent on other priorities e.g., education; Cleaning up pollution may create jobs / employment reduce unemployment may attract MNCs increase in incomes reduction in poverty; Recycling of some of the material collected could increase the country's output less use of non-renewable resources; Better working conditions productivity of labour could increase reduce costs of production increase output reduce unemployment reduce inflation; Improve the environment Increase tourism increase exports of services improve the current account position; Reduction in ocean pollution will improve fish stock improve quality of fish greater catches of fish leading to rise in incomes / revenue / GDP.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not countries specialising benefits consumers.
Markscheme: Why it might: countries may specialise in what they are best at producing; output may rise; greater availability; costs of production may fall; prices may fall; quality may rise; better paid jobs increasing purchasing power of consumers. Why it might not: a country may gain a monopoly of production, raising price and lowering quality; choice may be reduced / greater reliance on imports; other countries' producers may have lower health and safety standards; supply problems may affect a particular country, cutting off supplies; trade disputes may cut off supplies.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: US economy: very low unemployment, low inflation, rising productivity but persistent poverty
Question Text: Scenario about US macroeconomic performance
Define cyclical unemployment.
Markscheme: Workers without jobs due to a lack or fall in total (aggregate) demand; more people unemployed than there are job vacancies; in a recession / economic downturn.
Explanation: Nothing for a fall in demand for a product.

Question Text: Explain two ways a firm could increase the productivity of its workers.
Markscheme: Raise wages / performance related pay / commissions increase motivation of workers; Provide training increase workers' skills / efficiency; Division of labour by firm enables greater specialisation; Buy capital goods / invest workers will be working with better equipment; Reduce working hours workers may feel fresher / more alert; Improve working conditions reduce stress; Subsidise workers' healthcare making them fitter / fewer days lost through illness; Working from home reduce stress from travelling.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two ways identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the advantages of a low rate of inflation.
Markscheme: Low inflation means prices are still rising but not a high rate; May stop purchasing power being reduced too much / wages may keep pace with inflation; May increase international price competitiveness as may be below the inflation rate of other countries increasing exports reducing imports improving the current account position; May create greater certainty / stability as costs may not be rising significantly encouraging firms / MNCs to invest increasing output / GDP increasing employment / lowering unemployment; May encourage saving as real value may be maintained provide funds for investment; It may raise profit if demand-pull encouraging firms to expand increasing employment / lowering unemployment; May stop a random redistribution of income protecting savers; Low menu costs reduce pressure on firms' costs of production.
Explanation: Increasing employment / lowering unemployment' can only be credited once.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a fall in a country’s unemployment rate will reduce poverty in that country.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase the income of those who gain jobs; absolute poverty may be reduced with more people being able to buy basic necessities; tax revenue may rise; the government could spend more on benefits for the poor; the government could spend more on education, reducing future poverty. Why it might not: jobs may be part-time, low-paid, at national minimum wage; jobs may be seasonal; relative poverty may increase if extra jobs are gained by the well-paid; the unemployment rate may fall but unemployment may rise if there are more people in the labour force; may be more retired people living in poverty; sick and disabled people may not be able to get out of poverty; unemployment is only one of a number of factors affecting poverty; absolute poverty may remain.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Canada labour force: ageing population, policies to increase female participation, falling exchange rate and rising consumer expenditure
Question Text: Scenario about Canada
Define the labour force.
Markscheme: Those who are economically active; Those willing and able to (work); The employed and the unemployed.

Question Text: Explain two causes of an ageing population.
Markscheme: A fall in the death rate / increase in life expectancy resulting from better healthcare / better nutrition / higher income / better lifestyles; A fall in the birth rate resulting from higher cost of raising children / fall in child benefit / increase in women working / increase in use of family planning / fall in infant mortality / reduction in need for children to support parents due to provision of state pensions or healthcare; Rise in net emigration of mainly young-aged people resulting from higher incomes abroad / increase in job opportunities abroad.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two causes identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how a fall in the value of a country’s foreign exchange rate could reduce a deficit on the current account of its balance of payments.
Markscheme: Foreign exchange rate is the price / value of one currency in terms of another currency or currencies a fall in the value of a foreign exchange rate means that the domestic currency buys less foreign currency / depreciates; A fall will reduce price of exports raise demand for exports increase export revenue money flows into country which will reduce Balance of Payments deficit if demand is elastic; Increase price of imports reduce demand for imports reduce import expenditure reduces the money flows out of the country which reduces the Balance of Payments deficit if demand is elastic.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an economy will benefit from an increase in consumer expenditure.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase total (aggregate) demand which may encourage firms to expand; cause economic growth; reducing cyclical unemployment; raise tax revenue enabling the government to spend more on education and healthcare; eliminate / reduce deflation; raise inflation to the inflation target rate. Why it might not: may cause demand-pull inflation especially if economy is at full capacity; may increase spending on imports; may divert exports to the home market; may cause a deficit on the current account of the balance of payments; may reduce long run economic growth as resources may be diverted away from producing capital goods; may increase the consumption of demerit goods.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Australia drought reducing agricultural output; secondary and tertiary sectors stronger; central bank policies to avoid deflation
Question Text: Scenario about Australia
Identify two industries, other than agriculture, that operate in the primary sector.
Markscheme: Mining, fishing, forestry, oil extraction

Question Text: Explain two possible disadvantages to an economy of a fall in agricultural output.
Markscheme: Shortage of food / famine may have to import more food export less food worsen the current account position; Price of food may rise may increase poverty / might worsen health; Less food production unemployment may increase if lack of labour mobility / structural unemployment; May result in migration from rural to urban areas putting pressure on resources in urban areas; Economic growth / GDP will fall if lower agricultural output is not offset by rise in output of secondary and tertiary sectors.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two disadvantages identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how a central bank could avoid deflation.
Markscheme: Deflation is a fall in the price level; The central bank could lower the rate of interest reduce return from saving encourage borrowing encourage a rise in consumer expenditure encouraging firms to raise their prices; It could increase the money supply increasing bank lending increasing investment; Expansionary monetary policy increases total (aggregate) demand causing prices to rise.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a firm should have growth as its main objective.
Markscheme: Why it should: may increase market share; may increase market power; may increase profits / keep shareholders happy; may enable the firm to take greater advantage of economies of scale; may increase pay and job security of managers / directors; may make it more difficult for another firm to take it over as will involve a greater cost. Why it might not: if demand is falling, survival may be a more appropriate main objective; if demand is limited e.g. a niche market, growth may be an unlikely objective; profit maximisation as an objective, if successful, will provide the funds for growth in the longer run; state-owned enterprise may have social welfare as an objective; growth may result in diseconomies of scale; growth may put pressure on workers which could lower productivity / result in higher labour turnover; may make it more attractive for a firm to take it over.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Chile economy and cherry exports; specialisation risks; population structures of Chile and Haiti; evidence of inequality and market failure
Question Text: Source material about Chilean cherries and population structures
Calculate Chile’s agricultural output in 2017.
Explanation: Accept $11.8bn and $12bn. $ is not essential.

Question Text: Identify two disadvantages of a country specialising.
Markscheme: demand may fall / new competitors may appear; supply/output may fall / diseases / natural disasters / bad weather may affect agricultural output / costs may rise; non-renewable resources may run out; structural unemployment may occur; reliance on imports no longer produced by home country / overdependency on other countries
Explanation: One mark each for each of two disadvantages. Rely on a small number of industries is not sufficient.

Question Text: Explain one reason why demand for cherries is price-elastic.
Markscheme: A luxury product they do not have to be purchased / not essential / not a necessity; Has close substitutes example / rise in price will cause some people to switch to rival products.
Explanation: In terms of close substitutes, also accept: fall in price will cause some people to switch towards this product and away from rival products. One mark for the reason identified and one mark for the explanation. A candidate who writes a luxury product and has close substitutes and no more has identified two reasons, so just one mark. If a candidate identifies one of the two reasons but does not explain it and then identifies the second reason and explains it, the second reason can be taken and with the explanation, 2 marks.

Question Text: Explain how Chile’s population structure differs from Haiti’s population structure.
Markscheme: Chile has a higher proportion of its population aged over 65 / Haiti a lower proportion aged over 65; Chile has a lower proportion of the population aged under 15 / Haiti has a higher proportion aged under 15; Chile is likely to have a lower birth rate / Haiti is likely to have a higher birth rate; Chile likely to have a lower death rate / Haiti is likely to have a higher death rate; Chile's population pyramid indicates a relatively high level of development/high income country / Haiti's population pyramid indicates a relatively low level of development/low income country; Chile's population pyramid is less pyramid shaped / more stationary / constrictive / bulges out in the middle / more egg-shaped / Haiti's is more pyramid shaped / more expansive; Lower dependency ratio in Chile / higher dependency ratio in Haiti; Higher proportion of population of working age in Chile / lower proportion of population of working age in Haiti; Higher population size in Chile / lower population size in Haiti; Chile has a higher average age / older population / Haiti has a lower average age population / younger population.
Explanation: Accept for the first and second points other relevant age ranges e.g. over 60, under 10. Accept longer life expectancy for lower death rate and shorter life expectancy for higher death rate. Note that each country overall has more females than males, so not a difference.

Question Text: Analyse why China buys most of its cherries from Chile.
Markscheme: Chile has high production standards cherries are likely to be of a high quality; China does not impose tariffs on Chilean cherries may keep their price low.
Explanation: Free trade agreement on its own is not sufficient.

Question Text: Analyse why Chilean astronomers are paid more than Chilean farm workers.
Markscheme: Most astronomers have a university degree high cost of training / long period of training well qualified / well educated / high level of knowledge highly productive / highly skilled / efficient in short supply high demand more inelastic demand more inelastic supply; Most work in the public sector government may be more willing / able to pay higher wages public sector workers are more likely to belong to a trade union have stronger bargaining power.
Explanation: Also accept an approach based on an analysis of why Chilean farm workers are paid less than Chilean astronomers. This may include reference to immigrant farm workers who may be prepared to accept relatively low wages and Chilean farm workers being replaced by capital equipment / more replaceable than astronomers.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not Chilean consumers would benefit from more government intervention in the economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: evidence of market failure merit goods may be underproduced as underconsumed demerit goods may be overproduced as overconsumed external costs could be reduced external benefits may be increased external costs / external benefits are not taken into account by the private sector public goods may not be produced no profit incentive to make them as there can be free riders inequality poor consumers may lose out / there may be poverty resources may not be devoted to producing what the poor need may not be able to afford prices charged government subsidies can lower prices government could set maximum prices lower prices of necessities government could control (private sector) monopoly power prevent abuse of market power government investment in education could lower prices / raise quality of output. Reasons why they might not: government failure may occur e.g. government may lack information; market forces can provide wide choice of products may be consumer sovereignty; profit incentive industries may respond to changes in consumer demand; efficient industries prices may be low quality may be high; government intervention in the form of nationalisation may reduce competition; government taxes / tariffs / regulation / minimum wages may increase costs raise prices; maximum prices could reduce availability of products; government limits on immigration may reduce availability of products / raise prices.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the Haitian economy would benefit from fewer of its people working in Chile.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: wages may be low in Chile work involves unskilled labour; employment may be rising in Haiti the country could keep more of its skilled workers; larger labour force / more workers higher total demand increase GDP / increase economic growth / increase productive potential increase living standards increase exports; rise in the numbers employed in Haiti could increase tax revenue may enable more spending on e.g. education; fewer parents may leave behind dependent relatives reduce burden on the government; jobs could be found in other countries. Reasons why it might not: wages may be higher in Chile fewer emigrant workers may send money home living standards may be lower lower spending; less money coming into the country may increase a current account deficit; job opportunities may be higher in Chile unemployment may rise in Haiti; if unemployment rises in Haiti, cost of unemployment benefits may rise opportunity cost / higher taxation; opportunities to gain new skills may be reduced these skills could be brought back into the country; may reduce pressure to invest / spend on capital equipment reduce advances in technology / reduce increases in efficiency / productivity; may increase overcrowding / overpopulation increase external costs / pollution pressure on resources.
Explanation: For an approach which discusses whether or not the Haitian economy benefits from its people working in Chile, award a maximum of 3 marks.

Stimulus / Scenario: Cairo inequality: crowded housing, unemployment around 10%, rich households with luxury homes
Question Text: Scenario about inequality in Cairo
Identify two basic necessities, other than housing.
Markscheme: Food, water, clothing, healthcare and education.
Explanation: Accept any relevant basic necessity e.g. heating or electricity. If more than two examples given, only consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain how frictional unemployment differs from cyclical unemployment.
Markscheme: Frictional unemployment arises from people being in between jobs may be waiting for a better paid job / may be seasonally unemployed / may have worked on a short-term contract / may lack information about vacancies may exist even when there is full employment / may be self-correcting. Cyclical unemployment is caused by a lack of total (aggregate) demand more people unemployed than there are job vacancies may arise from a recession / economic downturn may need government action to reduce it. Frictional unemployment would be reduced by a rise in labour mobility cyclical unemployment would not be. Frictional unemployment may be short term cyclical unemployment may be long term.
Explanation: Up to 2 marks for each type of unemployment.

Question Text: Analyse why the children of the rich tend to become rich adults.
Markscheme: The children of the rich tend to receive a good education may not have to take part-time jobs / not have to help in family business when studying / not have to leave school early become well qualified / well educated / gain high level of knowledge become highly skilled / productive / efficient find it is relatively easy to find employment / get a good job well-paid. The children of the rich tend to receive good health care good nutrition have good housing unlikely to miss time at school unlikely to miss time at work income will be high. The children of the rich tend to have high expectations apply for better jobs. The children of the rich may inherit e.g. property may gain income from e.g. inherited property / business / savings. Rich parents may have connections with e.g. bosses of major employers more likely to gain high paid work.
Explanation: Note the focus here is on the children of the rich so no marks for e.g. the children of the poor may lack education. Reference to highly skilled / productive or efficient may be linked to education, healthcare or nutrition but can only be credited once.

Question Text: Discuss whether a government should provide subsidies to families to spend on housing.
Markscheme: Why it might: will increase the spending power of families who receive it; may increase quantity supplied; may give low-income families access to a basic necessity, reducing poverty; may enable families to spend more on e.g. education; may increase employment / reduce unemployment, creating jobs building housing / increase labour mobility; may increase quality of housing and so improve health. Why it might not: rich can already afford housing; some families may not spend it on housing; may be inflationary, demand increasing for housing and e.g. furniture, carpets; opportunity cost; taxes may be raised to finance subsidy; building more houses may result in environmental damage.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Uruguay processed food consumption increasing; firms becoming more capital-intensive; inflation rising
Question Text: Scenario about Uruguay’s food industry and inflation
Define enterprise.
Markscheme: Risk bearing setting up / owning a business key decision making / organisation of the other factors of production profit incentive / profit is the reward.

Question Text: Explain the influence of opportunity cost on consumers’ decisions.
Markscheme: Opportunity cost is the (next) best alternative forgone / sacrificed. Consumers have limited income / time have to make choices cannot have everything they want if buy more of one product may have to buy less of another / example of what product may be given up to buy another product.

Question Text: Analyse why a firm may become more capital-intensive.
Markscheme: The cost of capital may fall / the price of labour may rise lowering costs of production making the firm more price-competitive may increase profits. Advances in technology may improve the quality of capital making it more productive / efficient may increase the quality of products produced raise demand for the products produced. Firms may want to reduce human error / more consistent quality / uniform products reduce wastage. Firms may want to avoid disruption to production caused by industrial action / strikes / sickness capital equipment does not need to take breaks / can work 24 hours a day. There may be a shortage of labour making it difficult to recruit workers. A government may reduce taxes on capital goods provide subsidies the rate of interest may be reduced making capital goods more affordable. The firm's output may rise reducing the average fixed cost of capital / benefiting from economies of scale.

Question Text: Discuss whether inflation harms a country’s industries.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase costs of production; may reduce competitiveness, lowering sales at home and abroad; may upset industrial relations, workers pressing for wage rises; may make it difficult to plan; may discourage savings which can reduce funds available for investment; corporation tax may be moved into a higher tax bracket; may be menu costs / shoe leather costs. Why it might not: may be demand-pull inflation with demand for the firm's products increasing; may be low and stable; may enable a firm to reduce the real cost of any debt; may enable the firm to cut the real cost of wages; may be lower than other countries' inflation rates; may not be foreign substitutes; some industries may have inelastic demand.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Turkey labour supply changes: falling birth rate, improving labour quality, MNC investment, rising interest rates
Question Text: Scenario about Turkey’s economy
Define birth rate.
Markscheme: The number of births per thousand of the population in a year live births
Explanation: Not sufficient to have just number of births.

Question Text: Explain two causes of an increase in the quality of labour in a country.
Markscheme: Improvements in education qualifications / knowledge; Improvements in training increasing skills; Improvements in healthcare making workers physically/mentally stronger; Increases in pay raising motivation; Improvements in nutrition enabling workers to work harder / concentrate more; Reduction in working hours / length of working day keeping workers fresher / enabling them to concentrate more; Improvements in working conditions raising motivation / enabling workers to work harder / concentrate more; Immigration of skilled workers; Experience becoming more familiar with tasks; Specialisation become more familiar with one task / 'practise makes perfect'.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two causes identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how a rise in the interest rate could cause a recession.
Markscheme: A higher interest rate would increase the cost of borrowing reduce borrowing reduce the spending power of people who have borrowed in the past increase saving consumer expenditure / spending may fall investment may fall / may discourage MNCs unemployment may increase / employment may fall total demand may fall the output of goods and services / GDP may fall if output falls over a period of two quarters / six months there will be a recession. A higher interest rate may encourage more people to buy the currency to put money into the country's financial institutions raising the value of the foreign exchange rate raising the price of exports lowering demand for exports reducing the price of imports increasing demand for imports.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not MNCs improve the economic performance of the host countries in which they operate.
Markscheme: Why they might: may bring in new technology and methods of production, increasing economic growth; may create new jobs, may reduce unemployment; may add to exports, reducing a current account deficit / increasing a current account surplus; may create more competition / have greater efficiency, lowering inflation; may increase tax revenue, enabling the government to spend more on e.g. education and healthcare. Why they might not: may drive out domestic producers, leaving the country's output unchanged; may employ workers from their home country; may import capital equipment and raw materials from their home countries; may deplete non-renewable resources, reducing sustainable economic growth; may provide only low-skilled, low-paid jobs to locals; send profits back to home country; may cause external costs e.g. pollution.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Bangladesh money supply growth, exchange rate effects, commercial bank mergers
Question Text: Scenario about Bangladesh banking sector
Identify two functions of money.
Markscheme: Medium of exchange, store of value, standard of deferred payments, unit of account / measure of value.
Explanation: Note: a description of the function gets the mark e.g. money can be used to buy and sell products, may be used to trade. Nothing for characteristics of money.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why commercial banks may want to merge.
Markscheme: Greater market power/share eliminating a competitor / providing a greater range of services; More opportunity to take advantage of economies of scale lower costs of production / higher profit / example e.g. share ATMs; May enable the merged bank to operate in more than one country / become a multinational company access to new market; Become better known large banks may be more likely to have a brand image; May enable rationalisation eliminate duplication / raise efficiency; Survival may have been loss making / at risk of going out of business.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. Nothing for increase revenue.

Question Text: Analyse how a fall in a country’s foreign exchange rate could increase employment.
Markscheme: A fall in a country's foreign exchange rate will lower the price of exports raise the price of imports make domestically produced products more price competitive reduce demand for imports increase demand for exports increase total demand raise firms' revenue and/or profits encourage firms to increase output take on more workers.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not it is an advantage to keep a firm small.
Markscheme: Why it might: flexible, less people to consult, more in touch with consumers; may be able to provide more personal attention; may receive government subsidies; may be able to concentrate on a niche market; may have good labour relations; may avoid diseconomies of scale. Why it might not: may not be able to take advantage of economies of scale; may be driven out of business by larger competitors; may be difficult to raise finance; risk of being taken over by a larger firm; may have difficulty recruiting highly skilled workers; may not have the resources to survive a fall in demand.
Explanation: Accept an answer based on the advantages that large firms have over small firms. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Morocco’s “blue economy”: fishing industry growth, poverty among fishermen, global fish demand, environmental concerns about overfishing
Question Text: Source material about Morocco’s blue economy
Calculate, in $s, the contribution of the fishing industry to Morocco’s GDP in 2017.
Explanation: Accept 2.2 bn. $ sign not essential.

Question Text: Identify two macroeconomic aims, other than balance of payments stability, that the Moroccan government could achieve through the blue economy.
Markscheme: Economic growth / sustainable economic growth / development / output; Create employment / lower unemployment / full employment; Reduce poverty; Improve livelihoods / improve living standards / improve quality of life

Question Text: Explain why an increase in the global demand for fish could reduce a deficit in Morocco’s current account of its balance of payment.
Markscheme: Increase in global demand could lead to an increase in demand for Moroccan fish / more sales of Moroccan fish / increase supply of Moroccan fish increasing exports creating an inflow of money / raising export revenue improve trade in goods balance / trade balance balance out imports / exports may now equal imports / reduce gap between exports and imports.

Question Text: Explain two causes of poverty amongst fishermen.
Markscheme: Low wages cannot afford necessities such as food and proper housing high supply / low demand for fishermen; Structural unemployment fishermen might not have the skills to take up other jobs after the decline in the fishing industry in their area; Seasonal unemployment may not be possible to catch fish throughout the year instability of income.
Explanation: One mark for each cause identified and one mark for each explanation. One mark for unemployment.

Question Text: Analyse whether GDP per head always increases when the percentage contribution of the primary sector increases.
Markscheme: Overview: No / not always if % contribution of the primary sector increases, GDP per head is lower. Supporting evidence: e.g. Pakistan / India / Morocco have a high % contribution and low GDP per head, Germany has the lowest % contribution but second highest GDP per head comparison e.g. Germany has a higher GDP per head than India but a smaller contribution from the primary sector. Exception: There is an exception / on the other hand e.g. Australia – higher % contribution than Germany, but higher GDP per head or e.g. Malaysia – higher % contribution than Bulgaria, but higher GDP. Comments: High-income countries tend to have more resources concentrated in the secondary and tertiary sectors / tertiary sector incomes tend to be higher in the secondary and tertiary sectors / lower in the primary sector.

Question Text: Analyse how regulation of fishing could ensure economic growth is sustained for future generations.
Markscheme: Regulation could prevent people from overfishing regulation is backed up by law / fines may be imposed gives time for fish to breed / recover from overfishing so that future generations will still have access to fish stock / maintain fish stocks this means that future generations will still be able to generate revenue from fishing which could lead to increased or sustained investment / consumption / exports ensuring high total demand. Regulation on fishing could also help coastal tourism protect the environment increase revenue from tourism as people visit areas of natural beauty.
Explanation: Also allow an approach based on how overfishing may threaten sustainable economic growth.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not subsidies to fishermen could help the Moroccan economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it could: subsidies will decrease cost of production and enable Moroccan fishermen to use better technology / capital / equipment / enable them to take advantage of economies of scale / engage in research & development increase their productivity lead to an increase in supply / output price of Moroccan fish would decrease this will increase competitiveness of Moroccan fishermen increase demand create jobs / increase employment / reduce unemployment increase exports / reduce imports improve the current account balance increase revenues / income of fishermen investments / consumption increase total demand leading to economic growth. Reasons why it could not: opportunity cost for the government may result in a budget deficit less spending on education / healthcare decrease overall productivity of the economy less economic growth; subsidies may lead to complacency may become dependent on subsidies fishermen may not use the subsidies efficiently may result in overfishing may increase pollution / external costs; Fishing industry only contributes 2% of GDP even if it expands may not increase the country's output significantly; Competitiveness may not increase prices might not decrease other governments may subsidise their fishermen.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a reduction in trade protection by other African countries would be beneficial for Morocco.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might be beneficial: less protectionism will decrease the price of Moroccan exports increase exports improve current account balance less protectionism will allow Morocco to specialise more / enable it to concentrate on what it is best at producing demand for labour is derived demand, demand for domestic workers will increase therefore unemployment decreases income increases / poverty decreases consumption of goods and services can increase more total demand economic growth inflation may fall lower cost of imported raw materials / imported capital goods. Reasons why it might not be beneficial: decreased protection might lead to other countries products decreasing in price as well, Moroccan goods may decrease in competitiveness decrease the demand for Moroccan goods increase in imports infant / sunrise industries may go out of business unable to compete as not able to take full advantage of economies of scale sunset / declining industries may go out of business likely to have high costs of production decrease the demand for workers as less is produced unemployment increases less income consumption of goods and services decrease decreased total demand less economic growth.

Stimulus / Scenario: Latvia economy: fast growth, rising living standards, lower borrowing, falling unemployment but possible balance of payments instability
Question Text: Scenario about Latvia’s economy
Identify two indicators of living standards.
Markscheme: Real GDP per head; Human Development Index (HDI); healthcare; education
Explanation: Accept other valid indicators of living standards. Accept GDP per head.

Question Text: Explain how a decrease in borrowing could reduce the chance of high inflation.
Markscheme: Decrease in borrowing means people's purchasing power would reduce / less money to spend would lead to less consumption / less spending or investment this could lead to a decrease in total demand. Bank lending would be reduced the money supply may be reduced / less money in circulation / less money in the economy price of goods and services will remain low / stop prices rising less demand-pull inflation.

Question Text: Analyse how economic growth conflicts with balance of payments stability.
Markscheme: Economic growth may benefit from higher spending whereas balance of payments stability does not. Less balance of payment stability because economic growth leads to higher income higher employment increases spending / consumption increase the demand for goods and services including imported goods reducing net exports increase trade deficit / reduce trade surplus. Economic growth could increase demand for imported raw materials imported capital goods. Economic growth could lead to demand-pull inflation wage rises increase price of exports reduce competitiveness of exports.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not having a lower GDP will be a disadvantage to an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might be a disadvantage: lower income and lower purchasing power, lower standards of living; lower investment as less demand for goods and services, less production; lower healthcare and education standards; lower levels of employment; may be lower exports; lower tax revenue; may result in deflation. Why it might be an advantage: less production leads to less environmental problems; less work could lead to less stress; less income leads to less imports, better current account balance; less income leads to less inflation; GDP per head could rise if population fallen more than GDP.
Explanation: Accept discussion of low GDP. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Canadian cities: low cyclical unemployment due to government spending and tax cuts; productivity concerns; risk of structural unemployment
Question Text: Scenario about Canada labour market
Define structural unemployment.
Markscheme: Workers without jobs; Unemployment due to a change in the structure of the economy / change in demand and supply conditions / decline of some industries lack of the appropriate skills lack of labour mobility.

Question Text: Explain two causes of low productivity.
Markscheme: Poor education and training leading to lack of skills / qualifications; Poor technology slower or less output per capital / labour input; Poor management waste of resources; Lack of investment capital older / slower / out-of-date; Emigration of skilled workers output of unskilled workers who are left is lower; Poor working conditions making it difficult for workers to do their jobs efficiently; Low pay which may demotivate workers; Long working hours making workers tired.
Explanation: One mark for each cause identified and one mark for each explanation. Lack of specialisation is too vague.

Question Text: Analyse how higher government spending and tax cuts can lead to a fall in cyclical unemployment.
Markscheme: Higher spending on e.g. unemployment benefits / education / infrastructure / healthcare could lead to higher demand for goods and services increased demand for goods and services leads to higher revenues / profits for firms increased investments increasing output increased demand for workers to produce the output. Tax cuts such as income tax cuts increased disposable income increase consumer expenditure increased demand for goods and services higher revenues / profits for firms increased investments increasing output. Tax cuts such as corporation tax cuts increased after tax profits increased investments increasing output. Tax cuts such as indirect tax cuts decrease cost of production increased supply / output.
Explanation: Maximum of 4 marks for only higher spending OR tax cuts

Question Text: Discuss whether or not competition between firms in the same industry is always a disadvantage to workers.
Markscheme: Why it might be a disadvantage: more competition between firms means firms try to be more efficient, which could lead to lower job opportunities / greater instability of employment; there may be greater use of short-term employment contracts; more competition between firms could push down the wages of the workers; more competition between firms could lead to workers being exploited e.g. overtime work, worse working conditions. Why it might be an advantage: more competition between firms would lead to firms training workers to be more productive; increased productivity leads to higher wages; increased productivity could lead to more investments – leading to more demand for workers; more competition between firms could lead to lower prices, which benefits workers who are also consumers; lower prices could lead to more demand, which increases the demand for workers as well; greater efficiency could result in higher revenue some of which may be spent improving working conditions; more competition between firms could lead to them raising wages and / or improving working conditions to attract the best workers; more competition between firms gives more opportunities for workers to switch jobs.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Global healthcare spending rising; investment leading to medical technology; falling demand for some vaccinations
Question Text: Scenario about health sector investment
Define investment.
Markscheme: Spending on capital goods to increase output.

Question Text: Explain two causes of a shift of a product’s demand curve to the left.
Markscheme: Decrease in price / rise in quality of substitutes increased demand for substitutes; Increase in price of complements decrease demand for complements; Decrease in income decrease in purchasing power; Changing trends / tastes decrease popularity; Changing seasons changing demand; Decrease in population size fewer people to buy the product; Rise in the rate of interest less able to borrow to buy the product.
Explanation: One mark for each cause identified and one mark for each explanation. No mark for stating demand falls because of a rise in price. No diagram is required. No marks for a diagram.

Question Text: Analyse, using a PPC diagram, the effect of increased investment in education and healthcare.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled with different goods or services; Initial curve drawn as a curve / line sloping downward to the axes; New curve drawn to the right as a curve / line sloping downward to the axes; Shift indicated by arrow or letters. Written comments: Increased investments in education and healthcare leads to better health / qualification / higher quality resources better technology increased productivity better quality workforce / skills / qualification may be more resources increase productive capacity / the maximum quantity of products that can be produced within a certain time period / create economic growth.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written comments. Accept education on one axis and health on the other. Accept any other two relevant categories e.g. manufactured and agricultural goods. Accept a pivot out if education and health is on one axis and other goods on the other.

Question Text: Discuss whether consumers would benefit more from healthcare being provided by the private sector or the public sector.
Markscheme: Why private sector is better: profit maximising objective will lead to higher efficiency; lower costs; more choice; private firms will try to compete; could lead to higher quality; private sector more likely to be able to raise funds; private sector investment will lead to less opportunity cost for public sector – leading to more spending on other industries e.g. education / government do not have to raise taxes to fund healthcare services. Why public sector is better: private sector investment leads to market failure such as merit goods; public sector cares more about the wellbeing of the people; could lead to higher quality provisions; public sector may produce on a larger scale and take advantage of economies of scale; public sector may not charge for services whereas private sector will or public sector may charge a lower price.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Smartphones becoming more price inelastic; important for development in low-income countries; access to education and banking
Question Text: Scenario about smartphones and development
Define economic development.
Markscheme: When there is an increase in standards of living of the people of a country / quality of life / economic welfare. Increase in income per head / average income / GDP per head / education / healthcare / HDI. The process by which low-income economies become high-income economies. Movement from primary towards the secondary and tertiary sectors.
Explanation: Increase in GDP is not sufficient.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why children in rural areas may receive less education than those in cities.
Markscheme: Low income cannot afford to go to school; Poor infrastructure cannot get to school / no schools; Have to work on the family farm no time to go to school; Parental attitude they do not see benefit of education; Low tax revenue in rural areas government spending on schools lower; Difficult to recruit teachers poor housing / long distance to travel / low wages; Lack of schools difficult to get materials to build schools / may be insufficient children; Parents have not been educated don't see the value in (paying for) education
Explanation: One mark for each cause identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse the advantages of selling a product which is price-inelastic in demand.
Markscheme: Price-inelastic demand means when price increases, quantity demanded falls by a less than proportionate amount or vice versa. Relevant example. When demand is price-inelastic, price and revenue move in the same direction. PED of less than one may indicate lack of competition / substitutes necessities often have inelastic demand. Firms can increase prices total revenue increases may increase in profits if revenue rises faster than cost. Less market volatility stability of income / revenue making it easier to plan.
Explanation: Award up to 2 marks for a diagram that shows price rising and total revenue rising, but do not reward twice.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in the level of savings is beneficial for an economy.
Markscheme: Why an increase in the level of savings may be beneficial: increase in savings could provide more funds for firms to borrow to invest; could lead to more future expenditure for consumers on things such as education; increase in firms' productivity due to higher investments; increase in future standards of living / income for consumers; savings for old age / rainy day for consumers; may reduce the need to borrow and so reduce the risk of debt; may reduce inflation; may reduce spending on imports and so improve the current account of the balance of payments. Why increase in the level of savings may not be beneficial: increase in savings will lead to lower spending by consumers, lower total demand and lower economic growth now; lower expenditure on goods and services by consumers which leads to less profits for firms and less government tax revenue; increase in savings may reduce the incentive for firms to invest; increase in savings may be a sign of lower confidence in the economy; may reduce total demand and increase unemployment.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Laos economy: land-locked country, rapid growth from free market reforms, copper exports, foreign investment, growing banking sector, business regulation concerns
Question Text: Source material about Laos economy and business environment
Calculate, in $, the total exports of Laos to China in 2017.
Explanation: Accept 1.18 or 1.2 billion.

Question Text: Identify two roles of commercial banks.
Markscheme: Helps local entrepreneurs to borrow money; Encourages households to save / enables households to save.
Explanation: Accept loan / lending

Question Text: Explain how access to seaports may benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Enables an economy to take full advantage of international trade / take greater advantage of international trading opportunities enables immigration of skilled workers; Can export and import goods goods can be transported more cheaply (than by rail or air / over longer distances) / may encourage specialisation increases consumer choice; May encourage investment attract MNCs create economic growth.

Question Text: Explain two benefits of the free market reforms introduced by the government of Laos.
Markscheme: The free market reforms have enabled Laos to sell their abundant raw materials internationally increases export revenue / improves current account balance / creates employment / encourages firms to expand / increases GDP; Encourages foreign investment may lower costs of production / increases total demand / improves current account balance / creates employment / increase tax revenue.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two benefits identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. Accept improved infrastructure.

Question Text: Analyse the impact of improved education on the economy of Laos.
Markscheme: Increased adult literacy rate better skills/qualifications increase productivity / increase productive capacity / total supply increase demand for labour reduce unemployment / raise employment raise wages reduce poverty. More attractive for entrepreneurs / new businesses / investment increase output / GDP leading to higher living standards. Improvements in technology due to workers having higher IT skills Increase the consumption of merit goods / decrease the consumption of demerit goods

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between a country’s Ease of Doing Business ranking and its GDP per head.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Positive / direct if ease of doing business (EDB) rank is high, GDP per capita is high. Supporting evidence: Singapore has the highest EDB rank and GDP per capita is highest Timor-Leste has the lowest EDB rank and GDP per capita is the lowest. Exception: Brunei / Malaysia has lower EDB rank than Malaysia but higher GDP per head. Alternatively South Korea / Brunei Brunei has lower EDB rank but higher GDP per head. Analysis: The greater the ease of doing business, the more investment there is likely to be higher investment is likely to increase GDP.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not strict rules and regulations benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: Environmental rules e.g. cap on carbon emission, tax on polluting industries reduce market failure reduce external costs e.g. could decrease pollution / protect natural resources less health problems for the society better standards of living; Anti-competition rules e.g. restrictions on monopoly power improved consumer choice / lower prices / better quality foreign companies will not dominate the market opportunities for domestic firms to develop increased future economic growth; Restrictions on imports may protect domestic industries / allow growth of domestic industries; Bans e.g. prohibiting smoking in public places may reduce market failure / reduce external costs; Labour market legislation protecting workers' rights. Reasons why they might not: Strict regulation discourages business Laos ranks one of the lowest in the World Bank's Ease of Doing Business index lack of investment / MNCs discouraged lower productivity lack of competition lack of employment opportunities decreased total demand decreased economic growth / lower GDP lower standards of living.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a slowdown of China’s economy will be harmful to the economy of Laos.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: China is Laos's largest trading partner / 58% of Laos's exports are sold to China China slowing down will lead to less demand for Laos's exports less export revenue / worsen current account less total demand less economic growth may not be able to buy more, irreplaceable imports; China is the largest source of foreign investment into Laos less foreign investments will lead to less job creation less employment / higher unemployment lower standards of living. Reasons why it might not: Reduce dependence on China find new markets and investors other countries' economies could be expanding which could generate more exports and jobs; May lead to fewer environmental problems less pollution higher standard of living; Domestic investors and firms could fill the gap more employment higher GDP.

Stimulus / Scenario: France proposed 3% tax on large digital firms; possible redistribution of income but concerns about firms leaving
Question Text: Scenario about France digital tax
Define redistribution of income.
Markscheme: Transfer of income from some individuals to others. Tax taken from the rich is spent on the poor. Progressive / regressive taxation.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why governments redistribute income.
Markscheme: Reduce poverty higher purchasing power afford basic necessities e.g. healthcare / education / proper housing; Reduction in poverty reduces social problems / waste of resources as poverty may create e.g. poor health / reduce productivity; To promote equity / greater equality of income reducing the gap between rich and poor; To increase incentives if redistribute from poor to rich may increase effort / enterprise.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the economic effects of a reduction in a country’s export of services.
Markscheme: As export of services decreases, total exports will decrease total demand decreases less inflation lower economic growth / lower GDP. Less export of services, less demand for service workers less job opportunities / higher unemployment. Higher current account deficit / less current account surplus reduces ability to buy imports lower standards of living.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not supply-side policy measures will encourage firms to operate in an economy.
Markscheme: Why they might encourage firms: Makes it easier to hire and fire workers might lower costs of employing workers to firms; Better skills could increase productivity; Encouraging more workers into the labour market could increase the supply of workers; Reduction in trade union power can reduce cost / make it easier to start a business; Lower direct taxes will encourage more firms to start up; Deregulation will reduce barriers to operate in an economy; Privatisation will allow private firms to invest in the economy; Subsidies will give firms an incentive to stay in the country.
