Why they might not encourage firms: Less job security could decrease productivity, increasing cost of production for firms; Costs of hiring workers might still be lower elsewhere; Supply side measures (e.g. labour market reforms) may take a long time before the effects on the economy are apparent; Lower direct taxes may reduce the government's ability to spend on education / training which will increase firms' cost of production; Deregulation may enable other firms to engage in unfair practices; There may be a fear that subsidies might be withdrawn and if there is a fall in demand MNCs will not want to stay in the country anyway; Other governments may be giving larger subsidies.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Borneo rainforest benefits but dependence on oil; discovery of oil reserves elsewhere causing world oil price fall
Question Text: Scenario about Borneo oil economy
Define finite resource.
Markscheme: A resource is an aid to production which is limited in supply it cannot be replaced over time / fast enough / will run out to keep pace with consumption.
Explanation: No mark for an example

Question Text: Explain the difference between private and external benefits.
Markscheme: Private benefits are enjoyed by the producer and consumer first and second parties of a product, example e.g. revenue for the producer / satisfaction for the consumer. External benefits are enjoyed by a third party not directly involved in the production or consumption process example.
Explanation: Maximum 3 marks for only explaining 1 type of benefit.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, the effects on the world price of oil of the discovery of new reserves of oil.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or P and Q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New supply curve shifted to the right; Equilibrium – shown by lines P1, P2 / Q1, Q2: or marking the equilibrium points E1 and E2. Written comments: Discovery of new oil will enable an increase in supply price will be lower.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written comments.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the fall in the price of a product is a disadvantage to an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might be a disadvantage: It depends on size of fall in price and elasticity of demand for the product; It depends upon the importance of the product to the economy / how dependent on it; If demand is price inelastic, decrease in total revenue e.g. basic items / necessity, cannot cover the costs, firms may go out of business, less employment opportunities; Less profits, discourages investment, reduces economic growth; Less export revenue, may increase current account deficit; Fall in price may be due to cheaper imports, domestic firms lose out; The product may be a demerit good which previously was overconsumed; If the product is part of a deflationary situation, there could be recession / unemployment. Why it might not be a disadvantage: Depends on size of fall and whether it is short or long term; If demand is elastic, increase in total revenue e.g. luxury goods / many substitutes; Increase choices and affordability of product - better living standards; Fall in the price of a raw material may lower cost of production, reducing inflation and increasing economic growth; The product may be a merit good which previously was underconsumed; It may make a basic necessity more affordable - low income groups may be better off; It may be the result of an increase in productivity / a government subsidy; It may make the product more internationally competitive, improving the current account position.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Uruguay inflation 8.4%; central bank may raise interest rates affecting total demand and exchange rate
Question Text: Scenario about Uruguay inflation and interest rates
Define total revenue.
Markscheme: Total amount of money earned by firms for selling their products. Price times quantity, P × Q. Total costs plus profit.
Explanation: No mark for producing their products

Question Text: Explain two causes of inflation.
Markscheme: Demand-pull inflation increase in total demand e.g. increase in consumption / increase in investment / government spending / net exports e.g. cut in income tax / decrease interest rate / increase employment / increase in the money supply; Cost-push inflation increase in costs of production e.g. increase in wages / raw material cost / profit margin / tariffs e.g. wages may rise more than productivity / fall in exchange rate would increase raw material costs.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two causes identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. Full marks may be awarded for a detailed explanation of two causes of one type of inflation.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in the interest rate could reduce total demand in an economy.
Markscheme: Increase in interest rate will lead to an increase in the cost of borrowing there will be less borrowing less consumption / spending less investments. An increase in interest rate will lead to an increase in the returns from savings there will be more savings less consumption / less spending. An increase in interest rate may attract an inflow of money from other countries into the country's banks leading to an appreciation of the currency leading to increase in price of exports / decrease in price of imports increase exports / decrease imports decrease net exports.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an appreciation of a country’s domestic currency will have negative effects on its economy.
Markscheme: Why appreciation will have a negative impact: Price of exports will be higher - decrease export revenue; Price of imports will be lower - increase import spending; Decrease demand for domestic products - decrease demand for labour; Decrease current account surplus / increase current account deficit; Lower economic growth. Why appreciation will not have a negative impact: Price of imports cheaper - increase affordability of imports - increase standards of living; Price of imported raw materials / machinery lower - lower cost of production - lower final price; PED for exports and imports might be inelastic; May encourage foreign investment as there may be increased confidence in the country's future economic prospects.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Debate over government spending and trade unions; unions may also promote development
Question Text: Scenario about trade unions and government spending
Define market disequilibrium.
Markscheme: When quantity demanded does not equal quantity supplied. When there are shortages or surpluses of a product.

Question Text: Explain the difference between labour-intensive and capital-intensive industries.
Markscheme: Labour-intensive industries rely more on human labour i.e. workers low-cost labour may be available example of industry; Capital-intensive industries rely more on capital i.e. machines capital more efficient / cheaper than labour example of industry

Question Text: Analyse how trade unions could increase economic development.
Markscheme: Trade unions could help engage in collective bargaining on wages higher wages leads to higher purchasing power of workers. May increase motivation of workers increase output. Better working hours improved working conditions protect workers' rights better health and safety more job security leads to higher standards of living higher life expectancy. Increased productivity / efficiency of workers decreases cost of production decrease final price of goods and services produced in the country increase competitiveness higher output / economic growth. May help with training raise quality of output.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not high government spending can help a government achieve its macroeconomic aims.
Markscheme: Why high government spending can help: economic growth – increase total demand; full employment/low unemployment – increase demand for workers through more government infrastructure projects; government spending on education may raise workers' skills and so lower unemployment; balance of payments stability – through subsidies for producers; redistribution of income – through spending on social welfare. Why high government spending cannot help: increased total demand could lead to increase demand-pull inflation; too much government spending could lead to less private investments; overreliance of government subsidies could lead to complacency; high spending on social welfare could discourage the unemployed from trying to find jobs; may be accompanied by higher taxes which could be a disincentive to effort and enterprise; may increase budget deficit which could reduce confidence in the economy.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: India’s airline industry: Air India government ownership, increased competition, industry growth, current account implications, fuel tax and rising costs
Question Text: Source material about India’s airline industry
Calculate, in $, Air India’s loss in 2018.
Identify two substitutes for air travel.
Markscheme: Trains; ships
Explanation: For 'ships' accept water travel.

Question Text: Explain one characteristic that suggests that Air India was a monopoly in 1994.
Markscheme: Share of the market was 100% only firm / share above 25% of the market / share above 40% / a complete / pure monopoly / no competition; Able to set price a price-maker.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a government may privatise an industry.
Markscheme: Raise (tax) revenue which could be spent on e.g. education, healthcare / increase government spending; Increase efficiency / may perform better in the private sector / higher productivity introduce a profit motive / increase output / GDP / reduce costs of production; Increase competition lower price / increase quality / increase choice; Industry may be loss making / costly to run reduce need to use tax revenue to finance industry / tax revenue can be used for other purposes e.g. education / increase government spending / reduce national debt / tax rates might be cut.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how India’s position on the current account of its balance of payments in 2017 compares with the other four countries.
Markscheme: Brazil, India and Kenya had a current account deficit while China and Russia had a current account surplus. India's current account deficit is the largest in terms of $ but the second largest in % terms. China's current account had a larger impact on its economy in terms of $ and Kenya's in percentage terms. Russia's current account surplus is larger than China's in terms of % of GDP. China has the largest surplus in $.
Explanation: Up to 2 marks maximum for idea of size of current account balance without reference to deficit or surplus e.g. China has the largest current account balance and India the lowest. So maximum of 2 marks without reference to deficit / s or surplus / es. No marks for writing about a budget balance.

Question Text: Analyse how a successful airline industry can promote economic growth.
Markscheme: May increase number of tourists raise revenue from tourism / increase the size of the tourist industry may increase exports / imports / increase the ease of movement of people and goods into and out of the country. May attract more MNCs these will increase the country's output / GDP introduce new technology / new methods of production. May lower costs of production raising firms' profits providing the incentive / finance to expand increase the country's output / GDP. May increase employment raise incomes increase total demand increase tax revenue more could be spent on e.g. education. May increase (geographical) mobility of labour making it easier for expanding firms to recruit workers.

Question Text: Discuss whether the Indian government should increase the tax on airline fuel.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should: Raise revenue can be used to finance e.g. education / training of pilots / increase government spending burden more likely to fall on the rich / may enable tax on basic necessities to be reduced; May reduce market failure air travel causes external costs / social cost exceeds social benefit air / noise pollution a tax may reduce air travel / lower demand for air travel by raising price encourage people to switch to other forms of transport. Reasons why it should not: Will increase airlines' costs / reduce airlines' profits / cause a loss airlines' other costs are rising difficult for them to cover higher costs / raise prices due to elastic demand / price sensitive demand may go out of business may discourage investment causing unemployment; May reduce tourism in the country harming the current account position; Airline fuel is already highly taxed may be difficult to tax further; Air travel is safer than car travel in many countries so injuries may increase if there is a switch between the two.

Question Text: Discuss whether more people will apply to be pilots for Indian airlines in the future.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: Introduction of new technology will make the job easier; Improved staff training improve / increase skills improve motivation / satisfaction improve working conditions airlines want to retain staff; More routes are being flown / size of industry is increasing demand for pilots may increase / more job opportunities may raise wages; More fringe benefits may be provided example; Being a pilot is a highly regarded occupation in many countries. Reasons why they might not: There may not be more people qualified to become pilots education standards may fall / qualifications may be high; Period of training may increase / cost of training may increase; Other jobs may offer higher wages / better working conditions / wages of pilots may fall example of other jobs; Advances in technology may reduce the need for pilots may increase skills needed; Increased tax on fuel may reduce the size of the airline industry causing people to expect fewer job opportunities / unemployment of pilots; Price of substitute forms of transport may reduce lowering demand for pilots; Job may require workers to be away from their families people may want to stay close to home; Working hours may be unsociable people may want to spend more time with their families; May be considered in some cases to be a risky job.
Explanation: Note: some points may be given in reverse e.g. education standards may rise.

Stimulus / Scenario: Morocco capturing water from fog; movement of resources to tertiary sector; falling birth rate
Question Text: Scenario about Morocco’s resources and sectors
Define a free good.
Markscheme: No opportunity cost a good that takes no resources / factors of production to produce / naturally abundant in supply / does not use scarce resources.
Explanation: No marks for an example.

Question Text: Explain, with examples, the difference between the secondary sector and the tertiary sector.
Markscheme: Secondary sector covers manufacturing (and construction) / converts primary products into finished goods e.g. car industry; Tertiary sector covers services / final stage of production e.g. insurance.
Explanation: For tertiary sector, it is not sufficient to just have the sector which sells products as this is only part of the sector. But for the tertiary sector example accept the sale of a product e.g. the sale of jewellery.

Question Text: Analyse, using a PPC, the effect of an increase in the quality of resources on an economy.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled with different outputs; Initial curve drawn as a curve / line sloping downward to the axes; New curve drawn as a curve / line sloping downward to the axes; Shift indicated by arrow or letter. Written analysis: Better quality resources will increase productivity enable more of both types of goods to be produced with a given quantity of resources increase productive potential / productive capacity / total supply cause economic growth.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written analysis. Nothing for higher output, higher GDP as this is uncertain.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a fall in a country’s birth rate will benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: reduce the number of dependents / reduce the dependency ratio; may reduce government spending on benefits for children; may free up resources which could be invested and promote economic growth; may reduce poverty; may enable more women to enter the labour force; may reduce pressure on the environment / reduce pollution / reduce depletion of resources. Why it might not: reduce the size of the labour force in the longer term; reduce labour mobility; create an ageing population; reduce size of markets.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Uzbekistan cotton and gas industries; supply elasticity policies; child labour reduction; falling average costs
Question Text: Scenario about Uzbekistan industries
Identify two determinants of price elasticity of supply.
Markscheme: ability to store; level of stocks; perishability; production time; level of spare capacity; cost of altering supply; time period under consideration – short / long run
Explanation: Accept weather.

Question Text: Explain two advantages to an economy of ending child labour.
Markscheme: Enable children to be educated raise productivity / workers' skills / literacy rates / long run economic growth; Improve children's health increase life expectancy / raise living standards; Reduce cycle of poverty children will later gain higher paid jobs; Raise wages due to reduced supply of cheap labour; Remove international bans / encourage more foreign tourists increase exports; Increase job opportunities for adults lower unemployment / increase employment of adults.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two advantages identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how average cost can change as output increases.
Markscheme: Average cost may fall due to economies of scale example of an economy of scale e.g. financial economy explanation of the example e.g. banks may charge lower interest rates reducing firms' cost of borrowing. Another example e.g. buying economy explanation of the example e.g. able to buy in bulk and receive a discount. Higher output may enable fixed costs to be spread over a larger output which may reduce average fixed costs whether average total cost will fall will depend on what happens to average fixed cost plus average variable cost. Average cost may rise due to diseconomies of scale example e.g. managerial economy explanation of the example e.g. difficulty of keeping control of a large organisation leading to more mistakes / poor decision making. Another example e.g. poor labour relations explanation of the example e.g. lack of contact between workers and managers may be strikes.
Explanation: Nothing for reference to causes of changes in total costs e.g. employing more workers will increase wages paid.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an economy would benefit from allocating more of its resources to agriculture.
Markscheme: Why it might: specialisation may increase efficiency, raise output, increase exports; may improve the current account of the balance of payments; imports of food may be reduced, reduced reliance on other countries for basic necessities; health and safety standards may be maintained; the industry is labour intensive in some countries and so may reduce unemployment. Why it might not: wages in the industry tend to be relatively low; the supply of agricultural products can fluctuate significantly due to changes in weather conditions; opportunity cost of fewer resources for manufacturing goods and for services; demand for manufactured goods and for services tend to rise more as income increases; agriculture uses up considerable amounts of water.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Tunisia mixed resource allocation, falling savings, trade union strikes, falling exchange rate
Question Text: Scenario about Tunisia economy
Identify two of the three resource allocation decisions.
Markscheme: what to produce; how to produce; who to produce for

Question Text: Explain two reasons why households may save less even though their income has increased.
Markscheme: Fall in the rate of interest which would reduce the return from saving / spend / borrow rather than save; Inflation spend more now before prices rise further / may reduce the real rate of interest; Increase in confidence about the future less motive to save for hard times / less concerned will experience unemployment or fall in income; Fall in range, number or reliability of financial institutions which would reduce the safe places to save; Greater consumption opportunities / spend more / higher living standards with the introduction of new products / lower prices / may be able to afford private education / healthcare; Increase in taxes reducing disposable income; Rise in family size increasing household expenses; Rise in debt may reduce ability of households to e.g. to put money into a savings account; Change in social attitudes due to e.g. change in age of households.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how a trade union may benefit its members.
Markscheme: A trade union may negotiate with employers to raise wages improve working conditions example increase fringe benefits example through collective bargaining and sometimes industrial action / strikes. A trade union may settle disputes between the employer and the workers e.g. over changes in working practices protect workers' rights. A trade union may seek to protect the employment of its members in some cases negotiate favourable redundancy terms. A trade union may negotiate / put pressure on the government to e.g. raise a national minimum wage or reduce the retirement age. In some countries, trade unions provide benefits and services to members e.g. training services.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a fall in its foreign exchange rate will improve a country’s macroeconomic performance.
Markscheme: Why it might: export prices will fall and import prices will rise; demand for exports may rise and demand for imports may fall; domestic output will increase, economic growth may rise; more workers may be employed, reducing unemployment; a deficit on the current account of the balance of payments may be reduced or a surplus increased; may encourage foreign investment if it is thought assets can be bought more cheaply. Why it might not: rise in price of imported capital goods and raw materials may increase costs of production, causing cost-push inflation; higher total (aggregate) demand may cause demand-pull inflation, especially if the economy is working close to full capacity; demand for exports and imports may be price inelastic, causing export revenue to fall and import expenditure to rise - in such a case may benefit more from a rise in the exchange rate; trade restrictions imposed by other countries may prevent the country from exporting more products; may discourage foreign investment due to a lack of confidence in the country's future economic prospects.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: US market economy with high GDP per head but income inequality; falling life expectancy; supply-side policies
Question Text: Scenario about US economy
Identify two ways a government could reduce income inequality.
Markscheme: progressive taxation; state benefits; spending on state education / training; spending on state healthcare; equal pay legislation; minimum wage
Explanation: Accept higher taxes on high incomes and lower taxes on low incomes but do not accept increasing taxes on incomes as this is TV. Redistributing income is TV – could be redistributing from the poor to the rich.

Question Text: Explain two advantages of a market economic system.
Markscheme: Consumer sovereignty with consumers deciding what goods and services are produced; Low prices due to competition; High quality due to innovation; Consumer choice variety of products produced; Greater efficiency due to the profit motive; Automatic adjustment to changes in demand and supply / quick response to changes in demand and supply via changes in price / with use of the price mechanism / increase consumer satisfaction.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two advantages identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. Nothing for no taxes.

Question Text: Analyse why life expectancy may decrease.
Markscheme: Incomes may fall lowering living standards / reducing access to basic necessities; Reduction in healthcare e.g. limited access to medication; Reduction in education people may lead less healthy lives more smoking more 'junk food' less exercise; Rise in poor nutrition / malnutrition eating more low-quality food / people not having enough food; More pollution making people more susceptible to diseases; May be spread of new diseases / pandemics wars / civil unrest; Climate change may result in more natural disasters; More people employed in jobs with poor working conditions / more dangerous jobs resulting in more sickness / accidents.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not supply-side policy measures can reduce inflation.
Markscheme: Why they might: they may increase total (aggregate) supply, reducing cost-push inflation; they can allow total (aggregate) demand to increase without causing inflation; education and training can increase labour productivity, reducing labour costs; a cut in direct taxes may increase the incentive to be more productive and more innovative; labour market reforms may reduce power of trade unions and reduce rise in wages; subsidies can lower costs of production. Why they might not: privatisation does not always increase competition and so does not always lower prices; education and training can be expensive, may be in areas that are not in demand and will have a time lag; cuts in income tax may increase total (aggregate) demand as well as total (aggregate) supply; resource limitations / unable to increase productive capacity due to e.g. limited land available; inflation may be caused by rise in price of raw materials which have no domestic substitute e.g. oil.
Explanation: Only accept lower interest rates, if linked to increasing total supply. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Changing eating habits: obesity levels, rise of veganism in high-income countries, environmental effects of meat production, labour mobility and food demand changes
Question Text: Source material about global food consumption and labour markets
Calculate the number of obese people in the UK in 2016.
Explanation: Accept 18 million.

Question Text: Identify two reasons why the demand for vegan food has increased in the UK.
Markscheme: (concerns about) health; (concerns about) obesity; (compassion for) animal welfare; rise in income / getting richer / higher standard of living; advertising of vegan products; less consumption of meat
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons.

Question Text: Explain one external benefit that may arise from eating less meat.
Markscheme: Healthy / less obese people are more productive / higher output / live longer / government can spend more on education and raise skills; Improved environment / air / water quality less greenhouse gases / pollution / people sick.
Explanation: One mark for the external benefit identified and one mark for the explanation.

Question Text: Explain how food consumption in Senegal differed from the US in 2015.
Markscheme: Senegalese citizens consumed fewer calories consumed less than the recommended amount / US consumed more than the recommended amount; Senegalese may have suffered malnutrition US may have suffered from obesity; Senegalese had a healthier diet e.g. more fruit and vegetables; A higher proportion of the Senegalese diet consisted of cereals / grains because it was cheap; Senegalese citizens consumed less meat because it was expensive / low incomes; Senegalese citizens consumed less sugar and / or sweeteners; Senegalese consumed a higher proportion of fruit and / or vegetables; Senegalese consumed less added fats and / or oils.
Explanation: Accept a response from the viewpoint of the US e.g. US citizens ate more meat.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in labour mobility could improve the performance of a country’s trade in goods.
Markscheme: Labour markets will become more efficient workers become more skilled workers will move more easily between industries / become more occupationally mobile / respond quickly to changes in demand. Workers can move more easily from one place to another / become more geographically mobile. If demand for a product rises it will be easier to recruit new workers / fill vacancies more can be produced / supplied less need for imports import expenditure could fall more can be exported export revenue could rise.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in meat consumption could benefit African economies.
Markscheme: May reduce malnutrition / make people healthier make workers more productive / efficient improve quantity / quality of output lower costs of production more competitive internationally exports increase / imports reduce economic growth. Increase demand for vets and transport firms increase number of meat processing firms greater investment by firms will increase output raise employment raise incomes / standard of living. Reduces government spending on health care can spend more on education / unemployment benefits / infrastructure increasing skill levels / productivity.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in Senegalese government spending will stop the country experiencing a recession.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Government spending part of total (aggregate) demand may increase total (aggregate) demand may offset fall in consumer expenditure this may encourage firms to produce more / attract new firms to set up employ more staff / lower (cyclical) unemployment earn more income purchase more goods and services; Government spending on education / training / healthcare infrastructure improves productivity make products more internationally competitive increasing total (aggregate) demand raising output; Government subsidies will lower costs of production encouraging firms to produce more. Reasons why it might not: May not be great enough to offset fall in investment business confidence may be low; firms may not increase output after being given government subsidies; Consumer expenditure may fall investment may not rise exports may fall if consumer confidence is low; May be global recession exports may fall; If taxes are raised to pay for the increase in government spending government borrows effect on total (aggregate) demand may be small; Higher spending on education / health / infrastructure may not result in increased workforce / greater productivity.
Explanation: Award 1 mark for explanation of recession.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not emigration would reduce poverty in Senegal.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Workers' remittances may increase / emigrants may send money home to Senegal working abroad may enable them to provide more financial support for their families than working at home if wages are higher abroad; Unemployment may fall if potential emigrants could not gain work at home; Increase ability of people to gain skills / work experience increasing their ability to earn more can share those skills with workers when they return to Senegal; Emigration may move the country towards the optimum population if the country lacks resources this could increase income per head increasing ability to purchase basic necessities. Reasons why it might not: Skilled workers may leave the country reducing output discouraging MNCs reducing jobs reducing income; There will be fewer people to pay taxes lower tax revenue available to spend to reduce poverty e.g. on education; May be more dependents on the government if parents leave children and elderly relatives behind; Fewer workers in the population will make it harder to finance state pensions may reduce income of pensioners.

Stimulus / Scenario: Russia population structure and retirement age; increasing role of price mechanism; indirect taxes to reduce market failure
Question Text: Scenario about Russia’s economy
Identify two reasons why a country may have more women than men in its population.
Markscheme: women living longer; more girls being born; fewer female emigrants or male immigrants; more female immigrants or male emigrants; more men killed in wars

Question Text: Explain two advantages of raising the retirement age.
Markscheme: Reduction in pensions paid money could be spent for other purposes e.g. infrastructure; Increase in size of the labour force / increase in output / productive potential / GDP / reduction in firms' wage costs; Reduction in size of dependency ratio less burden on working population; Rise in tax revenue / improve budget position government can spend more on e.g. public sector wages; May increase health of the old work may be mentally challenging; People can work longer and earn more income / enjoy higher living standards; Older people have greater skills / more years of training can raise productivity / pass on skills to younger workers.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two advantages identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how the price mechanism answers the three key resource allocation questions.
Markscheme: Definition of price mechanism e.g. where forces of demand and supply determine the prices of goods and services in a market economy. What to produce the price mechanism acts a signal / the price mechanism acts as an incentive on what to produce rations goods and services price will rise for products in higher demand encouraging more to be made. How to produce e.g. more labour-intensive / more capital-intensive methods of production will be used if wages fall / if cost of capital goods fall. Who to produce for those whose services are most in demand will have the highest income will be able to buy the most.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not indirect taxation can reduce market failure.
Markscheme: Why it might: demerit goods are overconsumed and cause external costs; demerit goods and other products causing external costs can be taxed; indirect taxation on demerit goods can discourage their consumption; merit goods are under-consumed and create external benefits; indirect tax revenue can be used to subsidise or produce merit goods; public goods would not be produced by the private sector as they have the characteristics of non-rival and non-excludable; indirect tax revenue can be used to finance the production of public goods. Why it might not: difficult to measure external costs; demerit goods may be overtaxed, changing from being overconsumed to being under-consumed; demand for some demerit goods is price inelastic; indirect taxes may fall more heavily on the poor; demand may just shift to imports if other countries do not impose indirect tax or have lower tax rates; tax revenue may not be used to promote the consumption of merit and public goods.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: South Africa unemployment, stable inflation, wage increases and inflation, proposal to attract multinational companies
Question Text: Scenario about South Africa’s labour market and trade
Identify two ways unemployment is measured.
Markscheme: claimant count / claiming benefits; labour force survey / ILO measure / without a job and seeking work; formula for the unemployment rate / number of unemployed divided by labour force × 100 / through the unemployment rate; total labour force minus those currently employed

Question Text: Explain two ways an increase in wages can cause inflation.
Markscheme: Increase in wages may increase total (aggregate) demand / consumption causing demand-pull inflation; Increase in wages may increase costs of production causing cost-push inflation.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two ways identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. Allow 1 mark if refer to prices rise but no mention of demand-pull or cost-push inflation

Question Text: Analyse, using a PPC, the opportunity cost to an economy of producing more consumer goods.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled with capital / consumer goods; Curve drawn as a curve / line sloping downward to the axes; Movement along the curve / along the axes; Reduction in capital goods / increase in consumer goods shown by numbers or letters or arrows. Written analysis: Opportunity cost is the (next) best alternative forgone resources used to produce consumer goods cannot be used to produce capital goods. Producing more consumer goods now may mean fewer consumer goods in the future as there may be fewer capital goods to make them.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written analysis.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in foreign MNCs will reduce a deficit on the current account of the balance of payments of a host country.
Markscheme: Why it might: MNCs tend to export a relatively high proportion of their output; may produce at a low cost of production / be efficient; may be internationally competitive; may produce products that were previously imported; may bring in new technology. Why it might not: may buy raw materials and capital goods from their home countries; MNC may send profits back to home country; workers / managers may send remittances back to home country; may eliminate firms in the host country that had a good export record; may deplete non-renewable resources reducing the ability to export in the long run.
Explanation: For level 3, an answer must look at both sides of whether a deficit on the current account would be reduced. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Norway free trade expansion, floating exchange rate, employment changes requiring university degrees, increasing mergers between firms
Question Text: Scenario about Norway’s economy
Identify two benefits of free trade.
Markscheme: higher output / growth; higher income / living standards; more employment / more jobs; more choice / access to other markets; lower price; greater ability to take advantage of economies of scale / greater efficiency / specialisation; drives competition; better quality goods; stronger global co-operation

Question Text: Explain two differences between a floating foreign exchange rate and a fixed foreign exchange rate.
Markscheme: A floating exchange rate is determined by market forces a fixed exchange rate is set by the government / central bank; A floating exchange rate can change on a day-to-day basis whereas a fixed exchange does not often change in value; A rise in the value of floating exchange rate is an appreciation / a fall in the value of a floating exchange rate is a depreciation a rise in the value of a fixed exchange rate is a revaluation / a fall in value of a fixed exchange rate is a devaluation; A floating exchange rate does not require a central bank to buy and sell the currency a fixed exchange rate needs reserves of foreign currency to maintain it.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two differences identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how a change in the pattern of employment in a country may change its average wage.
Markscheme: A higher proportion of skilled workers due to better education / training will raise the average wage / skilled workers tend to be highly paid. An increase in proportion of workers in the tertiary sector / smaller proportion of workers in the primary sector example of industry / as economy develops there tends to be more workers in the tertiary sector / fewer in the primary sector will tend to increase the average wage. A greater proportion of women workers change in social attitudes / anti-discrimination legislation may increase the average wage. A higher proportion of workers in the public sector may be better training / may be more fringe benefits / may be more job security may increase / reduce the average wage. A larger proportion of workers in the formal economy more likely to be represented by a trade union / will have legal rights may increase the average wage. Age older workers may be more experienced / in promoted posts may be better paid.
Explanation: Allow up to 3 marks for why wages may change due to changes in demand and supply with reference to a particular industry. Note: answers must address a change in pattern of employment

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should stop firms merging.
Markscheme: Why it should: will have greater market share; may abuse greater market power; may become complacent; consumers may experience higher prices and lower quality; may engage in rationalisation; may increase unemployment; may experience diseconomies of scale. Why it should not: may innovate more; may provide consumers with lower prices and higher quality; may be more international competitive; may improve current account position; may increase economic growth; may experience economies of scale.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Vietnam industries becoming more competitive, increased division of labour, manufacturing growth
Question Text: Scenario about Vietnam’s industrial development
Define division of labour.
Markscheme: Workers specialising in particular tasks / repeating the same task; Breaking down production into different / separate tasks using different workers for the different tasks.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a loss-making firm may continue to produce.
Markscheme: May not expect the loss to last may think demand will rise / costs will fall in the future; May be subsidised by the government to e.g. promote social welfare / encourage consumption of merit goods / increase exports / respond to dumping by other countries; May lower prices to drive out competitors / increase market share raising price when successful; May accept losses in short-term in order to expand in long-term; May have high retained profits to allow for downturns in demand / cover the losses; May be a new firm in the process of growth / trying to survive; A firm's main objective may not be profit maximisation e.g. growing market share / charity / provides public goods.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the advantages that consumers may gain from a competitive market.
Markscheme: Wide choice of producers producing a similar product. Efficiency better quality greater choice of products firms may innovate as firms try to gain a larger share of the market. Lower price as firms try to beat competition increasing consumers' purchasing power / standard of living. Raise consumer sovereignty firms may respond quickly to changes in consumer demand.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in a country’s manufacturing output will increase its economic development.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase products available for households; increase capital goods enabling greater capacity; may raise incomes / profits; create employment; may promote growth of the other two sectors – greater demand for raw materials and greater demand for services e.g. insurance; may mean firms are getting larger – economies of scale, reduced average costs, increased exports; likely to have a higher value added than primary sector; greater stability in income than primary sector. Why it might not: may create pollution / reduce open spaces; jobs may have poor working conditions; employment may not rise if capital-intensive methods are used; opportunity cost / less resources used in primary and / or tertiary sectors; wages may be lower than tertiary sector; may reduce natural resources available for future; goods produced may be of poor quality / inefficiently produced / uncompetitive.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Bermuda economy: luxury tourism destination, ban on foreign fast-food chains, tax haven status, high GDP per head, large tertiary sector dominated by financial services, central-bank-type role of Bermudian Monetary Authority
Question Text: Source material about Bermuda economy
Calculate the number of Bermudian workers who were unemployed in 2018.
Identify two reasons why demand for holidays in Bermuda may be price-elastic.
Markscheme: Luxury (not a necessity); expensive / high price
Explanation: If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: State two reasons why a foreign MNC may want to operate in Bermuda.
Markscheme: Tax haven / no corporation tax; High GDP per head; High import tariffs; High literacy rate / skilled workers / educated workers; Low inflation.
Explanation: If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why Bermuda has a higher level of economic development than many other countries.
Markscheme: Large tertiary sector jobs in the tertiary sector tend to be better paid / have better working conditions; High literacy rate a higher literacy rate will tend to increase employability / provide more choices / increase quality of jobs obtained / increase pay / increase skills / produce better quality products / may suggest a good level of education / raise productivity / increase output; Low population growth a lower population growth may mean that dependency ratio may be low / less stress on resources / more resources can be devoted to e.g. investment; High GDP per head a higher GDP per head can increase living standards / result in high consumer expenditure; High life expectancy which can reflect good health care / may mean high quality labour.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two relevant reasons are identified, consider the first three and take the two which gain the highest marks. Note: no mark for high GDP, low unemployment or just e.g. population growth.

Question Text: Analyse the role of the Bermudian Monetary Authority (BMA).
Markscheme: Carries out (most) of the functions of a central bank; Acts as banker to the government receives tax payments / enables government to make payments / manages national debt / allows government to borrow and lend; Holds the country's reserves of foreign currency may use reserves to influence the value of Bermuda's currency / buy / sell currency; Carries out the government's monetary policy / keeps inflation low by changing interest rate / money supply.
Explanation: Maximum of 2 marks for recognising functions (including carrying out the functions of a central bank) from the source material.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP per head and life expectancy.
Markscheme: Overview: Generally, a positive relationship / the higher the GDP per head, the longer the life expectancy. Supporting evidence: Evidence in terms of an individual country or group of countries e.g. Monaco has the highest GDP per head and the longest life expectancy or e.g. the three countries with the highest GDP per head have the longest life expectancy evidence in terms of another country or group of countries e.g. Eswatini has the lowest GDP per head and the lowest life expectancy or e.g. Argentina, Bahamas and Eswatini have the lowest GDP per head and the lowest life expectancy. Exception: Argentina or Bahamas not in same order / reason for why difference may exist. Comments: Higher income allows more to be spent on healthcare better healthcare reduces illness / reduces death rate.
Explanation: No marks for just stating the figures e.g. Monaco has a GDP per head of approximately $185 000 and a life expectancy of 90 years and Eswatini has a GDP per head of approximately $4000 and a life expectancy of 58.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not Bermudian government policy measures designed to reduce unemployment would increase the inflation rate.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: Expansionary fiscal and/or monetary policy measures example of a measure could increase total demand / increase spending higher total demand could cause demand-pull inflation if total (aggregate) demand exceeds total (aggregate) supply / there is limited or no spare capacity; Shortage of unemployed workers / lower unemployment could push up wage rates / incomes higher output could push up costs of raw materials and cost of capital goods higher costs of production could cause cost-push inflation. Reasons why they might not: Supply-side policy measures could raise productivity example of a measure higher productivity could reduce costs of production total supply could increase to match higher total demand / productive potential/capacity could increase avoid / reduce cost-push inflation; Expectations of future inflation could be low resulting in e.g. lower wage claims; Unemployment rate is at 7% not at full employment unemployed workers may be willing to work at existing wage rates there is some spare capacity; Inflation rate is low and so expectations of inflation may be low; Expansionary demand-side policy measures may just result in higher savings.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not higher indirect taxes can reduce the market failure caused by demerit goods.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: Demerit goods are more harmful than consumers realise / are harmful / consuming less would make people healthier they usually have negative external costs examples include fast food, alcohol and tobacco; Demerit goods are overconsumed and overproduced higher indirect taxes will impose an extra cost / raise cost of production which may lower output / supply be likely to raise price which is likely to reduce consumption lower consumption/lower output/lower supply may move output to a point where social costs equal social benefits; May raise tax revenue which can be spent on campaigns to reduce e.g. smoking. Reasons why it might not: It may be difficult to change consumers' behaviour / spending patterns some demerit goods are addictive those with high incomes may not be affected by a rise in price demand for demerit goods may be inelastic; Producers may not pass on price rise to consumers / products may be sold on an illegal market to avoid fall in demand; It can be difficult to determine the output of demerit goods which will avoid market failure the output where social cost will equal social benefit.
Explanation: Examples of demerit goods may be rewarded in why might or why might not.

Stimulus / Scenario: South Africa recession and unemployment, falling exchange rate, debate about tariffs to protect infant industries
Question Text: Scenario about South Africa economy
Define frictional unemployment.
Markscheme: Workers in between jobs / caused by a delay between workers moving from one job to another job; temporary / short term unemployment; Seasonal / casual / search / voluntary unemployment; Unemployment that exists when there is full employment.

Question Text: Explain why an infant industry may need protection.
Markscheme: New industries need time / potential to grow / to survive / to establish themselves / to build up a reputation / lack resources need time to take advantage of economies of scale / may be competing with foreign firms which are more able to take advantage of economies of scale economies of scale lower costs / foreign firms may have lower costs. Lower costs may reduce price the infant industry can charge make the infant industry more competitive / be able to compete / foreign firms may be more competitive / foreign firms may drive the infant industry out of business. The growth of infant industry may increase employment / elimination may increase unemployment growth of infant industry may reduce imports / increase exports / promote domestic products / elimination of infant industry may increase imports / reduce exports growth of infant industry may increase economic growth / elimination of infant industry may reduce economic growth.

Question Text: Analyse how a fall in a country’s foreign exchange rate could reduce a deficit on the current account of its balance of payments.
Markscheme: Deficit occurs when debit items including imports on the current account of the balance of payments exceed credit items including exports. Fall in the exchange rate would reduce price / value of the currency / depreciation / devaluation has occurred this would reduce the price of exports increase price of imports make exports more competitive / imports less competitive demand for exports is likely to rise / exports increase increase export revenue / money flowing into the country demand for imports is likely to fall / imports reduce / switch from imports to domestically produced products reduce import expenditure / reduce money flowing out of the country increase net exports if demand is price-elastic. Value of remittances / money sent home may rise increasing secondary income.
Explanation: Accept imports exceed exports for understanding of a deficit on the current account of the balance of payments for the first mark.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a reduction in income tax will end a recession.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase disposable income; increase consumer expenditure; increase investment (spending on capital goods); increase total (aggregate) demand; increase employment; raise GDP. Why it might not: lack of confidence people save more; more could be sold from stocks / inventory; workers may work fewer hours keeping disposable income unchanged; people may spend more on imports; people may still delay spending if prices are falling; may reduce tax revenue and government spending on e.g. education.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: New Zealand small firms, minimum wage policy, rising income per head but continued poverty for some households
Question Text: Scenario about New Zealand economy
Define diseconomies of scale.
Markscheme: Higher average costs of production as a result of a larger scale of production / growing too large / internal diseconomies of scale affect a firm whereas external diseconomies of scale affect an industry.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a high-income household may borrow more than a low-income household.
Markscheme: Banks may be more willing to lend to them more confident of being repaid; Banks may charge a low interest rate as a high-income household may borrow a larger amount; High-income households may be more confident of being able to repay the loan / able to repay the loan quickly may expect income to rise / have greater job security; High-income households may have significant wealth / assets able to offer collateral; High-income households may borrow for a big-ticket items / luxuries e.g. to buy an expensive house, expensive car, to pay for children's education / to increase / maintain status / living standards.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation. Note: the focus here is on households not firms. Nothing for just high-income households spend more.

Question Text: Analyse, using a diagram, the effect of an increase in output on average fixed cost (AFC) and total fixed cost (TFC).
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled: costs and output; TFC horizontal line; AFC downward sloping; Curves correctly labelled: TFC / FC and AFC. Written analysis: A higher output will have no effect on total fixed cost / fixed costs do not change with output in the short run / fixed costs have to be paid even when output is zero; Average fixed cost will fall with output / as the same cost figure is divided by a higher output / AFC is TFC divided by output.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written analysis. Do not reward a demand / supply / demand and supply or PPC diagram.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the introduction of a national minimum wage will reduce poverty.
Markscheme: Why it might: low-paid workers may be better paid; higher income may enable people to buy basic necessities; reduce absolute poverty; pay gap between high-income and low-income workers may be reduced, lower relative poverty; higher pay may increase spending, creating more jobs and income; higher tax revenue may enable the government to spend more on reducing poverty; higher wages may increase productivity which could encourage firms to hire more workers. Why it might not: may be set below current low pay and so have no effect; may result in some workers losing their jobs; unemployed will have lower income; may discourage MNCs operating in the country; does not help people who are poor because of age, unemployment or sickness; may result in inflation, leaving purchasing power / real wages unchanged.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Botswana labour market changes, net migration patterns, rising female labour participation, low household saving
Question Text: Scenario about Botswana economy
Identify two reasons why immigration may increase.
Markscheme: higher income / better living standards (in the country) / lower income abroad; more job opportunities (in the country) / fewer job opportunities abroad; better working conditions (in the country) / worse working conditions abroad; better education (in the country) / worse education abroad; better healthcare (in the country) / worse health care abroad; lower cost of living / higher cost of living abroad; lower taxes / higher taxes abroad; lower immigration controls; wars / famine / political unrest / natural disasters abroad.
Explanation: Accept e.g. higher income on its own. If mention e.g. lower income must link to other countries / abroad.

Question Text: Explain two consequences of unemployment.
Markscheme: Lower output / lower GDP / lower economic growth / producing below full capacity inefficient use of resources / unused resources / lower exports; Lower income / lower wages / lower GDP per head causing a rise in poverty / reduction in ability to buy basic necessities / fall in living standards / lower development; Lower total (aggregate) demand lower inflation / deflation; Lower tax revenue from (direct and indirect taxes) due to lower income / reduction in government spending; Increased government spending on unemployment benefits opportunity cost / spending less on e.g. healthcare; Reduction in household spending on education / healthcare reduce living standards / life expectancy; Unemployed workers may lose skills / become out of date with new technology make it more difficult to gain employment; Unemployed workers may experience depression / low self-worth worsen health; Possibility of a rise in crime government may have to spend more on reducing crime / crime lowers people's quality of life.
Explanation: One mark for each of two consequences identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse why more women may join a country’s labour force.
Markscheme: Rise in pay better job opportunities due to a fall in discrimination change in social attitudes; Improvements in education for girls more female graduates / higher literacy higher productivity / skills higher demand for women workers; Rise in tertiary sector employment higher proportion of women employed in the tertiary sector; Fall in birth rate reduction in dependents / dependency ratio rise in availability of childcare making it easier to combine work and parenthood; Increase in population size more women available to seek work; Reduction in male workers due to e.g. emigration of male workers; Rise in part-time employment / increase in holidays / rise in flexible hours / increase in opportunity to work from home allowing women to combine work with bringing up a family; More women may need / want to support their families financially due to e.g. rise in cost of living / male workers losing their jobs / increase in divorce; May be government measures to encourage women to join the labour force; Rise in job security improvement in working conditions / fringe benefits.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should encourage an increase in saving.
Markscheme: Why it should: will reduce spending which can reduce demand-pull inflation; lower spending may reduce imports, improving the current account balance; will provide finance for investment; will provide a 'safety net' for households and firms; may be used to finance children's education and / or family's healthcare; may provide income when retired. Why it should not: may reduce total demand leading to lower economic growth; may increase unemployment; may increase the deficit on the current account if the country saves more than it invests; may reduce tax revenue; may reduce the incentive for people to work if interest rates are very low; may be inflationary if saving reduces output.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Ghana cocoa production and global chocolate industry; possible monopoly formation from merger of a large US chocolate firm
Question Text: Scenario about Ghana and chocolate industry
Define the economic problem.
Markscheme: Finite / limited resources unlimited / infinite wants; Wants exceeding resources; Scarcity.

Question Text: Explain whether land is mobile.
Markscheme: Most land is geographically immobile fixed in one place / cannot move from one place to another place; Some forms of land e.g. wildlife can be moved from one place to another; Land is occupationally mobile capable of changing its use / e.g. land can be used for farming or housing.

Question Text: Analyse the causes of a shift to the right in the supply curve of chocolate.
Markscheme: A shift to the right of the supply curve means that more will be supplied at each and every price / supply has increased due to a non-price factor / influence. Lower costs of production due to any cause (other than tax or subsidy) e.g. lower wages / higher productivity / lower transport costs. Subsidy providing an additional income / effectively lowering costs / providing a financial incentive. Advances in technology increasing output per unit of resource / increase efficiency / raise productivity of capital. Good harvest of cocoa / raw material perhaps due to improvement in weather conditions / improvements in fertilisers reducing price of raw material increasing supply of raw material. Lower tax on chocolate / lower indirect tax / lower corporate tax making it more profitable to produce. Fall in price / rise in cost of production / fall in profitability of another product produced by chocolate firms encouraging the switch of some resources to chocolate production.
Explanation: Note: no marks for a diagram.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not consumers would benefit from a firm becoming a monopoly.
Markscheme: Why they might: a monopoly may have lower average costs of production due to economies of scale; lower costs of production may result in lower prices; a monopoly may have larger profits which can be spent on R&D; higher R&D can improve the quality of the product; may be a state-owned monopoly which seeks to increase social welfare. Why they might not: monopoly may use market power to increase price; lack of competition may reduce pressure to improve quality; consumers will have less choice; there may be a lack of availability if the monopoly restricts supply to drive up price; a monopoly may have higher average costs due to diseconomies of scale.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Cyprus economy: rising life expectancy and GDP per head, grape market shortage, ageing workforce, retirement age debate, budget surplus but current account deficit, low interest rates, increasing capital-intensive production
Question Text: Source material about Cyprus economy
Calculate the number of births in Cyprus in 2019.
Identify two indicators of living standards.
Markscheme: GDP per head / per capita; HDI; Life expectancy / healthy people; Proportion of people in primary sector; Level of education / qualifications
Explanation: Do not reward GDP

Question Text: State why the global market for grapes was in disequilibrium in 2019.
Markscheme: Bad weather / failure of the grape harvest there was a shortage which means demand exceeded supply.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why more well-educated workers tend to work past retirement age than less-educated workers.
Markscheme: Well-paid / good work benefits which provides an incentive to work for longer / high job satisfaction / enter workforce late; Healthy and so more able to work for longer / remain productive; Work in jobs which do not require physical strength / less tiring older workers tend to be less strong / allows them to continue working as long as remain fit.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse how a rise in a country’s retirement age is likely to affect its government’s budget.
Markscheme: A rise in the retirement age / less people retired will reduce the cost of pensions may reduce government spending / government may spend on alternatives e.g. infrastructure, education or health. More people in work will increase incomes / businesses expand production raise direct tax revenue spending will increase raise indirect tax revenue.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between inflation rates and current account balances.
Markscheme: Overview: Generally, an inverse relationship / the lower the inflation rate, the higher the current account surplus or the higher the inflation rate, the higher the current account deficit. Supporting evidence: Mongolia has the highest inflation rate and the largest current account deficit; Mongolia / Kenya / Nepal have the highest inflation rates all have a current account deficit; Iceland / Slovenia have a low rate of inflation but have a current balance of payments surplus. Exception: Cyprus lowest inflation rate but a current account deficit / 3rd highest current account deficit. Comments: Most of the table shows the expected relationship. Low inflation rates may make products more price competitive / high inflation rates may make prices less price competitive.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not, after 2019, Cyprus’s production possibility curve (PPC) is likely to shift to the right.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Quantity / quality of resources may have increased retirement age may have increased people living longer so labour force is likely to have increased; Investment may have increased due to low interest rates more capital goods would increase productive capacity. Reasons why it might not: Quantity and quality of resources may have decreased land quality is decreasing which will reduce the productivity of land; Entrepreneurs may emigrate reducing the supply of enterprise / skilled workers; Birth rate is falling size of workforce may fall in future; Covid-19 may have reduced productivity.
Explanation: For recognising that any of these reasons could result in PPC shifting to the left / stay unchanged.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not adopting capital-intensive production will benefit consumers.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: It may reduce costs of production / more efficient / may result in economies of scale lower costs may reduce prices; It may avoid disruption to supply as no industrial action / strikes; It may be quicker / faster to respond to changes in consumer demand; It may raise quality as absence of human error. Reasons why it might not: Products may be standardised / lack variety individual wants may not be met; Capital equipment may break down / costly to maintain causing delays in production; Initial high cost of capital equipment may push up prices.

Stimulus / Scenario: Australia trade patterns and exchange rate; Papua New Guinea using income tax and policies to reduce inflation and promote growth
Question Text: Scenario about trade and macro policy
Define foreign exchange rate.
Markscheme: The price / value of a currency in terms of another currency / currencies.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why the value of a country’s exports may be greater than the value of its imports.
Markscheme: Price of exports may be lower than price of other countries' products due to e.g. devaluation / depreciation / weaker currency / higher productivity / lower inflation; Quality of exports may be higher than the quality of other countries' products due to e.g. more investment / better education / specialisation; Incomes abroad may have increased enabling foreigners to buy more of the country's products; Protective measures restrict imports / subsidise domestic firms or exports; May have high value exports e.g. oil with high (global) demand; Recession / low demand at home results in fewer imports / encourages firms to export.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in income tax can affect a country’s inflation rate.
Markscheme: An increase in income tax will reduce disposable income / purchasing power this may reduce consumer spending lower total demand this may encourage firms to reduce prices / reduce price rises lower demand-pull inflation. Increase in income tax may encourage workers to press for wage rises increase costs of production cause cost-push inflation. Income tax revenue can be used to provide e.g. subsidies to certain goods and services reducing prices.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not governments should aim for a high rate of economic growth.
Markscheme: Why it should: economic growth can raise incomes; higher incomes can raise living standards; economic growth can reduce unemployment; economic growth can increase tax revenue allowing the government to spend more on e.g. education. Why it should not: may deplete non-renewable resources which can reduce future growth; may result in pollution; may put other demands on workers who may have to work long hours; may lead to a deficit on current account of the balance of payment; may result in greater inequality; risk of inflation.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Sweden high trade union membership and tertiary employment; consumer spending including demerit goods
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and consumption
Identify two influences on what products a person buys.
Markscheme: Price; income; tastes / habit / culture; advertising; availability; social media / influencers; quality; government regulations / laws; age.
Explanation: Accept any reasonable example.

Question Text: Explain two ways a government could decrease the consumption of demerit goods.
Markscheme: Impose an (indirect) tax / tariff on goods raise the price; Provide information about harmful effects; Ban / regulate to stop or reduce the availability of the product; Set minimum price above market price raise prices; Import quota restricts availability for purchase.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse why workers may join a trade union.
Markscheme: Understanding of role of trade union to gain greater bargaining power through industrial action to negotiate with employers for higher wages better working conditions / health & safety fringe benefits. To represent them in disputes with employers e.g. to protect their rights against unfair dismissal / discrimination increase job security compensation for work injuries. To gain greater influence on the government to e.g. increase a national minimum wage increase job opportunities. Unions may provide services to members training courses improving health and safety benefits e.g. discounts at gyms financial and legal advice.
Explanation: Award no marks for examples of industrial action

Question Text: Discuss whether or not tertiary sector workers are paid more than primary sector workers.
Markscheme: Why they might be: may be more skilled / qualified; may be more productive; tertiary products may have higher value added; tertiary products may have higher demand, so industries may have greater ability to pay higher wages. Why they might not be: may be less likely to belong to a trade union; some workers may be in lower paid jobs e.g. cleaners; some tertiary sector firms / occupations may be declining; some may be less experienced; some may not be in promoted posts; primary sector products may have higher value added e.g. oil; primary sector workers in a developed country may be paid more than tertiary workers in an undeveloped country; primary jobs may be more dangerous e.g. mining.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Note: accept approach that discusses why primary sector workers may be better paid.

Stimulus / Scenario: Uganda agriculture specialisation, privatisation of firms, persistent poverty and unemployment, supply-side policies
Question Text: Scenario about Uganda economy
Define privatisation.
Markscheme: Privatisation is the sale of state-owned / public sector firms / public sector assets to the private sector / investors / individuals.
Explanation: Award 1 mark for an understanding of the private sector. Accept change / movement / turned from public to private sector

Question Text: Explain the difference between absolute poverty and relative poverty.
Markscheme: Absolute poverty is a lack of basic necessities e.g. food, clothing or housing / e.g. $1.90 a day. Relative poverty is being poor in comparison to other people (in their country) example of a measure such as less than 60% of average income / not able to participate fully in normal activities in their country.
Explanation: No mark for stating those in relative poverty can meet their basic needs but not their wants.

Question Text: Analyse how specialisation can benefit firms.
Markscheme: Understanding of specialisation of a firm / workers become skilled at producing a particular product lower cost of production gain a good reputation / high quality of good or service increase demand increase output raise revenue increase profit. May enable firms to gain advantage of economies of scale example become (internationally) competitive. Easier to mechanise / improve technology speeds up / faster production / become more efficient.
Explanation: Maximum of one mark for any number of examples of economies of scale. Note that some students only write about division of labour which is not the question but some of the features also apply to specialisation of firms.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the use of supply-side policy measures will reduce unemployment.
Markscheme: Why it might: education and training can improve the skills of workers, making them more attractive to employers; education and training can increase mobility of workers, reducing structural unemployment; lower income tax / lower unemployment benefit can increase the incentive to work / reduce voluntary unemployment; privatisation and deregulation may make firms more competitive, may expand and employ more workers; subsidies to employ more workers e.g. furlough schemes. Why it might not: education and training may be in the wrong areas; measures can take a long time to have an effect; privatisation and deregulation may result in a reduction in output and employment; supply-side policy will not reduce cyclical unemployment; cut in unemployment benefit may increase unemployment by reducing total demand.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Singapore market economy with government intervention promoting healthy eating and managing scarce land and labour
Question Text: Scenario about Singapore economy
Identify the reward to labour and the reward to land.
Markscheme: Wages; rent

Question Text: Explain how an increase in a worker’s income can affect their mobility of labour.
