Markscheme: An increase in income will enable a worker to spend more on their education increasing occupational mobility; Higher income will make housing more affordable increasing geographical mobility; Higher income will make high-tech products more affordable enabling a worker to be better informed increasing both occupational and geographical mobility; Higher incomes may discourage a worker from changing jobs / may be content with current job may be satisfied with income level reducing geographical and occupational mobility; Higher incomes make it easier to afford car / use public transport increases geographical mobility.
Explanation: No marks for causes of higher income.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how greater awareness of the health benefits of eating fruit will affect the market for fruit.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled-price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the right; Equilibriums—shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points E1 and E2. Written analysis: Greater awareness of health benefits will encourage more people to eat / demand fruit; Raise price / raise quantity traded.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written analysis. Note: Axes do not have to be labelled price of fruit and quantity of fruit to get mark. No marks for stating price changes from P1 to P2 or quantity changes from Q1 to Q2. To get first mark in written analysis they must refer to fruit.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a market economic system benefits an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: profit incentive and competition may increase efficiency; wage differentials may encourage effort; price may be low and quality high; country's products may be internationally competitive; economic growth may be high; consumer choice may be high; consumer sovereignty may be high. Why it might not: risk of market failure; public goods will not be provided; demerit goods are overconsumed and merit goods are under-consumed; problem of pollution and other external costs; unemployment may be high as lack of co-ordination; some countries may lack the support structures e.g. legal system to enforce property rights to allow market forces to work efficiently; monopolies can be created.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Happiness and development: comparison of Bhutan and New Zealand, World Happiness Index, sector employment differences, productivity differences, relationship between GDP per head and migration, debate over using wellbeing rather than GDP to measure progress
Question Text: Source material about happiness and economic development
Calculate Bhutan’s GDP in 2018.
Explanation: Accept the figure without the $ sign.

Question Text: Identify two indicators that are in both the World Happiness Index and the Human Development Index.
Markscheme: Life expectancy; GDP per head
Explanation: If more than two indicators are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: State why Bhutan’s 2018 government budget is likely to have increased total demand in Bhutan.
Markscheme: Government spending was greater than government revenue there was a budget deficit of $70 m the government may have used expansionary fiscal policy.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why someone may prefer to work in the tertiary sector rather than in the primary sector.
Markscheme: Better working conditions / non-wage factors e.g. working inside / not having to work in wet weather / less stress / more job satisfaction / fringe benefits; Shorter working hours permitting more leisure time; Type of work / less physically demanding / not manual work less risk to health / less tiring / less dangerous / fewer accidents / may lack physical strength; Better paid enabling more goods and services to be purchased / higher standard of living / as may require more qualifications / skills/training / value added may greater in the tertiary sector / tertiary sector may be expanding / primary sector contracting / may be higher demand for tertiary workers.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation. Note there is a degree of 'mix and match' here. For example, working hours or manual work can be taken as a development of working conditions. Accept an answer that explains the disadvantages of working in the primary sector e.g. may prefer to work in the tertiary sector as working hours are longer in the primary sector.

Question Text: Analyse how investment in capital goods and education can increase New Zealand’s productivity.
Markscheme: Higher investment in capital goods will enable workers to work with better equipment more output per worker / worker hour. New capital goods may incorporate advances in technology / be of higher quality result in innovation can work for long hours. Higher investment in education can enable more children to be educated / children to be educated for longer / improve quality of education / increase knowledge increase workers' skills / qualifications enable workers to use more advanced technology. Investment in capital goods and/or education can enable capital goods / workers to work at a faster rate be more efficient make fewer mistakes / less wastage.
Explanation: Up to 3 marks for analysis on investment in capital goods and up to 3 marks for investment in education. Note only credit increase efficiency once.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP per head and net migration.
Markscheme: Overview: Generally, the countries with higher GDP per head have net immigration / net migration / the countries with lower GDP per head tend to have net emigration / positive/direct relationship. Supporting evidence: Up to 2 marks for two pieces of supporting evidence e.g. New Zealand has the highest GDP per head and the highest net immigration / net migration / highest positive migration; Cyprus has the second highest GDP per head and the second highest net immigration / net migration / positive migration; the four countries with the highest GDP per head, all have net immigration / positive net migration; the two countries with lowest GDP per head both have net emigration / negative net migration. Exception: Exception is Kenya or Mozambique Kenya has higher GDP per head but higher net emigration than Mozambique. Comments: Higher GDP per head may encourage people to move to the country to increase their living standards / income / people who migrate into the country may be high skilled / lower GDP per head may encourage people to emigrate to increase their living standards/income other influences e.g. immigration controls / size of population / wars / political instability.
Explanation: There may be an implied comparison e.g. generally countries with a high GDP per head have net immigration. New Zealand has a GDP per head of approx. $55 000 and net immigration of about 40,000 whereas Kenya has a GDP per head of $2,000 and net emigration of about 50 000 (1 mark). For explanation of exception, do not accept that Kenya has lower net migration / Mozambique has higher net migration - a misinterpretation of the figure.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not enterprise is likely to have increased in New Zealand after 2019.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: High labour productivity / high-skilled workers may keep costs down and increase profits which will motivate more people to be entrepreneurs; Higher investment / better quality capital goods may increase the number of firms / result in expansion of firms and the opportunities to become entrepreneurs; Investment in education / better quality education may increase the people with the skills to become entrepreneurs / increase risk taking ability; Higher government spending on mental health / child poverty / pollution may increase workers' skills / increase workers' health / increase workers' productivity higher government subsidies reduce firms' costs of production higher government spending may increase total demand; Higher economic growth may have created more opportunities / greater confidence / higher total demand; Net immigration may have continued and some immigrants may have been entrepreneurs / may set up new firms more people in the country may raise total demand; Cut in income tax rates could increase demand firms' revenue / profits create greater profit incentive. Reasons why it might not: Uncertainty / lack of confidence reason e.g. there may have been a recession / negative economic growth / unclear what may happen to GDP which could have resulted in the reward to enterprise declining may think there is a greater risk of running / setting up a firm; Corporation tax may have increased / tax may have increased which would reduce retained profits / amount of profit that can be kept; Government attempts to reduce pollution / poverty / improve mental health may increase firms' costs of production and reduce profits may have an opportunity cost less spending on something that could increase enterprise e.g. reduction is spending on education.
Explanation: Accept tax revenue may have increased for tax may have increased for why it might not. Reward recognition that tax revenue may rise without a rise in tax rates or with a fall in tax rates.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a cut in income tax rates will increase living standards.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Will increase disposable income increase ability to buy goods and services / increase purchasing power buy basic necessities / reduce absolute poverty may be able to fulfil more wants / buy more luxuries; May increase spending increase total demand reduce unemployment / increase employment increase output / increase economic growth; May increase saving and so provide a safety net; May encourage investment which may lower costs of production reduce prices; May be able to buy more merit goods / better quality education/healthcare increase health / life expectancy; May increase the incentive to work may earn more in work than on unemployment benefits; May enable people to work fewer hours enjoy more leisure time. Reasons why it might not: Tax revenue may fall may result in a cut in government spending on e.g. education / health care / mental healthcare / child poverty / pollution / unemployment benefit poor may be most reliant on public services; It may increase congestion as people buy more cars may lead to pollution; It may increase depletion of non-renewable resources as consumption increases; It may benefit mostly the rich / poor may not benefit / will not benefit the unemployed and so not everyone may enjoy higher living standards; People may spend on demerit goods higher spending which could reduce health / life expectancy; Higher spending / demand may increase prices / cause inflation leave purchasing power unchanged / reduce purchasing power.
Explanation: Rise in income is not sufficient – need idea of disposable income.

Stimulus / Scenario: Angola money supply growth, high inflation concerns, unemployment reduction policy
Question Text: Scenario about Angola macroeconomic policy
Identify two forms of money.
Markscheme: coins; bank notes / paper money; bank deposits / accounts; digital money
Explanation: Do not accept credit cards, debit cards or cheques. If more than two forms are given, consider the first three. Accept cash instead of coins and/or bank notes/paper money for 1 mark. Also accept commodity money, fiat money/legal tender and fiduciary money. Reward but do not expect identification of reserves at the central bank. Also accept examples of items that have been used as money e.g. salt, gold. Accept a currency as alternative to either coins or paper money.

Question Text: Explain two functions a central bank performs for its government.
Markscheme: The central bank acts as a banker for the government it accepts tax payments to the government / provides facilities for the government to make payments / manages the national debt / lends to the government; It operates the government's monetary policy e.g. changing interest rates / to influence inflation; It holds reserves of foreign currency to influence the foreign exchange rate; It manages the government's debt issuing government securities / paying interest on government securities; It issues bank notes influencing the money supply; It may regulate commercial banks / banking system to ensure they act responsibly; It acts as a lender of last resort lending to commercial banks / to prevent banks going out of business / a financial collapse.
Explanation: One mark for each of two functions identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse why a government may have lower unemployment as its main aim.
Markscheme: Unemployment may be at a high level unemployment is an inefficient use of resources / waste of resources not working at full capacity. Lower unemployment would increase output / economic growth raise incomes / GDP per head increase living standards reduce poverty / more able to buy basic necessities may reduce the crime rate may increase exports. Lower unemployment will increase total demand / increase consumer spending / increase spending increase tax revenue lower government spending on unemployment benefits increase the government's ability to spend on (e.g. education) / reduce budget deficit / government spending on unemployment benefits involves an opportunity cost. Lower unemployment may reduce the chances of workers losing skills being in work keeps workers up to date with e.g. advances in technology.
Explanation: Accept an answer from the viewpoint of why unemployment may be harmful e.g., a government may want lower unemployment as unemployment reduces economic growth.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not inflation will harm producers.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase costs of production - cost-push inflation - reducing profit; may reduce international competitiveness; may cause menu costs; may cause shoe leather costs; may make it difficult to plan ahead. Why it might not: may be demand-pull inflation, increasing firms' revenue; may be low and stable; may be lower than other countries' inflation rates, increasing price competitiveness; may enable them to reduce real wages; may increase tax revenue, some of which may be spent on e.g. subsidies; cost of debt may fall / may be cheaper to borrow (in real terms); some producers may be making products with inelastic demand.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: France producing inside PPC, exports of luxury goods and soap, technological change and infrastructure investment
Question Text: Scenario about France economy
Define a production possibility curve.
Markscheme: A diagram showing maximum output / possible combinations of two types of products / of capital and consumer goods with given resources / given technology / in a given time period shows opportunity cost.
Explanation: Nothing for a diagram.

Question Text: Explain why demand for soap is more price-inelastic than demand for a luxury brand of perfume.
Markscheme: Soap is more essential / necessary than a luxury brand of perfume OR soap is a necessity luxury brand of perfume does not have to be purchased / not essential / a want; Soap has fewer substitutes than a luxury brand of perfume OR soap does not have close substitutes whereas a luxury brand of perfume may have close substitutes; Soap is likely to take up a smaller proportion of income than a luxury brand of soap OR soap is likely to take only a small proportion of income whereas a luxury brand of perfume is likely to take up a large proportion; It is harder to delay the purchase of soap than the purchase of a luxury brand of perfume OR the purchase of soap cannot be delayed while the purchase of a luxury brand of perfume can be.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how an increase in income will affect the market for a luxury brand of perfume.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the right; Equilibrium – shown by lines or equilibrium points. Written analysis: Luxury perfume will be more affordable / greater purchasing power / luxury perfume will take a smaller proportion of income / more will be demanded at the same price price will rise / quantity traded will increase.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written analysis.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the public sector should be responsible for the supply of all internet services.
Markscheme: Why it might: government may charge a low price; enable poor to gain access to the internet, help children with education; government may base decisions on social costs and benefits; government can control what is on the internet; may be more able to take advantage of economies of scale; avoidance of wasteful duplication; government may take a longer-term view on investing. Why it might not: opportunity cost - could have spent the money on e.g. healthcare; private sector may invest more; private sector may be more efficient / have lower costs of production; profit incentive may encourage private sector to raise quality; private sector may make decisions quicker.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: South Korea steel dumping dispute, multinational companies abroad, exchange rate depreciation, labour shortages due to low birth rate
Question Text: Scenario about South Korea economy
Define dumping.
Markscheme: The sale of a product at less than cost price in a foreign country to gain a larger market share to get rid of surplus stock / to prevent price falling on the domestic market.
Explanation: Accept the sale of a product at a low price.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a country’s foreign exchange rate may depreciate.
Markscheme: Inflation / fall in productivity may make domestic products less internationally competitive / reducing net exports / reducing exports / increasing imports; Income at home may rise increasing demand for imports / diverting products from the home market; Incomes abroad may have fallen reducing demand for exports; The rate of interest may have fallen discouraging an inflow of funds from other countries / encouraging an outflow of funds to other countries; There may be speculation that the currency will fall in value selling the currency before it falls in value; Inward foreign direct investment may fall / outward fdi may increase due to changes in economic activity; A central bank/government may sell the currency to increase exports / reduce imports / improve the balance of payments; Increase in supply of currency (could be shown by a diagram); Decrease in demand for the currency (could be shown on a diagram).
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation. A common response may be an increase in the supply of the currency to buy more imports; A decrease in demand for the currency if less exports are sold. Total = 4.

Question Text: Analyse how a MNC could reduce poverty in a host country.
Markscheme: A MNC could increase employment / create job opportunities some of the unemployed could gain jobs / unemployment could fall. It may pay higher wages / incomes may rise increasing workers' ability to buy products / buy basic necessities. It may provide accommodation and healthcare for workers. It may provide infrastructure making e.g. transport/energy more affordable. It may provide training / invest in education enabling people to earn higher wages in the long run. It may sell products at a cheaper price enabling more goods and services to be purchased. It may pay taxes to the government / tax revenue may rise enabling the government to spend on measures to reduce poverty.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a very low birth rate would be a cause for concern for a government.
Markscheme: Why it might: may create an ageing population; may reduce size of the labour force in the future; may increase dependency ratio; may reduce tax revenue; may take population below the optimum level. Why it might not: may maintain the size of the labour force in the short run as parents do not have to leave the labour force; may reduce overcrowding; may reduce pollution; may reduce depletion of non-renewable resources.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Nicaragua GDP decline, rising household spending, banana supply increase, merit goods, government encouraging output
Question Text: Scenario about Nicaragua economy
Define supply.
Markscheme: Willingness to sell a product and ability to sell a product. Supply at a given price / in a given time period.
Explanation: Allow one mark in total for amount produced or amount sold. Need idea of selling / making available to consumers for full two marks.

Question Text: Explain two ways a government could increase the consumption of merit goods.
Markscheme: Provide information/education about the benefits of merit goods / consumers may be unaware of full benefits; Provide merit goods free / direct provision e.g. education and healthcare / nationalise industries producing merit goods; Subsidise the production of merit goods to lower prices; Subsidise the consumption of merit goods by e.g. providing vouchers; Set a maximum price / price ceiling to make merit goods more affordable / to lower price; Pass laws / introduce regulation to make consumption of merit goods compulsory; Remove / cut indirect taxes on merit goods to make them more affordable / to lower price.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each explanation. Can accept lower price as a development of a subsidy for a mark and lower price as another mark if given as a development of indirect taxes or maximum price.

Question Text: Analyse how the level and pattern of household spending may change when GDP decreases.
Markscheme: Spending may decrease as households may have less income less able to buy goods and services / lower purchasing power. Lower GDP may reduce confidence / create greater uncertainty encouraging people to save more. May borrow less may be concerned cannot repay. May expect prices to fall further delay purchases. Spending may not decrease if households think the decrease is temporary may borrow as interest rates may be lower may use past saving to finance spending. May spend a higher proportion on basic necessities less on luxuries/wants. May spend a higher proportion of income as saving may fall by more than spending. Households may spend less on imports imports may be higher in price / lower in quality. GDP may decrease but GDP per head may rise if population falls by more than GDP.
Explanation: Reward but do not expect: reference to inferior and normal goods; dissaving; Nominal GDP may fall but real GDP may rise if there is deflation

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a firm will benefit from an increase in its output.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase average revenue, if higher output is result of higher demand; profits may rise if average revenue raises more than average cost; may be able to make greater use of spare capacity; higher output may increase a firm's market power; greater advantage may be taken of technical, marketing, managerial, labour and risk bearing economies of scale. Why it might not: average revenue may fall, higher output may reduce price; not all of the higher output may be sold / may be a lack of demand; diseconomies of scale may be experienced – managerial / communication problems may occur / labour relations may decline; growth achieved by vertical merger may lead to problems of co-ordination.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: eSports industry and Malta: rapid global growth in viewers and revenue, private and public investment, government strategy to diversify Malta’s economy, importance of labour adaptability and female labour force participation
Question Text: Source material about eSports and Malta’s economy
Calculate Europe’s percentage contribution to the global total eSports revenue in 2019.
Explanation: % sign not essential.

Question Text: Identify two conditions of demand for eSports.
Markscheme: Increasing incomes; Decrease in the price of technology needed to participate in this sport

Question Text: Explain how a change in social attitudes would lead to greater participation by women in the labour force.
Markscheme: Acceptance that women are equal to men / that it is acceptable for women to work and that their role is not only as a home maker childcare is not just female role; It is more acceptable for women to be educated / there is more encouragement for women to study which increases their job opportunities; Women feel more comfortable / accepted at work more jobs will be open to women; Wages paid to women may rise eSports are no longer seen as a male-only sport.

Question Text: Explain two differences between private sector investment and public sector investment.
Markscheme: Private sector investments are investments made by firms while the public sector investments are investments made by the government; Private sector investments are made to maximise profits while public sector investments are made to maximise welfare of society (e.g. provide employment); Private sector investments made based on private costs and benefits while public sector investments are made based on social costs and benefits; Profits from private sector investment go to shareholders profits from public sector investment go to public spending e.g. health care; The aim of private sector investment is to be more competitive / gain market share the aim of public sector investment is to improve macroeconomic performance; Private sector investment may be financed by retained profits/entrepreneurs public sector investment may be financed by tax revenue.
Explanation: For the 2nd mark for public sector investment, accept any of the macroeconomic objectives as the reason why public sector investment is made.

Question Text: Analyse how the development of eSports in Malta can help the government achieve its macroeconomic aims.
Markscheme: Economic growth is indicated as eSports is part of the service sector that contributes 90% of Malta's GDP development of eSports in Malta can increase Malta's GDP; Unemployment may be reduced / employment increased because it will be able to attract investments creating more jobs from building infrastructure and more professional e-sports players; Current account may also be improved by more inflow of money from increased expenditure on exports of services / tourism; Tax revenues may increase enabling the government to increase spending on e.g. education.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP per head and the proportion of females in the labour force.
Markscheme: Overview: Generally, GDP per head is higher when the proportion of females in the labour force is higher / positive relationship between GDP per head and proportion of females in the labour force. Supporting Evidence: (up to 2 marks) Yemen has the lowest female labour force participation rate and also the lowest GDP per head; Luxembourg has the highest female labour force participation rate and the highest GDP per head; Yemen, Saudi Arabia, and Bangladesh have the 3 lowest figures for both female participation rate and GDP per head; Luxembourg, France and Malta have the 3 highest figures for both female participation rate and GDP per head. Exception: However, exception is Saudi Arabia / Bangladesh Saudi Arabia has a lower female labour force participation rate but higher GDP per head than Bangladesh. Comments: This is because higher female labour force participation rate means there are higher quantities of labour which is a factor of production / more labour is available to contribute to the growth of the economy increased availability of education for women will positively affect GDP.
Explanation: Give BOD if answer states GDP alone and not GDP per head.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not specialisation at a national level is harmful to an economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might be harmful: Specialisation could lead to overdependence on a specific industry / danger of over-specialisation if this industry fails e.g. if there is a fall in demand / revenue from eSports, the whole Maltese economy could be adversely affected; Specialisation makes it difficult for the workers to retrain to other industries danger of structural unemployment; Specialisation might lead to diseconomies of scale where long run average costs increase as output increases; Resources may become exhausted making industry unsustainable. Reasons why it might not be harmful: Specialisation leads to increase in efficiency increased competitiveness as the economy focuses on what they do best, they will be able to produce more and at a better quality lower cost; Specialisation enables workers to become highly trained / skilled and increase their productivity leading to higher incomes and demand for their labour; Specialisation could help the economy achieve economies of scale where long-run average cost falls as output increases; Specialisation enables countries to trade more with other countries increased export earnings can enable countries to afford more imports as well leading to increased choices higher standards of living; As output increases and trade increases, governments receive more tax revenue.
Explanation: Reward but do not expect reference to comparative/absolute advantage.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not government investment in education and training will help the Maltese economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it could: Government investment in education and training will enable more students to gain access to e.g. higher education / create a more educated labour force which will enable them to increase their skills productivity / efficiency and enable them to get jobs in the future reducing unemployment increase incomes which would increase consumption / total demand and output / GDP; Organising work placements for Maltese students will enable students to gain work experience further increasing their skills and help firms develop with new ideas / extra help at low cost increasing output and economic growth; Tax from incomes of those employed will improve public finances; Bringing in professionals from other countries to train Maltese students could introduce more expertise helping to transfer new skills; May contribute to the growth of the tertiary sector increase development; A more educated labour force may attract MNCs more efficient production. Reasons why it could not: Investment in education and training is expensive using public finances; opportunity cost for the government less spending on government projects such as Healthcare could decrease overall productivity of the economy less economic growth; Education and training might not be successful work placements might not teach students the right skills needed in the future / scholarship schemes misused or not given out fairly / foreign professionals might not be able to transfer knowledge effectively; Education and training can take a very long time before it impacts the economy scholarship schemes usually at least 3 years for a degree level course; In the short run, it could reduce the size of the labour force reduce output / lower productive potential; There may be emigration of skilled workers other countries gaining the benefits.

Stimulus / Scenario: Voralberg region of Austria: high income and healthcare, benefits of free trade with neighbouring countries, higher inflation than most areas in Europe
Question Text: Scenario about regional development
Define free trade.
Markscheme: Free trade is when countries export and import between each other / countries exchange goods and services without any restrictions / protection such as tariffs or quotas.

Question Text: Explain two possible causes of better healthcare.
Markscheme: More government expenditure on healthcare e.g. on hospitals / training / due to higher tax revenue; Higher incomes can spend more on healthcare; Better / more hospitals better quality service / less waiting time; Better (health) training / education more qualified / specialised / doctors; Better medical technology better machines / life-saving equipment; Easier access to medicine more affordable.
Explanation: One mark for each of two causes identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse why few MNCs may choose to locate in a particular region.
Markscheme: The region may have higher cost of production / inflation than other regions. e.g. higher cost of labour / land reduces potential profit. May also be higher taxation e.g. corporation tax which may reduce the incentive of an MNC to locate in the region. May be more regulations e.g. labour laws which may make it more difficult for an MNC to locate in that region / which may increase costs. May be a lack of skilled workers due to poor education may reduce productivity. May be low/lack of demand limiting the revenues and profitability of the MNC. May be a lack of subsidies which would reduce the incentive of an MNC to locate in the region. May be a lack of resources e.g. raw materials, labour. Major competitors may have already located in that region. The region may have corrupt government(s) causing political instability / war. It may be outside a free trade area, e.g. the EU unable to gain benefits.
Explanation: Accept answers that analyse why MNC's may choose to locate in a region (rather than few choosing to locate there).

Question Text: Discuss whether or not inflation is always a disadvantage to an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might be a disadvantage: lower purchasing power for consumers – lower standards of living; lower price competitiveness of exports – lower exports / high imports; higher cost of production – lower profits; lose value of savings and lenders lose value of lending; difficult for consumers, firms, and governments to plan for the future; shoe leather costs, menu costs; may discourage investment as may be difficult to plan; those on fixed incomes may lose. Why it might be an advantage: borrowers may benefit – value of debt decreases; may encourage investment as may be cheaper to borrow; may be a sign that an economy is growing; may be demand-pull inflation – increasing firms' revenue / employment; if inflation is low and stable – it is better than prices decreasing (deflation); may be lower than other countries' inflation rates.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Jewellery industries in Qatar and Ukraine seeking removal of trade protection to expand trade and exploit internal economies of scale
Question Text: Scenario about trade protection and scale economies
Identify two internal economies of scale.
Markscheme: Purchasing / buying; managerial; technical; financial; risk-bearing; labour; marketing

Question Text: Explain two methods of protection in international trade.
Markscheme: tariffs are taxes on imports / which would increase the price of imports; import quotas are quantitative limitations on imports / reducing supply of imports; subsidies are grants given to domestic producers to reduce price of domestic products and exports / enable them to compete with foreign goods; embargoes / bans are total restrictions on a product from a country / total restrictions on particular products / usually for political / social reason.
Explanation: One mark for each of two methods identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, the effects of higher quality products on the market for jewellery.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Demand curve shifts to the right; Equilibrium – shown by lines; P1, Q1 and P2, Q2; or equilibrium points E1 and E2. Written comments: Higher quality products will encourage more people to buy jewellery; price / quantity will be higher.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written comments.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not protection in international trade benefits an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might be a benefit: reduce imports – reduce current account deficit; helps infant industries grow – increase competitiveness in the future; helps maintain sunset industries – ensures employment; develop specialisation; help strategic industries – such as defence; restrict imports of demerit goods – such as alcohol. Why it might not be a benefit: increase in the price of goods and services – inflation - decreasing purchasing power of consumers; retaliation – difficult to export goods and services to other countries; increase in cost of production if firms require raw materials / semi-manufactured goods from other countries; complacency – low quality domestic goods and services; may cause unemployment e.g. in industries relying on imported products; if PED is less than one it may not be effective; may encourage smuggling.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Nauru’s economy: once very high GDP per head from phosphate exports, resource depletion, pollution and unhealthy food imports causing market failure, current account surplus despite trade deficit in goods
Question Text: Scenario about Nauru economy
Define GDP per head.
Markscheme: Total output / income / expenditure divided by population / per person.
Explanation: Award 2 marks if correct formula given i.e. GDP per head = Total GDP / Population

Question Text: Explain how a country with more imports of goods than exports of goods can still record a surplus on the current account of its balance of payments.
Markscheme: Export prices may be high import prices may be low export revenue may exceed import expenditure. Current account consists of trade in goods, trade in services, primary income, and secondary income. Therefore, even if there are more imports of goods than exports of goods, which creates a trade of goods deficit if the other three components have a surplus then the overall current account will have a surplus. This is likely to be the case if there is a high level of income from investments abroad exports of services a lot of transfer payments such as inflow of aid to a country incomes of nationals working abroad / remittances.

Question Text: Analyse the causes of market failure.
Markscheme: Market failure is when market forces cause an inefficient allocation of resources. Demerit goods example imperfect information where consumers may not know about the actual costs. Merit goods example consumers may not know about actual benefits. External costs costs to the third party not considered by consumers and producers therefore goods are overproduced or overconsumed. External benefits benefits to the third party not considered by consumers and producers goods are underproduced and underconsumed. Public goods non-rivalry and non-excludable free rider problem people want to consume but don't want to pay for it no profit incentive for firms goods are underprovided by the market. Abuse of monopoly power when firms have no competitors they will push up prices or reduce quality. Factor immobility where labour cannot move from one job to another or to take up a job somewhere else leads to structural unemployment.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not having a large primary sector is a disadvantage to an economy.
Markscheme: Why primary sector is a disadvantage: lack of value added - low price, low income - low standards of living; unstable prices - unstable income; low productivity - low income; high tariffs - difficult to export; specialisation in any one sector is risky; unsustainable - natural resources could be exhausted. Why primary sector is an advantage: provides employment, especially low skilled in developing countries; provides employment, e.g. high skilled in Saudi Arabian oil; produces high value added exports, e.g. gold in S. Africa; produces exports with inelastic demand, e.g. oil; low start-up costs, low barriers to entry, e.g. farming; may be better than other countries at producing primary products; may be able to diversify with money earned from primary sector; could reduce inequality between urban and rural areas.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: France government decision-making, opportunity cost of public spending, supply-side policy focus, declining trade union membership
Question Text: Scenario about French economic policy
Define monetary policy.
Markscheme: Supply of money interest rates / foreign exchange rates affecting total demand / inflation

Question Text: Explain, using an example, the influence of opportunity cost on government decision-making.
Markscheme: Opportunity cost is the (next) best alternative forgone / given up / sacrificed. Governments (always) consider opportunity cost when making a decision on spending money to maximise the welfare of society. Governments have to make a choice as they have a limited income/budget. For example, if the government spends on building a new hospital the opportunity cost may be a new school as the government thinks that the opportunity cost of building a new hospital is lower than building a new school.
Explanation: Don't reward examples using personal consumption e.g. tea / coffee

Question Text: Analyse the reasons why trade union membership has reduced in some countries.
Markscheme: Improved working conditions improved wages in a country may have reduced the need for trade unions to negotiate them / have made unions more reluctant to take industrial action. High unemployment fear of losing jobs. Capital intensive production fewer workers available to join trade unions. Government measures against trade unions have reduced the ability of trade unions to influence wages and working conditions. Changes in the pattern of employment more self-employed / part-time employed / gig economy / zero hours contract more people working in the private sector where trade union membership has traditionally been low. Globalisation and growth of MNCs has increase competition amongst labour. Dissatisfaction over trade unions the high fees for joining trade unions not wanting to take strike action different political views disincentivises people from joining. Government introduction of health and safety regulations and minimum wages reduced the key reasons for the existence of trade unions. Removal of mandatory trade union membership (closed shops) means that membership is reduced because it is now voluntary.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not supply-side policy measures always promote economic growth.
Markscheme: Why supply-side policy measures are effective in promoting economic growth: education and training – increase productivity of labour; lower direct taxes – increase incentive to work (reduction in income tax) and increase incentive to invest (reduction in corporation tax); subsidies - reduce business costs and encourage investment; deregulation – reducing red tape / barriers to entry which could lead to an increase in number of firms and increase competition; privatisation – put in profit-maximisation incentive for private firms to invest and innovate and be more productive; reducing trade union power – increases incentive to work harder. Why supply-side policy measures are not effective in promoting economic growth: takes a long time for it to have an impact on the economy; reduces the revenues for government (e.g. cutting taxes); opportunity cost for government; subsidies can lead to over-reliance on them; could lower the quality of goods and services (race to the bottom) and welfare of society; lack of total demand.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Mauritius economy: shift from primary to tertiary sector, tourism growth, infrastructure investment, international trade and foreign investment, outward investment risks and benefits
Question Text: Source material about Mauritius economy
Calculate the total value of the tertiary sector in Mauritius in 2019.
Markscheme: $10.79 billion

Question Text: Explain the meaning of a change from an economy based on the primary sector to one based on the tertiary sector.
Markscheme: When an economy moves from mainly primary industries e.g. agriculture / fishing to mainly tertiary industries e.g. tourism.
Explanation: Accept any examples of primary sector industry for 1 mark and any examples of tertiary sector - based industry for 1 mark.

Question Text: Identify two indicators of improving healthcare in Mauritius.
Markscheme: Life expectancy increased; infant mortality rate fallen.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why millions of tourists visit Mauritius each year.
Markscheme: Mauritius has tourist attractions such as its beautiful beaches; Easy to travel to Mauritius as the government does not require visa / just need passport; Good infrastructure such as good airports / ports and public transport around the island.

Question Text: Explain two advantages of setting up a firm in Mauritius.
Markscheme: Fast growth higher incomes which leads to higher consumption of good and services; Political stability which reduces risk / increases confidence; Low levels of regulations to start and run a business lower cost of start up / easier to register for a business; Better infrastructure more efficient / lower cost of production.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between the percentage of international trade to GDP and GDP per head.
Markscheme: Overview: Generally, as percentage of international trade to GDP increases, GDP per head is increasing as well / positive relationship between percentage of international trade to GDP and GDP per head. Supporting Evidence: Kenya has the lowest percentage of international trade to GDP and lowest GDP per head while Luxembourg has the highest percentage of international trade to GDP and highest GDP per head. Exception: Mauritius where percentage of international trade to GDP is higher than Qatar but GDP per head is lower. Comments: This is because trade brings gains from specialisation / enables exploitation of economies of scale increases earnings from exports / total demand / economic growth.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not outward investment might benefit a Mauritian firm.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might be beneficial: Enables firms to enter new markets increase export increase revenues achieve economies of scale e.g. of economies of scale increase output increase profits; Import products at a lower cost lower cost of production; Increase access to foreign technology better quality / higher productivity decrease average cost. Reasons why it might not be beneficial: Capital abroad might not be reinvested back to help the firm in the home country lower firm's potential productivity decrease profits; Changes in international economy may affect potential earnings recession in another country will affect export earnings may create recession domestically as well decrease output; Other countries might impose restrictions on Mauritian investment or Mauritian government could impose restrictions on outward investment cost might be higher.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not government infrastructure spending benefits the Mauritian economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might be beneficial: Increase efficiency / productivity decrease cost of production for firms increase profits increase investment increase output; Decrease factor immobility decrease market failure easier for workers to move from one place to another in the economy decrease structural unemployment; Increase government spending increase total demand increase output economic growth; Increase job opportunities decrease unemployment. Reasons why it might not be beneficial: Increase total demand could lead to increase demand-pull inflation; Cost of providing infrastructure is very high opportunity cost of the government cannot spend on other projects such as education and healthcare which may be more beneficial than infrastructure spending; Infrastructure such as big new international airports not really needed domestic demand too low white elephant projects; Benefits foreign firms more than the local community.

Stimulus / Scenario: Medan (Indonesia) growth of small firms and food delivery industry, unemployment reduction, limited labour mobility
Question Text: Scenario about small firms and labour markets
Identify the difference between individual supply and market supply.
Markscheme: Individual supply is the amount that a single firm is willing and able to supply at a specific price while market supply is the total of all individual supply / the amount that all the firms in the market is willing and able to supply at a specific price.

Question Text: Explain two influences on the mobility of labour.
Markscheme: Good transport links such as public transport / highways ease of workers moving from one place to another to take up a job; Cheap cost of living / relocation such as good access to affordable homes in different places; Similar culture such as similar languages / work culture; Ease of workers moving from one job to another job lots of re-training opportunities good quality education skills/qualification needed between different jobs are similar.
Explanation: Also accept answers based on influences for low mobility of labour.

Question Text: Analyse the reasons for the existence of small firms.
Markscheme: Barriers to entry may be low easy for firms to enter e.g. low capital start-up cost / low government regulations; Small firms can be monopolist small / niche market; Lots of government support for small firms e.g. subsidies to SME to maintain competition; Firms desire to remain small keep it within family members easy to control satisfied with amount of profit; Difficult for firm to grow as there are dominant firms in the market or difficult to get enough capital to grow large; Economies of scale may be very small low long average costs at low levels of output e.g. not much bulk discount, not much expensive equipment; Diseconomies of scale may be achieved at low levels of output due to the productive inefficiency.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a reduction in the unemployment rate benefits an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might be beneficial: increase in output, increase economic growth / GDP; increase in tax revenue; decrease cost of unemployment benefits; increase income for individuals; decrease inequality; decrease crime rates; decrease stress level, decrease occurrence of homelessness and family breakdown; decrease indebtedness. Why it might not be beneficial: wages increasing, higher cost of production, increase cost-push inflation; increase consumption, increased total demand, increase demand-pull inflation; decrease competitiveness of exports; decrease attractiveness to foreign investors.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Jordan fixed exchange rate with the US dollar, global interest rate cuts to stimulate growth, possible inflation conflicts
Question Text: Scenario about exchange rate systems and macroeconomic policy
Define inflation.
Markscheme: Increase in price level over time / persistent. Rise in price.

Question Text: Explain the effects of low confidence on both spending and borrowing.
Markscheme: Low confidence will reduce spending as consumers will save instead e.g. lack of job security / risk of firms failing / asset prices falling; Falling confidence will reduce borrowing risk of borrowing and investing is too high.
Explanation: Maximum 2 marks for spending and maximum 2 marks for borrowing

Question Text: Analyse how a cut in interest rates might create conflicts between macroeconomic aims.
Markscheme: Cut in interest rates could lead to conflicts between full employment versus stable prices because cut in interest rates lead to increase borrowing decreased savings and increased spending this will lead to an increase in demand-pull inflation. Cut in interest rates could lead to conflicts between economic growth versus balance of payments stability because if people may also buy more imports and therefore current account deficit will increase / current account surplus will decrease. Cut in interest rates could lead to conflicts between full employment versus balance of payments stability because a cut in interest rates could lead to more investments more firms starting up increasing demand for workers increased wages which would lead to increase in spending including imports which will increase current account deficit / decrease current account surplus. Also accept conflict between economic growth and redistribution of income i.e. Cut in interest rates could lead to conflicts between economic growth and redistribution of income because a cut in interest rates could lead to more spending and investment leading to more economic growth and higher incomes for entrepreneurs but savers could get lower returns from savings profits may rise more than wages leading to an increase in inequality.
Explanation: Maximum 4 marks if only 1 conflict analysed

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a country will benefit from having a fixed foreign exchange rate system.
Markscheme: Why it might be an advantage: Certainty - with a fixed exchange rate, firms will always know the exchange rate and this makes trade and investment less risky; Absence of speculation - with a fixed exchange rate, there will be no speculation if people believe that the rate will stay fixed with no revaluation or devaluation; Constraint on government policy - if the exchange rate is fixed, then the government may be unable to pursue extreme or irresponsible macroeconomic policies as these would cause a run on the foreign exchange reserves and this would be unsustainable in the medium-term; Keep inflation low. Firms have an incentive to keep cutting costs to remain competitive. Governments who allow their exchange rate to depreciate may cause inflationary pressures to occur. Depreciation can cause inflation because total demand increases, import prices increase and firms have less incentive to cut costs; Maintain competitiveness - a fixed exchange rate can ensure that exports remain price competitive. A rapid appreciation in a floating exchange rate system will badly affect manufacturing firms who export; this may also cause a worsening of the current account. Why it might be a disadvantage: The economy may be unable to respond to shocks - a fixed exchange rate means that there may be no mechanism for the government to respond rapidly to balance of payments crises; Problems with reserves - fixed exchange rate systems require large foreign exchange reserves and there can be international liquidity problems as a result; Speculation – if foreign exchange markets believe that there may be a revaluation or devaluation, then there may be a run of speculation. Fighting this may cost the government significantly in terms of their foreign exchange reserves; Policy conflicts - the fixed exchange rate may not be compatible with other economic targets for growth, inflation and unemployment and this may cause conflicts of policies. This is especially true if the exchange rate is fixed at a level that is either too high or too low.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Firms with social welfare objectives, changing consumer demand toward environmentally friendly goods, mergers to survive
Question Text: Scenario about firms and market change
Identify two examples of social welfare objectives of firms.
Markscheme: Environmental protection; Provide jobs to disabled / ex-convicts / minorities; Promoting healthy lifestyle; Provide basic necessities to local communities; Good working conditions
Explanation: Accept any reasonable example.

Question Text: Explain two types of merger.
Markscheme: Horizontal is when two (or more) firms at the same stages of production in the same industry merge; Vertical is when two (or more) firms at different stages of production in the same industry merge; Conglomerate is when two (or more) firms in different industries merge.
Explanation: Accept forward and backward vertical integration for all 4 marks.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, the effects on the solar energy industry of the change in spending patterns towards environmentally friendly products.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Demand curve shifts to the right; Equilibriums shown by lines or e.g. E1 and E2. Written comments: Changing spending patterns towards greener products will encourage more people to demand solar energy since it is more environmentally friendly and cleaner decreased demand for polluting substitutes such as coal energy price and quantity of solar energy will be higher.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written comments.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a merger can help a firm survive.
Markscheme: Why merger can help a firm survive: Merger may enable a firm to take greater advantage of economies of scale; Merger will enable struggling firm to get more capital injection to enable more production / cover debts; Merger will enable transfer of technology from other firms which could lead to increase efficiency and productivity; Merger will increase awareness of struggling firm and could increase demand. Why merger cannot help a firm survive: Mergers could lead to diseconomies of scale which will increase the average cost; Mergers that lacks synergy will make it more difficult for the firm to operate; Mergers may eliminate the brand name of the struggling company altogether; Merger will close parts of the firm.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Bulgaria EU membership, low corporation tax, regulation discouraging foreign investment, falling labour supply affecting economic growth
Question Text: Scenario about Bulgaria economy
Define regulation.
Markscheme: a set of rules or laws normally imposed by government / form of government intervention to affect behaviour of various economic actors in the economy.
Explanation: Award 1 mark for any relevant examples e.g. No smoking for under 16s, not allowed to leave home, no chewing gum, prohibition of alcohol sales, compulsory face masks.

Question Text: Explain two causes of a fall in the quantity of labour in a country.
Markscheme: Negative net migration emigration rates higher than immigration rates as people search for better job opportunities / higher wages / higher living standards elsewhere; High death rates the ratio of deaths to the population (per thousand people per year) increases due to poor healthcare facilities / lack of medicines / diseases; Ageing population average population getting older lots of retirees / pensioners no longer contributing to the labour force.

Question Text: Analyse how differences in corporation tax rates between countries may affect multinational companies (MNCs).
Markscheme: Lower corporation tax in one country will attract more MNCs into the country this is because their potential after tax profits will increase. Lower corporation tax will attract more MNCs to reinvest in the economy this is because they will be able to get more profits and thus more capital for reinvestments this could to an increase in investment in capital which leads to increased productivity and increased output of MNCs. Lower corporation tax will enable more firms to expand their operations and therefore hire more workers.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a decrease in a country’s economic growth rate will harm its economy.
Markscheme: Why a decrease in economic growth rate will harm an economy: Less job creation – higher unemployment; Reduction in consumer confidence in the economy; Reduction in investor confidence in the economy; Lower tax revenues – less spending on development; Reduction in level of increase in living standards. Why a decrease in economic growth rate will not harm an economy: Reduce inflation; Less harm to the environment; Might reduce level of imports – less deficit in the current account of

Stimulus / Scenario: Bank mergers in Nigeria and the US; GDP growth, inflation, exchange rate, unemployment effects, education and birth rates, subsidies for farmers, minimum price for high-sugar products
Question Text: Source material about bank mergers and macroeconomic issues
Calculate how many dollars 6400 Nigerian naira would have bought in 2019.
Identify two ways a bank could increase demand for its services.
Markscheme: Open more branches; carry out an advertising campaign; improve online / mobile banking.

Question Text: State why a merger between two commercial banks is a horizontal merger.
Markscheme: Same stage of production / both in the tertiary (service) sector / same sector same industry / same product / same service can increase market share.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a large US commercial bank may charge a high price for its services.
Markscheme: High spending on opening new branches / advertising campaigns / developing online banking need to get back costs / make a profit / may experience diseconomies of scale; May offer high quality services have high demand / customers willing to pay a high price; High degree of monopoly / market power / control 32% of market customers may have brand loyalty / reluctant to switch banks / lack of substitutes / inelastic demand / can charge high prices to earn high profits/raise total revenue / be a price maker; High incomes in the US many customers have the ability to pay a high price; Employ skilled / well educated workers so may pay high wages / may have high costs of production.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in unemployment may affect the Nigerian government’s budget.
Markscheme: Likely to increase a deficit / reduce a surplus / may result in a budget deficit. Tax revenue likely to fall / budget likely to fall due to lower incomes less income tax revenue lower profits less corporation tax revenue lower spending less indirect tax revenue government may cut taxes to reduce unemployment. Government spending likely to rise more spending on e.g. benefits / subsidies to the poor / law and order/ healthcare may be more spending on measures to reduce unemployment / expansionary fiscal policy may spend less on other areas e.g. education.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between countries’ average years of schooling and birth rates.
Markscheme: Overview: Generally, an inverse relationship / the more years of schooling, the lower the birth rate / the fewer years of schooling, the higher the birth rate. Supporting evidence: Up to 2 marks maximum for two pieces of relevant supporting evidence e.g.: Germany has the highest years of schooling and lowest birth rate; Chad has lowest years of schooling and highest birth rate; US has more years of schooling than Argentina and so a lower birth rate; the three countries with three highest years of schooling have the three lowest birth rates. Exception: Nigeria / Eritrea Nigeria has more years of schooling than Eritrea but a higher birth rate. Comments: More years of schooling is likely to cause people to form partnerships / have children later / focus on careers / increase awareness of contraception/family planning / increase health / reduce size of families increase cost of raising children / having more children may reduce the ability of families to keep their children in education for many years / exception may be result of other influences e.g. differences in fertility rates / differences in social attitudes.
Explanation: No marks for just stating what the figures are e.g. Germany has 15 years of schooling and a birth rate of approximately 8. US has 14 years of schooling and a birth rate of approximately 12. Argentina has 11 years of schooling and a birth rate of approximately 16 etc.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a subsidy given to farmers by the Nigerian government would reduce the deficit on the current account of its balance of payments.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: It should raise supply / increase output equivalent to lower cost lower price increasing international competitiveness; There may be more funds available to invest raise quality increase exports / reduce imports. Reasons why it might not: It may increase inefficiency as firms rely on the subsidy may not pass on the benefit of the subsidy to consumers; Nigeria may have a high inflation rate which may offset the cost advantage of the subsidy; There may an increase in incomes at home which may increase imports / divert exports to the home market; There may be a recession abroad / decrease in incomes of trading partners which reduces exports; Lower export prices will not raise export revenue if demand is inelastic; The exchange rate may rise increasing export prices / lowering import prices; Farmers may import e.g. more machinery; There may be adverse weather conditions / disease which reduces quality / quality may remain poor; There may be import restrictions / increase in import restrictions imposed by other countries.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the US government should impose a minimum price on chocolate.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should: It will raise price reduce demand / demand contracts / quantity demand falls / consumption falls reduce obesity; Social cost of eating chocolate may exceed social benefit due to external costs example e.g. cost on health service / may reduce need for government spending on healthcare overconsumption of chocolate move market closer to where social benefit equals social cost increase welfare; May raise health of the population / eating chocolate may be unhealthy raise labour productivity; There may be information failure some chocolate is a demerit good. Reasons why it should not: Poor may not be able to afford chocolate regressive measure; Not everyone overweight / some may consume chocolate in quantities that are not harmful may cause demand to switch to other high sugar products / discriminate against other high sugar products; It will encourage supply to extend a surplus may develop; There may be an illegal market in chocolate; There may be a cost to the government it may have to pay to monitor that the price is being charged lower tax revenue from chocolate may be an increase in unemployment; Demand may be inelastic resulting in a relatively small fall in demand / chocolate may be addictive; It depends on how high the minimum price is may have no effect if set below the market equilibrium.
Explanation: A minimum price diagram without explanation can get a maximum of 2 marks. These marks may be the equivalent of a combination of two of higher price, demand extending, supply extending or a surplus being created.

Stimulus / Scenario: Economic problem, opportunity cost, labour mobility; teachers moving internationally for jobs
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and opportunity cost
Define opportunity cost.
Markscheme: The (next) best alternative / choice forgone / sacrificed.

Question Text: Explain the economic problem and why it is always likely to exist.
Markscheme: The economic problem is finite / limited resources and infinite / unlimited wants / wants exceed resources scarcity. It is always likely to exist as wants grow faster than resources / wants are increasing there will never be enough resources to produce all the products people would like to have. People are living longer / population is increasing more resources are needed / some resources are being depleted.

Question Text: Analyse why the wages of all teachers may increase.
Markscheme: The wages of teachers may increase if demand for teachers rises derived this may be because there are more children to educate / rise in population size smaller class sizes / children staying in education longer teachers being more productive / skilful. A decrease in supply will increase wages this may be due to a rise in qualifications needed long period of training rise in wages in other occupations / need to compete with other occupations need to compete with wages paid to teachers in other countries / recruit from higher paying countries. Bargaining power of teachers may increase more teachers may belong to trade unions may be more prepared to take strike action wages may be raised to keep up with inflation / maintain real wages. Government policy may favour teachers e.g. there may be an increase in school leaving age / increase provision of education. Increase in minimum wage.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a teacher would benefit from specialisation.
Markscheme: Why it might: may be trained more quickly; become more skilled / productive; may be in higher demand; may be better paid; may increase chance of promotion. Why it might not: subject may become less popular; may become bored teaching the same subject; if lose job, may find it difficult to get another job; may spend a long time developing the specialisation; may not get the opportunity to move into another subject.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Russia unemployment, government spending cuts, proportional tax system
Question Text: Scenario about fiscal policy and labour markets
Define full employment.
Markscheme: The lowest unemployment possible (very low percentage) / job vacancies matching the number unemployed; Low unemployment; May still be some workers in between jobs / some frictional unemployment; Zero unemployment.
Explanation: One mark for idea of full employment of resources e.g. all workers in employment.

Question Text: Explain the difference between a proportional income tax system and a progressive income tax system.
Markscheme: Proportional income tax system takes the same percentage / proportion of income from all income groups. In a proportional income tax rate system, the tax rate does not change as income changes has no effect on the distribution of income. Progressive income tax system takes a larger percentage / smaller percentage of the income of the rich / the poor / places a larger burden on the rich helps redistribute income from the rich to the poor / reduces the gap between rich and poor / may be used to promote equity. In a progressive income tax rate system, the tax rate increases as income increases.
Explanation: Three marks maximum for proportional income tax system and three marks maximum for progressive income tax system.

Question Text: Analyse why a cut in government spending might increase poverty.
Markscheme: A cut in government spending might reduce total demand increasing unemployment reducing incomes. Government spending on subsidies to firms might be reduced which may raise prices. Government spending on education might be reduced this might cause a decrease in skills reducing wages may lead to vicious cycle of poverty. Government spending on healthcare might be reduced lowering productivity and wages. Government spending on state / unemployment benefits might be reduced reducing goods and services that can be bought. Government spending on state pensions might be reduced the old may not be able to buy basic necessities.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a fall in a country’s unemployment rate always benefits workers.
Markscheme: Why it might be: increase choice of jobs; increase wages; raise living standards; increase job security; competition for workers may raise working conditions; more may be spent on training. Why it might not be: may be result of workers leaving the labour force e.g. emigrating; may not benefit all workers as some may still be unemployed; may not be high quality jobs; may be low-paid jobs; may be temporary jobs; may cause inflation reducing real wages.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Technology changes in wood production and furniture manufacturing, inelastic supply, public goods and economic systems
Question Text: Scenario about production and economic systems
Define inelastic supply.
Markscheme: Quantity supplied / supply changes by a smaller percentage change than price; Supply changing by less than price / PES less than 1 / PES < 1 / supply unresponsive to change in price.
Explanation: Allow one mark for PES = 0.

Question Text: Explain why public goods would not be supplied in a market economic system.
Markscheme: Public goods are non-excludable it is not possible to stop people who are not prepared to pay for them, from consuming them free rider problem. Public goods are non-rival if supplied for one, supplied for everyone and so cannot charge additional consumers. Private sector firms are profit motivated they will not produce products if they cannot get revenue from them would make a loss.
Explanation: No marks for examples.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, the effect on the market for furniture of an increase in the price of wood.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New supply curve shifted to the left; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points E1 and E2. Written analysis: Would increase costs of production raise price / reduce quantity (traded).
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written analysis. Accept increase in price or fall in quantity on their own but not an increase in price of wood or fall in quantity of wood.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not advances in technology will benefit a country’s population.
Markscheme: Why it might: may reduce costs of production; may increase significance of technical economies of scale; may lower prices; may make the country's firms more internationally competitive; may promote globalisation; may encourage firms to expand and take on more workers; economic growth may increase; incomes may increase; may increase quality - less subject to human error; may create new products; may improve working conditions; may extend education through online learning. Why it might not: may increase unemployment; may make products more standardised / not produced to individual needs; may increase stress; may be over dependence on technology which can fail; may increase fraud; may cause negative externalities e.g. fracking.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: US–Mexico trade relationship, protectionism, exchange rate depreciation, central bank interest rate changes
Question Text: Scenario about trade and macroeconomic policy
Identify two methods of protection.
Markscheme: Tariffs; quotas; subsidies; embargo.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a government may want to reduce imports.
Markscheme: To improve the current account of the balance of payments reduce a current account deficit move from a deficit to a surplus / move to a balance / achieve balance of payments stability increase net exports; To increase GDP / economic growth switch demand from imports to domestically produced products / increase demand for domestically produced goods / protect domestic firms allow infant industries to grow; Prevent dumping the sale of goods from abroad at below cost / predatory prices; To increase employment / reduce unemployment to raise living standards; To reduce dependence on other countries / make the country more independent as risk of supplies being cut off / price being increased; To stop / reduce a fall in the exchange rate less of the currency will be sold to buy imports.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation. So, for improve balance of payments reason, there is a maximum of 2 marks.

Question Text: Analyse how a fall in a country’s foreign exchange rate could increase its inflation rate.
Markscheme: Raise import prices increase price of imported raw materials / capital goods increase costs of production if demand is inelastic cause cost-push inflation / imported inflation. Reduce export prices increase demand for exports increase export revenue if demand is elastic switch demand for imports to domestic products increase total demand cause demand-pull inflation.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a decrease in the rate of interest will increase a country’s GDP.
Markscheme: Why it might: reduce saving; increase borrowing; increase consumer spending; raise total demand; encourage firms to produce more; increase investment; raise ability of firms to produce more. Why it might not: households and firms may be worried about the future and so may not spend more; there may be a culture of saving; households and firms may think the cut is only temporary; the rate may initially have been low; may spend more on imports, increasing other countries' GDP; an economy may initially have been at full employment.
