Explanation: For an answer which shows awareness of how a change in the rate of interest may affect GDP but writes about an increase in the rate of interest = maximum of 2 marks. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Ecuador economy changes: declining oil exports, diversification into tourism and textiles, recession due to falling demand and government spending cuts, road infrastructure investment, emigration and remittances
Question Text: Source material about Ecuador’s economy
Calculate the price elasticity of supply of Ecuador’s oil.
Explanation: Do not accept 16%

Question Text: Identify two key resource allocation questions.
Markscheme: What to produce / produce less of one product and more of another product / less oil and more textiles; How to produce it / how products are made / using fewer capital goods
Explanation: Do not accept: How much to produce or for whom to produce

Question Text: State why sunshine has no opportunity cost.
Markscheme: It is a free good; In unlimited supply / not scarce / a renewable source of energy; Does not take resources to produce it / nothing needs to be sacrificed to use it / no alternative / substitute so no opportunity cost; It is there naturally.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why Ecuador experienced a recession between 2014 and 2016.
Markscheme: Fall in demand for exports [of oil] lower export revenue / increase current account deficit / lower output of oil / lower total demand / lower GDP; Decrease in government spending lower employment / lower output / lower income from benefits / lower total demand / lower GDP.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse how building more roads can increase a country’s economic growth rate.
Markscheme: Building more roads would increase employment lower unemployment increase demand for road building materials increase in government spending on roads increase incomes leading to higher demand / expenditure on goods and services e.g. education / cars. Less congestion easier to transport goods / better infrastructure quicker to work / increases labour mobility / longer working hours / more productive / efficient workforce output rises. Lower transport costs encourage firms to increase output / attract MNCs / foreign investment. Lower costs may increase international competitiveness increase demand for country's exports.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP per head and car ownership.
Markscheme: Overview: Generally, a direct relationship / the higher the income, the higher the car ownership / the lower the income, the lower the car ownership. Supporting evidence – two comparison examples: Senegal has the lowest GDP per head and the lowest car ownership; Ecuador has the second lowest GDP per head and the second lowest car ownership / Spain has higher GDP per head and car ownership than Uruguay. Exception: New Zealand / Switzerland Switzerland has a higher GDP per head but lower car ownership than New Zealand. OR Switzerland has highest GDP per head but only second highest car ownership OR New Zealand has second highest GDP per head but highest car ownership. Comments: Higher income would increase ability to purchase cars / high income countries often have more than one car per household. For exception other influences on demand e.g. cost of public transport / road space / congestion.
Explanation: There must be a comparison between countries and/or GDP per head and car ownership. Merely describing the data gets no marks as it is not analysis. A direct comparison between two countries e.g. GDP per head is higher in Switzerland than Senegal and car ownership is higher in Switzerland than Senegal is worth the 2 marks.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the profits of Ecuador’s textile firms are likely to have increased between 2016 and 2019.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: Incomes / household spending increased in Ecuador increasing demand for textiles revenue increases; Costs of production may have fallen due to lower transport costs; Increase in scale of production increased ability to take advantage of economies of scale average cost falls profits may rise if prices unchanged but costs fall / profit is revenue – costs; Low increase in wage costs revenue may have increased by more than costs; Incomes abroad may have increased increasing demand for Ecuadorean textiles especially of luxury textiles. Reasons why they might not: The firms may have lost skilled workers abroad which could have reduced productivity increased costs / production falls; Strong competition from abroad they may have lost sales to competitors reducing revenue and or prices; Low wage increase workers may leave industry / lower productivity trade unions / workers may take action may strike / disrupting the firms' supply / lead to higher costs.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not Ecuador benefits from the emigration of some of its workers.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Some may be unskilled, may have been unemployed others leaving jobs create vacancies to be filled by unemployed and so the government would spend less on unemployment benefit / more for other public spending less poverty as a result; Some may be trained abroad and may bring back skills and ideas into the country increasing output; Some may send money home improve the current account balance will increase spending / total demand leading to economic growth; Net emigration results in lower population / reduces overpopulation less depletion of resources / pollution / shortage of resources e.g. housing. Reasons why it might not: May lose skilled workers lower productivity low quality products / reduce output / economic growth a relatively high proportion of people lost makes it more difficult to attract MNCs; If some will not support families at home dependency ratio will rise place more burden on the government; Large proportion / 3 million already working abroad shortage of workers / large reduction in size of labour force / increase (unit) wage costs less income less government tax revenue less government spending e.g. education, health; Younger workers emigrating leads to an ageing population.

Stimulus / Scenario: Apple production in Washington State, labour-intensive farming, market disequilibrium and competitive markets
Question Text: Scenario about agricultural markets
Define market disequilibrium.
Markscheme: A market where demand and supply are not equal / balanced / matched; A market where there is a surplus / excess supply or a shortage / excess demand.

Question Text: Explain opportunity cost and how it can influence a farmer’s decision to grow apples.
Markscheme: Opportunity cost is the (next) best alternative / choice / option sacrificed / forgone / given up. A farmer could grow another crop size of opportunity cost / choice made is influenced by revenue / cost / profit / resources available / weather.

Question Text: Analyse how labour-intensive production can benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Labour-intensive production uses a high proportion of labour it may increase employment lower unemployment raise incomes raise standard of living / reduce poverty. The country may have a large supply of labour the labour force may be relatively unskilled which is cheap to employ / cheaper than using capital saving money for firms more flexible than capital in adjusting supply less pollution / more environmentally friendly. Labour-intensive production may provide individually made products / handcrafted / higher quality raise skills level of workforce may be in high demand internationally increase exports / reduce deficit on current account balance.
Explanation: This is a static analysis so do not reward answers that write about higher government revenue and expenditure and rise in total demand and economic growth.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not consumers benefit from a competitive market.
Markscheme: Why they might: give consumers some power; may lower prices; may raise quality; increase choice; goods and service may be readily available. Why they might not: firms may be too small to take advantage of economies of scale; firms may not have much profit to invest in research and development; there may be wasteful duplication; there may be wasteful expenditure on advertising; there may a reduction in quality because of cost-cutting; there may be too much choice, taking time to make decisions; consumers may benefit more from monopoly where economies of scale result in lower prices than in a competitive market.
Explanation: This is a discussion of a market not of an economic system so public goods and merit goods are not relevant. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Romania indirect taxes, presence of multinational companies, productivity improvements and unemployment changes
Question Text: Scenario about MNCs and labour markets
Define, with an example, an indirect tax.
Markscheme: A tax on goods and services / tax on sales / purchases / consumer spending; VAT / GST / import tariff.
Explanation: Allow an example of a product that is taxed for the example mark e.g. motor cars. Allow an answer that explains that the tax is levied on a producer but passed onto consumers to pay. Do not reward answers that refer to a tax levied on a third party.

Question Text: Explain two benefits of MNCs to their home countries.
Markscheme: Increasing income / improving the current account balance / raise living standards / higher tax revenue if profits returned to home county; Reduces prices in the home country if goods now produced at lower costs in host countries; Reduce unemployment employment of workers from the home countries in host countries / may employ more in head office; Higher revenue for firms which may increase their profits can gain subsidies/grants from host countries governments; May e.g. be able to dump waste materials / reduce pollution host countries may have fewer rules and regulation.
Explanation: One mark for each of two benefits identified and one mark for each explanation. Note: common error is to confuse host and home country so before awarding marks be clear that the candidate is not writing about the host country.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in productivity can affect unemployment.
Markscheme: An increase in productivity will lower average costs of production may increase output / revenue raise firms' profits may encourage firms to expand / attract MNCs employ more workers reducing unemployment. An increase in productivity will make firms more competitive internationally leading to more exports and higher demand for labour further reducing unemployment. May enable the same or more output to be produced with fewer workers saving money / increasing profits may increase unemployment. Higher productivity may mean higher wages raising consumption / demand for goods and services more incentive to seek employment / leading to more employment. Increase productivity may be due to capital investment / better equipment /technology could reduce demand for labour.
Explanation: Candidates need to be clear about distinction between productivity and production.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a decrease in a country’s unemployment rate will reduce poverty.
Markscheme: Why it might: MNCs tend to export a relatively high proportion of their output; may produce at a low cost of production/be efficient; may be internationally competitive; may produce products that were previously imported; may bring in new technology. Why it might not: may buy raw materials and capital goods from their home countries; MNC may send profits back to home country; workers/managers may send remittances back to home country; may eliminate firms in the host country that had a good export record; may deplete non-renewable resources reducing the ability to export in the long run.
Explanation: For level 3, an answer must look at both sides of whether a deficit on the current account would be reduced. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Poland toothpaste industry, inelastic demand, horizontal mergers, rising wages for dentists
Question Text: Scenario about firms and labour markets
Identify two determinants of price elasticity of demand.
Markscheme: Availability of substitutes; Availability of complements; Proportion of income; Whether the product is a luxury or a necessity / essential; Whether it is addictive; Whether the purchase can be postponed; Impact of advertising / brand loyalty.

Question Text: Explain two advantages to a firm of a horizontal merger.
Markscheme: To gain market power or share / monopoly power / become larger by eliminating a competitor / controlling more of the market can increase prices as fewer competitors; To reduce average costs of production by being able to take greater advantage of economies of scale example of economy of scale more competitive internationally may decrease prices / profits; To keep up with competitors to avoid being driven out of the market.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how the market for toothpaste would be affected by an increase in population.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the right; Equilibrium – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points E1 and E2. Written analysis: An increase in population will mean there are more people to buy / demand toothpaste; Price will rise / quantity traded will increase.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for written analysis. Diagram does not need to refer to toothpaste but if not; the analysis must refer to increase in demand toothpaste.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in the wages of dentists will increase the number of dentists employed.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase in demand may cause supply to extend; may encourage more people to train to be dentists; higher pay may increase the standard of living of dentists; may persuade current dentists not to leave the occupation / delay retirement; may cause increased inward migration of qualified dentists. Why it might not: decrease in supply may cause demand to contract; long period of training may discourage people; people may lack qualifications / skills; people may not find the job enjoyable; people may think the job will be too physically demanding e.g. backache; wages may rise more in other occupations; may increase costs for dental firms employing dentists / consumers reducing demand for dentists.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Palau economy, tariffs, current account balance, bank lending and economic growth
Question Text: Scenario about trade and banking
Identify two components of the current account of the balance of payments.
Markscheme: Trade in goods; Trade in services; Primary income; Secondary income.
Explanation: Also accept visible balance, invisible balance, income and current transfers. In addition, accept exports and imports.

Question Text: Explain two reasons for differences in living standards between countries.
Markscheme: Rate of employment /level of income (GDP per head) will influence the ability to buy goods and services; Productivity resulting in differences in ability to enjoy a different quantity of goods and services; Availability of resources / factors of production resulting in differences in productive capacity; Level of technology resulting higher quality of goods and services / better sanitation / clean water; Healthcare resulting in differences in life expectancy; Education causing differences in skills / job outcomes; International trade resulting in differences in GDP per head; Cost of living affecting ability to buy goods and services; Environment level of pollution / density of housing; War/conflict causing reduced welfare / opportunity / diverted resources.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each explanation.

Question Text: Analyse the reasons why a country may impose tariffs on imports.
Markscheme: To increase the price of imports may reduce demand for imports reduce import expenditure improve the current account position / balance of payments increase demand for domestic firms reduce unemployment increase economic growth. To protect infant industries enabling them to take advantage of economies of scale / stop them from being driven out by foreign firms with much lower costs due to size. To protect declining industries prevent unemployment rising. To protect strategic industries ensure self-sufficiency. To discourage demerit goods to improve health / welfare of consumers. To stop dumping of overseas goods where goods sold at below cost price / aimed at driving out domestic firms. As a source of government revenue e.g. to use to subsidise domestic producers. As a countermeasure / retaliation in response to unilateral imposition of tariffs by another country.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in commercial bank lending will increase economic growth.
Markscheme: Why it might: households may spend more on goods and services; higher total demand may increase output; firms may invest more leading to higher employment; higher investment will increase total demand and productive capacity. Why it might not: economy may already be at full employment; higher total demand may increase prices / rate of inflation rather than output; households and firms may spend on imports; households and firms may borrow to repay past debts; variable interest rate increase could then affect level of future consumption and investment.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: US macroeconomic performance and policy: GDP growth, trade with China, tariffs, inflation changes, unemployment rise in 2020, government spending increases and central bank policy
Question Text: Source material about US macroeconomic performance
Calculate the value of US imports from China in 2020.
Explanation: Accept the correct figure without the $ sign. 560 000 000 000. 5.6 x 10^11

Question Text: Identify two causes of the increase in the quantity of US factors of production.
Markscheme: Land reclamation; increase in the labour force
Explanation: If more than two causes given, consider the first three. Accept increase in labour for increase in the labour force.

Question Text: Explain one way that import tariffs could improve the US economy.
Markscheme: Could improve the current account / trade in goods / trade in goods and services / balance of payments by reducing imports / making domestic goods more competitive with imports / raising the price of imports; Could increase output / cause economic growth by increasing demand for US goods / raise employment; Could raise tax revenue allowing the government to spend more on (e.g. education) / improve the (government budget position).
Explanation: One mark for a way identified and one mark for an explanation. If more than one way identified, consider the first two. To gain two marks, one mark must come from improving the current account, increase output or raise tax revenue. Could raise more revenue is not sufficient – need more tax revenue or more government revenue / income.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why the US inflation rate fell in 2020.
Markscheme: Fall in consumer expenditure lower total demand / reduce demand-pull inflation; Reduction in workers' bargaining power fall in wage rises / wages / fall in rise in costs / fall in costs / reduce cost-push inflation; Higher unemployment reduces confidence / lower total demand / fall in consumer spending; Fall in (real) GDP lower incomes / purchasing power / lower total demand; Current account deficit lower total demand / reduce demand-pull inflation.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three. Reduction in bargaining power may also be linked to lower total demand.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of an increase in the price of a complement on the market for ice cream.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Demand curve shifted to the left; Equilibrium – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between government spending and unemployment.
Markscheme: Overview: Inverse relationship / negative relationship; generally, the higher government spending, the lower the unemployment rate. Supporting evidence: E.g. 2015 to 2019, government spending increased and unemployment fell / 2015 to 2017 government spending increased and unemployment fell; processing / interpreting of data e.g. between 2015 to 2019, government spending rose by $700bn and unemployment fell by 1.6% (points). Analysis of the expected relationship: higher government spending will increase total demand (encouraging firms to expand and employ more workers); higher government spending may be on e.g. education, training, infrastructure, subsidies which could increase workers' chances of gaining jobs; higher employment will provide more tax revenue for the government to spend / lower employment will reduce tax revenue for the government to spend. Exception: 2020 both government spending and unemployment rose / government spending and unemployment both at their highest. Analysis of the exception: government spending on unemployment benefits may have increased / rise in government spending not enough to stop GDP / consumer expenditure falling / GDP fell / there may be a time delay before higher government spending reduces unemployment.
Explanation: Alternatively, some candidates may argue that changes in unemployment, could lead to changes in government spending. Credit both responses.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a central bank should aim for a low inflation rate.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should: can increase international price competitiveness improve the current account balance / increase exports increase employment / reduce unemployment; can create certainty which may increase confidence which may encourage investment / attract MNCs which may increase output / result in economic growth; can prevent a random redistribution of income e.g. protect savers; may protect purchasing power by promoting price stability; maintain / increase living standards if wages rise by more than the price level; can avoid fiscal drag prevent people being put in higher tax brackets; can reduce menu / shoe leather costs reduce firms' costs of production. Reasons why it should not: increases in the rate of interest contractionary monetary policy can reduce consumer expenditure / can reduce investment can reduce total demand; lower total demand can increase unemployment cyclical unemployment reduce economic growth; low inflation can turn into deflation leading to a recession; makes it harder to pay off debt increasing the risk of firms going out of business / households getting into difficulties; central bank may aim for economic growth / low unemployment higher demand-pull inflation may provide more encouragement to firms to expand.
Explanation: May approach the answer from the point of view of the disadvantages of high inflation. 2nd side should examine either the possible negative effects of what the central bank may do to reduce the inflation rate or the possible loss of any beneficial effects of high inflation. No marks for possible effects on the exchange rate.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not economic growth benefits everyone in the US.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: economic growth increases output / GDP increases income per head / higher incomes / higher wages enables more purchasing power / people to enjoy more goods and services; economic growth can increase employment raise living standards may reduce poverty; economic growth can increase tax revenue enabling the government to spend more on e.g. education and healthcare; economic growth may introduce better working conditions. Reasons why it might not: there may be an increase in unemployment due to lack of skills / some industries declining / greater use of capital / capital-intensive production structural unemployment may occur; income is unevenly distributed those on low incomes e.g. the sick may not benefit; higher output may result in external costs e.g. pollution people living near factories may suffer; higher output may be the result of more resources being devoted to capital goods may take time for consumers to benefit from more consumer goods; higher output may reduce natural resources reducing future generations' ability to benefit from them / reduce sustainability; higher output may be the result of workers working longer hours in poor working conditions; may cause inflation adversely affecting the poor / savers.
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: Vietnam entrepreneurs, competition with MNCs and public sector firms, government intervention to encourage merit goods
Question Text: Scenario about entrepreneurship and market intervention
Identify two reasons why people become entrepreneurs.
Markscheme: To make money / earn a profit; to be independent / not having to be told what to do; to follow an interest / be innovative / to run / own business; willingness to take risks; possess good leadership skills; easier to set up a firm / lower barriers to entry; for self-esteem / status / become well-known; flexible working hours; unable to find a job.
Explanation: If more than two reasons given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two benefits that an MNC can bring to its host country.
Markscheme: Increase employment raise living standards / increase income / higher wages / reduce poverty; Increase skills provide training to workers; Increase exports / reduce imports improve the current account of the balance of payments; Increase GDP / output raise economic growth; Increase competition raise efficiency / reduce prices; Bring in new technology raise quality / raise productive capacity / productivity / reduce costs of production; Increase the range of goods and services available in the country raise living standards; Increase tax revenue enable the government to spend more; Increase infrastructure an MNC may build e.g. roads / reduce e.g. transport costs.
Explanation: One mark for each of two benefits identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two benefits given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how a government could encourage the consumption of merit goods.
Markscheme: Provide a subsidy reduce any indirect tax lower price make the goods more affordable raise quality; Provide information about the benefits of consuming the goods e.g. health campaigns / advertisements overcome information failure; Use regulation make consumption compulsory / impose fines for non-consumption e.g. school attendance; Provide services such as education / healthcare free to consumers; Set maximum price to make the goods more affordable but may create a shortage; Measures to reduce demerit goods clearly linked to idea of encouraging substitution of merit goods relevant example.
Explanation: Note: reference to measures to reduce consumption of demerit goods can only be rewarded if clearly linked to switching to alternative merit goods.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not private sector firms are likely to charge lower prices than public sector firms.
Markscheme: Why they might: competition may encourage private sector firms to charge low prices; private sector firms may seek to reduce waste and costs to avoid going out of business; private sector firms may have more funds to invest; higher investment may reduce costs of production; may be an MNC with significant economies of scale. Why they might not: profit motive may encourage private sector firms to charge high prices; private sector firms may be monopolies; public sector firms may be subsidised; public sector firms may charge low prices to make the products affordable; public sector firms may produce on a large scale.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: India sugar production, low-income farmers, capital-intensive processing, minimum price and export subsidy policies
Question Text: Scenario about agriculture and government intervention
Define a minimum price.
Markscheme: A price set by e.g. government above the equilibrium price; A price floor to encourage higher supply will result in a surplus.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two advantages of capital-intensive production.
Markscheme: Lower costs of production can produce on a large scale / may result in lower prices / higher profits; Raise productivity / efficiency higher output / can produce faster; Quality may increase / consistency of quality may be greater may increase demand; Absence of human error less wastage; Capital goods can work long hours need few breaks / no need for holidays; Capital goods will not take industrial action will not disrupt production; Can eliminate boring / repetitive / physically demanding tasks increase workers' job satisfaction.
Explanation: One mark for each of two advantages identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two advantages are given, consider the first three. Accepting lower costs of production as overall lower costs. Nothing for lower labour costs.

Question Text: Analyse why low-income farmers are likely to have low living standards.
Markscheme: May be unable to afford basic necessities / may spend a high proportion of income on basic necessities / low purchasing power / low ability to consume goods and services; Unable to save much banks reluctant to lend to them examples of items they may need to borrow to buy which could improve their quality of life; Unable to afford good quality housing which can result in poor health / overcrowding; Unable to afford good health care good nutrition / food more likely to become ill lower life expectancy; Unable to afford good education reduce range of interests; Unable to buy good capital equipment for farms making work hard; May have to reinvest into their farms giving them less to spend; May experience poor working conditions / physically demanding work long hours reducing health; Live in rural areas which may lack infrastructure.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government subsidy on the export of sugar will help it achieve its macroeconomic aims.
Markscheme: Why it might: lower price of exports and increase their supply; increase export revenue and improve the current account balance; higher output of sugar can contribute to economic growth; more people may be employed in growing sugar and processing sugar cane. Why it might not: other countries' sugar may still be cheaper; may make farmers and mill owners lazy; sugar sellers may not lower their prices; demand for sugar may be price-inelastic; introduction of capital equipment may lower employment; other countries may retaliate against the subsidy and so current account position may not be improved; higher exports may increase total demand resulting in inflation; opportunity cost – e.g. spending on education which may be more effective.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Growth of female doctors in Turkey, rising productivity, technological change, population growth and balance of payments changes
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and development
Identify two causes of an increase in labour productivity.
Markscheme: improved education; improved training / higher skills; improved healthcare; higher pay / bonuses / lower income tax; better quality capital equipment / advances in technology; shorter working hours / longer holidays; better working conditions / better (fringe) benefits; increased experience; higher unemployment; specialisation
Explanation: Nothing for more motivated – TV.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why someone may choose to become a doctor.
Markscheme: High pay resulting in high living standards / caused by low supply / high demand / high number of job vacancies / strong bargaining power / high qualifications / high skills; Vocation wanting to help people; Interest / job satisfaction enjoying the challenges involved; Status doctors are highly regarded; Family tradition parents may have been doctors; High pensions enabling high living standards during retirement; May be good working conditions working inside; Job security difficult to replace / essential service; (Fringe / non-wage) benefits e.g. pension schemes.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how advances in technology may improve the current account balance on a country’s balance of payments.
Markscheme: Raise productivity / increase efficiency / increase speed of production / increase output reduce costs of production lower prices raise quality create new products increase international competitiveness increase exports reduce imports increase export revenue reduce import expenditure improve trade in goods (and services) balance / reduce a current account deficit / create a surplus / increase a surplus; Increase access to information about goods and services promote sales; Increase foreign tourism easier to book foreign holidays increase speed of transport; Make it easier to keep in contact with branches abroad encourage setting up branches in other countries receive profits from other countries increase primary income.
Explanation: Reward but do not expect reference to gaining a comparative advantage.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a country with a high population growth rate is likely to experience a high economic growth rate.
Markscheme: Why it might: may have an increasing labour force, capable of producing a higher output; likely to have higher demand, encouraging higher output; larger markets can enable greater advantage to be taken of economies of scale; may have higher tax revenue, enabling the government to spend more on e.g. education which can promote economic growth. Why it might not: may have an increase in the number of dependents, reducing resources which can be devoted to increasing productive capacity; rise in birth rate may reduce the size of the labour force as people leave the labour force to bring up children; resources may be depleted, reducing productive capacity; external costs e.g. congestion and pollution may increase which may reduce productivity.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Serbia raspberry industry, elastic demand, trade union membership decline, pensioners exceeding workers
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and government spending
Identify two determinants of price elasticity of demand.
Markscheme: substitutes; whether a luxury or necessity; proportion of income taken; whether habit forming / addictive; whether the purchase can be postponed; time period; breadth of definition; brand loyalty; number of uses; complements
Explanation: If more than two determinants given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain how the economic problem results in consumers having to make choices.
Markscheme: The economic problem is unlimited / infinite wants exceeding limited / finite / scare resources resulting in scarcity; Consumers have limited incomes and so cannot buy everything they want; Buying one product involves an opportunity cost in the form of another product sacrificed / example.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse why some workers decide not to join a trade union.
Markscheme: Trade unions charge a subscription fee workers cannot afford to join may be unemployed / have low income; Government legislation may prevent trade union membership / government may discourage membership may reduce the power of trade unions less benefit from joining; Employers may discourage membership may think it will reduce chance of employment / increase chance of unemployment chance of promotion; May be satisfied with wages working conditions may have job security no need for collective bargaining; May be employed in industries that do not have a history of trade union membership; May be reluctant to take industrial action may lose pay during a strike; May not agree with the objectives of the trade union; May follow example of fellow workers.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should reduce the amount of money it gives to each state pensioner.
Markscheme: Why it should: ageing population can mean the amount of government spending on pensions rises significantly; a reduction in the state pension could reduce a budget deficit; may persuade people to work longer, raising output and tax revenue; paying pensions involves an opportunity cost, could spend more on e.g. education. Why it should not: pensioners may be unable to find work; may be unable to work due to ill health; could increase poverty among the elderly; could increase inequality; pensioners may have saved less if they were expecting a higher pension; could result in a fall in total demand; could reduce economic growth.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Australian coal industry: declining global demand for coal due to environmental concerns, substitution with renewable energy, large coal firms, storage costs, international production and consumption, and government budget effects
Question Text: Source material about Australia’s coal industry
Calculate Australia’s unemployment rate in 2021.
Explanation: 0.56 / 14 × 100. Allow 4 without percentage

Question Text: Identify two substitutes for coal.
Markscheme: gas; offshore wind / wind power; solar power / panels
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain one economy of scale that could benefit a coal-mining firm.
Markscheme: Managerial / labour economy employing specialist workers e.g. mining engineers; Technical economy employing efficient / technologically advanced equipment e.g. drones; External economy of scale example e.g. skilled labour force.
Explanation: One mark for the economy of scale identified and one mark for an explanation.

Question Text: Explain two influences on the price elasticity of supply of coal.
Markscheme: Storage time expensive to store so inelastic supply; Construction of new mine / production time takes a long time, so inelastic supply; Stocks may make supply elastic.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two influences identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show how setting a maximum price below the equilibrium price will affect a market.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Price line set below the equilibrium; Demand shown as greater than supply e.g. has the two dotted lines downwards (it does not have to be labelled Qs and Qd).
Explanation: Note: Accept unlabelled price line / line drawn only to demand curve. Accept shortage if correctly identified on the diagram. Do not accept demand greater than supply if demand and supply curves are incorrectly drawn

Question Text: Analyse whether large coal-producing countries are likely to be net exporters of coal.
Markscheme: Expected outcome: Large coal producing countries may have a surplus to sell / may have grown their production in order to sell abroad most of the evidence supports this relationship. Supporting evidence: Five of the seven countries consume less coal than they produce calculation e.g. US could have exported 11 million tonnes / Australia could have exported 394 million tonnes or simply the difference. Analysis: Countries that produce more coal than they consume may benefit from economies of scale / can export because their prices are low. Exceptions: China or India are an exception as they consumed more coal than they produced. Analysis: they may have been net importers since had used up stocks of coal / need more energy as a developing country may not be able to export coal if world demand for coal is low / countries may both import and export coal.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected / normal outcome, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in the size of its coal industry will benefit the Australian economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: may increase GDP / economic growth increasing living standards / raise incomes / reduce poverty; may reduce unemployment structural unemployment; may improve current account position / have a surplus as Australia seems to be a net exporter of coal; may increase tax revenue increasing government's ability to spend; economies of scale result in lower prices for households and industries. Reasons why it might not: may increase pollution lowering living standards / cause health issues reduce tourism / negative impact on current account; other forms of power may not be developed example; some workers may leave other jobs these jobs may have better working conditions; coal mining is a relatively dangerous industry so life expectancy may be reduced / creates chronic illnesses / workers unable to work; Global demand for coal may fall impact upon Australia e.g. unsold stock; May rapidly deplete stocks leading to lower exports and less employment.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not Australia is likely to have a budget deficit in 2026.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: unemployment is forecast to increase so there may be more government spending on unemployment benefits less income tax revenue there may be more government spending on retraining unemployed workers some of the rise in population may result in more government spending e.g. on pensions / healthcare / education budget deficit in previous years would mean interest would have to be paid on the debt that has built up. Reasons why it might not: population and the labour force are forecast to increase so there may be more taxpayers / fewer claiming benefits tax revenue may rise due to higher profits / higher tax rates GDP is forecast to increase more people in work which would be likely to increase direct and indirect tax revenue state support for industries may fall may have been high in 2021 due to Covid-19.
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: Living standards and poverty in South Africa, rising household income and tax revenue, but persistent inequality and unemployment benefits policy
Question Text: Scenario about poverty and government policy
Identify two functions of money.
Markscheme: Medium of exchange; Unit of account / measure of value; Store of value; Standard of deferred payments
Explanation: Note: a description of the function, without the term, gets a mark e.g. money can be used to buy and sell products, may be used to trade. Nothing for characteristics of money. If more than two functions are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain how improved education may affect the demand for cigarettes and for fresh fruit.
Markscheme: It may reduce demand for cigarettes people may be better informed about harmful effects / cigarettes are a demerit good; It may increase demand for fresh fruit people may be better informed about beneficial effects / fresh fruit is a merit good / may gain better paid jobs and so able to afford fresh food.
Explanation: Only accept demand for cigarettes may increase if linked to higher income (resulting from improved education). One mark for each of two reasons identified and one for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the causes of an increase in a government’s tax revenue.
Markscheme: Increase in employment increase in income; More convenient easier to pay and collect the tax higher income tax revenue; Increase in spending higher indirect tax revenue; Increase in profits higher corporation tax; Increase in population more people to pay taxes; Change in tax rates higher tax rates can increase revenue without a change in income / spending / profits lower tax rates may reduce tax avoidance / evasion increase incentives; Greater efficiency in tax collection reduction in corruption / simpler forms; Wider tax base more products / more people taxed; Increase in imports higher revenue from tariffs.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in unemployment benefit payments would reduce poverty.
Markscheme: Why it might: raise incomes of those on low incomes; may enable them to buy more basic necessities; may reduce absolute poverty; may increase total demand; higher total demand may reduce unemployment; Also enable them to seek education / become more employable. Why it might not: may be a disincentive to find employment; people may become dependent on benefits; will not help all those on low incomes e.g. the retired and the sick; may not reduce relative poverty; some of the unemployed may not claim benefits; opportunity cost – government could spend on e.g. education which might be more effective; Size of unemployment benefit may be small and may be less than inflation.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Germany labour force growth, productivity increases, low inflation and current account surplus
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and macroeconomic performance
Identify two reasons why death rates may vary between countries.
Markscheme: Two from differences in: income / standard of living; healthcare; education; nutrition; lifestyles / suicide rates; average age; spread of Covid / infectious diseases; war / conflict; natural disasters; air pollution / water pollution; conditions of work; level of crime.
Explanation: Do not accept differences in population size as these affect number rather than rate. If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two causes of an increase in the size of a country’s labour force.
Markscheme: Change in the birth rate rise will mean more people of working age in the long run / fall will enable more parents to be in the labour force; Immigration many immigrants are of working age; Rise in retirement age people will work for longer; Fall in school leaving age people will be in education for a shorter period; Fall in the death rate / rise in life expectancy fewer people dying before reaching retirement age; Changes in social culture allowing more women to work; Increase in size of population of working age reason; Rise in wage levels attracts people to re-enter the labour market.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two causes identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in labour productivity in a country can increase a surplus on the current account of its balance of payments.
Markscheme: Output per worker (hour) increases may reduce average cost of production lower prices may increase the quality of exports. These changes may increase international competitiveness of exports / make exports cheaper demand for exports may increase export revenue may rise. Higher relative prices of imports lower relative quality of imports these changes may decrease international competitiveness of imports demand for imports may decrease import expenditure may fall. Output increases allowing more exports and less imports.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss why some countries may experience lower inflation in the future and some may not.
Markscheme: Why some might: advances in technology may reduce costs of production; increases in education and healthcare could raise labour productivity; globalisation may increase international competition; trade union power may fall, lowering wage increases; successful government policy measures e.g. reducing demand. Why some might not: consumers may become optimistic and spend more; governments may increase their spending; the rate of interest may fall; total demand may increase; raw materials may run out; rising cost of energy and rising food prices.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Filipino migrant workers, government tax revenue increases, spending on life expectancy and unemployment policies, effects of exchange rate changes
Question Text: Scenario about labour migration and macroeconomic policy
Identify two influences on which country a person decides to work in.
Markscheme: wages / GDP per head; working conditions; job opportunities / job security; qualifications required; family ties; language spoken; immigration restrictions; personal safety/ freedom from persecution; living standards / cost of living; better welfare / healthcare / education provision
Explanation: If more than two influences are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain, with examples, the difference between a merit good and a public good.
Markscheme: A merit good e.g. education / fruit is beneficial / is under-consumed / people unaware of benefits / so underproduced without government intervention / government encourage consumption. A public good e.g. defence / streetlighting / police / sea defences / roads would not be produced by the private sector / needs to be financed by the government/ non-rival/ non-excludable.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two differences identified and one mark each for each of two examples

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in government spending could increase life expectancy.
Markscheme: Healthcare could raise quality / accessibility of healthcare; Education may result in people leading healthier lives more exercise better nutrition; Benefits may increase access to basic necessities / reduce poverty; Green energy may reduce pollution; Infrastructure could reduce traffic accidents reduce traffic congestion reduce stress; Police reduces deaths through crime; Increased government spending (in total) may increase employment / raise income / raise standards of living.
Explanation: MAX of three marks for just identifying areas of government spending. MAX of three marks for analysis of any single area of spending. One item analysed well MAX 4 marks.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a rise in a country’s foreign exchange rate would benefit its economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: reduce price of imports this could lower inflation as raw material costs may fall and more competitive pressure for firms to keep price rises low; enables a country to buy more imports for any given quantity of exports; could reduce a current account surplus which may enable more products to be consumed. Why it might not: increase the price of exports this could reduce exports and export revenue; economic growth could fall; unemployment could rise; could increase a current account deficit.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Greece household spending, PPC utilisation, unemployment and mergers during economic downturn
Question Text: Scenario about PPC, unemployment and firm behaviour
Identify two reasons why rich households spend more than the average household.
Markscheme: higher incomes; find it easier to borrow; more confidence; more savings / more wealth; high cost of luxury goods and services; maintaining / showing status; rich households may choose to have large families
Explanation: If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain the significance of a production point inside a PPC and a production point on its PPC.
Markscheme: A point inside a PPC means output is lower than potentially achievable there are unemployed resources / there is inefficient use of resources. A point on a PPC means output is at its maximum all resources are employed / full employment / there is efficient use of resources. A production point shows a combination of goods produced example e.g. X capital goods and Y consumer goods.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two significances identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse why someone who has been unemployed for more than a year may not get another job.
Markscheme: May lose skills qualifications may become out of date; may experience ill-health lack of recent job experience high cost of retraining may become less attractive to employers; Skills may be outdated industry in decline will need retraining; Economy could be in recession with high number of people unemployed and few vacancies; May lose confidence / become discouraged / depressed put less effort into finding another job; Unemployed workers may be occupationally immobile geographically immobile; Unemployment/welfare benefits may be high may be above low wages / maybe sufficient to live on; May face discrimination on grounds of e.g. age; May retire and leave the labour force.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should encourage firms to merge.
Markscheme: Why it should: may result in economies of scale; lower average costs of production; lower prices for consumers; raise quality of products; increase international competitiveness and improve current account; improved performance may increase profits and corporation tax. Why it should not: may result in diseconomies of scale; may increase monopoly power / reduce competition; raise prices; may reduce innovation; rationalisation may result in some workers losing their jobs; should be left to market forces to decide.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Guyana economy and oil discovery: rapid economic growth, increased oil production by a multinational company, government plans for education, infrastructure and internet access, and possible population changes due to migration
Question Text: Source material about Guyana’s economy
Calculate the number of people who were living in poverty in Guyana in 2020.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Identify two causes of the decrease in the output of sugar in 2020.
Markscheme: Bad weather; falling (global) demand.
Explanation: If more than two causes given, consider the first three. If more than two ways given, consider the first three. Not accepting GDP per head for GDP.

Question Text: Explain how economic growth is measured.
Markscheme: Real GDP / national / country's output / income / expenditure.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two reasons why labour mobility may increase in Guyana in the future.
Markscheme: Increased government spending on education / improved education improved skills / productivity / occupational mobility; Improved / cheaper rail travel / increased spending on infrastructure / improved infrastructure easier to work further away / increased ability to work in other areas / increased geographical mobility; Increased government spending on the internet people learn new skills / gain information about job opportunities / increase occupational mobility; May be net immigration migrants may be skilled / may be more willing to move to different areas of the country.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of a decrease in the price of rail transport on the market for car transport.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Demand curve shifted to the left; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP per head and internet access.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Direct / positive the higher the GDP per head, the higher the internet access. Supporting evidence: Up to two marks. E.g. Iceland with the highest GDP per head had the highest % of the population with internet access; Eritrea with the lowest GDP per head had the least internet access; France with the second highest GDP per head has the second highest % of population with internet access. Analysis of expected relationship: Higher GDP per head increases household's ability to pay for internet access / higher internet access may increase productivity/efficiency / increase job opportunities; higher GDP per head increases tax revenue, some of which can be spent on the provision of internet access / higher productivity/efficiency could increase wages. Exception: Guyana / Morocco Guyana had a higher GDP per head but low internet access. Analysis of exception: Internet access is determined by other influences e.g. education levels.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected / normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. In terms of supporting evidence: no marks for just description of the figures e.g. Brazil had a GDP per head of $8717 and 72% of the population with internet access / Eritrea had a GDP per head of $643 and 2% of population with internet access. France had a GDP of $40 494 and 83% of the population with internet access etc. In terms of supporting evidence one mark maximum for an indirect comparison e.g. Iceland had a GDP per head of $66 945 and 99% population with internet access whereas in Zambia with a GDP per head of $1,305, only 29% of the population had access to the internet.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the discovery of oil is likely to increase living standards in Guyana.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: increase exports increase economic growth / higher national output and raise incomes / increase GDP per head increase purchasing power / able to buy more goods and services / able to buy basic necessities could reduce poverty; create jobs / reduce unemployment / increase employment discourage emigration / encourage immigration; raise tax revenue allowing the government to spend more on e.g. education and healthcare MNCs may spend on e.g. improving infrastructure / training. Reasons why it might not: may create pollution create external costs / damage the environment which will harm people's health / reduce life expectancy; may be poor working conditions in the industry affecting health of workers; the foreign MNC may send much of the profit back to its country; it may drive domestic oil firms out of business; may deplete the country's oil reserves; the foreign MNC may employ workers from its own country; global demand for oil may fall / may be overdependence on oil / other countries may impose trade restrictions on oil.
Explanation: 3rd bullet point: increase in government spending should be linked to a form of spending that could increase living standards.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not Guyana would benefit from an increase in the size of its population.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: population is below the optimum level / move to the optimum level, could make better use of resources; if rise due to immigration may bring in new ideas / new production methods / new skills / new technology; an increase in the size of the labour force if the number of people of working age increases rise in tax revenue allowing the government to spend more on e.g. education and healthcare; consumer expenditure may increase total (aggregate) demand may increase; output could increase / cause economic growth / increase productive potential; dependency ratio could fall. Reasons why it might not: larger population could result in pollution / congestion / overcrowding damage the environment / increase external costs; if rise due to fall in death rate or rise in the birth rate / increase in young / old population, the dependency ratio will increase some of the extra population may be unemployed; pressure may be put on e.g. housing / welfare payments increasing need for government expenditure there may be inflation population may increase to the extent that there is overpopulation resources may be depleted (more quickly).
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: Thai commercial bank merger, effects on prices and lending, role of private sector banks in a market economy
Question Text: Scenario about banking and market systems
Identify two influences, other than price, on a customer’s choice of bank.
Markscheme: services provided; amount willing to lend; size of bank; online facilities; location; interest paid or charged; where family banks; advertising; green investments; community involvement; reputation; confidence in the bank / reliability / safety; relationship with staff; whether foreign currency can be exchanged
Explanation: If more than two influences given, consider the first three. Not accepting brand loyalty. Accept whether the bank offers Islamic banking. Also accept interest on its own.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a merger may result in higher prices for consumers.
Markscheme: More market power / less competition / monopoly power / may become a monopoly can raise price as consumers will not be able to switch to other firms / demand may become more inelastic / can control price / become a price maker; May experience diseconomies of scale / example of a diseconomy of scale that may be experienced raise prices due to higher (average) costs; Carrying out the merger may be expensive price raised to cover the cost of e.g. retraining staff / making some staff redundant.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three. Can accept higher cost if linked to diseconomies of scale or carrying out the merger. Not accepting the idea of higher costs because the firm will be larger (idea of higher total costs).

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in bank lending can benefit an economy.
Markscheme: It could increase consumer expenditure increase total demand raise employment /reduce unemployment reduce deflation. It could increase investment / increase spending on capital goods benefit from advances in technology / innovation / better quality capital goods increase productivity raise the quality of products lower price increase exports lower imports improve the current account balance cause economic growth / increase GDP / increase output / encourage firms to expand / new firms to set up. May enable the people to improve skills / to set up small business / buy basic necessities / reduce poverty. Lend to firms in financial difficulties stop them going out of business.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a market economy allocates resources in the best possible way.
Markscheme: Why it might: price moves to reflect changes in demand and supply; resources move from products falling in demand towards those rising in demand; profit incentive for firms to produce what consumers demand / consumer sovereignty; competition results in efficiency which drives down costs and prices; changes in wages move labour to those jobs most in demand. Why it might not: merit goods are under-consumed so not enough resources are devoted to their production; demerit goods are overconsumed so too many resources are devoted to their production; no resources are devoted to public goods as there is no incentive to produce them; there may be factor immobility; monopolies may restrict output; external costs and benefits will not be taken into account when deciding on the allocation of resources.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Canadian firms increasing investment, improved quantity and quality of land and government efforts to prevent unemployment
Question Text: Scenario about factors of production and investment
Identify two objectives of private sector firms.
Markscheme: profit (maximisation); growth / high sales; greater market share / market dominance; survival; improving the environment
Explanation: Reward but do not expect profit satisficing, revenue maximisation, sales maximisation. Allow social welfare. If more than two objectives given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain one reason why the quantity of land may increase and one reason why the quality of land may increase.
Markscheme: Quantity of land: Reclamation creating land from the sea; Reduction in overfishing / hunting restoring stocks of fish / wildlife; Reduction in flooding avoiding soil erosion; Discovery of new natural resources e.g. of oil. Quality of land: Use of fertilisers raising productivity / efficiency / fertility of land; Reduction in pollution preventing damage / diseases; Good weather raising productivity efficiency / fertility of land.
Explanation: One mark for identifying one reason why the quantity of land may increase and one mark for explaining that reason. One mark for identifying one reason why the quality of land may increase and one mark for explaining that reason.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in investment may affect unemployment.
Markscheme: Spending on capital goods will increase total demand firms may expand their output / new firms may set up / economic growth may occur (may need more workers) to work with the capital equipment / demand for labour may increase / need more workers / increase job opportunities this can reduce cyclical unemployment. Investment in education / training / healthcare / research and development can raise productivity / skills increase mobility reduce structural unemployment. Investment in infrastructure increase geographical mobility reduce structural unemployment. It could cause unemployment as capital goods may replace workers / production may become more capital-intensive / demand for workers may decrease capital goods can be a substitute for labour cause technological unemployment.
Explanation: Credit fall in unemployment / rise in unemployment only once. Reference to cyclical / structural / technological (unemployment) can be given an additional mark in each case if linked to a relevant point.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should try to prevent a rise in unemployment.
Markscheme: Why it should not: unemployment may be low; reduction in unemployment may cause inflation; the resulting higher incomes may cause a current account deficit; unemployment can create flexibility; opportunity cost of government policy measures; a government may have other objectives.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Australian national minimum wage, dairy farming, substitutes between milk and soft drinks, exchange rate appreciation
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and exchange rates
Identify two reasons why a government may set a national minimum wage (NMW).
Markscheme: to raise wages; to reduce poverty / raise living standards; reduce income inequality; to correct market failure / ensure fair wages / prevent exploitation of workers; to encourage job seeking / reduce unemployment; reduce emigration
Explanation: If more than two reasons given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two causes of an increase in the value of a country’s floating foreign exchange rate.
Markscheme: Increase in demand for the currency rise in exports / exports exceeding imports / current account surplus due to higher incomes abroad lower inflation / deflation / lower prices for exports better quality; Increase in foreign investment in the country; Higher rate of interest attracts hot money flows; Speculation that the currency will rise in value; Decrease in supply of the currency fall in imports / fall in current account deficit fall in incomes at home lower inflation / higher prices of imports / fall in quality of imports; The country's firms reducing investment in other countries; Lower rate of interest in other countries reduce hot money going out of the country; Speculation that other currencies will fall in value.
Explanation: Causes may be linked here in different ways. Also allow explanation of two causes of an increase in demand for the currency or two causes of a decrease in supply of the currency. Credit higher demand mark only once and lower supply mark only once.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in the price of milk may affect the revenue earned by milk producers and soft drinks producers.
Markscheme: Demand for milk is likely to fall / contraction in demand. The effect on the revenue of milk producers will depend on price elasticity of demand if demand is elastic, revenue will fall as demand will fall by a greater percentage than the rise in price if demand is inelastic, or demand is constant, revenue will increase as demand will fall by a smaller percentage than the rise in price. Demand for soft drinks is likely to increase the revenue of soft drinks producers is likely to rise as soft drinks are a substitute / alternative to milk.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not small firms are more likely to go out of business than large firms.
Markscheme: Why they might: may have high average costs; less able to take advantage of economies of scale; may be less likely to get a loan from commercial banks; may have less retained profits; may be new, testing out whether there is demand, inexperienced entrepreneurs. Why they might not: less likely to experience diseconomies of scale; may receive financial assistance from the government; may have customer loyalty; may be more flexible; may be a monopoly in a niche / specialist market.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: China economic growth, increased household spending, rising incomes of sportspeople and tariffs imposed by the US on Chinese imports
Question Text: Scenario about trade and economic growth
Identify the opportunity cost of households spending their income and the opportunity cost of Chinese firms exporting goods and services.
Markscheme: Saving; selling the products at home / consuming the products.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two reasons why some top sportspeople have high earnings.
Markscheme: High demand / inelastic demand for top sportspeople large crowds watch top sportspeople / events top sports events can be sold to TV firms for large fees high merchandise sales / sponsorship deals / high gate receipts / can generate high profits for firms; Low supply / inelastic supply of sportspeople / lack of substitutes high bargaining power top sports people are skilled / talented it may take years of training high level of experience some sports are dangerous.
Explanation: Reasons may be linked in different ways. Also allow explanation of two reasons for high demand or two reasons for low supply. Reward but do not expect high mrp of labour and derived demand for labour.

Question Text: Analyse the reasons for imposing tariffs on imports.
Markscheme: To improve the current account balance make imports more expensive reduce demand for imports / increase net exports increase total demand. To protect domestic industries / infant industries / sunrise industries protect sunset / declining industries protect strategic industries to increase economic growth reduce unemployment / increase employment encourage people to switch from buying imports to buying domestically produced products. To raise revenue which the government can spend on e.g. education / reduce a budget deficit. To discourage the imports of harmful products / demerit goods example. To prevent dumping / unfair competition the selling of products at less than cost price / the selling of products produced by child labour. To retaliate against another country's tariffs. To prevent a fall in the exchange rate to prevent switch from domestic to foreign currency.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a country with a high economic growth rate will have a deficit on the current account of its balance of payments.
Markscheme: Why it might: incomes will rise and consumers may buy more imports; firms may switch products from foreign markets to the growing home market; firms may import more raw materials and capital goods; the country may experience inflation which may reduce its international price competitiveness. Why it might not: the economic growth may be export-led; economic growth may encourage more investment, this could raise the quality of domestically produced products and lower their prices; higher tax revenue may result in improvements in education and healthcare which could increase productivity; the country may impose trade restrictions / reduce exchange rate.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: New Zealand aims to become carbon-neutral by 2050, reducing pollution from industries like milk and cars, replacing petrol vehicles, limiting cow numbers, and considering effects on exports, inflation, unemployment and macroeconomic performance
Question Text: Source material about New Zealand’s carbon-neutral policy
Calculate New Zealand’s GDP per head in 2020.
Explanation: Accept the correct figure without the $ sign.

Question Text: Identify two external costs arising from the milk and car industries.
Markscheme: Air pollution; Damages the environment (where wild animals live)
Explanation: If more than two costs given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain the impact on a cow farmer’s profits if they are unable to find a different source of revenue.
Markscheme: Profit is revenue minus costs; Profits will fall due to reduction in quantity sold / decrease in supply. Less revenue, less income for farmers, farmers may become unemployed.
Explanation: Logical explanation required.

Question Text: Explain the two plans that the New Zealand government has to reduce external costs to the environment.
Markscheme: End imports of petrol-powered cars by 2032 decreasing the number of petrol-powered cars in New Zealand / reduce use of fossil fuels / by imposing an embargo/increasing the price of petrol-power cars / increasing usage of greener / electric cars. Limit the number of cows that each farmer has decreasing the amount of air pollution emitted by cows / less habitat damaged by cows / government fines to those who have more cows than permitted / imposing regulations.
Explanation: One mark for each of two plans identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of a cheaper substitute on the market for petrol-powered cars.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or P and Q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the left; Equilibrium – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: Demand and supply diagram required.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between a country’s share of world GDP (%) and its share of world carbon dioxide emissions (%).
Markscheme: Positive relationship – the lower / higher total GDP as a % of world GDP, the lower / higher the carbon dioxide emission as a % of world GDP. As countries produce more goods and services which increases their GDP, their carbon emissions also increase as more factories / energy / transport needed for production. Exception: China/United States; China has 2nd highest GDP as a % of world GDP after the US but China has the highest carbon dioxide emission as a % of world GDP.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in a set format but they should address the expected relationship, offer supporting evidence, highlight exceptions, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in exports of clean and green products will help the New Zealand government achieve its macroeconomic aims.
Markscheme: Increase economic growth / GDP as increased exports lead to increased total demand; clean and green products may have higher demand and higher prices. Reduce its current account of the balance of payment deficit as value of exports will increase, increasing net exports, more inflow into the current account. Decrease unemployment as more goods produced requires more workers. Not all firms benefit as some firms which are less clean / green will lose revenues / profits, cut costs, hire less workers, increase unemployment. Increased total demand will lead to inflation, firms increase the price of their products since demand is higher, lower purchasing power of consumers / lower real income.
Explanation: Each point may be credited only once, on either side of an argument, but separate development as to how/why the outcome may differ is rewarded.
