Question Text: Discuss whether or not job losses caused by a change to a carbon-neutral economy will be harmful.
Markscheme: Negative: increased poverty, possibility of increased crime, huge losses in the other firms that serve these communities, lost output, inefficient allocation of resources, occupational immobility, cost of retraining workers, double effect on government finances, structural unemployment, increases inequality. Not negative: temporary / frictional unemployment as other jobs in carbon-neutral economy are available, workers can move to other industries / develop skills for new jobs, new jobs may be higher paid, better air quality leads to better health / higher productivity, government spends less on health care.
Explanation: Each point may be credited only once, on either side of an argument, but separate development as to how/why the outcome may differ is rewarded.

Stimulus / Scenario: Liechtenstein economy with low tax rates and low regulation attracting foreign firms despite high production costs
Question Text: Scenario about taxation and banking
Identify two types of cost to a firm.
Markscheme: total cost, average total cost, fixed cost, variable cost, average fixed cost, average variable cost.
Explanation: Accept any relevant costs e.g. labour cost. Accept abbreviations e.g. TC. If more than two costs given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two roles of commercial banks for firms.
Markscheme: Provide loans / overdrafts to banks / source of finance to fund investments / expansion / start-up / day to day operations. Help process payments, internet banking / fund transfers / credit card / debit card payments. Issuing bank drafts / bank cheques to help firms make payments. Safe place to store money / savings, firms making deposits / earn interest. Safekeeping of documents and other items in safe deposit boxes. Currency exchange to facilitate transfers between firms / international transactions. Provides insurance to reduce various risks to the firm.
Explanation: One mark for each of two roles identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two roles given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse the causes of differences in the level of income between countries.
Markscheme: Difference in productivity, countries with higher productivity have higher incomes. Difference in availability of natural resources, e.g. oil or gold. Difference in population growth, countries with higher population growth have higher incomes as there are more people to produce goods and services. Differences in distribution of primary, secondary and tertiary sectors / level of development, those with higher secondary and tertiary sectors have higher incomes / more value added. Difference in level of saving and investment, higher savings leads to higher investment and higher productivity growth. Difference in education levels, higher education levels lead to higher incomes as workers have more qualification / skills / experience. Difference in health / healthcare standards, higher health / healthcare standards lead to higher incomes as workers produce more / are more productive.
Explanation: Alternatively, accept arguments which describe reasons for lower incomes.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not low tax rates benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why low tax rates might benefit: low corporation tax encourages investment, growth and employment; low income tax encourages work and increases disposable income/consumption; low indirect tax reduces costs and inflation/increases export competitiveness. Why low tax rates might not benefit: reduces government revenue for public goods/infrastructure; may increase pollution/environmental degradation; low direct tax may be inflationary or increase inequality; low tax on demerit goods may worsen health/market failure; low tariffs may worsen current account.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Decline in Chinese restaurants in the UK due to falling demand, increased competition and reluctance of small firms to invest in technology
Question Text: Scenario about small firms and demand
Identify two challenges facing small firms.
Markscheme: Raising finance; May not be able to employ specialist staff; Less well known; Managing cash flow; Survival / may have high level of competition; Not able to take advantage of economies of scale / high average cost.
Explanation: If more than two challenges given, consider the first three. Accept any reasonable challenges.

Question Text: Explain two possible opportunity costs for firms if they invest in the latest technology.
Markscheme: They may not be able to pay as much in wages, forgoing having extra workers to help in their business / more skilled workers. They may not be able to get good quality raw materials, sacrificing the quality of their product. They may not be able to operate in a good location, making it more difficult for customers to reach the firm. They may not be able to buy land for expansion. They may not be able to spend on advertising / marketing / spending on the brand, forgoing the opportunity to increase demand. Retained profits could have been distributed to the owners.
Explanation: One mark for each of two opportunity costs identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse two causes of a fall in demand for a product such as Chinese food.
Markscheme: Decrease in price of substitutes / better quality substitutes increasing demand for substitutes such as Indian food. Increase in the price of complements, e.g. delivery charges. Decrease in income decreasing demand for Chinese food as people eat out less, if eating out is seen as a luxury good. Change in tastes / trends moving away from Chinese food, increasing demand for other types of food. Decrease in quality of Chinese food due to lack of investments; a health report about the advantages of eating another type of food. Lack of advertising due to little investment in marketing, leading to less popularity / less awareness of product.
Explanation: If more than two causes given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not competition is harmful to a firm.
Markscheme: Why competition might be harmful: reduction in market share; less revenues / less profits; less growth; less reinvested profits – less innovation; may need to lower prices / spend more in order to compete; may be difficult to enter or stay in an industry if there are large firms with low average costs. Why competition might not be harmful: less complacency; more innovation; increase quality of goods and services produced; domestic competition leads to international competitiveness.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Albania experienced increased emigration after a recession and government policies aim to attract workers back
Question Text: Scenario about migration and labour markets
Define emigration.
Markscheme: Emigration is when people exit / leave a country to live in another country.

Question Text: Explain two causes of a recession.
Markscheme: Fall in total demand / low economic activity due to lower consumer confidence / falling consumption / falling government spending / falling exports / increase imports. Fall in investment leading to fall in productive capacity. Fall in population e.g. due to emigration, leading to a fall in number of workers. Wars / natural disasters causing destruction of factors of production. Higher interest rates leading to lower borrowing / consumption / investments. Decreased consumer / investor confidence decreasing consumption / capital flight. Higher income / corporation taxes leading to falling disposable income for consumption / falling after tax profits leading to lower investments. Lower productivity reducing demand for the country's output.
Explanation: If more than two causes given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse policy measures a government could use to attract workers to return to their home country.
Markscheme: Increase national minimum wage to attract more unskilled / low-paid workers to return. Expansionary fiscal policy / expansionary monetary policy to increase job opportunities. Improved education / healthcare / housing / higher pensions to raise living standards. Better working conditions / quality of life, regulations to protect workers, better fringe benefits. Greater security in the country e.g. gun controls. Labour market reforms making it easier for firms to hire, creating more job opportunities. Lower direct taxes to increase after-tax income / disposable income. Deregulation encourages more firms to start up, encouraging more entrepreneurs. Improving incentives to work and invest, leading to more demand for workers. Measures to reduce inflation, making lower cost of living attractive.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an economy benefits from many of its students studying abroad.
Markscheme: Why an economy might benefit: students gain better skills/experiences abroad; returning students increase productivity; learn about new technologies; make future business contacts; bring back new ideas/products; decrease poverty; less spent on education by government. Why an economy might not benefit: students might not return (brain drain); money spent abroad (money outflow); only a small proportion can afford it, increasing inequality; lack of development of domestic higher education; unemployment of teachers/lecturers in the country.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Wisconsin economy where industries such as food processing, tourism and education contribute to government budget and population growth, generating private and external benefits
Question Text: Scenario about government revenue and population growth
Define government budget.
Markscheme: Government budget is a plan / forecast for government expenditure and government revenue.

Question Text: Explain, with examples, the difference between private benefits and external benefits.
Markscheme: Private benefits are benefits incurred by the consumers or producers of a product; for example, getting a better job in the future for those consuming education / profits that a firm earns. External benefits are benefits incurred by the third party; for example, increased productivity due to better education and better healthcare as a result of better qualified doctors.
Explanation: Logical explanation required.

Question Text: Analyse the reasons why workers employed in education may earn more than workers employed in other industries.
Markscheme: Higher demand for education workers due to higher demand for education than other industries; demand for education workers is derived demand. Lower supply, less people available to work in education e.g., not many qualified people want to become teachers / lecturers. Higher bargaining strengths, stronger trade unions e.g., teachers' union is very strong. Less discrimination, everyone is paid according to their level of productivity. More favourable government policy for workers in education / government spends more on education, higher wages set by the government as education is essential. More skilled, long-term training required, higher productivity. Tertiary workers earn more, more value added.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not having a large population is beneficial for the economy.
Markscheme: Why a large population might be beneficial: high labour supply – high productive capacity; workers can be taxed – enabling spending on public services; high spending leads to high total demand – high economic growth; high investment as firms can gain more profit or find more workers; high competition amongst workers – high quality / productivity of workers; many entrepreneurs – increased competition – increased innovation; high demand – economies of scale for firms. Why a large population might not be beneficial: strain on resources – e.g., lack of proper housing, schools, hospitals; external costs – e.g., pollution; high total demand could lead to demand-pull inflation; shortage of jobs – increased unemployment; it depends upon the composition of the population e.g., large proportion of under 5's will not increase output in the short run.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Cambridge (UK) economy focused on education, research and high-tech industries, strong exports, highly educated workforce, free trade support, but high land and housing costs
Question Text: Source material about Cambridge economy and education sector
Calculate the total number of people in Cambridge aged 18–29 in 2019.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Identify two types of industries in Cambridge that operate in the tertiary sector.
Markscheme: Education; Healthcare; Research and development
Explanation: If more than two types are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Identify two benefits of having highly knowledge-intensive exports.
Markscheme: Exports highly priced; Price inelastic in demand; Increased demand for exports (due to increased global incomes)
Explanation: If more than two benefits are given, consider the first three. Accept high value for highly priced.

Question Text: Explain how having a more educated labour force can reduce poverty.
Markscheme: a more educated labour force will be more skilled / more productive enabling them to earn higher wages able to afford basic necessities. a more educated labour force may have more job opportunities / less unemployment wages may be higher than unemployment benefits. a more educated labour force has access to more information on health increasing the ability to work have access to better healthcare.
Explanation: Logical explanation required.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effects of increased global income on the market for education in Cambridge.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New curve shifted to the right; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: Demand and supply diagram required.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between the percentage of population aged 25–64 with a university degree and GDP per head.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Positive / direct relationship the higher percentage of population with a university degree, the higher the GDP per head. Analysis of expected relationship: as percentage of population with a university degree increases, the more skills / qualification they will have therefore, they have higher productivity and can earn more. Supporting evidence: e.g. Indonesia lowest percentage of population with a university degree, lowest GDP per head; Canada, Switzerland and UK have the highest three in each category; Colombia has the second lowest percentage of population with a university degree and the second lowest GDP per head (2). Exception: Switzerland has lower percentage of population with a university degree than Canada or the UK, but higher GDP per head. Analysis of the exception: Other influences, e.g. quality of secondary education, proportion of the population employed in banking.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected / normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not free trade is beneficial for a city such as Cambridge.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: easier to export increased export revenues increased revenue / profits for producers higher growth in the city. easier to import / more imports cheaper imports of goods and materials / lower costs for firms lower prices (of exports). more jobs created reducing the level of unemployment. increase size of the market economies of scale e.g. purchasing / technical / marketing / managerial / financial. increased choice for consumers from increased imports higher quality goods / variety of goods. more competition or more transfer of ideas and knowledge leads to more innovation lower prices encourage entrepreneurship / investment profit incentive. Reasons why it might not: overdependence on foreign markets economic shocks in other countries can negatively affect revenues of firms reduce economic growth and development in the city. free trade could reduce competitiveness of firms other economies might have relatively lower costs cheap imports / more imports stop development of infant industries may lead to job losses in the city. free trade only affects certain producers / firms / workers leads to increased inequality / uneven distribution of income. free trade leads to more environmental degradation due to more production / more need for cross-border transport creating external costs / negative externalities. may increase consumption of demerit goods reduce health. free trade could lead to the creation of global monopolies. reducing the ability of smaller domestic firms to compete.
Explanation: Each point may be credited only once, on either side of an argument, but separate development as to how / why the outcome may differ is rewarded.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not firms in Cambridge will continue to grow.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: ease of access to a large amount of highly-skilled and educated workers, from all over the world easy to find workers to produce goods and services or lower cost of labour; skilled workers are more productive more innovation / new ideas / high quality products; young workers may be up-to-date with modern technology; good infrastructure such as access to ultra-fast broadband productivity is higher faster production / less delays; good transport links to other cities and international airports easy to export / high demand for their exports easy to reach customers faster deliveries increased demand for goods and services. Reasons why it might not: high land and office costs high cost of production higher capital start-up costs lower profits; high house prices poor air quality may discourage workers from locating in Cambridge difficult for firms to find workers; small population small market limited demand limited scope for revenue / profits for Cambridge firms; overdependence on one sector (e.g. education) limits potential growth from other sectors; skilled workers are available in other cities.
Explanation: Each point may be credited only once, on either side of an argument, but separate development as to how / why the outcome may differ is rewarded.

Stimulus / Scenario: Ski tourism in the Alps with high prices, pandemic impact on tourism firms and role of government intervention
Question Text: Scenario about tourism firms and government regulation
State how average revenue is calculated.
Markscheme: Average revenue = total revenue / quantity sold
Explanation: Accept quantity without 'sold' as the divisor.

Question Text: Explain two advantages of a large firm.
Markscheme: economies of scale (average) cost lower than smaller firms; finance / capital available ease of getting loans from banks; technological development high productivity; large customer base high revenue / profits; may be well-known increase brand loyalty.
Explanation: One mark for each of two advantages identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Accept explanation of two different economies of scale. Credit another example of economies of scale e.g. financial economies.

Question Text: Analyse the possible causes of higher prices of a product such as ski holidays in the Alps.
Markscheme: higher demand / higher popularity from e.g. advertisements / reputation rise in quality rise in price of a substitute / another type of holiday; more inelastic demand quantity demanded less responsive to changes in price e.g. due to higher brand loyalty; higher income of target market luxury good; lower supply e.g. due to better quality; more inelastic supply quantity supplied less responsive to changes in price more regulations; higher cost of production higher labour costs higher minimum wage higher living costs of workers in ski industry; investment in improving facilities e.g. ski lifts may raise costs in the short run.
Explanation: Accept any relevant example of a fall in the price of a complement e.g. ski equipment, as a reason for higher demand.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not government intervention is beneficial for firms.
Markscheme: Why it might: government could provide subsidies to reduce firms' costs / help boost output / help during natural disasters / help firms survive; government could reduce taxes to reduce cost of production; minimum prices could increase revenues of firms; government spending on education could increase skills of workers; government spending on benefits could increase demand for products; regulations controlling monopolies could promote competition. Why it might not: government regulations could increase cost of production; minimum wage could add to firms' costs, reducing profits; indirect taxes or direct taxes could reduce firms' profits; maximum prices could reduce firms' revenues; trade restrictions could limit competition.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Growth of digital money and cryptocurrencies, with potential external costs from energy use and possibility of taxation to reduce market failure
Question Text: Scenario about digital money and market failure
Identify two types of tax.
Markscheme: Direct; Indirect; Progressive; Proportional; Regressive
Explanation: Accept any relevant examples of taxes e.g. income tax, tariffs. Accept, but do not expect, specific and ad valorem taxes.

Question Text: Explain two functions of money.
Markscheme: a medium of exchange to buy goods and services / to trade; a measure of value / unit of account to measure how much a product is worth; a store of value to allow people to save; a method of deferred payment for borrowing and lending.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two functions identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the consequences of market failure.
Markscheme: None
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a tax on a product can reduce external costs.
Markscheme: Why it might: tax will increase price, reducing the quantity demanded; if demand for the product is elastic, quantity demanded will be responsive to the price increase, quantity demanded will change significantly; tax will increase the cost of production, therefore decreasing supply; tax will decrease the profits of firms; tax will provide governments additional revenue to help with subsidising less-polluting products. Why it might not: tax is insignificant compared to the final price of the product; tax avoidance / evasion / unofficial or illegal markets; if demand for the product is inelastic, quantity demanded will be unresponsive to the price increase, quantity demanded will not change significantly, e.g. addictive products.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Malaysia moving from exporting natural rubber to producing manufactured goods using rubber
Question Text: Scenario about specialisation and industrial development
Identify, other than natural rubber, two primary-sector products.
Markscheme: e.g. coal, oil, fish, wood, wheat
Explanation: If more than two products are given, consider the first three. Accept process rather than product e.g. fishing.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a country may change its specialisation.
Markscheme: Change in quantity / quality of resources discovery of new resources or new technology other economies may be more / less efficient in production of certain products. Change in cost of production change in labour costs e.g. increase in minimum wage change in capital costs e.g. cheaper technology; Changes in demand changes in consumer behaviour change in revenue / profits; Changes in level of competition other countries' producers may gain a competitive advantage.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the economic benefits for a country in producing manufactured goods instead of primary-sector products.
Markscheme: higher price higher output higher revenues / profits higher value added; more exports increased total demand economic growth; higher productivity economies of scale; more demand for workers lower unemployment may be more skilled jobs higher income of workers higher standards of living / reduction in poverty; does not require stock of natural resources skills / technology could be learnt / imported; less dependent on weather changes more stable revenues / income easier to plan / budget; higher tax revenues for the government higher government spending more development spending e.g. healthcare services / education services / infrastructure resulting in higher levels of economic development.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not labour-intensive production will harm an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: labour may cost more than capital; labour may be more unreliable - inefficient / inconsistent; labour may be protected by trade unions - more disruptions; labour may be less productive; labour may need more breaks than machines; labour intensive production may be less cost-effective, reducing international competitiveness. Why it might not: provides high employment levels - high standards of living; lower levels of unemployment - less government spending on unemployment benefits; labour may be more flexible than machines - can change skills / production / more mobile; labour may be able to respond to people / customers more easily.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: High house prices in major cities due to low price elasticity of supply, calls for affordable housing, government policies and impact on monetary policy
Question Text: Scenario about housing markets
Identify two monetary policy measures.
Markscheme: Changes in: interest rates; money supply; foreign exchange rates.
Explanation: Accept, but do not expect, quantitative easing as an alternative to money supply. If more than two measures are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two microeconomic policy measures that can be taken by a government to reduce the price of a product.
Markscheme: maximum prices e.g. maximum rents / rent controls; subsidies reduce cost of production e.g. housing subsidies; nationalisation government sets lower prices; direct provision of goods increase supply of goods e.g. government providing public housing; regulation e.g. prohibiting foreign house buying reduce demand; deregulation allowing more firms in the market / more competition; lower (sales) taxes if sellers reduce prices as a result.
Explanation: Examples could be related to any market – does not have to be housing market. If more than two policy measures are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse the key determinants of price elasticity of supply of a product.
Markscheme: availability of stocks finished goods which are unsold with a high level of stocks, supply will be elastic; level of spare capacity factors of production available but unused e.g. workers not working full time supply will be more elastic if levels of spare capacity are high; time period under consideration short run / long run short run has inelastic supply with at least one fixed factor of production which makes it more difficult to produce long run has elastic supply when all factors of production are variable making it easier to produce more; difficulty of production process / length of production time produce that is easy to produce will be more elastic e.g. bread is easier to produce compared to a smartphone.
Explanation: Also accept an approach based on why PES may be inelastic as shown in third bullet point.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not trade unions benefit workers.
Markscheme: Why they might: collective bargaining can result in higher wages; better working hours and working conditions can be negotiated; trade unions may protect employment giving more job security; trade unions may influence governments to give favourable policies for workers, e.g. maternity pay; trade unions may provide services for workers e.g. training. Why they might not: higher wages negotiated by trade unions may increase costs of production, firms may reduce number of workers to cut costs; may promote industrial actions / strikes, reducing productivity, reducing wages of workers; may reduce competitiveness of the economy, reducing investment and therefore reducing the demand for workers; workers may not agree with the objectives of a trade union; workers may feel that the fees to join a trade union are too high compared to the benefits they could receive from membership.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Honduras economy: reliance on US trade, diversification from bananas and coffee into manufacturing such as clothes, poverty reduction policies and links between poverty and life expectancy
Question Text: Source material about Honduras economy and development
Calculate what percentage of Honduran people did not have access to electricity in 2020.
Explanation: Accept 8.0 or 8.3.

Question Text: Identify two benefits the Honduran economy could gain from a growth in the US economy.
Markscheme: Sell more exports to the US; Receive more money from Honduran workers in the US; More job opportunities for Honduran workers in the US.
Explanation: If more than two benefits are given, consider the first three. Accept increase trade for more exports. Accept increase employment / lower unemployment for more job opportunities.

Question Text: Explain one advantage of an economy specialising.
Markscheme: Gain skills raise productivity / raise efficiency / "practise makes perfect" / lower costs of production / raise quality / increase output / supply / less waste; Gain a good reputation increase demand.
Explanation: One mark for an advantage identified and one mark an explanation. Identification mark must come directly from the source material. A relevant explanation mark can be given even in the absence of an identification.

Question Text: Explain two ways a government could redistribute income.
Markscheme: Provision of unemployment benefit / state benefit / welfare benefit / benefits raise income of those on low or no income / enable the unemployed to buy basic necessities / reduce absolute poverty; Progressive income tax system takes a higher proportion of the income of the rich / use some of tax revenue raised to increase spending to help those on low incomes.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three. One mark for taxing the rich and giving to the poor.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show how a report stating that bananas are good for health would affect the market for bananas.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Demand curve shifted to the right; Equilibrium – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: If a candidate draws two diagrams, they may be able to get 3 marks: 1 mark for axes, 1 mark for D & S labelled correctly, 1 mark for the demand curve shifted to the right. They would not get the mark for the equilibriums as the two diagrams are likely to conflict on this.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between the percentage of population living in poverty and life expectancy.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Inverse / negative the higher the level of poverty, the lower the life expectancy / the lower the level of poverty, the higher the life expectancy. Supporting evidence: Sweden has the lowest % of population living in poverty and the highest life expectancy / The country / two countries / three countries / four countries with the lowest levels of poverty head had the longest life expectancy the two countries, Chad and South Sudan, with the highest level of poverty had the lowest life expectancy. Analysis of expected relationship: A high level of poverty is likely to mean poor healthcare low nutrition / poor housing / poor sanitation / low levels of education. Exception: Chad / South Sudan Chad had a lower level of poverty but also a lower level of life expectancy than South Sudan. Analysis of exception: Life expectancy is determined by other influences e.g. wars.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the cost of producing clothes in Honduras will fall in the future.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: more training better working conditions may increase labour productivity / efficiency / skills reduce labour costs; more use of capital goods may reduce disruption to production / make fewer mistakes / less waste speed up production / increase productivity / efficiency; improved working conditions may reduce industrial action more motivation; successful clothes industry so firms may grow in size more able to take advantage of economies of scale. Reasons why it might not: price of raw materials may rise may be subject to bad weather exchange rate may fall / tariffs may be imposed on imported raw materials reduce supply country has experience of inflation; unemployment may fall making it more difficult for the clothes industry to recruit labour strengthening the bargaining power of workers raising wages / increasing labour costs; providing training and better working conditions will involve a cost; more use of capital goods may involve initial high spending on equipment firms may experience diseconomies of scale; higher indirect tax / tax may be imposed (on clothes / clothes firms).
Explanation: Allow higher productivity / efficiency once either in connection with labour or capital. Some points may be given in reverse e.g. price of raw materials may fall. Not accepting subsidising the clothes industry as the source material indicates that it is a successful industry. Each point may be credited only once, on either side of an argument, but separate development as to how/why the outcome may differ is rewarded.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a fall in unemployment in Honduras is likely to cause inflation.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: unemployment is already low may move towards full employment may push up wages increase (average) costs cause cost-push inflation / total supply less than total demand; lower unemployment may increase incomes causing a rise in consumer spending / purchasing power increasing total demand causing demand-pull inflation / total demand higher than total supply; inflation already exists which may lead workers to expect it to continue increasing wage claims. Reasons why it might not: may be higher investment could increase total supply / total supply may rise in line with total demand reducing cost-push inflation; lower unemployment could reduce spending on unemployment benefit increase tax revenue reduce the budget deficit reduce the growth in total demand some of higher tax revenue could be spent on supply-side policy / unemployment may have fallen due to supply-side policy; the extra income may be saved / spent on imports due to uncertainty about the future; A fall in unemployment does not necessarily mean a rise in employment the unemployed could have e.g. retired / emigrated.
Explanation: Each point may be credited only once, on either side of an argument, but separate development as to how/why the outcome may differ is rewarded.

Stimulus / Scenario: Botswana diamond mining industry, economic growth, low inflation and movement toward free trade
Question Text: Scenario about growth and trade policy
Define, with an example, a capital good.
Markscheme: A good used to produce another good or service / a human-made good used for production e.g. a machine.
Explanation: If more than two examples are given, consider the first two.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a low inflation rate may increase a country’s economic growth rate.
Markscheme: make the country's products internationally competitive sell more exports / buy fewer imports; create greater certainty encourage investment / attract MNCs; keep increase in costs low may reduce claims for higher wages/ higher profits; increase confidence may increase demand / increase profits.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how a government could reduce protectionism and move towards free international trade.
Markscheme: remove tariffs reduce price of imports if firms have lower costs of production, they will be able to compete; remove quotas / increase quota (limit) remove bans / embargoes increase quantity of imports / reduce limits on imports; remove subsidies firms' products will not be artificially cheap; other countries may remove their protectionism enabling exports to be sold without restrictions.
Explanation: Allow deregulation as an alternative to remove quotas or embargoes. Reward removing / reducing bureaucracy, voluntary export restraints. Reward but do not expect reference to exchange control.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a country will benefit from diamond mining.
Markscheme: Why it might: high global demand can improve the current account of the balance of payments; create employment, both skilled and unskilled jobs; increase output / economic growth; may increase tax revenue. Why it might not: demand will fall in a recession; price may fluctuate; may be competition; can be accidents; can create external costs e.g. pollution; if mined by an MNC, profit may be sent to the home country; supply may be depleted; price may increase in the future so better to conserve diamonds now; may not be a large proportion of GDP.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Firms in Suriname stopping production due to costs and rising unemployment with government supply-side policies
Question Text: Scenario about firm costs and unemployment
Define, with an example, a fixed cost.
Markscheme: A cost that does not change with output / a cost that has to be paid even when output is zero e.g. rent.
Explanation: Not sufficient to state a cost that does not change. Accepting electricity / electricity bills and wages.

Question Text: Explain two types of unemployment.
Markscheme: frictional unemployment workers in between jobs; structural unemployment changes in demand and supply conditions / skills not matching vacancies / lack of labour mobility; cyclical / demand-deficient unemployment lack of total demand / occurs during a recession.
Explanation: Allow explanation of two types of frictional unemployment i.e. search, casual or seasonal and two types of structural unemployment i.e. regional and technological. Also allow voluntary and involuntary. One mark each for each of two types identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how supply-side policy measures could reduce unemployment.
Markscheme: Education can increase qualifications / literacy rate; training can increase skills / productivity / quality of labour raise mobility / increase job opportunities reduce structural unemployment. Lower income tax reduce unemployment benefit increases the incentive for the unemployed to find work quickly reduce frictional unemployment. Lower corporation tax / tax incentives encourage firms to invest / expand output. Privatisation deregulation can increase efficiency reduce firms' costs enabling firms to afford more workers. Reform of trade unions / labour market reforms Deregulation / removing labour regulations making it easier to hire and fire workers. Government spending on infrastructure can reduce firms' costs of production / reduce transport costs encouraging firms to expand increase labour mobility. Minimum wage legislation reducing may encourage employer to employ more workers / increasing may encourage the unemployed to search more actively for work. Subsidies encouraging firms to expand / prevent firms going out of business. Better/more healthcare can increase productivity / reduce working time lost.
Explanation: Reward the same point e.g. 'raise productivity' only once. Accept lower interest rate if linked to encouraging investment.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not having fewer firms in a market will benefit consumers.
Markscheme: Why it might: firms may be larger, may be able to benefit from economies of scale; economies of scale may include e.g. financial, managerial, technical; lower costs may result in lower prices; firms may have more market power - firms may make more profit and may reinvest which can improve quality; firms may have moved to another market in response to changes in consumer demand; firms that were creating pollution and other external costs may close down; may reduce the time consumers spend shopping around. Why it might not: firms may experience diseconomies of scale; diseconomies of scale may include e.g. communication problems, control problems and poor industrial relations; less choice; lack of competition / move towards monopoly resulting in lower quality and higher prices; lower availability.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: US emu meat farming, elastic supply, government subsidies for agriculture and labour issues in rural areas
Question Text: Scenario about agriculture and labour markets
Define elastic supply.
Markscheme: The percentage change in quantity supplied is greater than the percentage change in price.
Explanation: Change in supply is greater than the change in price / PES > 1 (1).

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a government may subsidise food production.
Markscheme: Increase output overcome a shortage; Reduce price / cost help consumers / reduce poverty / give access to a basic necessity / make food more affordable; Increase quality improve nutrition / increase health of population / raise life expectancy; Increase incomes of farmers / farm workers raise their living standards of farmers / stop farmers going out of business; Reduce imports of food / increase exports of food improve the current account of the balance of payments / agriculture is a strategic industry / reduce dependency on other countries for an essential good; Increase employment / reduce unemployment help in rural areas.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse what influences a farmer’s demand for labour.
Markscheme: Demand for the food they produce derived demand / a higher demand for food will encourage farmers to expand their output / employ more workers more profits earned; Wage rates the lower the wage rate, the less it will cost to employ workers; Skills of workers the more skilled workers are, the more productive they will be; Price of other factors of production / costs of production if the price of e.g. agricultural drones falls, demand for labour may fall; Productivity of other factors of production advances in technology may result in farm workers being replaced by machinery; Type of farming some farming is more labour-intensive / capital-intensive; Size of farm large farms likely to employ more; Subsidies subsidies may encourage a farmer to expand output and employ more workers; Seasons demand for labour is likely to be higher during harvesting.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not people living in cities have a higher living standard than those living in rural areas.
Markscheme: Why they might: may be more job opportunities; may be higher incomes; may be more forms of entertainment; may be shorter distance to schools and shops; may be more access to healthcare facilities; may be better quality education/healthcare. Why they might not: may be more traffic congestion; may be overcrowding; air and noise pollution levels may be higher; stress may be high; higher cost of living in cities.
Explanation: The answer may be from the view of why people living in rural areas may have a higher living standard. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Singapore experiencing falling population and labour force but increasing exports and markets moving from disequilibrium to equilibrium
Question Text: Scenario about population change and trade
Identify two benefits of a decrease in a country’s population size.
Markscheme: may be fewer dependents; less pressure on resources; less food needed; move population towards the optimum level / reduce overpopulation; reduce overcrowding / more land space; reduce pollution / less negative externalities; lower imports; lower government expenditure (on healthcare/education); less unemployment / less unemployment benefit; may increase GDP per head.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain how a market moves from disequilibrium to equilibrium.
Markscheme: If demand is greater than supply / there is a shortage / excess demand price will rise; If supply is greater than demand / there is a surplus / excess supply price will fall; Demand will again equal supply / demand would not initially have been equal to supply.
Explanation: One mark for reference to a change in price. Maximum of 2 marks for diagrams which show initial disequilibrium and change in price which restores equilibrium.

Question Text: Analyse, using a production possibility curve (PPC) diagram, the effect of a decrease in the size of a country’s labour force on its economy.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled with different outputs; Initial curve drawn as a curve/line sloping downward to the axes; New curve drawn as a curve/line sloping downward to the axes; Shift to the left indicated by arrow or letters. Analysis: A decrease in the size of the labour force reduces resources / factors of production available this lowers productive capacity / the maximum output that can be produced.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for coherent analysis. Note to gain the marks for the curves, these must be drawn to the axes.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in exports will benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase export revenue, improve the current account balance; may increase total demand; may increase investment; may increase output / economic growth; may reduce unemployment; may increase incomes. Why it might not: may cause demand-pull inflation; opportunity cost of selling more products on the home market; may lead to shortages on the home market; may deplete resources; may push up the exchange rate, reversing the increase in exports in the long run; may make the economy more subject to external shocks.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Montenegro economy: privatisation of state firms, growth of tourism and tertiary sector employment, possible minimum price for oranges, trade balances and risk of deflation
Question Text: Source material about economic change in Montenegro
Calculate Montenegro’s budget deficit as a percentage of its GDP.
Explanation: Calculation is -$0.6 bn divided by $4.8 bn. Allow 12.5

Question Text: Identify two examples of capital goods in Montenegro.
Markscheme: Computers; office buildings
Explanation: These are the only two capital goods mentioned in the text but accept answers such as telecommunications equipment and aluminium production factories

Question Text: Explain one way price elasticity of demand may influence firms’ decision making.
Markscheme: If demand is price elastic / high firms will know that if they raise price, revenue will fall / if they lower price, revenue will rise.
Explanation: One mark for the way identified and one mark for an explanation. Note: source material only mentions demand for holidays as being price-elastic. No mark for identifying price-inelastic but do allow second mark that price inelastic demand means if they raise price revenue will rise / if they lower price, revenue will fall. Do not reward impact on demand or on profits

Question Text: Explain two ways the pattern of employment has changed in Montenegro in recent years.
Markscheme: Higher proportion of workers employed in the tourism industry / tertiary sector growth in tourism / lower proportion employed in primary and secondary sectors. More workers employed in the private sector / commercial banks / telecommunications / aluminium products due to privatisation / less employed in state sector.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Note: question requires a reason for the change in pattern of employment

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of a minimum price set above the equilibrium price on the market for oranges.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled - price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Minimum Price / Price floor line set above the equilibrium and correctly labelled - accept Pmin or Pm or MP but not P2; Supply shown as greater than demand e.g. labelling of Qd<Qs.
Explanation: Note: No mark for showing minimum price if also shows a shift in supply or demand curve as it is no longer above market equilibrium. Ignore all written comments.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between countries’ trade in goods and services balances and their current account balances.
Markscheme: Approach A: Positive / direct relationship. Countries which have a deficit on the trade in goods and services balance might be expected to have a deficit on the current account balance. Countries which have a surplus on the trade in goods and services balance might be expected to have a surplus on the current account balance. Three countries / Bosnia & Herzegovina / Kosovo / Montenegro all have a deficit on the trade in goods and service balance also had a deficit on the current account balance two countries / Bulgaria / Slovenia had a surplus on the trade in goods and service balance but also had a surplus on the current account balance. Exception: Croatia had a deficit on trade in goods and services balance but a surplus on the current account balance. Approach B: There is no expected relationship as the current account balance contains other items which can either be positive or negative and are not dependent on the trade in goods and services.
Explanation: Allow income for primary income and current transfers for secondary income. Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Do not accept relationship is directly proportional.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not privatisation is likely to have benefited consumers in Montenegro.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Firms are profit driven may have increased investment / research & development raising quality of goods and services lowered cost of production more firms entering the market increased competition may reduce prices may give more choice to consumers firms may respond more quickly to changes in consumer demand. Reasons why it might not: state-owned firms may have been subsidised investment may be now lower privatised firms may be profit maximisers may become monopolies and may restrict supply to push up prices lowered quality of goods produced creates inequality as not all consumers can afford to buy privatised firms may be smaller and so not able to take advantage of economies of scale privatised firms do not take account of external costs e.g. pollution.
Explanation: Award one mark for an accurate explanation of what is meant by privatisation. Note: The question is about the impact on consumers. Do not reward answers that relate to the economy or the government.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not deflation would benefit the Montenegro economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: could make products more internationally competitive leading to greater exports reducing imports improving the current account balance / reducing a deficit on the current account; if caused by advances in technology / supply side measures e.g. higher labour productivity could increase output and employment and lead to economic growth / higher standard of living. Reasons why it might not: current inflation rate is only 0.4%; greater uncertainty could discourage consumer spending / investment by firms as households and firms could expect prices to fall further; if caused by a fall in total demand could reduce output and causing recession firms make losses / go out of business could cause more unemployment and unemployment is already high at 18%; could increase a budget deficit due to lower tax revenue from lower sales taxes / income taxes increase in unemployment benefits.
Explanation: Award one mark for an accurate explanation of what is meant by deflation e.g. a fall in the price level is sufficient here. Reward but do not expect explanation of benign and malign deflation. Allow reference to demand-side deflation but not demand-pull deflation or cost-push deflation as they are not acceptable terms.

Stimulus / Scenario: Mozambique storm damage, poverty and economic development, government intervention and tariffs
Question Text: Scenario about development and trade policy
Identify two reasons why a person may experience absolute poverty.
Markscheme: unemployment; sickness / mental health / poor health; old age; refugees; war / famine / natural disasters; lack of government benefits; low level of education / social skills; income too low to afford basic necessities.
Explanation: If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two causes of an increase in a country’s economic development.
Markscheme: Improvements in education improve production capacity can raise wages / reduce unemployment; Improvements in healthcare can increase life expectancy; Improvements in technology better quality goods and services; Increase in savings provide finance for investment; Increase in investment raise quality of output / economic growth; Increase in FDI can increase job opportunities; Increase in proportion of workers in the tertiary sector working conditions / pay may be better; Increase in incomes able to buy more goods and services; Increase in government expenditure on infrastructure improves labour mobility / access to resources; Increase in international trade / exports creates more jobs / income; Discovery of new resources e.g. oil improves production.
Explanation: One mark for each of two causes identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two causes are given, consider the first three. Note: the development mark might apply to several causes and are therefore interchangeable.

Question Text: Analyse, using a production possibility curve (PPC) diagram, the effect of a serious storm on an economy.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled with different outputs; Initial curve drawn as a curve/line sloping downward to the axes; New curve drawn as a curve/line sloping downward to the axes; Shift indicated by arrow or letter. Analysis: A serious storm will reduce resources / reduce availability of factors of production / destroy infrastructure reduce the ability to produce goods and services / reduce maximum capacity / reduce productive capacity.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 2 marks for coherent analysis. Note: both curves must touch both axes to get the marks. Note: Axes need labelling such as good X / good Y or capital goods and consumer goods. Labelling A and B is not sufficient.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not imposing tariffs on imports will increase a country’s output.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase price of imports which may reduce demand for imports; people may switch to buying domestically produced products; net exports may increase, raising total demand; the higher demand may encourage the country's firms to produce more goods and services; government could use tax revenue to subsidise domestic goods. Why it might not: even with tariffs, imports may still be cheaper than domestically produced products; there may not be domestically produced substitutes; if tariffs are put on raw materials and capital goods, costs of production could rise and demand may fall; other countries may retaliate, raising price of exports and reducing demand for the country's exports.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Note: level 1 would be knowledge and understanding of imports and tariffs or just identification of points.

Stimulus / Scenario: Saudi Arabia possible economic system changes, role of central bank and development of a car-free city
Question Text: Scenario about economic systems and demographics
Identify two types of economic system.
Markscheme: Market; mixed
Explanation: Also accept but do not expect: planned / command. No marks for free market system or controlled market system.

Question Text: Explain two functions of a central bank.
Markscheme: Operates monetary policy / controls inflation e.g. sets the rate of interest / manages the money supply; Carries out quantitative easing to stimulate economic activity during a financial crisis; Issues notes and coins often the only bank allowed to do so; Acts as bank to the government receives payments to the government and makes payments on behalf of the government / manages the national debt; Acts as lender of last resort lends to commercial banks in difficulties; Holds reserves of foreign currency to influence the exchange rate; May regulate banking system ensuring banks do not take too many risks.
Explanation: One mark for each of two functions identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two functions are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse the advantages to a country of having a young population.
Markscheme: A young population may: be more up to date with modern technology may have received more education / training / are more skillful be more productive increase output; add to the labour force increasing productive capacity greater tax revenue government can spend more e.g. to improve infrastructure; lead to lower government expenditure on pensions and health services for the elderly so can spend more on factors leading to economic growth e.g. education / infrastructure; be more geographically mobile e.g. have fewer family ties; be more occupationally mobile less attached to particular jobs / broader range of skills; spend more of their income than older people be more innovative able to start their own businesses increasing output / wealth; be physically fit / stronger less likely to be off sick; be a more skilled labour force will attract investment from abroad / FDI.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a ban on cars in a city will benefit the city’s households and firms.
Markscheme: Why it might: may reduce air and noise pollution; avoid traffic congestion; reduce staff absence through illness; avoid accidents; greater demand for public transport; greater employment in public transport; pedestrianised shopping centres may attract more customers; free up police and court time; raise quality of life; frees up funds to spend on better education / health / housing. Why it might not: journey times by e.g. bikes may take longer; people can enjoy driving; may reduce visitors to the area, lowering demand for shops; may lower house prices; car sales firms will move out of the area; reduces labour mobility.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Note: level 1 would be knowledge and understanding of terms such as ban, city, household or firms or just identification of points.

Stimulus / Scenario: Ecuador forms of money, technology affecting firms’ profits and government spending cuts
Question Text: Scenario about money, technology and unemployment
Identify two characteristics of money.
Markscheme: generally acceptable; portable; recognisable; divisible; durable; limited in supply / scarce; uniformity
Explanation: If more than two characteristics of money are given, consider the first three. Accept an explanation of a characteristic e.g. money is easy to carry rather than using the word portable.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a reduction in working hours may not cause public sector workers to move to the private sector.
Markscheme: Wages may still be higher in the public sector enabling workers to buy more goods and services; Fringe benefits may be better in the public sector e.g. more generous state pensions; Reduction in working hours results in more leisure time improving work life balance; Job security may be better in the public sector less worry about losing jobs / few job opportunities in the private sector; Skills are not transferable to the private sector e.g. occupational immobility; Working conditions may be better in the public sector e.g. better health and safety standards; Reduction in working hours may not result in less pay making working in the public sector more attractive / may benefit from more leisure time / reduction in stress at work.
Explanation: Also accept approach from the point of view of why workers would not want to work in the private sector. One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how advances in technology could affect firms’ profits.
Markscheme: Advances in technology: will improve the quality of capital goods this could raise productivity / efficiency higher output / lower costs of production increase profits; could raise the quality of products create new products increase demand / revenue; could lead to new products / innovation which may lead to lower demand for related products leading to loss of profits example e.g. development of electric cars has led to fall in demand for diesel cars; can enable online training of new skills improving productivity / speeding up training / increases number that can be trained; can improve internet and social media links able to contact more potential customers in other countries thereby increasing revenue / profit; due to the initial high cost of buying / developing advanced technological equipment can reduce profits in the short run; could lead to small firms being forced out of business due to the cost of new technology / and its maintenance reducing profits / making a loss.
Explanation: Note: Only reward lower cost of production once.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a decrease in government spending will increase unemployment.
Markscheme: Why it might: a cut in government spending could on education / training could reduce skills / qualifications and labour productivity; reduction in skills / qualifications could increase structural unemployment; cut in government spending on public sector workers' wages could reduce, their motivation, may resign; lower government spending on providing information about job vacancies could increase frictional unemployment; lower government spending could reduce total demand which could cause cyclical unemployment. Why it might not: may reduce inflation, making products more internationally competitive, raising net exports; cut in government spending on unemployment benefits may reduce frictional unemployment; may enable taxes to be cut which could increase consumer spending and investment, leaving total demand and unemployment unchanged; amount of government spending may fall but it may be spent more effectively e.g. training may be more directed at skills required to fill vacancies.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Note: Level 1 would be knowledge and understanding of unemployment and government spending or just identification of points.

Stimulus / Scenario: Paraguay inflation, exchange rate changes, emigration and government attempts to reduce market failure
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomics and migration
Define Consumer Prices Index.
Markscheme: A measure of inflation / cost of living using weights / basket of goods and services.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two ways a government could intervene to reduce market failure.
Markscheme: Subsidise merit goods (goods with positive externalities) to encourage consumption / production; Tax on demerit goods (goods with negative externalities) to discourage consumption / production; Apply maximum price on merit goods to increase consumption OR apply minimum price on demerit goods to reduce consumption; Finance / produce public goods as private sector firms will have no incentive to produce them; Regulate monopolies to restrict exploitation of market power; Provide education / training / public information campaign to raise awareness of benefits of a merit good / drawbacks of a demerit good; Provide education / training to increase labour mobility.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two ways of government intervention are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in a country’s foreign exchange rate could reduce inflation.
Markscheme: A rise in the exchange rate / appreciation of a currency can increase export prices lower import prices. Net exports may fall exports form part of total demand reducing demand-pull inflation. Lower import prices will increase pressure for firms to keep price rises low lower imported raw material prices reduce costs of production reduce cost-push inflation.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in a country’s economic growth rate will reduce emigration from the country.
Markscheme: Why it might: employment may increase; people will not have to seek jobs abroad; incomes may rise / poverty may decrease; tax revenue may be higher, allowing the government to spend more on e.g. education and healthcare. Why it might not: incomes may still be low; income can be unevenly distributed; may not expect rise in growth rate to last; there may be higher economic growth and job opportunities in other countries; pollution levels may increase; may lead to higher external costs e.g. pollution; economic growth comes at cost of worse working conditions; there are other reasons for emigrating.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Note: level 1 would be knowledge and understanding of economic growth and emigration or just identification of points.

Stimulus / Scenario: Denmark economy: high living standards, declining shipbuilding industry, growth of toy industry, government spending trends, international trade and increasing working from home
Question Text: Source material about economic changes in Denmark
Calculate the average wage paid in Denmark in 2020.
Explanation: Accept 84 000.

Question Text: Identify two possible opportunity costs of a Danish person using their leisure time to cycle.
Markscheme: Playing golf; jogging
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain one way that living standards are measured in Denmark.
Markscheme: Identification: HDI / average wages / income / GDP per head leisure hours. Explanation: HDI includes GDP per head / income / education / health/life expectancy / average wages / income / GDP per head influences the goods and services which can be purchased / leisure hours may affect physical / mental health.
Explanation: One mark for a way identified and one mark for an explanation. Do not accept GDP.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why the supply of ships is price-inelastic.
Markscheme: They take a long time to produce so supply cannot be adjusted quickly; They are expensive to store take up a large space / likely to be few or no spare ships to sell / low inventories / difficult to keep for future sales / if prices fall will have to sell ships as nowhere to store them.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Ignore comments on PED.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between government spending and GDP in Denmark.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Positive relationship; as government spending increases, GDP increases. Supporting evidence: 2015-2018 government spending and GDP rose / 2019-2021 government spending and GDP rose; Government spending and GDP rose over the period / government spending and GDP was lowest at the start of the period / 2015 and was highest at the end of the period 2021. Analysis of expected relationship: Higher government spending will increase total (aggregate) demand which will add to GDP; may be higher government spending on education/training/healthcare/subsidies which can raise productivity and output; higher GDP may increase tax revenue which can be used to increase government spending. Exception: 2018-2019 / 2018 / 2019 government spending rose but GDP fell. Analysis of exception: A government may spend more when GDP falls in a bid to stimulate an increase in GDP.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how an increase in the birth rate would affect the market for toys.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the right; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2. Written analysis: An increase in the birth rate would increase the number of children who parents may buy toys for / more consumers / higher demand the higher demand will raise price / increase quantity (traded).
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 1 mark for written analysis.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a surplus on the current account of its balance of payments would benefit the Danish economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: exports exceed imports / credit items have a higher value than debit items and so adds to total (aggregate) demand which may increase GDP it can increase employment which may increase incomes may attract foreign investment as may be taken to be a sign of a strong economy may raise the exchange rate which can enable exports to exchange for more imports. Reasons why it might not: it means more products are produced than consumed / reduces availability of products / reduces consumer choice reduce potential living standards may contribute to resource depletion it may add to inflationary pressure especially if the economy is close to full employment. demand for the currency will exceed the supply of the currency this may raise the exchange rate make exports less internationally competitive which can reduce the surplus / cause a deficit in the long run.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not more people working from home will benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: may raise productivity / efficiency / quality of output as workers may be less tired / less stressed / saves transport costs as a result of not having to travel to work may work longer hours increase GDP; may increase the quantity of labour more people e.g. those with dependents may be able to work / more flexible hours as save time travelling; workers may be happier and so may stay with firms longer which would reduce the cost of recruiting workers; pollution may be reduced traffic congestion may be reduced less need for transport infrastructure; reduce external costs improve health; firms' costs may fall as less need for office space; may reduce conflict between workers may reduce chances of industrial action. Reasons why it might not: transport firms may experience a fall in demand / revenue / less people travelling which may create unemployment of e.g. bus drivers; restaurants/cafes may have to close down in city centres which may create unemployment of e.g. waiters reduce tax revenue; may reduce productivity / efficiency / quality of output reduce GDP as workers may be distracted by e.g. small children / become lazy / may work fewer hours / may have poor internet connection may not be supervised may not co-ordinate with other workers / communication problems.
Explanation: Note 1 mark for recognising productivity may change. So, if a candidate writes productivity may increase and may decrease, award 1 mark. Two further marks could be gained if a candidate explains why productivity may increase and why it may decrease. Note: 1 mark for productivity increase or decrease and 1 mark for GDP increase or decrease.

Stimulus / Scenario: South Korea declining population and ageing population, government incentives for births and larger schools
Question Text: Scenario about population change and education
Identify two benefits people may gain from a good education.
Markscheme: high income / high living standards / less risk of poverty; skills / high productivity / high efficiency; qualifications; good job / good job opportunities / increased occupational mobility; wide range of interests; better health/better nutrition/avoidance of demerit goods; may gain entrepreneurial skills
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two reasons for a change in the amount of tax revenue a government receives.
Markscheme: Change in GDP / economic growth will affect revenue from direct taxes / move into different tax brackets; Change in employment / wages / incomes will affect (personal) income tax; Change in profits will affect corporate (income) tax; Change in spending will affect revenue from indirect taxes / VAT / GST; Change in demand for imports affect revenue from import tariffs / import duty; Change in tax rates e.g. higher sales tax rates on products with inelastic demand may increase tax revenue; Change in tax avoidance / tax evasion as a result of e.g. campaigns / greater penalties; Change in population size will affect number of people paying taxes; Inflation fiscal drag; Change in what is taxed the more products taxed, the more revenue might be raised.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the economies of scale a school may gain from an increase in its size.
Markscheme: Economies of scale reduce average cost of production / lower long run costs. Purchasing / buying economies buying e.g. desks / computers in bulk at a discount. Management economies employing e.g. school accountant. Labour economies teachers specialising e.g. in teaching biology. Technical economies e.g. a film studio for media studies. Financial economies a school may be able to borrow at a lower rate of interest find it easier to get a loan. Marketing / selling economies a private sector school may be able to advertise more cheaply.
Explanation: Up to 3 marks for one type of economy of scale that is well-developed. Allow one mark for internal economies of scale.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not having a high proportion of a country’s population over the age of 64 is a benefit to its economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: may still be in work and may be efficient due to experience; may provide family support and care; may be the result of a rise in life expectancy which may mean living standards have risen; may be a smaller proportion of young dependents. Why it might not: may be a high proportion of older dependents; may be high healthcare costs; may be a high cost of state pensions; older workers may be out of date with new technology / may be less innovative.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Monaco firms experiencing external growth, high wages, fiscal policy and VAT on fruit
Question Text: Scenario about firms and fiscal policy
Define external growth of firms.
Markscheme: Increase in size / output of a firm / expansion of a firm due to a merger / takeover.
Explanation: Accept but do not expect reference to acquisition as merger.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why low-skilled workers may be highly paid.
