Markscheme: High demand / inelastic demand due to e.g. expansion of the industry / economy; High cost of capital / relative cost of factors of production may encourage use of labour-intensive production; Low supply due to e.g. emigration / high employment / unsociable hours; Minimum wage set at a high level / government policy may be to raise wages of low-skilled workers; The job may be dangerous / unpleasant / poor working conditions so a high wage is needed to attract workers / retain workers / example; The workers may be in a trade union with strong/collective bargaining power / may take industrial action; May work long hours so total pay is high; May work in a very profitable/high value-added industry example.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Award 2 marks for high national minimum wage.

Question Text: Analyse how fiscal policy can increase employment.
Markscheme: Rise in government spending / reduction in taxation expansionary fiscal policy may increase total (aggregate) demand which can encourage firms to expand reduce cyclical unemployment. Increase in government spending on education / training / health care may increase skills / productivity of workers which may increase their mobility / attractiveness to employers. Increase in government spending on infrastructure can attract MNCs / attract FDI. Increase government spending on housing which can increase (geographical) mobility of labour. Increase subsidies which can encourage firms to expand and take on more workers. Cut in (personal) income tax cut in unemployment benefit increase disposable income may increase the incentive to work. Cut in indirect taxes which can encourage an increase in consumer spending which can encourage firms to expand and take on more workers / reduce costs of production. Cut in corporate (income) tax may give firms the funds / incentive to expand. Higher import tariffs may encourage domestic firms to expand.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should provide free fruit to consumers.
Markscheme: Why it should: fruit is a merit good; people may not appreciate its private benefits; social benefit of consuming it exceeds the private benefit; without government intervention, it may be under-consumed; may increase the health of the population and so may increase workers' productivity; may benefit fruit farmers; would enable the poor to consume it. Why it should not: rich can afford to buy it; may result in it being overconsumed; some may be wasted; may lose tax revenue if indirect tax imposed on food; opportunity cost – government spending on e.g. education may have to be reduced.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Suez Canal blockage affecting international trade, shortages and price elasticity differences between luxury chocolate and salt
Question Text: Scenario about supply disruption and PED
Define a shortage.
Markscheme: Demand exceeding supply.
Explanation: Too much demand / not enough supply / supply is low and demand is high (1).

Question Text: Explain two ways a firm can increase its profit.
Markscheme: Becoming more capital-intensive which may reduce cost of production; Buying cheaper raw materials which may reduce cost of production; Increasing efficiency / raising quality of product by e.g. employing skilled workers; Increase training of workers to raise productivity; Merging enabling greater advantage to be taken of economies of scale; Advertising / improved packaging / adding more features to increase demand / raise revenue; Gaining greater market power enabling price to rise and revenue to increase / making demand inelastic; Raise revenue by reducing price if demand is elastic / by raising price if demand is inelastic.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Note: in each case, reduce cost of production, raise demand, increase revenue can only be rewarded once. Nothing for change in price without relative reference to PED.

Question Text: Analyse the reasons why the price elasticity of demand for one brand of luxury chocolates is likely to be different from that of salt.
Markscheme: One brand of chocolates is likely to have substitutes / other brands to switch to; is narrowly defined; it is not a necessity / is a want; the purchase can be postponed; it takes up a large proportion of income; demand is likely to be price elastic; a rise in price will cause a greater percentage change in quantity demand. Salt does not have substitutes; is widely defined; it takes up a small proportion of income / is likely to be cheap (so price change less noticeable); people become addicted to the taste; may be seen as a necessity; demand is likely to be price inelastic; a rise in price will cause a smaller percentage change in quantity demanded.
Explanation: Demand for a luxury brand of chocolate may be inelastic if consumers have brand loyalty reinforced by advertising.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a country should import most of the food it consumes.
Markscheme: Why it should: may lack appropriate resources to produce food; will allow the country to produce other goods and services; value added may be greater in the secondary and tertiary sectors; wages may be higher in the secondary and tertiary sectors; working conditions may be better in the secondary and tertiary sectors; the output of food can be affected by changes in weather and natural disasters. Why it should not: may threaten food security; may reduce employment in agriculture; may reduce incomes of farmers; may increase imports and worsen the current account; may be more expensive due to transport costs; may be less fresh; may be less healthy if more processed; foreign suppliers may not be reliable.
Explanation: Reward but do not expect reference to comparative advantage or absolute advantage. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: New Zealand macroeconomic policy including cigarette bans and oil exploration
Question Text: Scenario about government policy and exports
Define unemployment rate.
Markscheme: The number of unemployed divided by the labour/work force (multiplied by 100) / the percentage of the labour force without a job.
Explanation: Those willing and able to work who cannot find employment (1).

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a government may discourage cigarette smoking.
Markscheme: Improve health of those who give up smoking / smoking harms the health of smokers reduce consumption of a demerit good / cigarettes are a demerit good cigarettes are overconsumed / overproduced; Increase health of the population / reduce the health damage caused to non-smokers reduce passive smoking / reduce air pollution; Reduce market failure reduce external cost / reduce the gap between social costs and social benefits; Reduce health care costs more could be spent on e.g. education; Increase productivity / stop fall in productivity raise income/GDP / stop fall in income / GDP.
Explanation: There is a degree of mix and match on this question. Most of the points may be interpreted as either identification or explanation. Essentially a question on why a government may discourage smoking.

Question Text: Analyse why a country’s exports may decrease.
Markscheme: Price of exports may rise / inflation / become less price competitive due to a rise in costs of production a rise in the exchange rate demand may fall; Quality of exports may fall workers may be less skilled investment may have decreased; Incomes abroad may have declined reducing the purchasing power of foreign buyers reducing foreign countries' need for capital goods and raw materials; Demand in the home country may increase encouraging firms to sell more of their output at home; Trade restrictions may be imposed e.g. tariffs / quotas / subsidies / embargoes may be imposed by other countries the government of the country itself may restrict, tax or ban exports to stop depletion of resources / prevent shortage / rise in price on domestic market; May be disruptions to production e.g. bad weather/war/pandemic decrease in resources; Government subsidies to exporting may be stopped government may switch to subsidising products sold on the home market.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the discovery of oil in a country will benefit its economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase GDP; may increase employment; may increase incomes; may increase tax revenue; may increase exports; reduce imports of oil. Why it might not: may create pollution; may damage wildlife habitats/areas of natural beauty; may raise the exchange rate; oil may not be in demand – replaced by renewable energy; working conditions may be poor; extraction may last for a short time – may deplete quickly; MNC may extract the oil and not many of the benefits may go to the domestic economy.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Nicaragua economy: large agricultural sector with coffee exports, income inequality, low GDP per head, growth of coffee shops and small firms
Question Text: Source material about Nicaragua’s economic structure and coffee industry
Calculate the price elasticity of demand for coffee.
Explanation: Accept 0.3

Question Text: Identify two rewards to factors of production in Nicaragua.
Markscheme: Wages; profits
Explanation: Only accept these answers if within the first three listed.

Question Text: Explain one way in which unemployment is measured.
Markscheme: Claimant count based on workers claiming unemployment benefits. OR Labour force survey asking people if they are out of work and looking for employment.
Explanation: One mark for a way identified and one mark for an explanation.

Question Text: Explain two functions of money in Nicaragua.
Markscheme: Medium of exchange used to buy and sell products / used to trade; Store of value used to save / keeps its value over a period of time.
Explanation: One mark for each of two functions identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Only accept these answers if within the first three listed.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between the percentage of the labour force employed in agriculture and GDP per head.
Markscheme: Relationship: Inverse or negative relationship / move in opposite directions. Countries with a high percentage of their labour force employed in agriculture tend to have a low GDP per head. Supporting evidence: The four countries with the lowest % of the labour force employed in agriculture had the highest GDP per head; Specific reference to one or more countries e.g. Bahamas had the lowest % employed in agriculture and the highest GDP per head; Use of GDP per head data to show comparison between country with high employment in agriculture and one with low percentage employment in agriculture. Analysis of expected relationship: Wages may be low in agriculture; wages / value added tends to be higher in the secondary and / or tertiary sector. Exception: Nicaragua / Dominica; Nicaragua has the second highest % of labour force employed in agriculture but the lowest GDP per head / Dominica had the highest % of labour force employed in agriculture but the second to lowest GDP. Analysis of exception: Explanation of another influence on GDP per head.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Note the four are: Bahamas, Barbados, Costa Rica, Saint Lucia.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how a drought could affect the market for coffee.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled - price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New supply curve shifted to the left; Equilibriums - shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2. Written analysis: A drought will lead to a shortage / will increase costs of production. OR The lower supply will push up price / reduce quantity (traded).
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 1 mark for written analysis.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not small firms benefit Nicaraguan consumers.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: know customers' requirements provide a personal service / see them often may be flexible; may be many small firms / competitive markets prices may be low quality may be high may offer wider choice collectively than a few large firms; may provide products for which there is only a low demand niche market; makes goods more accessible in rural areas. Reasons why they might not: may not be able to take advantage of economies of scale examples higher average cost of production higher price; small firms may have lower profits may limit investment / funds available fail to raise quality; may limit choice individual small firms may have a limited range of products cannot provide all that consumers may want.
Explanation: Only reward reference to quality or prices once.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not Nicaragua should devote more of its resources to coffee production.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should: global demand for coffee is increasing demand for coffee is price inelastic revenue / profits may increase more tax revenue for government; firms grow in size greater specialisation / economies of scale may reduce average costs raise quality increase profits more competitive; exports may increase improve the current account of its balance of payments lead to economic growth/higher standard of living; may lead to more job opportunities less poverty / less government payments to unemployed; already twelfth biggest world producer growth will give them more monopoly power more power to raise price of coffee. Reasons why it should not: other countries produce coffee these may be more competitive; output may be affected by changes in weather; natural disasters overproduction can exhaust land leading to lower yields in future; opportunity cost resources could be used to produce other products example coffee workers only receive low pay; world market for coffee may fall / demand is unpredictable resulting in surplus capacity / wasted resources; more dependent on other countries for imports of other (agricultural) products; firms may suffer from diseconomies of scale reduce profits.
Explanation: Do not expect, but reward reference to absolute or comparative advantage.

Stimulus / Scenario: Bangladesh labour mobility, gender wage differences, central bank approving new banks and strong GDP growth
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and economic growth
Identify two influences on the mobility of labour.
Markscheme: age; skills / training; qualifications / level of education; price / availability of housing; cost of living / level of income; family ties; information on vacancies; disabilities / access; quality / cost of transport; distance to place of work; discrimination
Explanation: Only accept these answers if within the first three influences mentioned.

Question Text: Explain two benefits that consumers may gain from having more commercial banks.
Markscheme: More choice / competition greater range of services / better quality of services / lower bank charges; Lower interest rate charged on loans / higher interest paid on savings due to higher competition; May be easier to obtain a loan which may increase the products consumes can buy; May be more convenient closer to home / less time travelling / more likely to be able to speak to a person.
Explanation: One mark for each of two benefits identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse why women may be paid less than men.
Markscheme: Women may be less skilled have fewer qualifications and so be less productive if they have received less education / training may have less bargaining strength than men; Fewer women may be in promoted posts if they have taken time off to raise children / maternity leave / may have less experience; Women may be concentrated in low paying occupations / industries example e.g. clothes manufacture may only want part-time jobs because of family commitments; Discrimination some employers may underestimate / undervalue the productivity of women there may be no law or an ineffective law against discrimination example e.g. tv presenters / football; Lower demand / higher supply in some occupations due to lower revenue / value of product.
Explanation: Allow the counter argument on why men may be paid more than women.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not people living in countries with a high GDP enjoy higher living standards than people in countries with a low GDP.
Markscheme: Why they might: may be able to afford better standard of housing; may be able to afford better nutrition and healthcare which can increase life expectancy; higher incomes enable more and greater quality of products to be purchased; may be able to afford better education which may enable them to gain more interesting jobs with better working conditions. Why they might not: high GDP does not necessarily mean that GDP per head is high and so incomes could be low; income may be unevenly distributed, and some people may be living in poverty; may have a large informal sector so GDP under-records income; countries with higher GDP may also have higher price levels; people may work long hours and so enjoy little leisure and may be stressed; there may be pollution and traffic congestion; people's wants for goods and services may grow at a faster rate than their income – the more people have, the more they may want.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Indonesia mixed economic system, population growth and central bank aiming for stable prices
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomic stability and growth
Identify who allocates resources in a mixed economic system.
Markscheme: Firms / producers the government OR The private sector / private firms the public sector / nationalised firms
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two benefits a firm may gain from an increase in the size of the country’s population.
Markscheme: Higher demand / more consumers / larger market which may increase revenue / profits; May increase ability to take advantage of economies of scale lower average cost of production; May be higher supply of labour may make it easier to employ workers / reduce wages can increase output / supply.
Explanation: One mark for each of two benefits identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the reasons why governments aim for stable prices.
Markscheme: Stable prices give consumers confidence to buy prices remain affordable / knowing they can afford it; Stable prices attract MNCs leads to greater employment government / firms more able to plan use of resources increases investment and economic growth; To avoid inflation which can discourage investment due to less certainty could lead to lower wage demands can make exports more / imports less price competitive which may cause a current account deficit on the balance of payments; Inflation can cause a random redistribution of income lenders / savers may lose borrowers may gain; Inflation can cause menu costs e.g. the cost of changing prices and may cause shoe leather costs e.g. cost of shopping around for lowest prices; To avoid deflation which can result in a downward spiral of demand and prices consumers and firms may delay their purchases this can result in a rise in unemployment and a recession.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the global economy would benefit from an increase in air travel.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase employment in the air travel industry; may increase employment and output in related industries e.g. tourism; may reduce firms' costs of production if quicker than other forms of transport; may increase mobility of labour; air travel is a safer form of travel than e.g. road travel; encourages tourism and economic development of some countries. Why it might not: may cause noise and air pollution (carbon footprint); may contribute to climate change; may need to build new airports / extend existing airports which may reduce land available for e.g. housing; may reduce demand for other forms of transport; unemployment may increase in e.g. sea travel; high costs prevent movement of heavy goods.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: US healthcare sector, mergers between large firms and multinational healthcare companies operating in host countries
Question Text: Scenario about firms, mergers and multinational companies
Define tertiary sector using an example apart from healthcare.
Markscheme: The sector covering the provision of services. Example e.g. banking / car showroom / coffee shop / education.
Explanation: Only accept the first example given.

Question Text: Explain the difference between a private benefit and an external benefit of healthcare.
Markscheme: A private benefit is enjoyed by the person consuming the product e.g. a person may enjoy better health / longer life expectancy. OR A provider of healthcare e.g. hospital and revenue / profits made. An external benefit is a benefit enjoyed by a third party / someone not directly involved in the consumption or production of the product e.g. less risk of catching a virus when other people are vaccinated / higher output.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse why a government may prevent a horizontal merger.
Markscheme: A horizontal merger is a merger between two firms in the same industry and same stage of production. A government may prevent a merger if it thinks it will give the new firm monopoly power and result in market failure. Less competition may result in higher prices lower quality there may be a substantial loss of jobs through rationalisation. A government may be concerned that the new firm may be too large may experience diseconomies of scale example may become less internationally competitive lower exports. A government may want to avoid a reduction the number of suppliers resulting in less choice for consumers / less innovation.
Explanation: Note: need both same industry and same stage of production to get the mark for horizontal merger.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a foreign multinational company (MNC) will continue to produce in a host country for many years.
Markscheme: Why it might: may earn a high revenue / profit due to high demand; may experience low costs due to low wages / low raw material costs in the country; may have access to high quality labour due to good education and training in the country; may be subsidised by the host country's government. Why it might not: natural resources it was extracting may run out; tax in the host country may increase; a rise in the host country's foreign exchange rate may increase the price of its exports; there may be industrial action undertaken by trade unions in the host country; more profitable opportunities may occur in other countries; may be changes in regulations.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Vietnam economy with rising output, exports and imports and low unemployment
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomic performance and fiscal policy
Identify how the effect of a fall in the price of gold would be shown on a demand curve for gold.
Markscheme: A movement down the demand curve from left to right / extension. A movement along the demand curve.
Explanation: Accept, as an alternative, a diagram showing a movement down the demand curve. Allow one mark for the idea that demand will rise.

Question Text: Explain two disadvantages of a recession.
Markscheme: Lower output / lower GDP lower living standards / higher poverty / less income / less consumer spending; Higher unemployment / lower employment higher cost of unemployment benefit; Lower investment due to reduction in confidence; Firms shutdown due to lower profits; Lower tax revenue may cause budget deficit / reduce ability to spend e.g. on education / increase in national debt.
Explanation: One mark for each of two disadvantages identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse why a country may demand more imports.
Markscheme: Price of imports may fall due to a fall in costs of production a rise in the exchange rate; Quality of imports may rise foreign workers may be less skilled investment in foreign countries may have decreased; Incomes may have increased raising the purchasing power of buyers increasing the country's' need for consumer goods which the country cannot produce due to specialisation; Trade restrictions may be removed or reduced e.g. lower tariffs / quotas / subsidies / embargoes will make it easier to import; Country needs more raw materials / lacks the technology to produce certain goods to enable growth of domestic industries; Failure of harvest /natural disaster e.g. rice means need to import the good instead.
Explanation: Do not expect, but reward reference to absolute or comparative advantage. A maximum of three marks for only identifying the reasons.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not fiscal policy can achieve full employment.
Markscheme: Why it might: higher government spending and/or lower taxation may increase total (aggregate) demand which could reduce cyclical unemployment; government spending on education and training could raise workers' skills could increase their mobility and may reduce frictional unemployment; government spending on the provision of labour market information may reduce frictional unemployment; government spending on subsidies may lead to firms increasing job opportunities. Why it might not: government spending may not be raised enough / a cut in taxation may not be sufficient; consumer spending and investment may fall if there is a lack of confidence; net exports may fall if there is a global recession; there may still be some labour immobility due to e.g. differences in house prices in different parts of the country so structural unemployment; increased government spending on benefits may be an encouragement not to work and stay unemployed.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Do not reward a simple reversal of policies in why it might achieve full employment in the why it might not part of the response.

Stimulus / Scenario: Tunisia economy: government budget deficit, tax reforms, price controls on flour and milk, electricity subsidy, wage changes, inflation and unemployment policies
Question Text: Source material about economic challenges facing the Tunisian government
Calculate Tunisia’s balance on the current account of its balance of payments.
Explanation: Accept –2.4bn. Also accept –6.8 or –6.9 billion TND.

Question Text: Identify two qualities of a good tax that the Tunisian government aimed to achieve.
Markscheme: Efficiency / efficient / the tax should improve market performance / reduce market failure; Economical / economy / the tax should raise more in revenue than it costs to collect it.
Explanation: If more than 2 qualities are given, consider the first 3.

Question Text: Explain one reason why the Tunisian government regulates the price of flour and milk.
Markscheme: Reduce poverty flour and milk are basic necessities / essential products / people may be able to buy enough basic necessities / make them affordable / keep price relatively low. OR Prevent monopoly firms exploiting their market power stop the firms raising price to a high level.
Explanation: One mark for a reason identified and one mark for an explanation.

Question Text: Explain two ways the Tunisian government tried to reduce frictional unemployment.
Markscheme: Increased the labour market information available to workers and employers so workers would be more aware of job vacancies / skills and qualifications required / employers more aware of those seeking jobs / may enable workers to move more quickly/easily between jobs / reduce search time / increase mobility of workers; Did not raise unemployment benefit (in line with inflation) so, the purchasing power of unemployment benefit would fall / increase the incentive to work.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than 2 ways are given, consider the first 3.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between the change in Tunisia’s average wage and inflation rate.
Markscheme: Relationship (up to 2 marks): Direct relationship / positive relationship as change in the average wage increases so does the inflation rate / if change in average wage / rise in average wage falls so does the inflation rate / both fall and rose together / same trend. Evidence (up to 3 marks): 2016 – 2017 / 2018 both the average wage / change in average wage and inflation rate rose; Between 2014 – 2018, the average wage rose more rapidly than the inflation rate real wages would have increased between 2014 – 2018; Between 2018 – 2020 / in 2019 – 2020, the inflation rate was higher than the rise in the average wage real wages would have fallen; Between 2014 – 2015/2016 / 2018 – 2019/2020 both the change in average wage and inflation rate fell; 2018 had the highest rise in the average wage and the highest inflation; 2016 had the lowest increase in the inflation rate but not the lowest increase in the average wage / 2020 had the lowest increase in the average wage but not the lowest inflation rate; Over the whole period the inflation rate rose while the increase in the average wage fell. Explanation (up to 2 marks): A rise in the average wage may increase costs of production / cause cost push inflation / a higher inflation rate will encourage workers to press for a wage rise; A rise in the average wage may increase disposable income / consumer expenditure / total demand / cause demand-pull inflation.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Note: average wage did not fall.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how an increase in wage costs would affect the market for shoes.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New supply curve shifted to the left; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2. Written analysis: An increase in wage costs would increase costs of production which may raise price.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 1 mark for written analysis. Note: higher price needs to be linked to higher costs of production/higher costs/higher wage costs.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the Tunisian government should continue to subsidise electricity production.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: reduce costs of producing electricity increase supply of electricity lower price of electricity make electricity more affordable / reduce poverty; lower costs of firms that use electricity which may encourage them to expand / attract MNCs / increase output / increase GDP / cause economic growth; increase employment / lower unemployment; reduce inflation / firms lower prices make Tunisia's products more internationally competitive / increase exports / lower imports / improve the current account balance; may reduce pollution / external costs if subsidising green sources of energy. Reasons why it might not: electricity firms may rely on the subsidy may not keep costs low / become inefficient may not pass on the subsidy in the form of lower price; opportunity cost government could spend money on e.g. healthcare; may result in a budget deficit / increase in taxes; may cause pollution / external costs if e.g. coal powered / deplete non-renewable resources.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a rise in the value of the Tunisian dinar would benefit the Tunisian economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: may reduce inflation imports would be cheaper; enable households to buy more imports / higher purchasing power more choice may lower price of some raw materials and/or capital goods increase competitive pressure on domestic firms lower costs of production; lower total (aggregate) demand which may reduce demand-pull inflation; may be taken as an indicator of a strong economy / increase confidence encourage investment / attract MNCs; if demand for exports is price inelastic, export revenue may rise. Reasons why it might not: higher export prices / may reduce exports / export revenue lower price of imports may increase imports / import expenditure reduce international competitiveness increase the deficit / reduce surplus on the current account of Tunisia's balance of payments; lower net exports may reduce GDP / economic growth which may increase unemployment / reduce employment; may discourage MNCs / investment because of higher cost of setting up in the country.
Explanation: Note: lower total demand may be credited on either side but only once. Note: the generic advice above in terms of MNCs. One mark for attract or discourage MNCs. Another mark could be gained by explaining why they may be attracted and another mark for explaining why they may be discouraged.

Stimulus / Scenario: Mexico wages falling, poverty rising, firm closures and severe traffic congestion
Question Text: Scenario about poverty, firms and urban transport
Identify two possible opportunity costs of producing primary sector products.
Markscheme: Secondary sector products / capital goods / manufactured goods; tertiary sector products / services; Wildlife habitats/quality of the ecosystem
Explanation: If more than 2 are given, consider the first 3.

Question Text: Explain two policy measures which may reduce poverty.
Markscheme: Introduce / increase minimum wage help low-paid workers / raise income of low-paid workers; Provide state benefits e.g. unemployment benefit can enable those who have lost their jobs to purchase basic necessities; Reduce indirect taxation tax on goods and services tends to fall more heavily on the poor; Provide education / training increase earning potential / income / employment opportunities; Job creation schemes / increase jobs in the public sector e.g. expanding state-owned firms / increase employment / raise incomes / Subsidising private sector firms lower prices; Reduction in interest rate / expansionary monetary policy encouraging firms to expand / take on more workers; Increase in government spending increase employment opportunities; Reduce direct taxation / expansionary fiscal policy which may encourage firms to expand and take on more workers / increase disposable income (of the poor); Progressive taxes reduce relative poverty; Provide job information reduce frictional unemployment; Introduce a maximum price to make necessities more affordable.
Explanation: One mark for each of two policy measures identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than 2 policy measures are given, consider the first 3. Explanation marks e.g. reduce unemployment can be given without identification of policy measure as a candidate may write e.g. use a policy measure that will reduce unemployment.

Question Text: Analyse how the closure of firms may harm consumers.
Markscheme: May reduce competition increase monopoly power / market share may result in higher prices may not innovate / spend on R&D / become inefficient / complacent lower quality slower to respond to changes in consumer demand. May mean that consumers have to travel further to purchase products may take time to find alternatives there may be a reduction in the range of products available / less choice / less variety lower quantity / it may not be possible to buy some products. May reduce consumer confidence lowering quality of life / reduce consumer welfare / living standards. May reduce external economies of scale example raise average cost which may raise price.
Explanation: Focus should be on consumers. One mark in total for higher price.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should encourage people to walk to school and work.
Markscheme: Why it might: may make people healthier; may raise productivity; may reduce healthcare costs; may reduce pollution and traffic congestion; may be less need for car parking space. Why it might not: may not be safe; may make people late; may increase stress and lower productivity; may reduce demand for bus and train use; may cause some bus and train drivers to lose their jobs; may restrict job choice due to distance from home.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: South America education losses during pandemic, minimum wage and macroeconomic conditions
Question Text: Scenario about education, wages and macroeconomics
Identify the difference between microeconomics and macroeconomics.
Markscheme: Microeconomics is concerned with individual markets / individual decisions / individual firms / households; Macroeconomics is concerned with the whole economy.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain how less education can reduce a person’s lifetime earnings.
Markscheme: May reduce qualifications lower mobility reduce chance of gaining employment / increase chance of experiencing unemployment may lack information on job vacancies may be more likely to work in the primary sector / do manual work; May reduce skills / adult literacy lower productivity reduce chance of gaining promotion; May have less bargaining power / less aware or less able to enforce labour market rights e.g. to a minimum wage as easier to replace lower demand for them / higher supply; May reduce understanding about the benefits of good nutrition / good healthcare experience more time off work / shorter life expectancy; May have large families be out of the labour force for some time; May lack financial awareness / lower savings reduce ability to buy equipment e.g. a computer that could increase earnings.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse how the introduction of a minimum wage could affect unemployment.
Markscheme: A minimum wage is the lowest amount that an employer can pay a worker / price (wage) floor. Its introduction may result in the supply of labour exceeding demand for labour / surplus of labour resulting in higher unemployment. Firms may have to pay higher wages / wages may increase may want to cut costs of production / higher wages may increase their costs of production this may reduce their profits reduce output they may fire some of their workers / increase unemployment replace workers with machines / capital goods. Higher pay may increase productivity increase total demand which may encourage firms to increase output take on more workers / increase employment reduce cyclical unemployment. May increase the incentive to work reduce frictional unemployment. If the minimum wage is set below the equilibrium level it will have no effect.
Explanation: One mark in total for increase unemployment and / or increase employment. One mark for reduce output and / or increase output. Supply exceeding demand for labour / surplus supply of labour may be shown on a diagram. Award but do not expect a reference to lower real wage unemployment.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an economy with a high inflation rate will have a low economic growth rate.
Markscheme: Why it might: may make the country's products less internationally competitive; lower exports and increase imports; reduce total demand; may discourage investment; if caused by increases in costs of production (cost-push inflation may reduce output); hyperinflation may result in a collapse in the economic system. Why it might not: may be caused by increases in total demand / demand-pull inflation which may encourage higher output / GDP; may increase tax revenue which could be spent on e.g. education which could increase the economic growth rate in the long run; may reduce the real cost of borrowing which may increase consumer spending and investment; may be lower than rival countries' inflation rates.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Tajikistan economy, workers abroad, possible recession and firms investing in capital equipment
Question Text: Scenario about recession, migration and investment
Identify two reasons why tax revenue is likely to fall during a recession.
Markscheme: lower spending / lower demand; lower incomes / lower wages / higher unemployment / lower employment; lower profits; lower tax rates; lower imports; fewer tax payers; lower output; lower revenue
Explanation: If more than 2 reasons are given, consider the first 3.

Question Text: Explain two characteristics of money.
Markscheme: Generally acceptable people willing to accept it as a payment / reward / in settlement of a debt; Portable easy to carry; Recognisable easy to see it is the country's currency; Durable will last some time / can be saved; Limited in supply so maintains value; Divisible there should be units of different value; Homogeneous / uniform people not preferring e.g. one note of a certain value other another note of the same value.
Explanation: One mark for each of two characteristics identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than 2 characteristics are given, consider the first 3. Accepting 'breakable' for 'divisible'. Explanation marks e.g. maintain value or accept as a payment can be given without identification up to a maximum of 2 marks.

Question Text: Analyse the advantages an economy may gain from having some of its people working in other countries.
Markscheme: The workers may send money home to (support) their families staying at home reduce poverty / raise living standards; Will improve the current account position of the balance of payments / bring in foreign currency secondary income; The workers may receive training gain skills ideas / knowledge / awareness of new technology experience higher productivity / efficiency which they can bring back to the country increase quality of output; Start their own firms in the country when they return increase output / economic growth / increase GDP; May reduce unemployment if lack of jobs at home and so may reduce government spending on unemployment benefits / may reduce government spending on dependents.
Explanation: No marks for general benefits of emigration.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not consumers would benefit from firms buying new capital equipment.
Markscheme: Why they might: may embody new technology; more innovation / new products; may increase efficiency / lower costs of production; may lower prices; may reduce mistakes; increase quality of output; may increase output and so availability. Why they might not: may raise prices in the short run to cover the cost of the purchase; may reduce quality / output in the short run as workers get used to the new capital equipment; firms may switch from producing consumer to capital goods; products may become more standardised.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Poland consumption of free and economic goods and rising demand for goods and housing
Question Text: Scenario about demand and government spending
Identify two free goods.
Markscheme: air / oxygen; sunlight; water; wind
Explanation: BOD on 'sun', trees (not wood), wild flowers, wild fruit. Not accepting fish or stone.

Question Text: Explain two influences on demand for factors of production.
Markscheme: Demand for the product produced / total demand / economic activity rise in demand for the product/products will be likely to increase demand for the factor/factors of production / derived demand; Price of the factor itself lower price will be likely to result in higher demand; Price of substitute factors of production e.g. may demand more labour if price of capital rises; Price of complementary factors of production e.g. if labour is used with capital, a fall in the price of capital may increase demand for labour; Productivity higher productivity will be likely to increase demand; Quality / advances in technology / changes in standards of education and training higher quality will increase demand; Occupational mobility the more occupationally mobile, the higher demand will tend to be; Method of production switch from labour-intensive production will increase demand for capital goods.
Explanation: One mark for each of two influences identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than 2 influences are given, consider the first 3. Accept price and / or demand on its own but looking for price of a complement or price of a substitute not just complement or substitute.

Question Text: Analyse the effect of an increase in demand for football shirts on demand for football shorts and demand for basketball shirts.
Markscheme: Demand for football shorts is likely to increase football shirts and shorts are complements bought to be used together / bought to complete the set. Demand for basketball shirts is likely to fall basketball shirts and football shirts may be substitutes consumers may switch between them.
Explanation: Changes in demand may be shown in the written answer or on diagrams.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in government spending on housing would benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase total demand; may increase output / GDP; may increase employment / reduce unemployment; may lower the price of housing; may increase living standards; may reduce homelessness; may reduce poverty. Why it might not: will have an opportunity cost; may increase taxes; quality of housing may be low; private sector may provide better housing; may be external costs during the construction.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Indonesia climate change: flooding in Jakarta, migration, farmland loss, environmental spending and relocation of capital city to Nusantara
Question Text: Source material about climate change and economic policy in Indonesia
Calculate the percentage contribution of Jakarta to Indonesia’s GDP in 2021.
Explanation: Accept without the percentage sign.

Question Text: Identify two examples of external costs ignored by the free market.
Markscheme: air pollution; rising sea levels/floods; disease / mosquitoes; climate change
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain how real GDP per head can be an indicator of living standards.
Markscheme: Real GDP per head has been adjusted for population / inflation / takes account of the cost of living. Low real GDP per head leads to a fall in living standards because lower GDP per head would mean lower income / real GDP per head is average income reducing consumption / material wellbeing / influencing how much people can spend / whether they can buy basic necessities.
Explanation: Accept answers based on either higher or lower GDP per head.

Question Text: Explain two ways climate change can lead to a fall in the Human Development Index (HDI) of Indonesia.
Markscheme: Schools have been flooded reducing education levels / mean years of schooling / expected years of schooling; Floods have led to an increase in diseases (transmitted through water and mosquitoes which breed in water) reducing health level / life expectancy / increasing death rate; Farmland has been submerged, (cutting off a vital source of income of many farmers) / roads have been destroyed reducing income levels / GNI per capita / GDP per capita / living standards.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Accept an explanation combining two different ways, e.g. increase in diseases reducing education levels.

Question Text: Draw a production possibility curve (PPC) diagram to show the impact of climate change on an economy such as Indonesia.
Markscheme: Axes correctly with different outputs; Initial curve drawn as a curve/line sloping downward to the axes; New curve drawn as a curve/line sloping downward to the axes; Shift to the left indicated by arrow(s) or letters or numbers e.g. PPC1 to PPC2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP and government spending on the environment.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: direct relationship / move in the same direction higher GDP may lead to higher government spending on the environment / higher government spending on the environment may increase GDP. Supporting evidence: Japan has highest GDP and highest spending on environment; France has second highest GDP and second highest spending on environment; Thailand has lowest GDP and lowest spending on environment. Analysis: Higher GDP may create more pollution, therefore countries have more to spend on environment / high government spending on the environment can reduce pollution/climate change. Higher tax revenue / more education and so more concern for the environment / a cleaner environment can raise productivity. Higher GDP therefore more developed and can spend more on other objectives such as protection of the environment. Exception: Switzerland/Indonesia Switzerland has lower GDP than Indonesia but higher expenditure on environment/Indonesia has higher GDP and lower expenditure on environment than Switzerland. Analysis of the exception: These countries may be facing different pressures on government spending e.g. higher level of poverty.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not internal migration benefits an economy such as Indonesia.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: higher production of different crops more income / higher living standards (for farmers) less pressure on government resources / natural resources e.g., less overcrowding less spending on healthcare if people move away from areas of flooding/ mosquitoes labour force may increase in some areas moving from low productivity areas to high productivity areas increasing output housing may be cheaper / more available in some areas reducing cost of living for workers migration may be to high wage areas boosting total demand fewer resources may be wasted if migration is to an area where they are under-used e.g. schools, hospitals. Reasons why it might not: as workers migrate, it could also lead to the collapse of entire communities without workers less demand for goods and services in the area increased inequality between more mobile labour and less mobile labour as some workers who cannot move will not be able to find jobs and gain higher income more pressure on government resources in areas they move to more overcrowding in those areas / housing shortages external costs more pollution / congestion.
Explanation: Accept the reverse argument, (e.g. for Q1g internal migration may be to a less polluted area) but recognise that each point may be credited only once, on either side of an argument, but separate development as to how/why the outcome may differ is rewarded.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not relocating the capital city from Jakarta will benefit the Indonesian economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: the new city will provide new job opportunities decreasing unemployment in Indonesia increasing incomes / economic growth reduce inequality between the different regions of Indonesia reducing social dissatisfaction / increase standards of living of everyone in Indonesia better environment as new city is carbon-neutral / less pollution reduced external costs better health for population of Indonesia higher productivity fewer floods that require expenditure to repair the damage foreign investors may be more willing to relocate to Indonesia. Reasons why it might not: cost of the move may be too high opportunity cost in terms of other areas of government spending e.g. education / healthcare in other regions could result in higher taxes / government budget deficit the relocation is expected to benefit only a small number of people who already have the financial capital / connection to the government inequality could rise people may be reluctant to leave Jakarta causing the resources invested in the new city to be wasted.
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: Shenzhen development from fishing village to industrial city, migration and housing shortages
Question Text: Scenario about development, migration and housing prices
Define secondary sector using an example apart from construction.
Markscheme: The sector covering construction and manufacturing / the sector that processes raw materials e.g. car manufacturing.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two key resource allocation decisions.
Markscheme: what to produce a decision on what types of products that an economy needs to produce e.g. capital goods vs consumer goods / necessities vs luxuries; how to produce seeking the most efficient methods / where the methods to produce the goods and services needs to be decided / capital-intensive vs labour-intensive; for whom to produce for those who can pay for the goods and services / for the target market / sector of the economy for whom the goods should be produced / those who need it vs those who want it.
Explanation: One mark for each of two decisions identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Accept answers based on resource allocation decisions made by firms e.g. demand for a firm's products.

Question Text: Analyse why workers may want to migrate to another area.
Markscheme: to gain higher incomes as there are more / better jobs opportunities available in another area / more opportunities to use skills since there are more employers / economic activity; to have better living standards better public amenities such as better health and education facilities; to move from areas with housing shortages for cheaper / better housing; to move from areas where prices / cost of living is high move to area with lower taxes increase purchasing power / living standards; to have better working conditions more job security; to be closer to friends / family who have migrated previously; to escape wars / high crime more security to avoid losing lives / lives of family members; to gain more freedom fewer laws / restrictive regulations; climate change original area no longer habitable / cannot plant crops relocate to areas where there are better soil / fewer floods / fewer natural disasters / less pollution.
Explanation: Accept internal and external migration. Maximum of 3 marks for identifying reasons.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should introduce a maximum price on housing.
Markscheme: Why it might: reduces price of housing / makes housing more affordable; to control (cost-push) inflation; to improve labour mobility / increase labour force available; ensures everyone has shelter, a basic necessity; leaves enough money to buy other needs and wants, increasing living standards; less inequality / reduces relative poverty. Why it might not: discourages firms from supplying housing / reduces profits; may reduce housing quality if firms wish to maintain profit levels; shortage of housing if maximum price is below the equilibrium; no effect if maximum price is set above the equilibrium price; creation of informal markets at higher prices – lower standards; maximum prices might still be too high and unaffordable; the maximum price introduced may not take location into account.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Global video game industry growth, substitutes for console gaming and government regulation of gaming time
Question Text: Scenario about substitutes, revenue and government regulation
Identify two pairs of products, not in the gaming industry, which are substitutes.
Markscheme: tea and coffee; Netflix and Disney streaming services; iPhones and Android Phones; glasses and contact lenses
Explanation: Accept any relevant pairs of products: 1 mark for each pair.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a firm’s revenue may increase.
Markscheme: higher demand due to e.g. increase in income / successful advertising / change in taste / fashion; change in price of the product if demand is inelastic a rise in price will cause a rise in revenue / if demand is elastic a fall in price will cause a rise in revenue; increased market power / market share / monopoly; price of substitutes increased / fewer substitutes available demand shifted to this product; price of complements decreased raising demand for the complement; higher incomes of consumers increasing affordability to buy the product; firm selling higher quality product more consumers want to buy the product; government subsidy received extra source of revenue.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Do not award for decreased costs or increased profits.

Question Text: Analyse two economic consequences of having a large proportion of the population aged under 16.
Markscheme: requirement for substantial spending on education opportunity cost for other spending e.g. healthcare / infrastructure / any examples of other spending high demand for teachers; high dependency ratio few income earners and many dependents reduced tax revenues lower living standards income has to be shared between many people; low levels of economic activity young population not in the labour force / not working lack of experienced workers less productivity smaller GDP / lower economic growth; greater demand for items consumed by children e.g. toys smaller demand for items consumed by adults e.g. cars; future economic growth potential high when young reach adulthood they enter the labour force increasing the supply of labour lowering labour costs encouraging investments.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not it would benefit the economy if a government imposed regulations on video game use by young people.
Markscheme: Why it might: government can ban a product or limit the quantity allowed to be bought, decreasing demand for the product; government can impose age limits on certain products; government can ensure the product produced doesn't create too many external costs e.g. violent crime; improve educational achievement, skills of worker, higher output; improve health, reduce healthcare costs; reduce consumption of a demerit good i.e. violent video games. Why it might not: policing might not be effective; unofficial / illegal markets could be formed, government spending required to police them; opportunity cost, spending on e.g. healthcare instead of policing; could increase unemployment in the video gaming industry; could reduce GDP / economic growth if the industry is significant; could reduce tax revenue if games are subject to tax; video games can be creative, giving participants skills; could reduce exports and worsen the current account balance.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: United States trade deficit, protection of industries and high government healthcare spending
Question Text: Scenario about trade protection and government spending
Define a trade in goods deficit.
Markscheme: Expenditure on imports (of goods) exceeds revenue from exports (of goods).
Explanation: Imports exceed exports (1).

Question Text: Explain two ways firms can be classified.
Markscheme: By stage: primary / secondary / tertiary according to the product produced; By sector private sector / public sector according to who owns the firm; By size according to amount of output produced / number of workers employed; By location MNC or domestically-based according to whether operates in more than one country; By level of competition may be a monopoly or competitive firm; By use of factors of production capital intensive or labour intensive.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse why a government may protect its country’s industries from foreign competition.
Markscheme: To improve / stop decline of the balance of payments by increasing exports / reducing imports increasing economic growth; To protect infant (sunrise) industries allow growth in order to take advantage of economies of scale charge lower prices; To protect declining (sunset) industries allow industries to decline gradually unable to replace workers leaving / retiring to protect jobs / avoid rising unemployment; To protect strategic industries to ensure supply of e.g. food not disrupted by e.g. economic sanctions; To protect industries from unfair competition e.g. dumping government subsidies.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should increase its spending on healthcare.
Markscheme: Why it should: to prevent illness / the spread of diseases / reduce death rates; may improve productivity; healthcare may be under-consumed; healthcare is a merit good; the poor may not be able to afford healthcare; may increase life expectancy; may increase employment in healthcare; may boost total (aggregate) demand / economic growth; may reduce unemployment; may increase government tax revenues. Why it should not: increased cost of healthcare may cause a budget deficit; may have to raise taxes; increased spending may cause inflation; opportunity cost e.g. spending on education; private sector provision may be more efficient; doesn't guarantee higher quality healthcare.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Vaccine industry investment and potential horizontal mergers creating external benefits
Question Text: Scenario about mergers and investment
Identify two benefits of a horizontal merger.
Markscheme: economies of scale / lower costs; lower prices (for consumers); sharing of each firm's strengths; more capital available; market power / higher prices; higher quality; more efficient use of resources; higher profit; international competitiveness
Explanation: Higher revenue not accepted.

Question Text: Explain two possible external benefits of vaccines.
Markscheme: healthier overall population reduced risk for others to become infected / for those who have been vaccinated to spread the disease to the rest of the population; improved HDI life expectancy is part of a country's HDI; reduced long term spending on healthcare freeing funds for other areas; healthier labour force higher productivity / higher output / lower prices; more jobs to the community near the vaccine production site higher incomes / lower unemployment; increased medical research and development/ innovation helps other fields in medicine and science.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse potential reasons why a merged firm may fail.
Markscheme: diseconomies of scale higher long run (average) cost as output increases decreasing profits; lack of coordination difficult to coordinate between the different departments in a larger company may require additional managers / processes; lack of control difficult to ensure everyone in a large company is working towards the same goal increased absences / slacking-off; lack of cooperation / disagreements no sense of belonging and personal connections and therefore workers may not want to work with each other; lack of synergy different parts of the merged companies can't work well with each other don't have the same philosophy / management styles; reduced competition may increase complacency / reduce efficiency / innovation lower quality reduce demand; other possible reasons for failure, not directly related to merger, e.g. fall in demand supply chain difficulties.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not investment is beneficial to an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: improves productivity / technological innovation; creates jobs – decreasing unemployment; consumers may benefit if investment reduces prices, improves quality of products; increases total demand and output of the economy – economic growth; improves quality of education; improves quality of health; if FDI, it provides funds not available in home country. Why it might not: if financed by borrowing, affected by interest rate changes; creates inflation in short run; increases pollution – external cost; if foreign investment – overdependence on foreign parties; increased inequality; increased unemployment if capital goods act as a substitute for labour; opportunity cost in the form of fewer consumer goods in the short run.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: UAE economy: high migrant labour, globalisation through World Expo, oil exports, technological progress, tourism in Dubai and fixed exchange rate system
Question Text: Source material about the UAE economy
Calculate the percentage of the population who were foreign nationals living in the UAE.
Explanation: Accept answer without %

Question Text: Identify two features of globalisation.
Markscheme: The increased connection of people from all over the world through migration; the trade of goods and services; the sharing of ideas; technological development.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain one factor that can influence labour mobility between countries.
Markscheme: Good transport helping workers to move availability of flights / development of air travel; Technological progress / development increasing quality / quantity of factors production
Explanation: One mark for a factor identified and one mark for an explanation.

Question Text: Explain one advantage and one disadvantage of producing a product which is price-inelastic in demand such as oil.
Markscheme: Advantage: when price increases, there is a less than proportionate fall in quantity demanded revenue generated from the product increases because it's a necessity / has no substitutes. Disadvantage: when price decreases, there is a less than proportionate rise in quantity demanded revenue generated from this product decreases.
Explanation: One mark for each effect identified and one mark for each explanation. Accept responses that consider perfect price inelasticity (PED = 0)

Question Text: Explain the relationship between the oil price and the UAE’s current account balance.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Positive / direct relationship the oil price and the current account balance would be expected to move in the same direction. Supporting Evidence: As the % change in the price of oil increases, the % change in the current account balance increases (and vice versa). When oil price goes up, the current account balance goes up, e.g. 2016 - 2018, 2020 - 2022. When oil price goes down, the current account balance goes down, e.g. 2019 - 2020. When the % change in the price of oil is highest, the % change in the current account balance is also the highest e.g. 2021. When the % change in the price of oil is lowest, the % change in the current account is also the lowest e.g. 2020. Analysis: Higher oil prices increase export revenue as demand for oil is inelastic increases inflows into current account. Exception: There is no exception evident in the figure.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse, using a production possibility curve (PPC) diagram, the effect of technological progress on an economy such as the UAE.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled with different outputs; Initial curve drawn as a curve / line sloping downward to the axes; New curve drawn to the right of the initial curve as a curve / line sloping downward to the axes; Shift to the right indicated by arrow or PPC1 to PPC2. Written analysis: Technological progress can increase productive capacity / the economy is able to produce more.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 1 mark for written analysis.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not luxury tourism and financial services have helped Dubai achieve economic development.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: they attract foreign tourists who spend on goods and services in Dubai increasing total demand / economic growth employment is generated increasing incomes and improving living standards increased government's tax revenues may be spent on public services / infrastructure / education / healthcare increased export revenues improve the current account of the balance of payments increase profits of firms in tourism and financial services. Reasons why it might not: increased pollution environmental damage / external costs e.g. health problems high use of water resources inequality in the distribution of income some workers may be paid much lower than others spending on luxury tourism and financial services may change with income depending on them could be risky workers may lack the skills for these occupations be unable to gain employment to improve living standards.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not having a fixed exchange rate is beneficial for a country such as the UAE.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: currency has more stability it will not increase / decrease in value avoiding reduced / increased competitiveness; maintains investor confidence in the economy increasing investments increasing total demand / employment; may maintain consumer confidence by controlling inflation; it can be set at a low rate to gain a competitive advantage. Reasons why it might not: limits the government's flexibility with monetary policy as interest rate changes may be used to maintain the fixed exchange rate; other macroeconomic objectives cannot be met e.g. if a low exchange rate is fixed, inflation might occur; reserves of foreign currency have to be kept to maintain the exchange rate; the fixed exchange rate might not reflect market conditions (supply and demand) for the currency.
Explanation: Maximum 3 marks for only identifying benefits / disbenefits.

Stimulus / Scenario: UK unemployment falling but living standards not improving and trade with the EU declining
Question Text: Scenario about unemployment, trade and wages
Identify two ways of measuring unemployment.
Markscheme: claimant count; (labour force) survey
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two factors that could influence living standards, apart from unemployment rates.
Markscheme: income / wage levels higher wages allow people to afford more goods and services; education high education levels lead greater skills and to better paid jobs / lead to high human development index; health standards / healthcare high health standards lead to better enjoyment of life / less illness / better wellbeing; cost of living / inflation rate affecting purchasing power; working hours lower working hours gives more leisure time; hygiene / sanitation good water quality prevents diseases; environmental standards e.g. pollution reduces quality of life; availability of public / merit goods e.g. street lights / parks; crime rates low crime rates improve safety / quality of life.
Explanation: One mark for each factor identified and one mark for each explanation. Accept arguments which describe reasons for either low / high living standards. Accept answers that give HDI with reference to either GDP per head, education or health.

Question Text: Analyse how market forces can increase wages.
Markscheme: an increase in demand for labour may increase wages if the demand for a product increases i.e. derived demand, there will be an increase in demand for workers producing that product for example, if price of substitutes such as robots increases, there will be an increase in the demand for human labour; a decrease in supply of labour may increase wages if it is caused by a decrease in the labour force or a decrease in number of people qualified / having skills in that occupation.
Explanation: Reward, but do not expect, answers that analyse this question using elasticity of demand and supply.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not free trade between countries increases economic growth.
Markscheme: Why it might: may enable countries to specialise on what they are best at producing; higher competition may increase productivity and lower costs of production, increasing demand for their products; may enable firms to buy raw materials and capital goods at a lower price; may give firms access to larger markets, enabling them to expand output. Why it might not: may prevent infant industry from growing; may speed up the closure of declining industries; may replace domestic output with imports; may reduce tax revenue; firms may gain monopoly power and may restrict output; firms may engage in unfair competition e.g. dumping.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Private schooling in Macao and competition between schools with technological innovation
Question Text: Scenario about education markets and inequality
Define privatisation.
Markscheme: Privatisation is the sale / transfer of assets from the public sector to the private sector.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain how technological innovation can benefit firms.
Markscheme: technological innovation can lead to new / better technology increasing efficiency / productivity; higher levels of output economies of scale / reducing average cost leading to lower prices attracting consumers leading to higher profits; improved / faster capital equipment / IT systems / software producing better quality products; more capital-intensive firms reduced labour costs reduced errors.
Explanation: One mark for each identification and one mark for each relevant explanation.

Question Text: Analyse the effects of increased competition amongst firms.
Markscheme: lower price as firms try to attract more consumers this will benefit consumers but could cause firms to close down; improved quality / innovation such as longer-lasting products / products that provide more satisfaction to consumers / products that consumers want to buy; increased choice consumers can to choose between different firms for products; reduced profits for firms costs may increase because of the need to improve quality each firm will have fewer consumers since demand will be distributed between more firms; firms may need to spend more on advertising / marketing increasing costs this may cause external costs e.g. pollution but may provide better information to consumers.
Explanation: Accept lower quality due to firms reducing costs

Question Text: Discuss whether or not improving education can help a government achieve its macroeconomic aims.
Markscheme: Why it might: a more skilled / productive workforce attracts more investments leading to higher total demand and economic growth; improved education can make finding work easier and may lead to full employment / lower unemployment; high productivity could lead to lower average cost of production leading to more stable prices / low inflation; higher level of exports leads to more balance of payments stability; education could give everyone the chance to get a well-paid job leading to a more equal redistribution of income. Why it might not: improving education requires increased government expenditure leading to higher total demand and may cause inflation; improved education in the country could lead to emigration of skilled workers, causing lower economic growth; there might not be enough jobs for those highly qualified leading to increase unemployment amongst graduates / underemployment; better education might only be accessible to those on higher incomes, therefore increasing inequality in income distribution; a better educated workforce may demand higher wages causing increase costs of production, decreasing price competitiveness, worsening the current account of the balance of payments; the gains from improved education are long term, not short term.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: India trade protection policies and high tariffs on coffee imports
Question Text: Scenario about tariffs and protectionism
Identify two methods of trade protection apart from a tariff.
Markscheme: import quotas; subsidies; embargoes/bans; licences; product standards/regulations
Explanation: Accept any relevant methods.

Question Text: Explain how the price of coffee substitutes and complements can affect the demand for coffee.
Markscheme: Relationship between price and demand of substitutes is positive: when price of coffee substitutes increases, demand for coffee increases, or vice versa because as price of coffee substitutes increases, coffee is relatively cheaper demand for the substitute will decrease, and the demand for coffee will increase e.g. coffee and tea. Relationship between price and demand of complements is negative: when price of coffee complements increases, demand for coffee falls or vice versa because as price for coffee complements increases, the demand for the complement will fall, and therefore the demand for coffee will decrease e.g. coffee and sugar.
