Explanation: Maximum of 3 marks if only substitutes or complements are explained.

Question Text: Analyse possible reasons why a government may want to intervene in a market.
Markscheme: Government may want to intervene in a market due to market failure / when the market mechanism leads to inefficient allocation of resources. Reasons for intervention: to provide public goods not provided by the market due to non-rivalry / non-excludability creating the free rider problem / no profit-incentive for private producers; to provide merit goods under-consumed and under-produced by the market external benefits which are ignored by the market e.g. vaccinations; to reduce provision of demerit goods over-consumed and over-produced by the market external costs are ignored by the market e.g. pollution; to reduce monopoly power that creates high prices while reducing consumer choices and reducing quality; to reduce factor immobility factors of production unable to move around to its best possible use e.g. labour immobility where workers cannot move around and therefore are unemployed; to protect consumers of essential items by setting a maximum price keeping the price below the market price; to protect low paid workers by setting a minimum wage keeping the wage above the market wage; to give subsidies / impose tariffs e.g. protect infant industries so that they can grow in the long-term / protect employment.
Explanation: Maximum of three marks for identification of reasons

Question Text: Discuss whether or not high tariffs can reduce unemployment.
Markscheme: Why it might: high tariffs lead to increase price of imports reducing the demand for imports; high tariffs make consumers choose domestic products instead which increases demand for domestic workers; high tariffs provide government with extra revenue which could be used to subsidise domestic firms / domestic employment; high tariffs can be a major source of tax revenue. Some of this could be spent on e.g. education and training which will increase workers' skills and mobility, and so reduce unemployment. Why it might not: high tariffs lead to retaliation which leads to decrease in demand for exports and therefore decrease demand for domestic workers; high tariffs still not enough to offset lower cost of imports; domestic production may not be able to increase due to full employment / lack of natural resources; higher prices of imported raw materials could increase costs of production, so reducing profits and lowering output; domestic prices may rise as domestic firms become complacent as they are protected.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Hawaii economy with tourism growth, inflation, poverty and an ageing population
Question Text: Scenario about tourism, inflation and demographic change
Define labour mobility.
Markscheme: The ability of workers to move occupationally or geographically to take up another job.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain the effects of an ageing population on spending and saving levels.
Markscheme: An ageing population may mean fewer people are working and not earning an income therefore savings need to be withdrawn to fund living and therefore savings will decrease. An ageing population may mean people have reached an age where they do not need to save for the future have accumulated savings therefore spending increases. An ageing population may mean more people are approaching retirement / have reached retirement there may be an increase in saving to support living standards in retirement and decrease current spending.
Explanation: Maximum of 3 marks for a relevant explanation of either spending or saving.

Question Text: Analyse how a rise in tourism can increase inflation in an economy.
Markscheme: Tourism leads to an increase in demand for resources e.g. food or accommodation therefore these prices will increase causing demand-pull inflation. Tourists may have higher incomes and higher purchasing power therefore, producers are able to charge higher prices and those living the country will have to pay higher prices as well. Depletion of resources due to tourism decreases supply of these resources increases the price of these resources for everyone. Increased demand for factors of production in tourism e.g. labour increases costs of production may cause cost-push inflation.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not fiscal policy is effective in reducing poverty.
Markscheme: Why it might: policy measures to promote economic growth may increase investment and create jobs; increased spending on education providing more qualifications / skills, making it easier to find jobs, higher wages; more generous state benefits increasing income; progressive taxation may increase government revenue and allow more spending to reduce poverty; cuts in indirect taxation e.g. VAT, may lower prices of goods and services, enabling the poor to buy more and reduce poverty. Why it might not: contractionary fiscal policy / increased taxes could lead to more people losing jobs / income; expansionary fiscal policy / tax cuts may be inflationary, causing higher prices for essential items and increasing poverty; education may only be accessible to those not in poverty; state benefits may lead to a cycle of poverty; progressive taxation may lead to brain drain and therefore less government revenue; cuts in higher income tax rates may not reduce absolute poverty but may increase relative poverty.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Jordan economy: current account deficit, tourism industry mergers, population growth, renewable energy projects and possible interest rate rise
Question Text: Source material about economic changes in Jordan
Calculate the percentage of the Jordanian labour force employed in the secondary sector.
Explanation: Accept 19.

Question Text: Identify two components of the current account of Jordan’s balance of payments.
Markscheme: Trade in services; primary income
Explanation: If more than two suggested components are given, consider the first three. Accept invisibles for trade in services.

Question Text: Explain the main type of unemployment experienced by Jordan in 2020.
Markscheme: Cyclical unemployment lower total demand / fall in employment in most industries / number of workers greater than the number of jobs available.
Explanation: Fail in demand is not sufficient here for fall in total demand as total demand is given in the source material – must be macro. Accept 'demand deficient unemployment for cyclical unemployment.

Question Text: Explain two advantages the Jordanian economy may gain from the mergers between its tourism firms.
Markscheme: Lower prices may raise output/GDP/employment/living standards / may be the result of taking advantage of economies of scale; Higher quality may raise exports / reduce current account deficit / increase international competitiveness / increase tourism revenue / more foreign currency / may be the result of sharing ideas.
Explanation: One mark for each of two advantages identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Lower prices may be linked to either 'raise output' etc. or 'raise exports' etc. Similarly higher quality may be linked to 'raise exports etc.' or 'raise output etc.'.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between birth rate and average age.
Markscheme: Expected relationship (up to 2 marks): Countries with a low birth rate are likely to have a high average age an inverse relationship / negative relationship / move in opposite directions. Supporting evidence (up to 2 marks): Four countries with the lowest birth rates had the highest average age; Monaco had the lowest birth rate and the highest average age; Niger with the highest birth rate has the lowest average age; e.g. Germany has a lower birth rate and a higher average age than Jordan. Analysis of expected relationship (up to 2 marks): A low birth rate would mean that children would form a small proportion of the population / a high birth rate may mean children would form a high proportion of the population / it would increase the proportion of people aged over 65; A high birth rate may indicate low living standards / lower income which may be associated with low life expectancy; A high average age may mean that there are fewer people of child-bearing age. Exception (up to 2 marks): Venezuela or Maldives; Maldives had a lower birth rate than Venezuela but the same average age. Analysis of exception (up to 1 mark): The average age of a country's population is also influenced by the death rate and net migration.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Accept analysis based on how differences in the average of population may affect the birth rate. For supporting evidence and the second 'exception' mark, comparisons must be made. No marks for just stating the birth rate and average age figures.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how an increase in population size will affect the market for clothing.
Markscheme: Diagram: Axes correctly labelled - price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the right; Equilibriums - shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2. Written analysis: An increase in population size will increase the number of consumers / increase demand for clothes / price increases.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 1 mark for written analysis.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the Jordanian government should spend more on renewable energy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should: need for more energy due to an increase in population; non-renewable sources of energy will run out / reduced dependency on fossil fuels; may reduce import bills; would reduce environmental damage; reduce external costs; lower pollution; reduce extent of climate change / global warming / promote sustainable development; improve health; cheaper in the longer run; solar and wind power are free goods; lower costs of production; reduce household bills. Reasons why it should not: expensive to build; opportunity cost; example; relies on weather; supplies may be disrupted / insufficient / unreliable; cost of renewable energy projects may increase with high interest rates; may be funded by borrowing; may increase taxes; may cause visual pollution e.g. wind turbines.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the Jordanian central bank should have raised the rate of interest in 2021.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should have: total demand predicted to increase / may have reduced total demand; encouraged saving; discouraged spending / borrowing; lowered demand-pull inflation; reduced imports; may have attracted more funds for the Jordanian government to borrow / use for e.g. renewable energy projects. Reasons why it should not have: cost of government borrowing would have increased / may have difficulty paying off government debt; investment may have been discouraged; lowered economic growth; reduced growth in employment / caused unemployment; may have increased the exchange rate; foreigners depositing money in the country's banks / attracted hot money flow; raised export prices / lowered import prices / decreased exports / increased imports; reduced tourism; increased the current account deficit.
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: UK wages rising, restaurant demand increasing and new free trade agreements
Question Text: Scenario about wages, consumer demand and free trade
Identify two places where a production point could be located on a PPC diagram.
Markscheme: point inside the curve / left of the curve; point on the curve / along the curve; point outside the curve / right of the curve
Explanation: The points may be identified on a diagram. Accept below the curve and above the curve.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why someone may switch from eating in one restaurant to eating in another restaurant.
Markscheme: (Change in) prices lower price making it more affordable / substitutes / save money; (Change in) quality better ingredients / better cleanliness / quicker service / new menu / more satisfaction / better taste / change in staff; (Change in) convenience e.g. longer opening hours / better location; (Change in) facilities e.g. better restrooms; (Change in) income enabling some to eat in more expensive restaurants; (Change in) consumer tastes switch to e.g. vegan restaurant; Advertising may attract/persuade customers / increase attractiveness; (Change in) quantity provided lower quantity may represent lower value for money.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse why the wage of a chief executive of a large commercial bank is higher than that of a waiter.
Markscheme: Chief executive may be more qualified / more educated received more training more skilled / talented more productive more experienced higher level of responsibility / higher level of stress / more demanding job in shorter supply / harder to replace more inelastic supply. Chief executive may be in higher demand more inelastic demand. Chief executive may have stronger bargaining power may be more likely to be in a trade union / professional body. May be less likely to be in a group that is discriminated against e.g. migrant workers. Chief executive may work longer hours.
Explanation: Note: may also be answered from the point of view why the wage of a waiter may be lower than that of a chief executive. No marks here for banks may be more profitable than restaurants as e.g. a cleaner in a bank may have a lower wage than a waiter.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not free trade will increase economic growth.
Markscheme: Why it might: may enable countries to specialise on what they are best at producing; higher competition may increase productivity and lower costs of production, increasing demand for their products; may enable firms to buy raw materials and capital goods at a lower price; may give firms access to larger markets, enabling them to expand output. Why it might not: may prevent infant industry from growing; may speed up the closure of declining industries; may replace domestic output with imports; may reduce tax revenue; firms may gain monopoly power and may restrict output; firms may engage in unfair competition e.g. dumping.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Madagascar market economy, labour-intensive industries and poverty
Question Text: Scenario about poverty and economic systems
Define foreign exchange market.
Markscheme: An arrangement / place where different countries' currencies are bought/demanded and sold/supplied one currency is exchanged for another currency.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a firm may adopt labour-intensive production.
Markscheme: Labour may be low in price / wages may be low which might make labour cheaper than capital / there may be a high supply of labour; Labour may be very productive / efficient which can make employment profitable / result in low costs of production; The government may subsidise the employment of workers to reduce unemployment; The firm may provide personal services with individual attention; Labour may be more creative / adaptable / provide feedback produce high quality products / improve methods of production; Labour may be unskilled / low level of education unable to use high-tech capital equipment.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how the macroeconomic aims of economic growth and balance of payments stability may conflict.
Markscheme: Economic growth may increase employment increase wages / incomes / purchasing power this may increase imports. Higher consumer spending may encourage firms to switch products to the domestic market reduce exports increase / cause a current account deficit. To produce more goods and services, firms may import more raw materials capital goods. Economic growth may result in inflation which would make domestic products less price competitive. Economic growth may deplete resources increase need for imports. Economic growth may be export-led may be achieved by restricting imports result in a current account surplus. Migrant workers may send more money home. Export-led growth may increase the exchange rate which may increase imports / reduce exports.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a high level of poverty is likely to exist in a market economic system.
Markscheme: Why it might: may be unemployment; lack of welfare benefits; likely to be income inequality; merit goods including healthcare and education may be under-consumed and so underproduced; monopolies may develop resulting in higher prices. Why it might not: competition / profit motive may result in low prices; efficiency is encouraged which can result in high wages and high employment; greater response to changes in consumer demand, may increase economic growth; countries with efficient private sector firms may have relative poverty but low absolute poverty.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: South Africa oil exploration, growth in tertiary sector and high unemployment
Question Text: Scenario about environmental issues and employment
Define an extension in demand.
Markscheme: A movement along a demand curve higher demand caused by a fall in price.
Explanation: May be shown by a movement down a demand curve caused by a fall in price. Not accepting: an increase in demand but accepting: an increase in quantity demanded.

Question Text: Explain two external costs that may be caused by exploring for oil.
Markscheme: Air pollution / pollution those living nearby may experience a reduction in air quality / contribute to climate change/global warming / reduced health; Noise pollution disturbing sleep; Water pollution / oil spillage damage health; Damage to the natural environment / land / deforestation destroy wildlife habitats / cause global warming / climate change; Reduce tourism lower employment; Congestion increasing transport costs.
Explanation: One mark for each of two costs identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Reduce tourism may be given as an identification or as an explanation of e.g. 'Damage to the natural environment'

Question Text: Analyse how growth in a country’s tertiary sector can increase its living standards.
Markscheme: May have high productivity increase output increase employment / reduce unemployment. May pay higher wages allowing workers to consume more goods and services / afford education / afford healthcare / reduce poverty / buy more necessities / buy some luxuries/wants. May provide good working conditions less tiring / less physically demanding resulting in good health of workers few accidents. May involve expansion of education increasing future employment / workers' skills earning prospects increase quality of output increase HDI value. More transport services could reduce prices / lower congestion / reduce stress. Growth of entertainment industry improve leisure time. May involve expansion of healthcare increase life expectancy / better health. May increase tax revenue enabling the government to spend more on e.g. education. Less pollution less environmental damage / less climate change / less global warming.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not workers who lose their jobs are likely to stay unemployed for a long time.
Markscheme: Why they might: may lose skills / unskilled workers may have greater difficulty than skilled workers in finding another job; may become out of date with advances in technology / working practices; may be expensive for firms to employ as may need extra training; may lose confidence; may become less attractive to employers; more likely to stay unemployed if a result of cyclical/structural rather than frictional unemployment; may be high unemployment benefits. Why they might not: may receive government training; may be geographically/occupationally mobile; gap between wages and state benefits may encourage the unemployed to seek work; economy may grow, expanding employment opportunities; may leave the labour force, e.g. retire / emigrate; may be seasonally unemployed.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Reward but do not expect reference to hysteresis.

Stimulus / Scenario: Peru tourism and fruit farming industries with rising trade union membership
Question Text: Scenario about transport infrastructure, competition and labour unions
Identify two examples of labour employed in the tourism industry.
Markscheme: tour guides; hotel manager / hotel worker; travel agent; flight attendant; coach / bus driver
Explanation: Also accept skilled and unskilled. Do not accept driver or car driver on their own. Could accept e.g. a driver who transports tourists.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why countries with a good transport system often have a high GDP.
Markscheme: Lower firms' costs of production cheaper to move goods/imports/exports / move goods/raw materials/imports/exports more efficiently / increase international competitiveness and so increase exports / higher income / increase size of markets / higher total demand; Reduce wastage food etc more likely to arrive fresh and so increase earnings for sale of e.g food / higher profits; Increase labour mobility / easier to get to work reduce unemployment / increase productivity; Less congestion less stress / less time lost; Increase (earnings from) tourism cheaper / easier to move around the country; Attract MNCs/FDI / increase investment increase employment increase productive capacity; Fewer accidents avoid loss of workers / avoid loss of working hours; Less pollution healthier labour force.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. Accept: increase accessibility.

Question Text: Analyse the benefits consumers may gain from a competitive market.
Markscheme: A competitive market has a large number of firms producing the product / no barriers to entry substitutes available. Low prices as firms try to gain a larger share of the market / attract consumers / increase purchasing power. High quality as competition can increase efficiency increase innovation. Choice different versions of the product may be produced / greater range of products / greater variety. Responsive to changes in consumer demand consumer sovereignty.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in trade union membership will benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: rise in wages and improved working conditions may increase productivity; higher wages may increase total demand which may increase real GDP; improved working conditions would increase living standards; by providing a channel of communication, it may reduce conflicts between workers and employers; by helping with training may raise output. Why it might not: higher wages may increase inflation; may take industrial action e.g. strikes which could lower output / GDP; may resist job losses / introduction of new technology which may prevent increases in efficiency.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Maximum of L2 4 marks if candidate answers in reverse based on a misinterpretation – an increase in trade union fees.

Stimulus / Scenario: Belarus economy: rising GDP per head, inflation from energy costs, milk production, electric vehicles, unemployment law and mixed economy
Question Text: Source material about Belarus’ economy and development
Calculate the average cost of producing a car in Belarus in 2021.
Explanation: Accept 10 650 or 0.010650million or 0.1065m. Needs to be to at least 2 dp e.g. 0.011m

Question Text: Identify two reasons why Belarus is described as a mixed economy.
Markscheme: There is a public sector / some people work in the public sector / there are state-owned firms / there is government intervention e.g. fining the unemployed; There is a private sector.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain the type of inflation experienced by Belarus.
Markscheme: Cost-push due to a rise in the price of energy / increase in costs of production of firms.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two reasons why the production possibility curve (PPC) of Belarus may have shifted to the left between 2011 and 2021.
Markscheme: a decline in the size of the country's labour force reduction in the quantity of resources / fewer workers will be likely to reduce country's ability to produce products; a decrease in the number of students going to university reduce quality of resources / labour skills / productivity.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. No marks for drawing a PPC diagram.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between life expectancy and HDI value.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: a high life expectancy would be expected to be associated with a high HDI value; positive relationship / direct relationship / move in the same direction. Supporting evidence: two countries with the longest life expectancy have the highest HDI value OR two countries with the lowest life expectancy also have the lowest HDI value; specific reference to a country e.g. Norway had the longest life expectancy and the highest HDI value. Analysis of expected relationship: life expectancy is one of the components of HDI; accounts for a third of its value / explanation of how better health / life expectancy raises standard of living. Exception: Belarus / Thailand Belarus has a shorter life expectancy but a higher HDI value. Analysis of exception: there are other components of the HDI: education / GDP (GNI) per head.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Note: simply repeating the data is not enough for a mark as it is only description whereas the skill being tested is analysis.

Question Text: Analyse, using a demand and supply diagram, how subsidising the production of milk would affect the market for milk.
Markscheme: Diagram: axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or P and Q; original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; new supply curve shifted to the right; equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2. Written analysis: reduction in cost of production / increase in supply / increase in quantity traded / reduction in price.
Explanation: Up to 4 marks for the diagram and up to 1 mark for written analysis.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the construction of electric vehicle charging stations would benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might benefit: likely to encourage use of electric vehicles stations and vehicles are complements improve the environment reduce external costs including air / noise pollution reduce healthcare costs / improve health of workers building the stations / building more electric cars may create employment government / private sector investment / purchase of electric vehicles may raise total (aggregate) demand causing higher output / economic growth. Reasons why it might not benefit: other forms of transport may have more beneficial effects on the environment e.g. cycling / buses some people may be reluctant to switch price of electric cars may be too high resources are wasted if people do switch, it may cause unemployment in e.g. oil firms / production of diesel and petrol cars / less public transport Increase in electric vehicles on road causes road congestion Opportunity cost of government investment monies could have been spent e.g. education / health / infrastructure If economy at full employment investment may lead to demand-pull inflation.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not people should be fined for being unemployed.
Markscheme: Reasons why they should be fined: if frictional unemployment a fine would encourage workers to put more effort into finding a job spend less time between jobs help move economy towards full employment higher output and economic growth fines may increase government revenue which could be spent on e.g. healthcare / support to find a job may not be seeking a job as receive benefits fines may encourage them to find work and reduce government spending on unemployment benefits. Reasons why they should not be fined: may be a lack of suitable jobs available the unemployed may have lacked the skills required may not have been able to move to other areas of the country may have disabilities / ill-health providing support may reduce unemployment more quickly unemployment may be structural / cyclical it would have been more effective to increase training increase total demand unemployed may have little income to pay the fine may increase their debt lead to greater poverty / unable to pay for basic necessities affect their mental health / wellbeing / causes depression may encourage the growth of the shadow/unofficial/informal economy as not registered as unemployed may reduce wages workers may be forced to accept low-paid jobs.
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: Luxembourg: developed economy with government intervention, monopolies, high saving rates and immigration-driven population growth
Question Text: Scenario about economic systems, savings and migration
Identify two influences on the proportion of income saved by households.
Markscheme: rate of interest; level of (disposable) income / earnings / wealth / taxation; confidence / economic outlook; advertising / availability of saving schemes; strength of financial institutions; age of / number of dependents in household; social convention / culture; cost of living / spend on basic necessities / inflation.
Explanation: These are the most common influences. Give credit for other relevant influences. Accept any two from the first three responses.

Question Text: Explain two characteristics of monopoly.
Markscheme: only firm dominant market share / no effective competition / substitutes / controls the market; high barriers to entry difficult for new firms to enter industry; price maker able to set the price / demand is inelastic / only choice for consumers; can control supply causing higher prices / makes high profits; unique product result of profits reinvested in R&D; large size benefits from economies of scale / low average costs.
Explanation: One mark for each of two characteristics identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the causes of migration between countries.
Markscheme: Pull factors: people may move to gain higher income / move to high-income countries / countries with higher wages move to countries with more job opportunities / lower unemployment people move to increase goods and services they can consume / higher standard of living; people may move to gain better healthcare to increase life expectancy / quality of life; people may move to gain better education / attend university to increase their children's chance of gaining employment and high pay; may have family members in another country example of why move to be with family members. Push factors: people may move to countries to escape conflict / high crime / high corruption racial and religious intolerance famine pollution climate change / better environment escape poverty high taxes to increase safety.
Explanation: Note: apply a limit of three marks for simply identifying causes with no analysis given.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not government intervention can overcome the disadvantages of a market economic system.
Markscheme: Why it might: governments provide merit goods which may be under-consumed; restrict demerit goods which may be over-consumed; provide public goods which would not be supplied by the private sector; may provide products or benefits for those with low purchasing power; may regulate monopolies / prevent mergers; may address external costs such as pollution; set price ceiling to keep prices low; set minimum wages to reduce exploitation of workers. Why it might not: may be government failure; government may lack accurate information; may take time to make decisions / bureaucratic; may reduce incentives; may be corruption.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Indonesia sports participation, badminton popularity and large wage differences between athletes and cleaners
Question Text: Scenario about wages and consumer demand
Identify two decision-makers involved in economics.
Markscheme: households / consumers; workers / trade unions; firms / producers / entrepreneurs / suppliers; the government; banks
Explanation: Accept any two from first three responses made. Accept buyers and sellers. If more than two are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain the relationship between: demand for tennis racquets and demand for badminton racquets; demand for tennis racquets and demand for tennis balls.
Markscheme: (i) demand for tennis racquets and demand for badminton racquets: Substitutes; an inverse / indirect / negative relationship / competitive goods; rise in the price of one would cause the demand for the other to increase / people switch between the two. (ii) demand for tennis racquets and demand for tennis balls: Complements; a direct / positive relationship; bought to be used together / rise in the price of one would cause the demand for the other to decrease.
Explanation: One mark for each of two relationships identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse why most cleaners earn a low wage.
Markscheme: high supply / most people can do it / easily replaced elastic supply have low occupational mobility firms do not have to pay high wages to attract sufficient number of cleaners; low qualifications / education low skills / training required may have low productivity / add little value to the business low risk for workers; may not belong to a trade union low bargaining power hard to move to another job so willing to accept low pay rather than not have a job; may be from groups that are discriminated against e.g. women / older persons / immigrants; some work part-time so take-home pay may be low; might be replaced by cleaning machines / robots if wages rise too high.
Explanation: Note: low demand is not acceptable as an answer unless it is in the context of comparison to higher supply.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should tax sports equipment.
Markscheme: Why it should tax sports equipment: an additional source of tax revenue; other goods and services/forms of leisure equipment are taxed; not a necessity; the rich may spend more on sports equipment; elite / professional sports persons are well paid / receive sponsorship so can afford increased prices. Why it should not tax sports equipment: increase in prices may discourage people from playing sport especially those on low incomes; sport may be considered to be a merit good; may reduce health and fitness of people; may reduce output of sports equipment and increase unemployment; many people employed in sport and leisure industries.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Davos ski resort with hospitality sector relying on seasonal migrant labour
Question Text: Scenario about migrant labour and economic development
Define supply.
Markscheme: The willingness and ability to sell/provide a good/service.
Explanation: Accept an incomplete answer for one mark e.g. amount of a good/service produced by a firm / available in the market.

Question Text: Explain the difference between the private sector and the public sector.
Markscheme: private sector is operated and owned by individuals / firms and decisions are made based on self-interest / profits / own utility / efficiency; public sector is operated and owned by the government and decisions are made based on the overall welfare of society / people / externalities.
Explanation: Alternatively candidates may present as Motive: profit v social welfare; Ownership: individuals / firms v government

Question Text: Analyse the causes of differences in economic development between countries.
Markscheme: level of income affecting levels of affordability and consumption of various goods and services; education affecting skills / productivity job opportunities purchasing power; healthcare affecting life expectancy level of labour absences from work output; levels of population growth affecting size of workforce affecting dependency ratios which could also affect cost of labour; size of primary, secondary and tertiary sectors large size primary sector usually leads to lower levels of development than the other two sectors lower value added in primary / higher value added in secondary and tertiary; government expenditure / overseas investment (FDI) e.g. in infrastructure projects / education/healthcare raising job opportunities / reducing poverty; availability / development of resources / factors of production e.g. beautiful landscape / labour impact on economic development.
Explanation: Do not reward economic growth as being the same as economic development

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the employment of migrant workers can benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might benefit: migrant labour increases supply of labour - reduce wage cost; increases the quantity of factors of production in the economy; increase productive capacity of the economy and economic growth; more skilled; more productive; takes up jobs that locals don't want to take up; income received may create new jobs. Why it might not benefit: migrant labour Increases unemployment - takes jobs from local workers; usually willing to take jobs at lower pay than local labour lowering wage rates for all workers; means economy may not have capacity to accommodate them leading to lack of housing and other infrastructure.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Turkey interest rate cuts, rising inflation and depreciation of the Turkish lira
Question Text: Scenario about banking, inflation and exchange rates
Define a central bank.
Markscheme: A government owned bank; Operates monetary policy / sets interest rates / manages foreign exchange rates; Provides banking services to the government / commercial banks.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two ways a commercial bank could attract more customers.
Markscheme: lower interest rate on loans attracts new customers for loans; higher interest rates on savings attracts more customers to save; make loan conditions easier e.g. lend to people with lower incomes / give longer to repay; improve / increase range of services e.g. offer insurance / bank cards with added benefits; increase accessibility e.g. by opening more branches or increasing range of internet banking; advertising may increase brand awareness.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse the possible causes of a fall in a country’s foreign exchange rate.
Markscheme: a central bank / government may reduce a fixed exchange rate leading to devaluation of a currency in order to correct a balance of payments deficit; a central bank/government may try to reduce a floating exchange rate by decreasing the interest rate or selling the domestic currency; increase in the supply of the currency due to increase in imports increased investment in other countries / higher interest rates abroad expectation that the exchange rate will fall; reduction in demand for the currency due to a decrease in exports fall in the rate of interest decrease in money sent home by country's people working in other countries.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a cut in the interest rate will reduce inflation.
Markscheme: Why it will reduce inflation: may reduce firms' costs of production; may increase investment; may result in total (aggregate) supply increasing more rapidly than total (aggregate) demand; reduce cost-push inflation; increased imports lowering total (aggregate) demand; reduce demand-pull inflation. Why it will not reduce inflation: may increase consumer spending; reduced return from saving / less saving; reduced cost of borrowing / more borrowing; increase total (aggregate) demand; increase demand-pull inflation.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Romania economy: population decline, migration, household spending growth, electricity price ceiling, poverty and women in labour force
Question Text: Source material about population, spending and poverty in Romania
Calculate Romania’s current account deficit on its balance of payments in $s.
Markscheme: $27bn

Question Text: Identify two reasons why Romania’s population fell between 1990 and 2022.
Markscheme: Fall in birth rate; emigration / net emigration / people leaving the country.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain why immigration into Romania may increase in the future.
Markscheme: High economic growth rate / economic growth higher wages / higher income / more chance of employment / more job opportunities / higher living standards.
Explanation: 1 mark for identification of high economic growth rate, and 1 mark for explanation.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why household spending has increased in Romania.
Markscheme: Romanians have become wealthier more money to spend / greater purchasing power / higher income / higher GDP per head / can sell assets to get money to spend / more able to borrow / greater confidence; More confident about the future / increased optimism about future job prospects / higher earning / job security / may encourage more borrowing / less saving; Low interest rates encourage borrowing / reduce cost of borrowing / discourage saving; High rate of inflation spending more to maintain living standards.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of setting a maximum price on electricity.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Demand and supply curves correctly labelled; Maximum price set below the equilibrium price – labelled as max price, P1, PX or any other P; Quantity demanded and quantity supplied at the maximum price identified.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP per head and average spending on food as a percentage of total household spending.
Markscheme: Relationship (up to 2 marks): Generally, an inverse relationship / negative relationship the higher the GDP per head, the lower the average household spending on food as a % of household spending. Supporting evidence (up to 2 marks): E.g. the five countries with the lowest GDP per head had the highest % average household spending on food. Ethiopia had the lowest GDP per head and the highest average household spending on food as a % of total spending Cambodia had the second lowest GDP per head and the second highest spending on food as a % of total spending Egypt had the third lowest GDP per head and the third highest spending on food as a % of total spending. Analysis of relationship (up to 2 marks): As households get richer, they can afford to spend more in total on food / higher quality food but can afford to buy more of other items / buy more luxury items / spend less on necessities. Exception (up to 2 marks): Norway / USA Norway had a higher GDP per head but also a higher % of household spending on food. Analysis of exception (up to 1 mark): Food may be more expensive in Norway / Norwegians may have a different diet / buy a higher proportion of high quality food.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Supporting evidence should involve interpretation, not just description but note: A mark can be given for an implied comparison e.g. after stating that there is a negative relationship: e.g. Belgium had a GDP per head of $52 100 and only spent 13% of their total spending on food whereas Romania had a GDP per head of $15 000 and spent 26% of their spending on food. Rewarding GDP per head (income) but not just GDP.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not foreign MNCs reduce poverty in their host countries.
Markscheme: Reasons why they might: they may increase employment / reduce unemployment which may increase incomes / increase wages cause economic growth / raise GDP enabling people to buy more basic necessities / raise purchasing power; may train workers increase skills; they may have lower costs of production / more efficient use more advanced technology may take advantage of economies of scale able to charge lower prices; they may pay tax / less may be spent on unemployment benefit some of this could be spent on e.g. education / healthcare / training unemployed income could be redistributed. Reasons why they might not: they may replace domestic firms / drive domestic firms out of business may employ workers from their own countries leaving employment / unemployment unchanged; they may use capital-intensive methods reducing demand for unskilled workers; they may use up non-renewable resources reducing incomes in the future / raising prices; they may send profits back home making them unavailable for re-investment in the host country; may increase relative poverty / not reduce relative poverty may widen gap in pay between e.g. skilled and unskilled workers; may become a monopoly charge high prices.
Explanation: Extract from the source material: More MNCs may affect poverty because of the possible effects on unemployment and prices.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a higher proportion of women is likely to enter Romania’s labour force in the future.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: there may be an increase in childcare facilities making it easier for women who are parents to work; more part-time work / more job opportunities for women / shortage of labour / higher demand for labour lower birth rate which may enable women to combine work and child rearing; increase in female education / qualifications / training which may increase skills / productivity which may increase wages; increase in healthcare / family planning reduce size of families; may seek to reduce poverty / improve living standards by earning an income. Reasons why it might not: discrimination may occur in terms of jobs available / wages; negative social attitudes may continue e.g. other workers may make the working environment uncomfortable for women; women may emigrate leaving the labour force; working hours may increase making it more difficult for women to combine work and home life; An increase in income / higher household income may enable women to work fewer hours / reduce need to work.
Explanation: Extract from the source material: There is a range of influences on whether women enter the labour force. These include social attitudes, female education and whether childcare facilities and part-time work are available. No marks for just reproducing these words. Interpretation needed e.g. more part-time work. Many of the influences can be given either way but only reward identification of the influence once e.g., one mark for there may be increase in childcare facilities or a decrease in childcare facilities.

Stimulus / Scenario: South Korea: economic goods vs free goods, infrastructure investment, climate change policies
Question Text: Scenario about opportunity cost and environmental policy
Identify a difference between economic goods and free goods.
Markscheme: Economic goods use up resources / need factors of production to produce them free goods do not; Economic goods are scarce / in limited supply free goods are in unlimited supply; Economic goods have an opportunity cost free goods do not.
Explanation: No marks for examples. One mark for economic goods and one mark for free goods.

Question Text: Explain how opportunity cost will influence a government’s decision on whether to spend tax revenue on a large infrastructure project.
Markscheme: Opportunity cost is the (next) best alternative forgone / given up. Tax revenue is limited if a government spends some of it on an infrastructure project, it will not be able to spend on something else / could use the money to spend on something else an example. Governments have to decide on their priorities / consider which form of spending will provide the greatest benefit reason why spending on infrastructure may be the best option / may not be the best option.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the influences on whether supply of a product is elastic or inelastic.
Markscheme: Elasticity of supply is a measure of the responsiveness of supply to a change in price / formula / elastic supply is when pes is >1 / inelastic supply is when pes < 1. Elastic supply occurs when supply changes by a greater percentage than the change in price / inelastic supply is when supply changes by a smaller percentage than the change in price. Whether the product can be stored / non-perishable if it can be, it will be elastic able to adjust supply quickly to changes in price as if price rises, goods can be taken out of storage. Whether the production time is short if so, output can be increased quickly to take advantage of higher prices. Cost of altering supply if it is costly to alter the supply, supply is likely to be inelastic. Whether there are unemployed resources / level of capacity if there are, supply can be adjusted by taking on more resources. Whether resources / factors of production are mobile if they can change use / location. Time supply usually becomes more elastic, the longer the time period. Level of competition supply is likely to be more elastic in a competitive than in a monopoly market.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should ban the burning of wood to heat homes.
Markscheme: Why it should: reduce external costs / pollution; correct market failure / information failure; improve people's health; protect forests; replace wood with a more environmentally-friendly fuel; wood could be used for other purposes. Why it should not: wood may be bought mainly by the poor, increase poverty; other sources of fuel may be more expensive; may be difficult to enforce; may cause unemployment; may be better to tax wood as a fuel, could use revenue to subsidise more environmentally-friendly sources of fuel; some alternative fuels may be more polluting.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Estonia labour market: shortage of teachers, wage incentives and different firm sizes
Question Text: Scenario about labour mobility and firms
Define factor of production.
Markscheme: A resource / input used to produce goods and services / used in the production process.
Explanation: No marks for examples.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why building workers may not become teachers.
Markscheme: May lack skills occupationally immobile; May not have the necessary qualifications / may not have sufficient education / may lack training / may lack willingness or ability to undertake training; May be paid more as building workers a more physically demanding / more dangerous job enjoy a higher standard of living as building workers may be in more demand than teachers; May be geographically immobile unable to move to where the teaching jobs are; May not like the working conditions experienced by teachers e.g. may want flexible working hours / less stress; May be unaware of job vacancies information failure; May lack job vacancies demand for teachers may fall; May enjoy working outside / gain more job satisfaction from working as a builder benefit of fresh air.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how a small firm can compete successfully with a large firm in the same industry.
Markscheme: It may provide a personal service / customised service specialist product / unique product catering for a niche part of the market. It may build up a relationship with customers / develop consumer loyalty / have a good reputation more aware of their customers' requirements good location may be easy to manage. It may be subsidised by the government / given a government grant may be charged a lower rate of (corporate income) tax more revenue / lower cost this could enable it to charge a low / competitive price. It may have a good relationship with workers / good communication with staff increase their motivation. May be innovative produce a high quality product. It may be more flexible responding quickly to changes in market conditions. A large firm may experience diseconomies of scale example of a diseconomy of scale higher average cost / higher cost of production higher prices.
Explanation: Two relatively easy marks to gain are 'lower prices' and 'higher quality'.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in the money supply will benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: raise consumer expenditure / government spending / investment; reduce interest rate; increase total demand; increase economic growth and employment; increase bank lending; provide finance for firms to expand. Why it might not: cause demand-pull inflation / fall in internal value of money; increase imports; current account deficit may increase / current account surplus may fall; may cause a fall in the rate of interest; increase in bank lending may result in some households and firms getting into debt.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Nigeria economy: mixed system, high unemployment and inflation, rising demand for foreign currency
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomic problems
Identify two costs of unemployment.
Markscheme: loss of output / low economic growth; loss of tax revenue / budget deficit; high government spending on benefits; higher government spending on measures to reduce unemployment / training the unemployed; loss of income / poverty / low living standards; loss of skills; health problems / high government spending on healthcare; more crime; movement of a production point inside the PPC / not producing at full capacity.
Explanation: If more than two costs given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain how the key resource allocation decisions are made in a mixed economic system.
Markscheme: Private sector / individuals / households / firms / consumers will use price mechanism demand and supply / demand / market forces influenced by the profit motive use most cost-effective methods of production. Public sector / government / state will use its spending taxation regulation / maximum prices / minimum prices / directives influenced by social costs / social benefits / economic welfare.
Explanation: Maximum of three marks if only one sector is covered. For the last mark: accept, but do not expect reference to 'may carry out a cost-benefit analysis'.

Question Text: Analyse the causes of an increase in the demand for foreign currency.
Markscheme: To buy more goods and services from that country / higher demand by other countries for that country's products increased foreign tourism may be the result of higher inflation in the buying country may be due to removal of trade restrictions rise in quality of the other country's products higher incomes in the buying country. To speculate / increased confidence in the currency that it will rise in price / value make a profit. To invest more in that country e.g. set up a branch of a MNC / FDI MNC sending profit home. To send more money home to relatives workers' remittances. The government / central bank may buy it to influence the exchange rate add to the reserves. To take advantage of a rise in interest rates in that country increase hot money flows increase return on saving / financial investment. More people may migrate to that country to e.g. buy a home in another country.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not inflation will harm a country’s firms.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase price of raw materials and capital goods; increase costs of production; increase menu and shoe leather costs; workers may take industrial action to gain wage rises; increase costs and disrupt output; cost-push inflation; less internationally competitive; profits may fall; may make it difficult to plan. Why it might not: demand-pull inflation can increase sales; revenue may rise by more than costs, increase profits; if lower than rival countries can make products more internationally competitive; if low and stable, may not be a problem; may reduce real value of debts; may be able to reduce real costs of wages.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Finland economy: ageing population, strong banking sector and budget deficit
Question Text: Scenario about exports, lending and fiscal balance
Identify a difference between an export and an import.
Markscheme: Export is a good or service that leaves the country / sold to another country, import is a good or service that enters the country / bought from another country; Export is a credit item in the current account of the balance of payments imports are a debit item; Foreigners spend on exports the home country's population spend on imports; Exports lead to money coming into an economy, while imports lead to money going out of an economy.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain why commercial banks may increase their lending.
Markscheme: To increase profits / revenue earn more interest / high interest rates as most commercial banks are in the private sector main aim is likely to be profit maximisation; Increase in demand for loans due to low interest rates increase optimism about the future higher demand for bank loans may increase profits; Increased confidence loans will be repaid due to e.g. rise in incomes; Increase in bank deposits more money to lend; Take advantage of economies of scale lower average costs; Central bank / government encouragement / (expansionary) monetary policy / instructions to increase total demand / increase consumer spending / increase investment; To promote social welfare by reducing poverty; To keep up with competitors attract more customers / gain more market share.
Explanation: Reward but do not expect reference to reduced required liquidity ratio and quantitative easing. One mark in total for either high or low interest rates but explanation marks can be given.

Question Text: Analyse how an ageing population may affect a country’s economic growth rate.
Markscheme: May increase economic growth / GDP / output as an ageing population may have experience more productive / more efficient / more skilled; May have more spending power increase total demand; May train young workers to be more reliable / older workers may be more reliable; May reduce economic growth / GDP / output as may have out-of-date skills / not able to operate new technology lack mobility; More retired people / more economically inactive people may mean a decrease in size of the labour force increase dependency ratio / more dependents; May save more reducing total demand; May be less healthy workers more days absent from work due to sickness; The government may have to spend more on state benefits / pensions / healthcare lower tax revenue reducing spending on items that could increase economic growth e.g. education, infrastructure / opportunity cost.
Explanation: 1 mark for either increase and / or decrease economic growth. Similarly, 1 mark for either more productive and / or less productive labour force, more or less saving etc. Not crediting comments on the change in the pattern of consumer spending e.g. spending less on toys and more on teeth implants.

Question Text: Discuss whether supply-side policy measures will reduce a government’s budget deficit.
Markscheme: Why they might: education and training may raise productivity; increase economic growth and employment; raise direct and indirect tax revenue; privatisation may reduce government spending to support state-owned firms; revenue will be raised from the sale of privatised firms; privatised firms may earn high profits which may raise tax revenue; subsidies may encourage firms to expand and earn higher profits; unemployment benefit may be cut to increase incentive to work; budget deficit may be increased in short term but reduced in the long term. Why they might not: education and training will increase government spending and may not be successful; lower tax rates may reduce tax revenue; regulation will involve costs to enforce; spending on infrastructure projects may involve high government spending; sale of profitable government-owned firms to the private sector may reduce government revenue from profits.
Explanation: Only accept a lower interest rate if linked to encouraging a rise in investment. Answers that show an awareness of supply-side policy measures but which confuse a budget deficit with a bop deficit will not be analysing the question, so will be in Level 1. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Gabon economy: reliance on oil, timber and agriculture, rainforest conservation, tourism growth, youth unemployment and plans to expand textile industry
Question Text: Source material about Gabon’s industries and labour force
Calculate the value of Gabon’s oil production as a percentage of Gabon’s GDP.
Explanation: Accept 30 without %.

Question Text: Identify two external benefits of rainforests.
Markscheme: reduce air pollution / pollution / improves air quality; protect against floods; prevent soil erosion.
Explanation: If more than two benefits given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain what effect an increase in tourism may have on Gabon’s current account of the balance of payments.
Markscheme: Increase in exports; increase trade in services (invisible) surplus / reduces deficit; Improves the current account balance surplus / reduces deficit.
Explanation: No marks for simply saying current account of the balance of payments increases.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why the Gabonese Government wants to reduce reliance on oil.
Markscheme: Running out of oil / create a shortage revenue from the industry will decline / employment in the industry will decline / GDP will decline / cause recession / conserve for future generations / may need to import oil / price for oil may rise; Oil industry creates water pollution / pollution an external cost / harms health / reduces standard of living / protect the environment / attract tourists.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. Only these two reasons are acceptable.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of the discovery of new gold deposits on the market for gold.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New supply curve shifted to the right; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between the literacy rate and the percentage of the labour force employed in agriculture.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Generally, an inverse / negative relationship the higher the literacy rate, the smaller the % of the labour force employed in agriculture. Supporting evidence: South Africa had the highest literacy rate and the lowest % of the labour force employed in agriculture; Zambia / Sierra Leone / Mali had the lowest literacy rates and the highest % of labour force employed in agriculture. Analysis of expected relationship: A high literacy rate may increase the chance of workers gaining jobs in high paid tertiary jobs a low literacy rate may mean workers have to undertake unskilled jobs in agriculture / a high proportion of workers employed in agriculture may mean that high literacy rate is not required. Exception: Gabon Gabon has a high literacy rate but also a high % of the labour force employed in agriculture. Analysis of exception: Some jobs in agriculture are skilled / secondary and tertiary sectors may be small.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Supporting evidence should involve interpretation, not just description. Simply stating percentages without analysis of being high or low gets no marks. Accept as analysis where two countries with different levels of adult literacy and percentage employed in agriculture are directly compared.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should pay private sector firms to give work experience to unemployed young people.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should: can enable young workers to develop their skills / raise their productivity increase their confidence / motivation to get a job / get higher paid jobs young people may make mistakes and cost firm money while being trained can increase the chances of young people gaining a job reduce unemployment reduce the cost of unemployment benefits reduce future training costs increase tax revenue leads to economic growth. Reasons why it should not: the quality of the work experience may not be good the young may be given unskilled jobs poor working conditions / not regulated opportunity cost [implied] government spending could be used to e.g. improve state education government incurs additional expenditure / raises taxes to pay for it the young may have been employed by the private sector without payment by the government it may reduce the efficiency of private firms reduce their output firms may be corrupt.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a growth in the size of firms in Gabon’s textile industry will reduce their average cost of production.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: labour economies workers can specialise / increase labour productivity; managerial economies employ specialists; technical economies using advanced capital equipment; buying / purchasing economies receiving a discount as buying in bulk; selling economies reducing transport costs; marketing economies spreads fixed costs over more output; financial economies easier / cheaper to borrow; research and development economies introduce new products / new production methods; risk bearing economies able to suffer fall in demand for one product or supply problems connected to one product; may experience external economies of scale example. Reasons why it might not: difficulty of controlling a large firm may be slower to make decisions; communication problems may be more difficult to keep everyone informed; poor industrial relations may be less contact between managers and workers / decreasing productivity.
Explanation: Maximum of 2 marks for just listing different types of economies and diseconomies of scale or just stating economies of scale and diseconomies of scale may be experienced without references to examples. Essentially full marks can be gained for identification and explanation of two economies of scale and one diseconomy of scale OR one economy of scale and two diseconomies of scale. Note there is no marks awarded for simply stating that average costs may or rise or for drawing a diagram showing average costs changing as a firm grows in size.

Stimulus / Scenario: Colombia population changes, income inequality and footwear industry in a mixed economic system
Question Text: Scenario about inequality and demand for products
Define population pyramid.
Markscheme: age; gender
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two reasons why income may become more unevenly distributed.
Markscheme: A rise in: Unemployment those who lose their jobs are likely to experience a fall in their income; Illness the sick may not be able to earn an income; Profits benefitting entrepreneurs; Interest rates benefitting those with a high value of savings; Wealth with rich people gaining more interest/profit; Regressive taxation reducing the income of the poor/placing greater burden on poor; Discrimination of workers example e.g. reducing income of women; Education provision / attainment but only for some. A fall in: National minimum wage the low-paid experience a fall in income; Progressive taxation increases the income of the rich; Welfare / state benefits reduce the income of the poor. Growth: Economic growth benefits go more to skilled than to unskilled workers; Changes in growth rate of different industries workers in expanding industries may earn more in wages than workers in declining industries; Changes in growth rate of different regions workers in high growth areas may earn higher wages than in low growth / declining regions; Changes in growth rate of private and public sector private sector earn higher increase in wages.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three. MAX of two marks if not identifying what changes.

Question Text: Analyse the influences on whether demand for shoes is elastic or inelastic.
Markscheme: Shoes have substitutes in the form of sandals and boots may make demand (price) elastic as a change in price will cause consumers to switch between different forms of footwear especially if the types are close substitutes / close in price. PED of particular brands of shoes will be more elastic than demand for shoes as a whole. Proportion of income spent on the shoes the lower the proportion, the more inelastic the demand as a change in price will not have such a significant impact on demand. Whether shoes are considered to be a necessity or a luxury shoes are likely to be considered a necessity and therefore inelastic. It may not be possible to delay the purchase of shoes making demand inelastic. Durability of shoes the more durable the more inelastic. Demand for some shoe may be addictive making demand inelastic.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a mixed economic system will benefit the people who live in that economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: public goods may be provided; production of demerit goods may be discouraged; production of merit goods may be encouraged; state benefits may be provided; income may be redistributed to help the poor; unemployment may be kept low by government policies; private sector benefits e.g. generate high incomes / lower prices. Why it might not: may be market failure in the private sector; rich may lose if there is high progressive taxation; high taxation may discourage enterprise; state-owned enterprises may be inefficient; may be less choice in the public sector.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Latvia changes in factors of production, falling enterprise and rising minimum wage
Question Text: Scenario about supply conditions and labour markets
Identify the rewards to capital and land.
Markscheme: Interest; rent
Explanation: If just names the two rewards, they can be in any order.

Question Text: Explain two causes of a decrease in enterprise in an economy.
Markscheme: Decrease in profit / increase in corporate income (corporation) tax increase in interest rates for borrowing reduce financial incentive to become an entrepreneur; Reduction in education reduce those with the skills to become entrepreneurs; Reduction in immigration / reduction in population reduce the number of people who can become entrepreneurs; Entrepreneurs move abroad better prospects / less tax; Reduction in the retirement age reduces supply of potential entrepreneurs; Recession / war firms may go out of business / reduces willingness to take risks / reduces confidence / certainty; Increased government regulation excessive rules may discourage entrepreneurs; Nationalisation of an industry / growth of monopolies removal of entrepreneurs.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two causes identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two causes given, consider the first three. MAX of two marks if not identifying what changes.

Question Text: Analyse how changes in conditions of supply can affect a firm’s prices.
Markscheme: Increase in costs of production due to rise in wages / raw material costs / capital goods costs / tariffs raise price to maintain profit; Natural disasters can create a shortage raise price; Changes in weather good weather can create a surplus in supply e.g. agriculture prices fall or poor weather can create a shortage prices rise; Increase in indirect tax / corporate income (corporation) tax a firm may pass this onto consumers as higher prices especially if demand is inelastic; Government subsidies to firms reduce the cost of production the firm may be able to gain the same revenue and profit with a lower price; Discovery of new source of raw materials enabling the firm to supply more at a lower price; Advances in technology example could lower costs of production reducing price; Improvements in education and training raise productivity lower prices.
Explanation: Only allow one mark for reference to a rise in price and one mark for reference to a fall in price. MAX 4 marks if not identifying change in conditions of supply.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a rise in a national minimum wage will harm an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase unemployment if firms cannot afford to pay higher wages; higher unemployment would reduce total demand; lower total demand would reduce output; tax revenue may fall, and expenditure on state benefits may rise; if higher wages are paid, there may be inflation. Why it might not: reduce poverty as raise the income of the low-paid; encourage more people to enter the labour force; increase productivity as workers may be more motivated; encourage firms to train workers to gain better return; may be set below the existing equilibrium wage level.
Explanation: Accurate diagram can achieve Level 1. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: India population growth, corporate tax cuts and inflation target
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomic policy
Identify two macroeconomic aims of government that may conflict.
Markscheme: Full employment / low unemployment and price stability; Economic growth current account balance; Price stability redistribution of income / poverty reduction; Economic growth economic sustainability.
Explanation: If more than two macroeconomic aims given, consider the first three. Allow any macroeconomic aim.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a country may have a high population growth rate.
Markscheme: High birth rate due to e.g. low age of marriage / lack of education on birth control; Low death rate / high life expectancy due to e.g. good quality healthcare; Immigration /net immigration due to e.g. high incomes; High fertility rates due to e.g. young female population; Birth rate higher than the death rate results in higher natural growth rate.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how a cut in the corporation tax rate can increase economic growth.
Markscheme: Cut in corporation (corporate income) tax will increase the amount of profits that can be kept by firms may attract MNCs to country this will increase the funds firms have to spend on capital goods and increase incentive to invest more to spend on wages may create job opportunities / reduce unemployment raise consumer expenditure firms grow faster. Higher investment will increase total demand. It may also increase the quality of capital goods may introduce new technology may increase productivity /productive capacity increase output / GDP. Provide an incentive to enterprise as more profits can be retained / higher profits.
Explanation: Do not credit answers that refer to income tax or any other tax than corporation tax.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a decrease in government spending will reduce inflation.
Markscheme: Why it might: may reduce total demand; lower total demand may reduce demand-pull inflation; if near full employment, lower demand may have a significant impact on inflation; may reduce need for taxes, lower indirect taxes may reduce prices.
