Why it might not: lower government subsidies may raise prices; lower government spending on education and training may reduce productivity and may increase cost of production; lower government spending on infrastructure could increase transport costs; higher costs of production may increase cost-push inflation; may be other causes of inflation e.g. rise in wages.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Australia multinational companies, low tariffs, floating exchange rate and rising HDI
Question Text: Scenario about trade and investment
Identify two benefits an MNC may provide for its home country.
Markscheme: profits; (increased) exports; raw materials; job opportunity / wages for workers; raise country's income
Explanation: If more than two benefits given, consider the first three. Identify means that the benefit does not have to be explained.

Question Text: Explain two disadvantages of import tariffs.
Markscheme: Reduce (free) trade lower competition; Risk of retaliation resulting in trade war / unemployment; Raise price of imports which may raise price of domestically produced goods (because of the lack of competition); Raise the price of imported raw materials / capital goods cause cost-push inflation; Reduce quality due to lower competition; Protected industries / infant industries / declining industries may become inefficient if become reliant on tariff (protection); Reduce output / consumption / less quality / less choice lower living standards; Reduce ability of countries to specialise less efficient use of resources / loss of potential economies of scale.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two disadvantages identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two disadvantages given, consider the first three. Only allow one mark for reference to lower competition.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in investment can result in a rise in a country’s HDI value.
Markscheme: Investment can increase GDP / GNI employment which may increase GDP / GNI per head higher GDP / GNI may raise tax revenue enabling the government to spend more on education and healthcare. Higher personal income / wages enabling families to afford sending children to school pay for private healthcare. An increase in investment in healthcare facilities can increase life expectancy. An increase in investment in education will increase mean / expected and expected years of schooling increase skills/productivity which may increase GDP per head raise awareness about nutrition increase life expectancy. An increase in investment may introduce more advanced technology which can make work less physically demanding improving health / life expectancy.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not having a floating exchange rate benefits an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: may eliminate a current account imbalance if there is a current account deficit, exchange rate may fall making exports fall in price and imports rise in price; no need to keep reserves of foreign exchange; no need to change interest rates to influence the exchange rate; enable government to concentrate on other aims as does not have to keep the exchange rate fixed. Why it might not: may make it difficult for firms to plan, not knowing how much they will have to pay for imports and earn from exports; may create uncertainty; discourage investment; speculation can cause (significant) destabilising changes in the exchange rate.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt. Note: reward candidates if they refer to the impact of appreciation and depreciation of the currency in their answers.

Stimulus / Scenario: St. Kitts and Nevis economy: tourism, financial services, agriculture, fishing industry, deflation and environmental pressures
Question Text: Source material about the future of the economy of St. Kitts and Nevis
Calculate St. Kitts and Nevis’s government budget deficit in 2022.
Identify two examples of the factor of production ‘land’ in St. Kitts and Nevis.
Markscheme: beaches; (coral) reefs; marine life / fish; rainforests; soil.
Explanation: If more than two examples given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain how tourism causes external costs in St. Kitts and Nevis.
Markscheme: Social costs are greater than private costs / social costs include private costs and external costs / harmful effects on third parties / those not directly involved in producing or consuming the product. Example of an external cost caused by tourism in St Kitts and Nevis e.g. pollution / environmental damage / death of wildlife / death of marine life / overcrowding.
Explanation: One mark for explanation of external costs. One mark for example based on tourism.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a foreign bank may open a branch in St. Kitts and Nevis.
Markscheme: High incomes in the country high demand for banking services / high demand for loans / may save more / borrowers likely to be able to repay loans / high profit / high revenue; Absence (low) of personal income tax workers would be able to keep all of their income / more disposable income / firms may be able to pay lower wages / increase workers' motivation; Relatively high literacy rates awareness of benefits of having a bank account / saving / skilled workers / high productivity.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three. Most of the explanation points e.g. high profit could be linked to any of the three identification points except high disposable income should be linked to absence of personal income tax. Do not give two explanation marks for one identification. Explanation marks here are dependent on the identifications.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of an increase in the productivity of onion farmers on the market for onions.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New supply curve shifted to the right; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between healthcare spending as a percentage of GDP and life expectancy.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Direct/positive relationship. Generally, the higher healthcare spending, the higher the life expectancy. Supporting evidence: E.g.: Haiti has the second lowest % healthcare spending and the shortest life expectancy; Canada spends a higher % on healthcare than Egypt and has a longer life expectancy / Canada spends the highest percentage on health care and has the second highest longest life expectancy / Canada and Sweden spend a high % on healthcare and have long life expectancy. Analysis of the supporting information: Higher spending on healthcare would be expected to raise the health of the population / provide better quality healthcare increase awareness of need for healthy living / provide more check-ups / preventative medical care / reduce the death rate. Exception: Monaco spends the lowest % on healthcare but has the longest life expectancy. Analysis of exception: 1.9% of a very high GDP can mean higher spending than e.g. 12.9% of a lower GDP / Monaco may have a high GDP. There are other influences on life expectancy e.g. nutrition / education/housing/war.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected/normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Supporting evidence should involve interpretation, not just description.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in the size of its fishing industry would benefit St. Kitts and Nevis.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: may reduce imports of fish reduce risk of supply-side shocks / imports being cut off enable fish to be exported may improve the current account balance / improve the balance of payments; may increase employment / increase job opportunities / reduce unemployment raise incomes increase living standards increase GDP / increase output / cause economic growth higher tax revenue; may enable greater advantage to be taken of economies of scale example lower average cost lower fish prices; (larger boats) may be more efficient / more productive make the industry more profitable / increase profits from fishing. Reasons why it might not: opportunity cost could use resources in other industries / government can spend more on e.g. education / increase budget deficit; over-fishing deplete fish stocks / lose marine life / deplete resources raise fish prices in the long run; may experience diseconomies of scale example higher average cost reduce profit; may cause external costs / environmental harm may damage coral reefs / cause pollution harm tourism; may be a dangerous occupation may be low-paid low-skilled may experience seasonal unemployment; risk of over specialisation / too great a dependency on the industry may be harmed by a fall in demand for fish / rise in competition from other countries.
Explanation: Award one mark in total for higher / lower average costs, one mark for lower / higher fish prices, one mark for higher / lower profits. Higher / lower profits should be linked to the fishing industry.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not deflation is likely to have harmed St. Kitts and Nevis.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: may have increased real value of debt may default may have decreased investment; may have resulted in a deflationary spiral may have resulted from lower total demand lower GDP / lower output / reduce economic growth / may cause a recession lower employment / increased unemployment lowered wages / income; may reduce confidence may discourage MNCs; may reduce consumer expenditure / save more / create uncertainty delay buying goods and services (until price is lower). Reasons why it might not: may have increased purchasing power / real income / with the same income more goods and services can be purchased (some) people may spend / consume more; value of saving may have increased increasing wealth; may be the result of higher total supply due to advances in technology / lower costs of production higher output / economic growth higher employment; may have increased international competitiveness of its products / lower export prices increased exports / export revenue reduced imports / import expenditure improve current account balance.
Explanation: Award one mark for malign (bad) deflation is harmful whereas benign (good) deflation is beneficial. Mark for higher employment and mark for higher GDP can only be awarded if come from higher total supply. 'save more' may be credited on either side but only once.

Stimulus / Scenario: Global accountancy firms experiencing lower demand and labour mobility affecting employment
Question Text: Scenario about mergers, labour mobility and job losses
Identify two advantages a firm may gain from merging with another firm.
Markscheme: reduce competition / increase market power / increase market share / may become a monopoly / become a price maker / higher barriers to entry; gain economies of scale / reduce (average) cost / increased efficiency / higher productivity; gain control over outlets; gain control over raw materials; rationalisation; greater profit / revenue; new ideas / access to improved technology; spread risks; more resources / more labour / more capital / more enterprise / more land; higher demand / more sales / larger size of firm; gain consumer data
Explanation: If more than two advantages given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two disadvantages workers may experience as a result of losing their jobs.
Markscheme: Loss of income resulting in lower living standards / lower quality of life / poverty / inability to buy necessities / lower purchasing power / lower spending / lower consumption / lower spending on a particular good or services e.g. education/healthcare/housing/holidays; Loss of skills may need to gain new skills / as miss out on training / make it more difficult to get another job / lower paid job / reduced mobility of labour; May be more likely to get into debt harder to repay any past loans; Harder to get a loan banks may not expect them to be able to repay loan; Loss of fringe benefits example e.g. health insurance / home; Less attractive to future employers / bad reputation make it more difficult to get another job; Mental health problems / stress / illness / divorce loss of self-esteem / loss of self-confidence / making it more difficult to get another job / reduced life expectancy / due to difficulty/time to find another job; May have to sell possessions to gain money / to meet bills; Demotivation give up looking for work / become a discouraged worker / become voluntarily unemployed; Reduced ability to save reduced ability to cope with unexpected events / to finance e.g. education / healthcare / retirement.
Explanation: One mark each for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons given, consider the first three. Reward 'making it more difficult to get another job' only once.

Question Text: Analyse the advantages of an increase in labour mobility.
Markscheme: An increase in the ability of workers to change jobs / provide more choice of jobs occupational mobility of labour improve skills / productivity / experience by changing jobs; An increase in the ability of workers to move from one area to another geographical mobility of labour move to a more pleasant area; Can reduce structural / regional / frictional unemployment enable workers to get new jobs more quickly / reduce long term unemployment may enable some workers to find higher paid jobs / gain higher wages / reduce poverty / higher standard of living / better working conditions raise tax revenue / reduce government spending on benefits; It can reduce labour shortages reduce costs / cost-push inflation increase output / increase GDP / cause economic growth; It can enable firms to respond more quickly/more fully to changes in consumer demand / increase flexibility / change in size reduce surpluses and shortages of products.
Explanation: Reward but do not expect reference to reduce labour market failure.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the demand for chocolate is likely to be price elastic.
Markscheme: Why it might: may have substitutes; may be seen as a luxury; PED of particular brands will be more elastic than demand for chocolate as a whole; the longer the time period, the easier it will be for consumers to change their spending patterns. Why it might not: may be addictive; may be seen as a necessity; may take up a small % of income.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Paraguay economy: PPC growth, merit and public goods, shampoo industry structure
Question Text: Scenario about production and market structure
Identify what is measured on the axes of a production possibility curve.
Markscheme: (Output of) two types of product.
Explanation: Output one type of product another type of product. Two types of products may be shown on the axes on a PPC diagram for two marks. If more than two items are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain, with an example of each, the difference between a merit good and a public good.
Markscheme: A merit good is under-consumed / underproduced / consumers do not appreciate its full value / has external benefits / can be produced by the private sector / can be produced by the private sector and the public sector / is a private good / is rival and excludable e.g. education / healthcare / fruit. A public good would not be produced by the private sector / is (usually) supplied / produced by the government / funded by the government non-excludable / non-rival e.g. street lighting / defence / flood control.
Explanation: One mark for an explanation of a merit good and one mark for an example of a merit good. One mark for an explanation of a public good and one mark for an example of a public good. Accept but do not expect reference to non-rejectable / zero cost of producing an extra unit for a public good. Allow public park. No mark for roads unless qualified by e.g. uncrowded roads with many entry and exit points.

Question Text: Analyse how division of labour can benefit firms.
Markscheme: Workers can specialise in what they are best at producing save time / speed up production reduce waste reduce mistakes increase productivity / skill / efficiency increase output / supply lower (average) cost; Increase quality lower prices raise demand / increase sales increase revenue increase profits; Make it easier to mechanise production reduce labour required; Reduce training / training costs as reduce tasks undertaken / division of labour involves breaking production down into different tasks.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not consumers would benefit from only one firm producing shampoo.
Markscheme: Why they might: one firm may be able to benefit from economies of scale; lower average cost may lead to lower prices; firm may spend a large amount on research and development due to high profits and security; quality of product may be high; less time / less effort choosing shampoo. Why they might not: lack of competition may drive up prices; lack of choice; if quality is poor, cannot switch to substitutes; firm may experience diseconomies of scale.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Sri Lanka economic crisis with inflation, migration of workers and higher taxation
Question Text: Scenario about migration and unemployment
Identify two types of unemployment.
Markscheme: Frictional; structural; cyclical / demand deficient
Explanation: If more than two types given, consider the first three. Also accept voluntary unemployment, involuntary unemployment, seasonal, classical, casual, regional, technological. Accept demand-deficient as an alternative to cyclical.

Question Text: Explain two ways that money received from relatives working abroad could raise their families’ living standards.
Markscheme: Could spend more on food / buy more products / buy more basic necessities have higher purchasing power / more income / could increase overall spending reduce poverty; Could spend more on better quality food improve nutrition / improve health; Could spend money on education / training increase earning potential / wages; Could spend on healthcare raise life expectancy; Could spend on housing reduce illness; May be able to afford luxuries e.g. may be able to afford a holiday / car; Could set up a small business which could earn them an income; Could pay off debts / reduce need to borrow / reduce cost of borrowing reduce stress; Could save more earn interest / pay for e.g. healthcare/education of children in the future; Could work fewer hours / fewer jobs less stress.
Explanation: One mark for each of two ways identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two ways given, consider the first three. No marks for why the money received may be large due to e.g. lower tax rates abroad, high exchange rate.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in direct taxation could increase unemployment.
Markscheme: (Increase in) (personal) income tax (increase in) tax on wealth would reduce disposable income / purchasing power / money available / the amount of their wages they can keep reduce consumer spending lower total demand lower demand for workers as demand for workers depends on demand for goods and services / derived demand increase cyclical unemployment. Reduce incentive to work / encourage workers to leave their jobs may now rely on unemployment benefit / as may receive more from unemployment benefit than from a now lower paid job increase frictional/voluntary unemployment. (Increase in) corporation (corporate income) tax will reduce the amount of profit that can be kept / reduce profit firms may reduce output / reduce economic growth / may cause a recession / reduce supply / less incentive to expand reduce investment result in cyclical unemployment may reduce employment to cut costs (to maintain profit).
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a doctor would benefit from working in another country.
Markscheme: Why they might: may be higher paid, higher living standards; may benefit from lower inflation; may benefit from lower taxes; may be more chances of gaining a job or gaining a promoted post; may have better working conditions; may have more fringe benefits; may gain new skills and experience; may be safer environment; may be able to send money home to family; may provide better opportunities for their children. Why they might not: may have longer working hours; may have less holidays; may have less job security; may miss friends / relatives; may face discrimination; may face language problems/ cultural differences.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: China economic policy goals: growth, trade expansion, inflation risk and ageing population
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomic policy
Identify two influences on household spending.
Markscheme: income; interest rate; taxation; confidence; inflation rate / cost of living / prices / price; wealth; age; size of household; culture; advertising; quality; tastes
Explanation: If more than two influences given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two advantages of employing older workers.
Markscheme: May be more experienced may have high productivity / high efficiency / high skills; May make few mistakes produce high quality products; May be reliable / easy to manage punctual / carry out duties carefully; May need little training / may have received more training keep firms' costs low; May be less likely to take maternity / paternity leave less disruption to output / lower costs; May train young workers pass on experience; May be less likely to leave job in search of a better paid job; May keep older people healthy lowering healthcare costs / increasing life expectancy; May increase the incomes of older people reducing poverty; May increase the size of the labour force encourage more older workers to seek employment / increase productive potential; Make older people more financially independent reduce financial burden on family / government; May be prepared to work fewer hours make firms more flexible; May be prepared to work for relatively low wages in order to e.g. stay active; May relate better to older customers a growing market in most countries.
Explanation: One mark for each of two advantages identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two advantages given, consider the first three. There is an element of mix and match here with some explanations being linked with different identifications but do not reward the same point twice. Award increase in incomes rather than increase in wages.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in household spending could cause inflation.
Markscheme: Household spending / consumer expenditure / consumption is a component of total demand. An increase in household spending would increase total demand. Total demand could exceed total supply higher demand may encourage firms to raise prices resulting in demand-pull inflation. An increase in household spending could encourage firms to invest / increase investment increase their output they may hire more workers this may drive up wages increase demand for raw materials / capital goods raise price of raw materials / capital goods increase costs of production cause cost-push inflation cause a wage price spiral.
Explanation: Formula AD = C + I + G + (X - M) can be taken as recognition that C is a component on total demand. Higher total (aggregate) demand and prices being pushed up can be shown on a diagram - one mark each. Reward but do not expect reference to the multiplier effect.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should aim for an increase in imports.
Markscheme: Why it should: may save scarce resources; may reduce shortages in the home market; may lower the exchange rate; may increase choice and quality of products in the home market; may import technology; may reduce firms' costs by importing low price raw materials and capital goods; may increase competition, making domestic firms more efficient; may increase revenue from import tariffs; may enable a country to consume more than it produces in the short term; may increase incomes abroad raising demand for this country's exports. Why it should not: may worsen the current account balance; may cause some domestic firms to go out of business; may reduce exchange rate; may reduce employment; may lower incomes / GDP per head; may lower living standards in the long term; will not want to import demerit goods.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Egyptian economy: tourism, building boom, investment, deregulation in education, currency depreciation
Question Text: Source material about the Egyptian economy
Calculate the number of people who are in poverty in Egypt.
Markscheme: 30.69 million
Explanation: 30 690 000. Accept 30.7 million.

Question Text: Identify two of Egypt’s primary sector exports.
Markscheme: oil; citrus fruits; gold
Explanation: Accept any two

Question Text: Explain how building a new city can reduce unemployment.
Markscheme: Building a new city requires labour / creates job opportunities / increases demand for labour to work in construction / build new buildings.
Explanation: Only one mark if not linked to building.

Question Text: Explain why tourism is a good source of economic growth for Egypt.
Markscheme: Egypt has many ancient / human-made attractions and buildings not found in many parts of the world / that tourists want to visit creating more employment / tax revenue; Egypt has natural attractions, such as beautiful beaches / warm climate / good weather these attract tourists / low government spending required / low investments required; Egypt has good infrastructure this can attract tourists as its easier to travel to the country or attractions / can attract investors with lower cost of production; Egypt has good security tourists feel safe / investors are confident in the security of their investments; Egypt has experienced a fall its currency value making holidays more affordable.
Explanation: Award a maximum of 2 marks for just identification of points without explanation.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the likely effect of falling incomes on the market for housing in the new city.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the left; Equilibrium – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between investment (as a % of GDP) and economic growth rates for Egypt.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Positive / direct higher / lower investment should lead to higher / lower economic growth rates. Supporting evidence: From 2017–2019, Investment rising and economic growth also rising; From 2019–2020, investment falling and economic growth also falling. Analysis: Higher investment leads to higher economic growth due to higher total demand / investment is a component of total demand and increased productivity / lower cost of production. Exception: identification of an exception 2015–2017 or 2020–21; 2015–2017, investment rising but economic growth falling slightly / relatively stable / largely unchanged; 2020–2021, investment falling but economic growth rate rising. Analysis of the exception: Current investment increase now could lead to a delayed response in economic growth rates data shows percentage not total investment.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected / normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not deregulation in education in Egypt will increase living standards.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: deregulation has led to an increased number of schools / universities in Egypt may increase competition between private schools / universities may lower fees raise quality; more people have opportunities for education and training making people more educated / skilled / qualified / literate / productive easier to find jobs earn an income increasing affordability of goods and services more educated people tend to enjoy better health / spend more on healthcare longer life expectancy increase HDI; more school buildings will increase employment of builders teachers; may reduce government spending on education allowing the government to spend more on items that could increase living standards e.g. healthcare. Reasons why it might not: opening more private schools and universities in Egypt might increase the level of inequality as private education remains unaffordable to lower income groups restricting their employment opportunities as private education is usually more expensive than state-provided education; standards of living only improve for those from higher income groups lower income groups remain in relative poverty widen gap between rich and poor / increase relative poverty; private schools and universities may prioritise profits over the quality / standards of education provided.
Explanation: Answers not referring to education, max 3 marks.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a fall in the value of the Egyptian currency would harm the Egyptian economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: fall in the value of the Egyptian currency may cause cost push inflation reducing the purchasing power of Egyptians as imported goods become more expensive standards of living could decrease; cost of production could increase increasing the price of domestically produced goods some firms may try to cut cost by reducing jobs increase unemployment; may create uncertainty / reduce confidence discourage investment. Reasons why it might not: Egyptian exports become cheaper increasing demand for exports / increasing revenues of domestic producers / incomes causing economic growth demand may switch from imports to domestically produced products may reduce current account deficit; industries such as the tourism industry may benefit as holidays in Egypt become more affordable for non-Egyptians; workers also have more job opportunities since there is more demand for domestically produced goods and services; may increase investment / hot money flows reduced cost to set up an MNC / may expect profit if currency value rises later.
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: Montenegro road construction supported by foreign bank and MNC
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomic aims and international trade
Define international trade.
Markscheme: Movement / exchange of goods and services / products between countries. Exports and imports of goods and services / products.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain the importance of commercial banks to governments.
Markscheme: facilitating transactions for the government e.g. managing government payments to suppliers / workers / receive tax payments; financing government borrowing commercial banks may also lend money to the government / buy bonds from government; lending to the private sector enables investment facilitating economic growth may increase employment; may help to maintain financial stability by helping other commercial banks in trouble; they influence the money supply by lending / creating credit.
Explanation: Accept the role of commercial banks in helping implement monetary policy they are likely to change their interest rates in line with that of the central bank.

Question Text: Analyse how building roads can help a government achieve its macroeconomic aims.
Markscheme: Building roads may involve government spending creates jobs / requires labour such as road builders reducing unemployment increase income increase consumption support other industries such as raw material suppliers / private contractors which increases total demand in the economy. Building roads may lead to reduced transport costs / cheaper transport due to greater efficiency / productivity / time saving reducing overall costs of production increasing investment / attracting MNCs causing economic growth increased exports improving the current account balance reducing cost-push inflation. Building roads could lead to better quality transportation / easier access to places of work reducing unemployment may reduce poverty.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not MNCs benefit host countries.
Markscheme: Why MNCs might benefit host countries: bring in new capital and technology; bring in investment; create jobs; may provide high quality training; pay taxes; may increase competition; may buy raw materials from the country. Why MNCs might not benefit host countries: may exploit domestic labour by poor work conditions; may take profits out of the country and not reinvest domestically; may avoid taxes; may dominate domestic market and drive local firms out of the market; are footloose and may leave anytime – more instability; may deplete natural resources; may damage the environment.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Andorra healthcare, biotechnology and winter sports industries; mergers in sports industry
Question Text: Scenario about market competition and economic development
Define public sector.
Markscheme: Sector which is owned / managed / economic decisions made by the government.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two types of merger.
Markscheme: Horizontal merger where two or more firms in the same industry at the same stage of production merge / relevant example; Vertical merger where two or more firms in the same industry but different stage of production merge / relevant example; Conglomerate where two or more firms in different industries merge / relevant example.
Explanation: Accept, but do not expect, explanation of lateral mergers. Allow vertical backwards and vertical forwards as two different types of merger.

Question Text: Analyse how differences in healthcare can lead to differences in economic development between countries.
Markscheme: If healthcare is better in one country than another, workers will be healthier / take less time off sick the productivity of labour may be higher leading to higher incomes of workers improved standard of living (for workers) longer life expectancy / lower death rate higher HDI. Firms may gain higher profits due to lower average cost / higher revenue higher quality output productivity capacity of the economy will increase leading to economic growth exports may increase due to higher competitiveness investment may increase creating more jobs reducing unemployment increasing incomes and reducing (child) poverty may increase spending on education.
Explanation: The question can be analysed from either poorer or better healthcare provision in one country compared with another but the same point should not be rewarded twice.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not competitive markets are beneficial for consumers.
Markscheme: Why it might: more firms in the market leads to lower price; higher quality; more choice; firms may be smaller and so may not experience diseconomies of scale; more innovation in terms of production methods and products. Why it might not: lower profits for firms – less innovation and quality improvement; too many choices – takes up consumer time and effort; firms may spend on advertising, increasing their costs and prices; lower quality due to race to the bottom; firms may be smaller and may not experience economies of scale; competition may lead to resource depletion / negative externalities.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Tall buildings and economic growth in cities
Question Text: Scenario about demand, supply and cost limits of skyscrapers
Define total cost.
Markscheme: Total cost is addition of all costs / overall costs fixed cost plus variable cost.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two causes of an increase in the supply of a product.
Markscheme: lower costs e.g. cost of raw materials / greater potential profitability; increased productivity e.g. due to better technology; reduced regulations easier / cheaper to produce more; reduced taxes reducing cost of production; increased subsidies enabling producers to be able to produce more with the same amount of financial capital / money / cover some costs / provide additional revenue; better weather / more suitable season increasing crop yield / less destruction or damage to crops; increases in stocks / inventories.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark for each of two explanations. If more than two causes given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse factors that can increase the willingness and ability of consumers to buy a certain product.
Markscheme: decrease in the price of the product itself / subsidy makes the product more affordable increasing demand; increase in the price of substitutes which are goods that provide the similar levels of satisfaction / example of substitutes increasing demand; decrease in the price of complements goods that are in joint demand / example of complements increasing demand; increase in expected price in future consumers willing to buy more now when prices are lower instead of the future when prices go up; increase in incomes consumers more able to buy the product now since affordability / purchasing power increase; change in trends / popularity some products become more fashionable / more popular; increase in quality making the product more desirable; increase in or more effective advertising / marketing informing consumers about the product persuading consumers to buy the product; increased peer pressure to buy the product e.g. conspicuous consumption / to keep in with friends; government health campaigns encouraging consumption of merit goods; change in seasons / weather some products become more needed / desired at certain times relevant example.
Explanation: Maximum of two marks for just listing different reasons for increase in willingness and ability of consumers to buy a certain product. Reward increase in demand only once and reward decrease in demand only once.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not economic growth will improve living standards in a city.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase job opportunities in the city – increase income; increase government revenue – more public and merit goods for the city; increase amount of investment in the city – more choice of goods and services. Why it might not: increase negative externalities such as pollution and congestion; overcrowding as more people attracted to come to cities; increase inequality; may produce inflationary pressures – reducing affordability.
Explanation: Maximum of four marks if no reference to a city. Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: African Space Agency and international trade coordination
Question Text: Scenario about the basic economic problem and government spending
Define the basic economic problem.
Markscheme: The basic economic problem is the scarcity of resources / factors of production compared to unlimited wants of humans.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain how a market allocates resources.
Markscheme: A market is made up of buyers and sellers who trade products through the interaction of demand and supply creating market equilibrium. Price signals / price mechanism indicate to sellers how to allocate their resources profit provides the incentive for sellers to respond to changes in demand. If demand for the product increases, price will rise more resources will be devoted to its production. If demand for the product decreases, price will fall fewer resources will be devoted to its production.
Explanation: One mark for more resources devoted to products in high demand / fewer resources devoted to products in low demand.

Question Text: Analyse the benefits a country may gain from exporting to and importing from other countries.
Markscheme: Allows countries to specialise in what they are best at producing develop skills increase efficiency. Exports create demand for the output of domestic producers causing economic growth in that country creating employment. Exports create larger markets able to take advantage of economies of scale produce at a lower average cost. Incomes in other countries maybe higher allowing more income to be earned by exporting. Exports are a component of total (aggregate) demand so increased exports will lead to higher output / cause economic growth. Imports allow consumers to enjoy more goods and services greater choice / variety at low prices increase living standards. Imports provide greater competition which can increase quality. Imports allow countries to obtain goods / raw materials they cannot produce at a lower price reducing costs of production obtain tariff revenue. Spread of ideas / technology increasing productivity.
Explanation: Reward but do not expect reference to comparative or absolute advantage.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in government spending will benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase total demand; some forms of government spending e.g. education / healthcare may increase productivity; reduce unemployment; increase economic growth; reduce inequality; increase access to merit goods; provide public goods. Why it might not: increased inflation; increase a current account deficit if spent on e.g. imported capital goods; increased taxation to pay for increased spending; increase government debt; government spending may be inefficient – the private sector may be more efficient.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Swiss economy: inflation, subsidies for solar energy, current account surplus, strong currency and bank merger
Question Text: Source material about the Swiss economy
Calculate the total financial sector contribution (in $) to Switzerland’s GDP.
Markscheme: $72.7 billion
Explanation: Accept $73 billion or 73 billion. $72 720 000 000. 7.27 × 10^10

Question Text: Identify two microeconomic policy measures.
Markscheme: maximum prices; subsidies
Explanation: Accept organising mergers. If more than two suggested measures are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain one reason why overdependence on foreign markets is a disadvantage to the Swiss economy.
Markscheme: Makes it more exposed to external shocks as anything that happens in other countries could also affect the Swiss economy, for example reduced exports / increased import prices / potential shortages.
Explanation: One mark for the reason and one mark for an explanation.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show how a subsidy to solar energy producers would affect the market for solar energy.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New supply curve shifted to the right; Equilibrium – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Explain two reasons why Switzerland had a current account surplus.
Markscheme: low inflation price of Swiss exports relatively lower, leading to increased exports / price of domestically produced goods relatively lower leading to decreased imports; high-quality products demand for Swiss exports higher, leading to increased exports / demand for domestically produced goods higher, leading to decreased imports; strong currency has kept cost of imported raw materials in Switzerland low, reducing costs of production.
Explanation: One mark for each of two reasons identified and one mark each for each of two explanations. If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between the global GDP growth rate and the change in the value of the Swiss franc.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: generally negative / inverse relationship as the global growth rate falls, the value of the Swiss Franc rises / as the global growth rate rises, the value of the Swiss Franc falls. Supporting evidence: fall in global growth rate led to rise in the value of Swiss Franc from 2018-2019 or 2019-2020, rise in global growth rate led to fall in the value of Swiss Franc from 2020-2021. Analysis: Swiss Franc's status as a 'safe haven' currency when investors are unsure about the economy, they usually keep their money in Swiss francs. Exception: 2021-2022 where global growth was falling but Swiss Franc was stable / rising only slightly. Analysis for exception: Other reasons could affect the value of Swiss Franc, for example Switzerland was also affected by the Covid-19 pandemic.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected / normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in interest rates will harm the Swiss economy.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: cost of borrowing increases for firms increasing total costs reducing profits firms may invest less may have to shut down unemployment rises decreasing incomes decreasing total demand decreasing economic growth consumers may borrow less save more less spending demand for goods and services decreases as returns from savings increase decreasing revenues of firms decreasing profits Government borrowing costs more leaving less funding available for e.g. education / health. Reasons why it might not: hot money inflows strengthen the Swiss Franc reduce cost of e.g. imported raw materials reducing inflation increasing export competitiveness reduced demand-pull inflationary pressures as cost of borrowing increases decreasing demand for loans increasing saving / decreasing consumption investment decreasing total demand firms may reduce prices increasing affordability of goods and services for consumers costs of production may decrease reducing cost-push inflationary pressures.
Explanation: Falling profits can only be rewarded once.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a bank merger will benefit Swiss consumers and workers.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: to avoid one of them collapsing reducing confidence in the whole banking system greater job security less chance of bank customers losing their savings; economies of scale as output increases (average) costs fall prices may decrease goods and services more affordable for consumers; the merged firm may make higher profits able to pay higher wages to workers who remain employed; workers in merged firm can share skills improving efficiency. Reasons why it might not: monopoly power may increase restricting supply decreasing consumer choice increasing prices decreasing quality; workers may lose their jobs as the bank might shut down some operations / reduce duplication increasing unemployment; the merged firm may be too large / experience diseconomies of scale example reducing efficiency.
Explanation: Award a maximum of 4 marks if candidates only mention one of consumers or workers. Reward, but do not expect reference to inelasticity of demand in monopoly.

Stimulus / Scenario: Guyana’s rapid GDP growth driven by primary sector expansion and government spending on development projects
Question Text: Scenario about economic growth and housing prices
Identify two examples of primary sector industries.
Markscheme: farming / agriculture; mining; fishing; forestry
Explanation: If more than two primary industries are given, consider the first three. Accept two specific examples, e.g. growing wheat, catching tuna, but not from the same industry.

Question Text: Explain two causes of rising GDP.
Markscheme: increased consumption decrease in taxes such as income tax / corporation tax increased disposable income / profits; increase in investment leading to increase in total demand; increase in government spending e.g. spending on infrastructure increasing total (aggregate) demand in the economy; improvements in technology increase in productivity; increase in the quantity and quality of factors of production e.g. increase in size of labour force improved labour skills increase in productive capacity of the economy; decrease in interest rates cost of borrowing falls borrowing increases; decreases in exchange rate price of exports falls / price of imports rises increase exports / decrease imports / increase net exports.
Explanation: One mark each for two causes identified and one each for two explanations. Only reward increase in total demand once.

Question Text: Analyse why house prices may increase.
Markscheme: Increase in demand due to lower interest rates increasing borrowing for house purchases higher population higher incomes increasing ability to purchase houses higher confidence increasing willingness to buy houses. Decrease in supply reduction in subsidies on new house building increased taxation on housing government building fewer houses stricter planning regulation land is used for other things e.g. infrastructure. Increasing cost of production of new houses cost of labour cost of raw materials cost of land increased costs passed on to buyers of houses as higher prices. Less government intervention removal of price controls.
Explanation: MAXIMUM 4 MARKS for identification of reasons without analysis in terms of demand and / or supply.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not increased government spending on education will lead to economic development.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase skills / qualifications / knowledge; increase job opportunities; encourage more investments; increase productive capacity; reduce consumption of demerit goods; improved health / life expectancy; higher HDI. Why it might not: takes a long time to have an impact on the economy; financial cost of government spending e.g. taxation / borrowing; opportunity cost e.g. health spending; reduced benefit to the economy if there is emigration of skilled workers / net migration is negative; government might spend the money inefficiently; education programmes may be ineffective.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Japan’s long periods of deflation and policy attempts to stimulate economic growth
Question Text: Scenario about fiscal and monetary policy
Identify two fiscal policy measures.
Markscheme: government spending; taxation
Explanation: If more than two measures are given, consider the first three. Accept two types of spending, or two types of taxes. Allow contractionary expansionary measures.

Question Text: Explain two influences on productivity.
Markscheme: Quality of factors of production better quality improves efficiency / poor quality reduces efficiency; Working conditions / wages influencing worker motivation; technology machine speed / effects on capital productivity; education skilled workers / effects on labour productivity; Healthcare healthier workers capable of producing higher output / sick workers take time off work; Effectiveness of institutions amount of bureaucracy affects efficiency; use of resources whether wasted or used efficiently.
Explanation: One mark each for two influences identified and one mark each for two explanations. If more than two influences are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse reasons why central banks are important for an economy.
Markscheme: control inflation by implementing monetary policy such as changes in interest rates prevent prices from rising too much prevents deterioration of purchasing power / affordability of goods and services prevent deflation; maintain financial stability monitoring health of banks preventing collapse of banks assuring savings are protected ensuring consumers have access to credit ensuring liquidity of the economy; provision of economic information which can help decision-makers; buying / selling currency to influence exchange rates; lenders of last resort providing liquidity to commercial banks.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not deflation is a problem for an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might be a problem: decreases confidence in the economy; deflationary cycle; loss of jobs; delays in spending reduce demand; lower profits for firms from lower prices. Why it might not be a problem: decreases prices, increasing affordability for consumers; if it is caused by decreases in cost, problems for the economy are less significant; increase in international competitiveness; it depends on the extent and duration of the deflation.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Lanzarote tourism growth reducing unemployment and attracting workers through low taxes and improved labour mobility
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets
Identify two non-wage factors that may influence a worker’s job choice.
Markscheme: job security; fringe benefits; recognition at work; company car; holidays; working hours; flexibility / ability to work from home; location of workplace; available transport; nearness to family
Explanation: Accept any relevant non-wage factors. If more than two non-wage factors are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain how unemployment can lead to poverty.
Markscheme: unemployment may lead to a loss of a source of income unable to afford basic necessities leading to absolute poverty may reduce ability to provide a good standard of living for family poor education health problems child poverty; long term unemployment loss of skills cannot re-enter the job market unable to earn a living; the unemployed may claim unemployment benefits with lower income than those working leading to relative poverty; unemployment will lead reduced (income) tax revenue less available to spend on public services that reduce poverty.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the influences on the mobility of labour.
Markscheme: labour could be occupationally mobile influenced by skills / qualification / knowledge if they are transferable then labour is more occupationally mobile; labour could be geographically mobile influenced by government regulations e.g. visa requirements cost of living in another area / country access to transport access to public transport / proximity to railway / ports family ties may make workers immobile access to working online / ability to work from home house prices in different areas / access to housing in different areas low house prices make it easier to move for work incomes / tax systems in other countries proficiency in another language influencing workers to move abroad.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not low taxes are beneficial for an economy.
Markscheme: Why they might be: low income taxes increase disposable income, increasing total (aggregate) demand; low corporate income (corporation) taxes increase profits after tax; low indirect taxes, e.g. VAT, reduce prices of goods and services; low taxes on imports reduce prices of imports; low taxes on firms encourage competition; low taxes on demerit goods increase external costs; low income taxes prevent a 'brain drain' / attract foreign workers; low corporate taxes attract overseas companies e.g. MNCs. Why they might not be: government revenue will be low – budget deficit / debt; not enough revenue to provide for public / merit goods; e.g. education / healthcare; higher inequality if progressive taxes are low; higher inflation if direct taxes are low; low taxes on merit goods improve external benefits.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Street vendors entering the formal economy increasing tax revenue and productivity
Question Text: Scenario about regulation and economic growth
Identify two reasons why workers may choose to work in the formal economy.
Markscheme: higher incomes in the formal economy; may have access to employment protection; may be covered by a national minimum wage; may be able to join a trade union; may be entitled to receive unemployment benefit; may be entitled to retirement benefits / pensions; will have a formal income record needed for e.g. bank loans; formal economy may be regulated by government.
Explanation: Accept any relevant reason. If more than two suggested measures are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two effects of economic growth on a country’s population.
Markscheme: more goods and services available / greater ability to purchase health care higher living standards / life expectancy less poverty; higher employment / more job opportunities more income; more choice due to more firms starting up / expanding; lower inflation if due to higher productivity / lower costs / more competition; higher tax revenue allows more government spending on e.g. education / health; higher prices due to increasing total demand; more pollution / external costs due to higher output of manufactured goods; depletion of non-renewable resources reducing future economic growth; attracts immigrants / deters emigrants due to benefits from high living standards e.g. job opportunities.
Explanation: One mark each for effects identified and one mark each for explanations. If more than two effects are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how government regulation can reduce market failure.
Markscheme: market failure occurs when there is an inefficient allocation of resources in a free market. laws / rules can be used: to promote the use of merit goods that are under-consumed e.g. making it compulsory to wear seat belts in a car; to reduce the consumption of demerit goods that are over-consumed e.g. banning smoking in public places; to ensure minimum standards for goods and services e.g. making it compulsory for restaurants to reach certain standards of cleanliness can overcome the problem of sellers having more information than buyers; to prevent abuse of monopoly power by imposing maximum prices e.g. a limit can be imposed on the price that can be charged by a monopoly water company; to reduce exploitation of labour by imposing minimum wages.
Explanation: Only reward answers that refer to laws and regulations, not taxes and subsidies.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in the number of firms will benefit an economy.
Markscheme: Why it might: may increase competition; higher competition may lower costs and prices and increase quality; may increase choice; may reduce monopoly power; more firms may increase output; higher output may increase employment and living standards. Why it might not: firms may be smaller; may not be able to take advantage of economies of scale; may be wasteful duplication; may have lower profits and so invest less, lower innovation.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Japan labour shortages, ageing population, immigration, savings behaviour and air travel demand
Question Text: Source material about labour shortages in Japan
Calculate foreign workers as a percentage of Japan’s labour force in 2022.
Explanation: Accept 2.5 without %

Question Text: Identify two reasons why Japan has a high average age of population.
Markscheme: Low birth rate; high life expectancy; good healthcare; young adults moving abroad.
Explanation: If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain why some young Japanese people emigrate.
Markscheme: To gain high wages enjoy a better living standard / able to purchase more goods and services.
Explanation: One mark for identification and one mark for explanation.

Question Text: Explain two ways Japan could attract more foreign workers.
Markscheme: remove the Japanese language test more foreigners would be able to work in the country; allow foreign workers to bring their families with them foreign workers are more likely to settle in the country if they are not separated from their families; allow unskilled workers to stay for more than five years if gain high wages / enjoy living in the country would not want to leave / attract workers who want to live permanently in the country / give workers the opportunity to stay permanently / increase international mobility of labour.
Explanation: One mark each for two ways identified and one mark each for two explanations. If more than two ways are given, consider the first three. Only the 3 listed identifications can be credited, and explanation marks can only be awarded if applied to a valid identification.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of a decrease in population on the market for furniture.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New demand curve shifted to the left; OR New supply curve shifted to the left; OR New demand curve shifted to the left AND new supply curve shifted to the left; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between global GDP per head and global air passenger numbers.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Generally, a positive relationship the higher the global GDP head, the higher the air passenger numbers. Supporting evidence: 2016–2019 GDP per head rose and air passenger numbers increased. Analysis of expected relationship: Higher GDP per head increases purchasing power / ability to pay for air travel as people get richer, they travel more for leisure and business. Exception: 2021 GDP per head rose but air passenger numbers decreased. Analysis of exception: May have been a time delay in people gaining confidence in flying after COVID-19 pandemic / other influences on demand e.g. air fares.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected / normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Supporting evidence should involve interpretation, not just description.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a high savings rate is likely to benefit Japan.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: may keep inflation low as spending will not be high reduce (total) demand reduce demand-pull inflation; may reduce imports improve the current account of the balance of payments; provide finance for investment firms able to borrow from banks; may enable households to finance future expenditure on e.g. healthcare / education / provide for retirement / provide support for individuals in difficult times; may enable government to cut benefits, e.g. pensions spend money saved on other priorities, e.g. healthcare. Reasons why it might not: may lower total demand reduce GDP increase unemployment cyclical unemployment could cause a recession; may reduce investment as demand is low reduce improvements in technology; Less indirect tax raised may lead to reduced government spending.
Explanation: Reference to AD / total demand falling can be credited on either side of the argument, but only once.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not labour mobility in Japan is likely to increase in the future.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: Improvements in education and training could increase skills / qualifications increase occupational mobility; Improvements in transport / subsidised travel could enable people to move to different areas raise geographical mobility. Reasons why it might not: Ageing population may have out-of-date skills more difficult to change jobs reduce occupational mobility may be more reluctant to move to a different area reason reduce geographical mobility; House prices may rise making it difficult to move from areas with low house prices to areas with high prices.
Explanation: Reference to occupational and geographical mobility can be credited on either side of the argument, but only once. Reference to falling house prices or improved transport must be linked to improved mobility for credit and vice versa.

Stimulus / Scenario: Belgium markets, price elasticity of demand and workers’ training
Question Text: Scenario about market equilibrium and productivity
Define market equilibrium.
Markscheme: Demand equalling supply.
Explanation: No shortage no surplus. Balance of demand and supply.

Question Text: Explain two ways an economy could reach a point outside its current PPC.
Markscheme: Increase investment devoting more resources to capital goods; Encourage immigration increase size of the labour force; Land reclamation creating more land on which to e.g. grow crops; Increase in education and training raise quality / efficiency / productivity of labour; Increase spending on research and development / new technology increase quality / efficiency / productivity of capital; Discovery of sources of resources example/enables extra output to be produced.
Explanation: One mark each for two ways identified and one mark each for two explanations. If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how price elasticity of demand can influence a firm’s pricing decisions.
Markscheme: Price elasticity of demand is a measure of the responsiveness of demand to a change in price. If demand is elastic, a rise in price will cause a greater percentage change in demand may indicate the firm's product has close substitutes / is not a necessity / takes a significant proportion of consumers' incomes may encourage firms to lower price raise revenue. If demand is inelastic, a rise in price will cause a smaller percentage change in demand may indicate the firm's product has few substitutes / is a necessity / takes a small proportion of consumers' incomes may encourage firms to raise prices revenue would increase.
Explanation: Reward, but do not expect: Facilitate price discrimination charging higher prices in markets with inelastic demand valid example, e.g. higher rail fares at peak times.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not firms benefit from their workers undertaking training.
Markscheme: Why they might: may increase productivity; may enable workers to work with more advanced technology; may lower costs of production; may be able to fill promoted posts; reduce waste; raise quality of output; training may be provided by the government at no direct cost for firms; increase workers motivation, reducing labour turnover; increase flexibility to cover absences. Why they might not: if the firms provide the training, increase costs / opportunity cost; output may fall while workers are being trained; training may not be successful; trained workers may demand higher wages; trained workers may leave to work for other firms - other firms will gain the benefits of the training.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: International cosmetics firm merger and sustainable production
Question Text: Scenario about economies of scale and taxation
Define average total cost.
Markscheme: Total cost divided by output.
Explanation: Cost per unit.

Question Text: Explain how using renewable raw materials may affect a firm’s profits.
Markscheme: Using renewable resources may be more expensive increase costs of production reduce profits; Can advertise that renewable resources which create less pollution / improve environmental sustainability are being used increase image of the firm raise demand increase profits; Using renewable resources may be subsidised. May become cheaper to use renewable resources in the long run; Resources will not be depleted / firms have a constant supply of raw materials raise profits.
Explanation: A simple statement that profits rise or fall with no relevant explanation is 0 marks. Profits rising and profits falling can both be credited if each is explained.

Question Text: Analyse the internal economies of scale that a cosmetics firm could experience.
Markscheme: Buying / purchasing economies buy ingredients in bulk at a discount; Labour economies workers specialising e.g. designing the packaging; Managerial economies e.g. specialist human resource manager; Technical economies advanced technology could be used to produce perfume; Financial economies the firm finding it cheaper / easier to borrow from banks; Research and development economies develop new perfumes; Selling / marketing economies lower average advertising costs; Risk-bearing economies selling perfume in more markets.
Explanation: Maximum of 3 marks for a list of different types of internal economies of scale with no development / explanation.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not perfume should be taxed at a higher rate than food.
Markscheme: Why it should: perfume is a luxury whereas food is a necessity; rich spend more on perfume than the poor; taxing food more may increase poverty as poor spend a high proportion of their income on food; perfume is less likely than food to be a merit good; a higher price for perfume will not significantly contribute to inflation, not a significant item in CPI; perfume may be imported while food may be supplied by domestic firms. Why it should not: demand for perfume may be elastic and so may not raise much revenue. In contrast, inelastic demand for food so may raise high tax revenue; perfume may be an important domestic industry. Higher taxes may result in closure / unemployment; some food is a demerit good e.g. food with a high sugar content; taxing demerit food may improve health; taxing food may reduce obesity.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Government and central bank policies to reduce unemployment and maintain price stability
Question Text: Scenario about macroeconomic policy targets
Identify two policy measures a central bank could use to maintain price stability.
Markscheme: the rate of interest; the money supply; the exchange rate; quantitative easing / tightening.
Explanation: If more than two measures are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two benefits to an economy of full employment.
Markscheme: High output / GDP maximum use of labour resource; High tax revenue government could spend on e.g. education / healthcare; Low spending on state benefits can keep taxes low / money saved can be spent on other things; Low poverty / increase incomes enable people to purchase basic necessities / which could increase living standards.
Explanation: One mark each for two benefits identified and one mark each for two explanations.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in government spending on building houses could reduce unemployment.
Markscheme: An increase in government spending on building homes may increase demand for building workers may increase demand for building materials demand for building materials workers / other workers related to building industry labour is a derived demand reduce structural unemployment increase incomes increase consumer expenditure increase total demand reduce cyclical unemployment increase GDP / economic growth may increase quality of homes improve health fewer workers may lose their jobs because of illness increase labour productivity which may increase demand for labour increase supply of houses reduce price of houses improve mobility of labour.
Explanation: Reward, but do not expect reference to multiplier / multiplier effects.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not an increase in a country’s economic growth rate will increase its HDI value.
Markscheme: Why it might: increase in GDP may increase GDP per head if increase in GDP is greater than any rise in population; higher GDP per head may increase spending on good nutrition / good housing and as a result increase life expectancy; higher GDP per head may increase tax revenue, enabling a government to spend on education and healthcare; higher GDP per head may enable families to keep their children in education for longer. Why it might not: higher GDP per head may result in more spending on demerit goods, lowering life expectancy; may lead to inflation hindering access to healthcare and education; and increase inequality in the distribution of income; may be the result of higher military expenditure which will not increase HDI; income may become more unevenly distributed, meaning life expectancy and years of education of the poor may fall; more pollution may be created, lowering life expectancy; working hours and stress may increase, lowering life expectancy.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Cambodia economic policy, dumping, infant industries and current account deficit
Question Text: Scenario about trade and macroeconomic policy
Identify two motives for a firm dumping some of its products in a foreign market.
Markscheme: Drive domestic firms out of the market / increase market share / gain monopoly power in (overseas) market; Clear away surplus stock keep price high on the domestic market increase exports / sales / revenue.
Explanation: If more than two motives are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain two differences between monetary policy and supply-side policy.
Markscheme: Monetary policy is usually implemented by the central bank supply-side policy by the government; Monetary policy may be used to increase or reduce economic activity supply-side policy is only used to increase economic activity; They have different policy measures e.g. monetary policy has the rate of interest whereas supply side policy has privatisation; Monetary policy aims to influence total demand supply-side policy aims to increase total supply; Supply side policies tend to take longer to implement and have an impact than monetary policy which tends to have a more short-term impact.
Explanation: If more than two differences are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how a government subsidy could help to protect an infant industry against foreign competition.
Markscheme: An infant industry's costs may initially be high government subsidy is an extra payment to an industry some of the subsidy could be used to buy more capital goods / technological innovation employ skilled workers enable an infant industry to grow in size / increase output obtain economies of scale lower (average) cost / improve productivity lower their prices making their products more (internationally) competitive increase demand for their goods increase exports raise quality increase quality competitiveness.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not cuts in taxes will reduce a deficit on the current account of a country’s balance of payments.
Markscheme: Why it might: cut in corporation (corporate income) tax may increase willingness and ability to invest; higher investment may raise quality and reduce costs; increase exports and lower imports; cut in personal income tax may increase workers' motivation, raise productivity and cut costs; lower corporation (corporate income) tax could be imposed on infant industries; attract MNCs which can increase exports and reduce imports; cuts in indirect taxes may lead to lower prices which may increase the demand for domestic goods and reduce the demand for imported substitutes; cuts in tariffs may make it easier for firms to import cheaper raw materials from abroad which may enable them to reduce prices increasing export competitiveness. Why it might not: cut in personal income tax may increase spending which may increase total demand leading to inflation reducing international competitiveness; more imports may be purchased; exports may be diverted to the home market; reduce tax revenue and lower government spending; lower government spending on education, healthcare, transport could reduce productivity; lower taxes on imports (tariffs) may increase imports.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Malawi economy: agriculture dependence, natural disasters, electricity access, solar energy potential and exchange rate depreciation
Question Text: Source material about changes in the Malawian economy
Calculate the number of Malawians who had access to electricity in 2022.
Explanation: Accept 3.3 × 10^6.

Question Text: Identify two capital goods used in Malawi.
Markscheme: Factories; power stations; solar panels; electricity; fertilisers.
Explanation: If more than two capital goods are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Explain what is likely to have happened to Malawi’s production possibility curve (PPC) in January 2022.
