Markscheme: It is likely to have shifted to the left / inside / inwards / contract due to natural disasters / Storm (Ana) / resources destroyed / productive capacity (or land) reduced / less labour.
Explanation: Accept an accurate and fully labelled PPC diagram showing the PPC shifting to the left for the first mark. Second mark for any relevant reason from the source material.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why the supply of Malawian tea may increase.
Markscheme: good weather conditions would increase crop yields / prevent crops being destroyed; government subsidies provide a financial incentive / additional payment to that received from consumers / reduce costs of production; fall in price of fertiliser which reduces cost of production / increase crop yields / allows farmers to purchase more fertiliser; fall in exchange rate higher demand for Malawian tea.
Explanation: One mark each for two reasons identified and one mark each for two explanations. If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three. Do not accept an answer that links tea with sugar.

Question Text: Draw a demand and supply diagram to show the effect of a report stating the health risks of consuming sugar on the market for sugar.
Markscheme: Axes correctly labelled – price and quantity or p and q; Original demand and supply curves correctly labelled; New labelled demand curve shifted to the left; Equilibriums – shown by lines P1 and Q1 and P2 and Q2 or equilibrium points marked as E1 and E2.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Analyse the relationship between GDP per head and the percentage of children who complete primary education.
Markscheme: Expected relationship: Generally, a direct / positive relationship the higher the GDP per head, the higher the percentage of children who complete primary education. Supporting evidence: Four countries with the highest GDP per head had the highest percentage of children who complete primary education / Malawi / Uganda have the lowest of both. South Africa had the highest GDP per head and the highest percentage of children who complete primary education. Comparison of two countries (e.g. Namibia and Senegal where GDP falls as does percentage of children completing primary education). Analysis of the expected relationship: Parents in countries with high incomes are more likely to be able to afford to keep their children in primary education. Government tax revenue is likely to be higher in countries with high incomes and so will be able to spend more on primary education. Completing primary education may increase the chance of skilled / occupationally mobile high income earning workers. Exception: Malawi / Uganda Malawi has a lower GDP per head but a higher percentage of children who complete primary education than Uganda. Analysis of exception: A higher percentage of children may complete primary education but the quality of the education may be low leading to a low GDP per head / GDP per head may be high but there may be a high degree of inequality with a relatively high proportion of the population unable to keep their children in primary education.
Explanation: Responses do not have to be in the format suggested but they should address the expected / normal relationship, offer supporting evidence of that, highlight any exceptions to that, and analyse the overall data. Supporting evidence should involve interpretation, not just description e.g. simply quoting data without reference to high or low.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not Malawi should develop a solar energy industry.
Markscheme: Reasons why it might: a sustainable source of energy enables future generations to access energy would create less pollution / external costs than burning wood and charcoal / improve air quality reduce deforestation improve health may enable more Malawian households to gain access to electricity increase living standards / reduce inequality setting up solar panels creates jobs leads to increased output / economic growth / access to electricity for Malawian firms long hours / 3000 hours of sunshine a good / viable source of energy less need to import energy reduce current account deficit develops employment opportunities raising incomes. Reasons why it might not: opportunity cost of installing example; opportunity cost of land use reduced agricultural output; dependent on weather / sunshine and days lost unstable supply may experience storm damage / natural disasters; visual pollution might reduce tourism / reduce house prices; cost of developing an electricity distribution network may be prohibitive; cost of switching from fossil-fuel burning domestic appliances to electrical ones may not be affordable to households limiting the impact of the switch; expensive to install worsen government budget position; solar panels could be damaged by storms making them inefficient.
Explanation: None

Question Text: Discuss whether or not a government should try to stop its country’s foreign exchange rate falling in value.
Markscheme: Reasons why it should try to stop it falling in value (disadvantages of the fall): will increase import prices reducing imports may increase cost of imported raw materials and capital goods cost cause cost-push inflation cause slow economic growth; may reduce pressure on domestic firms to keep prices low quality high; may reduce confidence in the economy reduce investment increase debt. Reasons why it should allow a fall in value (advantages of the fall): will reduce export prices increase exports reduce imports; make domestic goods more competitive reduce a current account deficit / improve the balance of payments; may increase economic growth reduce unemployment attract MNCs / attract investment increase tourism; opportunity cost / costly for government to intervene e.g. less funding for education / healthcare / housing; may increase government tax revenue.
Explanation: Note: many candidates provide incorrect analysis e.g. wrong way around. Maximum of 3 marks if there is no development / evaluation of reasons given.

Stimulus / Scenario: Swiss multinational food company increasing prices across countries
Question Text: Scenario about multinational companies and opportunity cost
Define multinational company.
Markscheme: A firm that is based / has its headquarters in one country. Operates / produces / sets up / works in another country or countries.
Explanation: No mark for an example of an MNC.

Question Text: Explain two reasons why a firm may charge a different price for the same product in two different countries.
Markscheme: demand may be higher / lower in one country resulting in higher / lower prices; supply maybe higher / lower in one country resulting in lower prices; higher income / GNP per head in one country means more able to afford to pay higher price; price elasticity of demand may vary more power to raise price where demand is inelastic; greater competition in one country / more substitutes leads to lower prices to be competitive; indirect tax rates may vary e.g. higher GST / VAT / import tariffs will lead to higher price; government subsidies may be given in some countries lowering price; local material costs / wages may be lower in one country lowering costs of production mean lower prices; different transport costs may be cheaper to transport to countries close by; quality of good may be higher enable firm to charge a higher price.
Explanation: One mark each for two reasons identified and one mark each for two explanations. If more than two reasons are given, consider the first three. Note: There will be other relevant reasons. To get the development mark there must be a justification of why that factor will affect prices. Do not accept answers based on foreign exchange rate.

Question Text: Analyse how opportunity cost influences the decisions made by consumers, workers and producers.
Markscheme: opportunity cost is the (next) best alternative forgone / given up; example of consumers e.g. must decide between buying different products due to limited income; example of workers e.g. must decide between different jobs or between work and leisure as time is limited; example of producers / firms e.g. must decide what to make / how to make it / where to locate as they have limited (financial) resources.
Explanation: Maximum of 4 marks if candidates do not cover at least two out of consumers / workers / producers.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not consumers would benefit from an increase in the number of small firms in an industry.
Markscheme: Why they might: more competition, lower price, higher quality; small firms may provide a more personal service; may produce niche products; may be more flexible / react quickly to consumer needs; may introduce new ideas / new varieties of products; may be easier access – consumers may not have to travel so far. Why they might not: may be wasteful duplication; may waste consumers time with too much information to process; may have higher costs as unable to take advantage of economies of scale; may reduce profits of larger firms, reducing their spending on research and development; may not stay in business for long.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Central banks, commercial banks and technological change in banking
Question Text: Scenario about banking services and division of labour
Identify two ways central banks differ from commercial banks.
Markscheme: in the public sector / owned by the government; operate monetary policy; main objective is usually price stability; hold the accounts of the government; lend to governments and sometimes commercial banks; manage foreign exchange rate. OR: usually in the private sector / owned by individuals; provide services for consumers; main objective is profit maximization or growth; hold the accounts of firms and households; lend to households and firms; buy and sell foreign currency to firms and households.
Explanation: If more than two ways are given, consider the first three. The second mark must be a linked difference and not a negative comment e.g. central bank controls money supply, but commercial banks do not, only gets one mark.

Question Text: Explain two characteristics that coins possess which mean they can perform the functions of money.
Markscheme: generally acceptable can be used to buy and sell products / acts as medium of exchange; limited in supply minted by the government; durable last some time / does not perish / hard to break / made of metal / acts as store of wealth / store of value; portable coins can be carried in pockets / bags; homogeneous all coins of the same value are the same; divisible coins have different values / acts as medium of exchange.
Explanation: One mark each for two characteristics identified and one mark each for two developments. If more than two characteristics are given, consider the first three. Only accept functions of money when used as a development of an identified characteristic of money.

Question Text: Analyse the services provided to households by commercial banks.
Markscheme: enable households to save their money provide deposit / time accounts / deposit boxes earn interest; enable households to make and receive payments provide current / sight accounts credit cards; lend to households by providing a loan / overdraft / mortgage to e.g. buy a house / a car pay interest; give financial advice e.g. on how to manage debt / retirement plans / buy stocks and shares; arrange insurance e.g. for house / car; Convert foreign currency buying / selling goods and service abroad / travelling abroad charge commission.
Explanation: Do not reward more than three services as the main assessment objective is analysis, not knowledge and understanding.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not division of labour will benefit workers.
Markscheme: Why they might: can be trained quickly; rise in productivity might be linked to a rise in income; can become skilled at a particular task – practice makes perfect; can concentrate on what they are most interested in; can gain confidence in the task they undertake – less stressful. Why they might not: can be boring; occupational mobility is limited; may not make full use of their skills; may be replaced by machinery; work may be disrupted by absence of other staff.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.

Stimulus / Scenario: Chad poverty, population structure and balance of payments deficit
Question Text: Scenario about trade deficits and population pyramid
Define primary income.
Markscheme: Component of the current account of the balance of payments. Example of an item: profit / interest / dividends / wages earned abroad.
Explanation: Very few candidates understand this term. Award one mark if there is any understanding that it relates to the balance of payments with other countries.

Question Text: Explain two influences on the shape of a country’s population pyramid.
Markscheme: birth rate / fertility rate a high birth rate / fertility rate will result in a wide base for the pyramid; life expectancy / death rate / aging population high death rate / low life expectancy will result in a narrow top for the pyramid; migration high immigration of people of working age would result in a wide middle in the pyramid; gender balance example of imbalance in overall shape of pyramid.
Explanation: One mark each for two influences identified and one mark each for two explanations. If more than two influences are given, consider the first three. Note: accept low birth rate, high life expectancy, emigration with reverse explanation.

Question Text: Analyse the causes of poverty.
Markscheme: Low quantity / quality of education can reduce job opportunities low skilled jobs low paid jobs; Unemployment lower income reliant on welfare benefits; Illness / disability poor healthcare reduce ability to earn an income; Old age may have low or no state benefits absolute poverty; Uneven distribution of income / wealth resulting in relative poverty; Corruption reducing tax revenue available to spend on reducing poverty; Large families many children trapped in / child poverty; Single parent families parent unable to work reliant on state benefits; Poor housing harms health limited access to employment high cost of living; Natural disaster / conflict causes loss of ability to work / earn income; Geographical immobility prevents access to paid jobs; Work in agriculture where wages are low / not regular income; High inflation cannot afford to buy basic necessities.
Explanation: Answer requires more than one cause of poverty to get full marks. Reference to absolute and relative poverty may be relevant to more than one bullet point but only reward each once. Maximum of three causes can be given a mark.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not imposing import quotas will reduce a trade in goods deficit.
Markscheme: None
Explanation: None

Stimulus / Scenario: Canadian immigration policy and labour shortages
Question Text: Scenario about labour markets and economic growth
Identify two causes of an increase in life expectancy.
Markscheme: falling death rate / more / better healthcare / healthier lifestyles; better education; higher (disposable) income; improvement in nutrition; more / better housing / living standards; better environment / less pollution; better working conditions; less child poverty; ending of a war / famine / natural disaster.
Explanation: If more than two causes are given, consider the first three. There may be other relevant causes. These are the most common ones.

Question Text: Explain two ways a firm could attract skilled workers from another country.
Markscheme: offer higher wages / prospects of promotion enabling the workers to enjoy high living standards; offer better working conditions e.g. regular breaks; offer fringe benefits e.g. good pension scheme; offer training increase chance of a good job in home country later; strong / targeted marketing overseas highlighting benefits of job; meet relocation costs removes geographical barrier; reputation e.g. good employer / outstanding product/service.
Explanation: One mark each for two ways identified and one mark each for two explanations. If more than two ways are given, consider the first three.

Question Text: Analyse how an increase in consumer spending could increase economic growth.
Markscheme: increase total demand this may increase confidence may encourage firms to increase their output firms' revenue / profits increase leads to more investment / innovation new firms may start up raise GDP; increase output, firms may buy capital goods / invest this will increase productive capacity; increase output, more workers may be employed this will reduce unemployment increase incomes increase consumer spending further; increase (indirect) tax revenue which can be spent on e.g. infrastructure / education / healthcare leading to further increase in productive capacity / GDP.
Explanation: Only reward increasing productive capacity and raising GDP once. Reward but do not expect reference to the multiplier effect.

Question Text: Discuss whether or not the wages of workers employed by car firms will increase in the future.
Markscheme: Why they might: demand for cars might rise; revenue / profit may increase, enabling firms to pay more; car workers may become more skilled / more productive; bargaining strength to car workers may increase / their trade unions may become more powerful / inflation rate may increase causing higher wage demands; governments may subsidise car production. Why they might not: advances in technology may reduce the need for car workers; rise in unemployment will increase competition among workers for jobs; increased immigration may increase the supply of car workers; demand for cars may fall if public (mass) transport improves; inflation may reduce real wages.
Explanation: Levels of response: Level 3 (6-8 marks) for reasoned discussion examining both sides; Level 2 (3-5 marks) for reasoned discussion, may be one-sided; Level 1 (1-2 marks) for simple attempt.
